topics.title: Introduction to the Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to the Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Embedded System?

An **Embedded System** is a purpose-built computing system that integrates hardware and software (firmware) to perform a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electronic system. Unlike a general-purpose computer (like a PC or laptop), an embedded system is **task-specific**, **resource-constrained**, and operates under **real-time constraints**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> An embedded system is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based system designed to perform a specific task, where the hardware and software are tightly coupled, and the system is part of a larger device that is *not* typically regarded as a computer.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **washing machine**. It contains a microcontroller that runs a fixed set of programs — wash, rinse, spin, dry. It does not browse the internet or play games. Its **entire purpose** is to wash clothes. The microcontroller, sensors (water level, temperature), and actuators (motor, valve) are all *embedded* inside the machine, invisible to the user. That invisible, dedicated "brain" is an **embedded system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - A **Computer** is *general-purpose* — it can run any software.
> - An **Embedded System** is *special-purpose* — it runs only the firmware it was designed for.

## 1.2 What is a Microcontroller?

A **Microcontroller (MCU)** is a single-chip computer (an IC) that contains:
- A **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**
- **Program Memory** (ROM/Flash)
- **Data Memory** (RAM)
- **I/O Ports**
- **Timers/Counters**
- **Communication peripherals** (UART, SPI, I²C)
- **Analog peripherals** (ADC, DAC — in some MCUs)

All integrated onto **one silicon die**. This is why it is often called a **"System on Chip" (SoC)** in modern terminology.

### The Famous Formula

$$
\text{Microcontroller} = \text{CPU} + \text{Memory (ROM + RAM)} + \text{I/O} + \text{Peripherals}
$$

## 1.3 Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller

| Feature | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | External (off-chip) | Internal (on-chip) |
| Peripherals | External | Internal |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Power Consumption | High | Low |
| Use case | PC, Laptop, Server | Washing machine, Car ECU, IoT sensor |
| Bit-width examples | 32-bit, 64-bit | 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit |
| Clock speed | High (GHz) | Lower (MHz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam:** If a question says "with a single chip" or "all peripherals on-chip" → **Microcontroller**. If it says "requires external memory and peripherals" → **Microprocessor**.

## 1.4 Classification of Embedded Systems (KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight)

1. **Small-scale:** 8/16-bit MCU, single task (e.g., remote control, toy)
2. **Medium-scale:** 16/32-bit MCU, RTOS, multiple tasks (e.g., washing machine, printer)
3. **Large-scale/Complex:** 32/64-bit MPU + RTOS, networking (e.g., smartphones, automotive ECUs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Von Neumann vs. Harvard Architecture

These are the **two fundamental processor-memory architectures** a KTU examiner loves to test.

### 2.1.1 Von Neumann (Princeton) Architecture

- **Single memory** stores both **program (code)** and **data**.
- **Single bus** carries both instructions and data → the famous **Von Neumann Bottleneck**.
- During one clock cycle, the CPU can either fetch an *instruction* OR read/write *data*, not both.

### 2.1.2 Harvard Architecture

- **Physically separate memories** for program (ROM/Flash) and data (RAM).
- **Separate buses** for instruction fetch and data access.
- The CPU can fetch an instruction **and** read/write data **simultaneously** → higher throughput.

### 2.1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Unified (one memory for code & data) | Separate code + data memory |
| Bus | Single shared bus | Separate instruction + data buses |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster (parallel access) |
| Complexity | Simple, cheaper | Complex, costlier |
| Cycle utilization | Sequential | Parallel (pipelining friendly) |
| Example | 8086, ARM7 (uses modified VN) | 8051, AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers use a **Modified Harvard** architecture — *physically* separate Flash and SRAM, but *logically* unified address space. The 8051 is a *pure* Harvard.

## 2.2 RISC vs. CISC

| Feature | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) | CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction set | Small, fixed length | Large, variable length |
| Execution time | 1 cycle per instruction (pipelined) | Multiple cycles per instruction |
| Addressing modes | Few | Many |
| Compiler dependency | High | Low |
| Examples | ARM, AVR, MIPS, PIC | 8051, x86 (early), 68HC11 |
| Power efficiency | Excellent (used in mobile/IoT) | Lower |

## 2.3 Bus Organization (The Information Highway)

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway. Three types:

1. **Address Bus** — carries the *location* of memory/I/O. **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory). Width = addressable memory size.
2. **Data Bus** — carries the *actual data*. **Bidirectional**. Width = data word size.
3. **Control Bus** — carries *timing & control* signals (Read, Write, Interrupt Acknowledge, etc.).

### The Golden Bus Formula

$$
\text{Maximum Addressable Memory} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}
$$

where $n$ = number of **address bus lines**.

**Example:** 8051 has a 16-bit address bus (for external memory).

$$
2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The bus width directly determines the memory map of an embedded system. When designing an automotive ECU, choosing an MCU with a 24-bit address bus gives $2^{24} = 16 \text{ MB}$ of addressable space — critical for code-heavy applications like ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems).

## 2.4 CPU Internal Architecture — The Brain of the MCU

The CPU consists of:

- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs +, −, ×, ÷, AND, OR, XOR, shift, rotate, compare.
- **CU (Control Unit):** Decodes instructions and generates control signals.
- **Register File:** Small, ultra-fast on-chip memory (Accumulator, B-register, Program Counter, Stack Pointer, etc.).
- **Program Counter (PC):** Holds the address of the *next* instruction to fetch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** The Program Counter (PC) is a *register*, NOT a counter in the hardware-counter sense. Its size = address bus width (e.g., 16-bit PC in 8051).

## 2.5 Memory Organization (Code vs. Data vs. Stack)

| Memory Type | Stores | Volatile? | Example in 8051 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ROM / Flash** | Program code | Non-volatile | 4 KB internal ROM |
| **RAM** | Variables, data | Volatile | 128 bytes internal RAM |
| **Stack** | Return addresses, local vars | Volatile (in RAM) | Grows upward in 8051 |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Addressable memory | $2^{n}$ bytes | $n$ = address bus lines |
| Data word length | $m$ bits | $m$ = data bus lines |
| Clock period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds |
| Machine cycle (8051) | $\dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | 12 oscillator periods |
| Instruction cycle | $1 \times$ to $4 \times$ machine cycle | depends on opcode |
| CPU throughput | $\dfrac{f_{clk}}{\text{Cycles per Instruction}}$ | instructions per second |
| Stack depth | $2^{k}$ entries | $k$ = Stack Pointer bits |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> Although Module 1 is largely conceptual, KTU frequently tests **memory addressing, bus width calculations, and execution time** — which require full derivations.

---

## Example 3.1: Derivation of Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

**Problem:** A microcontroller has a 20-bit address bus and a 16-bit data bus. Find (a) the maximum addressable memory, (b) the size of one memory location, and (c) the total memory size in KB.

### Step 1: Identify the given parameters
Given: $n = 20$ (address lines), $m = 16$ (data lines).

### Step 2: Apply the addressable memory formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Addressable Memory} &= 2^{n} \text{ locations} \\
&= 2^{20} \text{ locations} \\
&= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ locations} \\
&= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{since each location} = 1 \text{ byte}) \\
&= 1024 \text{ KB} \\
&= 1 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Size of one memory location

$$
\text{Size of one location} = m \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 4: Total memory in bits

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total memory} &= 2^{20} \text{ locations} \times 16 \text{ bits/location} \\
&= 2^{20} \times 2^{4} \text{ bits} \\
&= 2^{24} \text{ bits} \\
&= 16 \text{ Mbits} \\
&= 2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion used:** $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$.

---

## Example 3.2: Derivation of Execution Time for an Instruction

**Problem:** An 8051-based system uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. The 8051 takes 12 oscillator periods per machine cycle, and 1 machine cycle per single-cycle instruction. Find (a) the machine cycle duration, (b) instruction execution time, and (c) instructions per second.

### Step 1: Convert crystal frequency to period

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s} = 90.42 \text{ ns}
$$

### Step 2: Machine cycle duration

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= 12 \times T_{osc} \\
&= 12 \times \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 1.085 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Execution time for a 1-machine-cycle instruction

$$
T_{inst} = 1 \times T_{MC} = 1.085 \text{ μs}
$$

### Step 4: Instructions per second (IPS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
IPS &= \frac{1}{T_{inst}} = \frac{1}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 921{,}600 \text{ instructions/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** It is a *magic number* in 8051 because it divides perfectly to give standard baud rates (9600, 19200, etc.) with **0% error** — a classic KTU/industry fact.

---

## Example 3.3: Derivation of Stack Capacity

**Problem:** A microcontroller has a Stack Pointer of 8 bits. What is the maximum stack depth?

### Step 1: Identify the Stack Pointer width
Given: SP = 8 bits, so $k = 8$.

### Step 2: Apply the formula

$$
\text{Stack Depth} = 2^{k} = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ entries}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat:** In the 8051, the SP is 8-bit but the stack resides in *internal RAM* (128 bytes), so the *practical* stack depth is **128 bytes** — only 56 bytes available to the user (rest is register banks + bit-addressable RAM). This is a common KTU 14-mark question.

---

## Example 3.4: Identifying Architecture from a Given Block Diagram

**Given a system with separate program and data memory, each with its own bus connecting to the CPU.**

**Solution Logic:**

1. The defining feature is **separate memories + separate buses**.
2. By definition, this is **Harvard Architecture**.
3. If the buses are *physically* separate but *logically* share an address space, it is **Modified Harvard** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M3).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Identification Rule:**
> - One memory, one bus → **Von Neumann**
> - Two memories, two buses → **Harvard**
> - Two memories, two buses, unified address space → **Modified Harvard**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 4.1: Generic Block Diagram of a Microcontroller (Modified Harvard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU1[CPU]
    ALU1[ALU]
    CU1[Control Unit]
    RF1[Register File - PC, SP, Acc, B]
    ROM1[Program Memory - Flash / ROM]
    RAM1[Data Memory - SRAM]
    IOP1[I/O Ports - P0 to P3]
    TIM1[Timers and Counters - T0, T1, T2]
    COM1[Communication - UART, SPI, I2C]
    INT1[Interrupt Controller]
    ADC1[Analog to Digital Converter]
    IBUS[Instruction Bus]
    DBUS[Data Bus]
    CBUS[Control Bus]

    CPU1 --- ALU1
    CPU1 --- CU1
    CPU1 --- RF1
    CPU1 --- IBUS
    CPU1 --- DBUS
    CPU1 --- CBUS

    IBUS --- ROM1
    DBUS --- RAM1

    CPU1 --- IOP1
    CPU1 --- TIM1
    CPU1 --- COM1
    CPU1 --- INT1
    CPU1 --- ADC1

    CBUS --- ROM1
    CBUS --- RAM1
    CBUS --- IOP1
    CBUS --- TIM1
    CBUS --- COM1
    CBUS --- INT1
    CBUS --- ADC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The instruction bus connects **only** the CPU to program memory, while the data bus connects the CPU to data memory and all peripherals — the hallmark of a **Modified Harvard** architecture like the 8051.

---

## Diagram 4.2: Von Neumann vs. Harvard — Data Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VON[Von Neumann Architecture]
        CPUV[CPU]
        MEMV[Single Memory - Code and Data]
        BUSV[Single Shared Bus]
        CPUV --- BUSV
        BUSV --- MEMV
    end

    subgraph HARV[Harvard Architecture]
        CPUH[CPU]
        ROMH[Program Memory]
        RAMH[Data Memory]
        IBUSH[Instruction Bus]
        DBUSH[Data Bus]
        CPUH --- IBUSH
        CPUH --- DBUSH
        IBUSH --- ROMH
        DBUSH --- RAMH
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key visual takeaway:** In Von Neumann, the bus is a *single lane*; in Harvard, the instruction and data paths are *parallel lanes*, which is why Harvard-based MCUs (8051, AVR) can execute faster.

---

## Diagram 4.3: Embedded System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ES[Embedded System]
    HW[Hardware Layer - MCU, Sensors, Actuators, Power Supply]
    FW[Firmware Layer - Program in ROM / Flash]
    RTOS[RTOS Layer - Task Scheduler - optional]
    APP[Application Layer - User Interface, Communication]

    ES --> HW
    ES --> FW
    ES --> RTOS
    ES --> APP
```

---

## Diagram 4.4: CPU Internal Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[Instruction Register]
    ID[Instruction Decoder]
    PC1[Program Counter]
    MAR[Memory Address Register]
    MBR[Memory Data Register]
    ALU2[ALU]
    ACC[Accumulator]
    FLAG[Flag Register]
    TMP[Temporary Register]

    PC1 --> MAR
    MAR --> MBR
    MBR --> IR
    IR --> ID
    ID --> ALU2
    ALU2 --> ACC
    ALU2 --> FLAG
    ACC --> TMP
    TMP --> ALU2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle:** The Program Counter (PC) drives the address to MAR → memory returns instruction to MBR → loaded into IR → decoded by ID → executed in ALU → result goes to Accumulator or memory. This is the **Instruction Cycle** — guaranteed 14-mark KTU question territory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architecture. Which architecture is used in 8051 and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single unified memory for code & data | Separate program & data memory |
| Bus | Single bus | Separate instruction & data buses |
| Bottleneck | Yes (single bus shared) | No (parallel access possible) |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Example | 8086 | 8051, AVR, PIC |

The **8051 uses Harvard architecture** because it has separate internal program memory (4 KB ROM) and data memory (128 bytes RAM) with separate buses, enabling simultaneous instruction fetch and data access, which speeds up execution. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a microcontroller? How is it different from a microprocessor?**

**Model Answer:**

A **microcontroller** is a single-chip computer that contains a CPU, program memory (ROM/Flash), data memory (RAM), I/O ports, and peripherals like timers and communication interfaces, all integrated on one IC.

**Difference from Microprocessor:**
- A **microprocessor** contains *only* the CPU; memory and peripherals must be connected *externally*.
- A **microcontroller** has *all* required components *on-chip*, making it compact, low-cost, and ideal for embedded applications.

**Examples:** Microprocessor → 8086, 8085; Microcontroller → 8051, ATmega328, PIC16F877. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Module-level internal choice. Solve **either (a) or (b)** from a 14-mark question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a typical microcontroller and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. Compare them on the basis of memory organization, bus structure, speed, and application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A typical microcontroller consists of the following blocks:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** The brain that fetches, decodes, and executes instructions. Contains the ALU, Control Unit, and Register File.
2. **Program Memory (ROM/Flash):** Stores the program code permanently.
3. **Data Memory (RAM):** Stores temporary variables, the stack, and runtime data. Volatile.
4. **I/O Ports:** Allow the MCU to interface with external devices (LEDs, switches, sensors, motors).
5. **Timers/Counters:** Generate precise time delays or count external events.
6. **Serial Communication Interface (UART, SPI, I²C):** Enables communication with other devices.
7. **Interrupt Controller:** Allows the MCU to respond to asynchronous external/internal events without polling.
8. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Converts analog sensor signals (e.g., temperature) into digital values.

*(Block diagram: refer to Diagram 4.1 above — 7 marks: 4 for diagram + 3 for explanation.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Von Neumann Architecture:**
- A single memory holds *both* program and data.
- A single bus transfers instructions and data.
- **Disadvantage:** The single bus becomes a *bottleneck* because instruction fetch and data access cannot occur in the same cycle.

**Harvard Architecture:**
- Program and data are stored in *separate* memories.
- *Separate* instruction and data buses allow simultaneous access.
- **Advantage:** Higher throughput due to parallelism.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | One unified memory | Two separate memories |
| Bus | Single bus | Two buses (parallel) |
| Speed | Slower (sequential) | Faster (parallel) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Power | Higher | Lower (better for embedded) |
| Application | PCs, servers | 8051, AVR, ARM Cortex-M |

*(Neat diagram: refer to Diagram 4.2 — 7 marks: 3 for diagram + 4 for comparison/explanation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the function of the CPU, ALU, Register File, and Program Counter in a microcontroller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microcontroller uses a 24-bit address bus and a 32-bit data bus. Calculate (i) the maximum addressable memory, (ii) the size of one memory location, and (iii) the total memory in MB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Orchestrates the entire system. Fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and coordinates execution. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shift). **(2 marks)**

3. **Register File:** A set of high-speed internal storage locations inside the CPU. Includes:
   - **Accumulator (A):** Holds the result of most ALU operations. **(1.5 marks)**
   - **Stack Pointer (SP):** Points to the top of the stack in RAM.
   - **General Purpose Registers (R0–R7):** Hold intermediate data.

4. **Program Counter (PC):** A register that holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. After each fetch, it is automatically incremented. Its width = address bus width. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** Address bus $n = 24$ bits, Data bus $m = 32$ bits.

**(i) Maximum addressable memory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory} &= 2^{n} \text{ locations} \\
&= 2^{24} \text{ locations} \\
&= 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations} \\
&= 16 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Size of one memory location:**

$$
\text{Size} = m \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(iii) Total memory in bytes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 2^{24} \text{ locations} \times 4 \text{ bytes/location} \\
&= 2^{24} \times 2^{2} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 2^{26} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 64 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Full marks: 2 for (i), 1 for (ii), 4 for (iii) — including unit conversion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students write $2^{24} = 16$ MB and stop, but the data bus width determines *bytes per location*. Total memory = *number of locations × size per location*.
> 2. **Forgetting "per location":** Always multiply $2^{n} \times (m/8)$ for total memory in bytes.
> 3. **Architecture confusion:** The 8051 is **Harvard**, *not* Von Neumann. Students lose marks by saying "8051 is Von Neumann."
> 4. **PC vs. Counter:** Writing "PC is a counter that counts instructions" is **wrong**. The PC is a *register holding an address*, not a hardware counter.
> 5. **Modified Harvard ≠ Harvard:** Modern ARM MCUs are Modified Harvard. Pure Harvard is 8051, AVR, PIC.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **embedded system** is *task-specific*, *resource-constrained*, often *real-time*.
- ✅ A **microcontroller** = CPU + ROM + RAM + I/O + Peripherals on a *single chip*.
- ✅ **Microprocessor** needs *external* memory and peripherals; **Microcontroller** has them *internal*.
- ✅ **Von Neumann:** One memory, one bus → bottleneck.
- ✅ **Harvard:** Two memories, two buses → faster, parallel access.
- ✅ **Modified Harvard** = physically separate memories, but unified address space (e.g., ARM Cortex-M).
- ✅ **RISC** = small fixed-length instructions, 1 cycle/instruction (ARM, AVR).
- ✅ **CISC** = complex variable-length instructions, multiple cycles (8051, x86).
- ✅ **Addressable memory** $= 2^{n}$ bytes, where $n$ = address bus lines.
- ✅ **CPU components:** ALU, CU, Register File, PC, SP, Accumulator.
- ✅ **Memory types:** ROM/Flash (code, non-volatile), RAM (data, volatile), Stack (in RAM).
- ✅ **8051 uses Harvard** with 16-bit PC, 12 oscillator periods per machine cycle.
- ✅ **11.0592 MHz** is the standard 8051 crystal because it gives exact standard baud rates.
- ✅ **Embedded systems** are classified as *small*, *medium*, *large* scale based on performance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmer's Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Programmer's Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmer's Model (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *Programmer's Model* of a microcontroller is the abstract, architecture-level view of the CPU as seen by the software developer. It defines the **set of CPU registers, their bit-widths, their access privileges, the logical memory map, the supported operating modes, the stack model, and the data types/endianness** that any instruction (assembly or compiled C) can legally manipulate.

In the context of the **ARM Cortex-M series** (the de-facto reference architecture for the KTU PCRAT302 syllabus), the Programmer's Model specifies **16 general-purpose integer registers (R0–R15)**, **special-purpose registers** such as the Program Status Register (xPSR), the **Memory Map**, the **Operating Modes** (Thread, Handler), **Stack Pointer bank** (MSP/PSP), and the **privilege levels** (Privileged/Unprivileged).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint of a Building"**
>
> Imagine you are an architect given a plot of land. Before you start drawing rooms, you must know:
> 1. **How many workers (registers) you have** and what each worker is specialized for.
> 2. **Where the warehouse is (memory map)** — where raw materials (data) and instructions (blueprints) are stored.
> 3. **What uniform each worker wears today (operating mode/privilege)** — a site engineer (Privileged) can access restricted zones, while a laborer (Unprivileged) cannot.
> 4. **Where the toolbox (stack pointer)** is currently kept, so a new worker knows exactly where to pick the next tool.
>
> The **Programmer's Model is exactly this blueprint** for the software developer. It does not describe the *internal transistor-level hardware* — that is the *Architecture*. It tells the programmer *what is visible, accessible, and legal* to use.

## 1.3 Core Entities of a Programmer's Model

| # | Entity | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** | Fast on-chip scratchpad storage for operands, addresses, intermediate results. |
| 2 | **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Holds the address of the top of the active stack. |
| 3 | **Link Register (LR / R14)** | Stores the return address after a subroutine call. |
| 4 | **Program Counter (PC / R15)** | Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch. |
| 5 | **Program Status Registers (xPSR)** | Flags such as Negative, Zero, Carry, Overflow, plus execution state. |
| 6 | **Memory Map** | Defines the address ranges for Code, SRAM, Peripherals, and System space. |
| 7 | **Operating Modes & Privilege** | Defines code execution context (Thread/Handler, Privileged/Unprivileged). |
| 8 | **Data Type & Endianness** | Word/half-word/byte support and byte-ordering convention. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Architectural Constant:** The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 (used in STM32, LPC1768, Tiva C) implements the **Thumb-2 ISA only** and uses a **32-bit linear address space** of size **$2^{32}$ bytes = 4 GB**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CPU Register Set — The 16 Core Registers

The ARM Cortex-M Programmer's Model exposes **sixteen 32-bit main registers**, physically allocated as **two banked copies of SP** (Main Stack Pointer and Process Stack Pointer), giving a developer-visible count of effectively 17 logical register instances.

### 2.1.1 General-Purpose Registers: R0 – R12

* **R0 – R12** are **true general-purpose 32-bit registers** with no special architectural function.
* They can be used freely by the programmer for:
  * Holding **32-bit operands** (`int`, `long`, pointers).
  * Holding **64-bit operands** (using register pairs such as `R1:R0`, since ARM does not natively have an 8-byte GP register).
  * Passing **function arguments** (AAPCS convention passes the first four 32-bit words in R0–R3).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 16 registers?** A larger register file reduces the number of slow memory loads/stores, but increases context-switch latency. 16 is the sweet-spot for embedded real-time performance (RISC design philosophy).

### 2.1.2 Stack Pointer (SP) — R13 (Banked)

R13 is **physically banked** into two independent 32-bit registers:

* **MSP — Main Stack Pointer (SP_main):** Used by the OS kernel, exception handlers, and all code running at **Privileged** level by default.
* **PSP — Process Stack Pointer (SP_process):** Used by application threads running in **Thread mode**. This allows clean separation of kernel and process stacks — a foundational requirement for **RTOS implementations (FreeRTOS, RTX)**.

Selection is controlled by the **SPSEL bit** in the **CONTROL register**:

* `SPSEL = 0` → use **MSP**.
* `SPSEL = 1` → use **PSP**.

The stack model is **Full Descending (FD)**:
* Push → decrement SP, then write.
* Pop → read, then increment SP.

### 2.1.3 Link Register (LR) — R14

* Holds the **return address** when a function (`BL`, `BLX`) is called.
* On exception entry, the Cortex-M hardware **automatically pushes LR with a special value `EXC_RETURN`**, which is used by the exception-exit mechanism to return to the correct pre-emption context.
* This eliminates the need for a software save/restore of the return address on every ISR call.

### 2.1.4 Program Counter (PC) — R15

* Always contains the **address of the next instruction to be fetched**.
* Since Thumb-2 instructions are 16-bit or 32-bit aligned, **PC bit[0] is always 0** when read.
* Writing to PC triggers an immediate **branch** (control flow change).

### 2.1.5 Program Status Registers (xPSR)

The Programmer's Model groups three conceptual PSRs into one physical 32-bit register called **xPSR**:

| Sub-register | Full Name | Key Bits |
|--------------|-----------|----------|
| **APSR** | Application PSR | N, Z, C, V, Q |
| **IPSR** | Interrupt PSR | ISR_NUMBER (bits [5:0]) — exception number currently active |
| **EPSR** | Execution PSR | T-bit (Thumb state), ICI/IT bits for interrupt-continuable instructions |

**Condition Flags (APSR):**

* **N — Negative flag:** Set when the result of the last ALU op is bit-31 = 1 (two's complement negative).
* **Z — Zero flag:** Set when the result is exactly zero.
* **C — Carry flag:** Set on unsigned overflow / borrow-not-generated.
* **V — Overflow flag:** Set on signed two's-complement overflow.
* **Q — Saturation flag:** Sticky flag set by `SSAT`/`USAT` instructions when saturation occurs.

## 2.2 Memory Map — The 4 GB Linear Address Space

The Cortex-M defines a **single, flat 4 GB address space** partitioned into well-defined regions:

| Region | Address Range | Size | Typical Use |
|--------|---------------|------|-------------|
| **Code (Flash)** | `0x0000 0000` – `0x1FFF FFFF` | 512 MB | Program code, vector table, constant data |
| **SRAM** | `0x2000 0000` – `0x3FFF FFFF` | 512 MB | Static RAM, stack, heap |
| **Peripheral** | `0x4000 0000` – `0x5FFF FFFF` | 512 MB | On-chip peripherals (GPIO, UART, timers) |
| **External RAM** | `0x6000 0000` – `0x7FFF FFFF` | 512 MB | External memory (FSMC) |
| **External Device** | `0x8000 0000` – `0x9FFF FFFF` | 512 MB | External peripherals |
| **System** | `0xE000 0000` – `0xFFFF FFFF` | 512 MB | NVIC, SysTick, MPU, debug |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Banding:** Two 1-MB aliases in the SRAM (`0x2200 0000`) and Peripheral (`0x4200 0000`) regions allow **atomic single-bit manipulation** using a simple load-store, avoiding the standard read-modify-write sequence.

## 2.3 Operating Modes & Privilege Levels

| Mode | Privilege | Stack | Typical Use |
|------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **Thread — Privileged** | Privileged | MSP (default) | Main `main()` execution, bare-metal |
| **Thread — Unprivileged** | Unprivileged | PSP | Application tasks (RTOS) |
| **Handler** | Always Privileged | MSP | All exception/interrupt service routines |

Privilege is gated by the **CONTROL[0] bit**:

* `0` → Privileged.
* `1` → Unprivileged.

Once in Unprivileged mode, the only way to return to Privileged is via an **exception** (e.g., `SVC` instruction), enforcing strong security/isolation.

## 2.4 Data Types, Endianness & Alignment

* **Native data widths:** 8-bit byte, 16-bit half-word, **32-bit word**.
* **Endianness:** **Configurable** per device; most Cortex-M3/M4 parts default to **Little-Endian**.
* **Unaligned access:** Supported (no fault) for most load/store instructions on Cortex-M3/M4.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **essential constants and addressing formulas** for the Cortex-M Programmer's Model, frequently tested in KTU ESE numerical and short-answer questions.

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Unit / Notes |
|---------|--------------------|--------------|
| Address Space Size | $N = 2^{32}$ | **bytes** (= 4 GB) |
| Addressable Units per word | $b = 8$ | bits/byte |
| Bit-Band Alias Address (Peripheral bit `b` of byte `A`) | $\text{Alias} = 0x42000000 + 32 \cdot (A - 0x40000000) + 4 \cdot b$ | bytes |
| Bit-Band Alias Address (SRAM bit `b` of byte `A`) | $\text{Alias} = 0x22000000 + 32 \cdot (A - 0x20000000) + 4 \cdot b$ | bytes |
| Stack Pointer size | $W = 32$ | bits |
| Number of main registers | $R = 16$ | R0–R15 |
| Banked SP instances | $S = 2$ | MSP, PSP |
| Number of PSR flags monitored in exams | $F = 4$ | N, Z, C, V |
| Return address alignment | $\text{PC} \bmod 4 = 0$ | (Thumb-2) |
| EXC_RETURN constant | $0xFFFF_FFxx$ | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **RTOS Design:** The dual stack pointer (MSP/PSP) and privilege separation are the bedrock of FreeRTOS task isolation.
* **Bit-Banding:** Used in industrial motor-control code to safely toggle GPIO pins without read-modify-write hazards on shared variables.
* **Unified Memory Map:** Simplifies linker-script generation (`STM32_FLASH.ld`, `LPC1768.ld`) — the compiler always knows where code, data, and peripherals live.
* **PSR flags:** Drive **conditional branching** in high-reliability control loops (`IT` blocks, branch instructions like `BNE`, `BHS`).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Band Alias Address Formula

The Cortex-M bit-banding feature is a **favourite 14-mark derivation question** in KTU ESE. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step proof** of the alias address formulas.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider a peripheral register located at the **bit-band region** of the peripheral memory map:
* **Bit-band region base:** $A_{\text{periph\_base}} = 0x4000\,0000$
* **Bit-band alias base:** $A_{\text{alias\_base}} = 0x4200\,0000$
* **Bit-band region size:** $S_{\text{region}} = 0x100000$ bytes (= 1 MB)

We want to map **bit $b$ (where $0 \le b \le 31$) of the byte at offset $\Delta A$ from $A_{\text{periph\_base}}$** into the alias region.

### 3.1.2 Derivation (Peripheral Region)

**Step 1 — Compute the word offset inside the bit-band region.**

Each byte in the bit-band region corresponds to **32 addressable bits** in the alias region (since each byte = 8 bits, and bit-banding expands 1 bit-band byte into 32 alias words).

$$
\text{byte\_offset} = \Delta A = A - A_{\text{periph\_base}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total number of alias words required to span this byte.**

Each bit-band byte expands into 8 bits × 4 bytes/word = 32 alias words.

$$
\text{words\_per\_byte} = 8 \text{ bits} \times 4 \,\frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{word}} = 32
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the alias word offset for the *bit* in question.**

For the $b$-th bit of the byte, we shift the byte offset by an additional $4 \cdot b$ bytes (since each preceding bit consumes one 32-bit alias word).

$$
\text{word\_offset} = 32 \cdot \Delta A + 4 \cdot b
$$

**Step 4 — Add the alias base to get the absolute alias address.**

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = A_{\text{alias\_base}} + \text{word\_offset}
$$

Substituting the constants:

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{alias}} = 0x42000000 + 32 \cdot (A - 0x40000000) + 4 \cdot b}
$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation (SRAM Region)

By the **identical mathematical logic** applied to the SRAM bit-band region:

* $A_{\text{sram\_base}} = 0x2000\,0000$
* $A_{\text{alias\_base}} = 0x2200\,0000$

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + 32 \cdot (A - 0x20000000) + 4 \cdot b}
$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Find the alias address for bit 7 of the GPIOA->ODR register located at `0x4001 081C` on an STM32-style Cortex-M3.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
A = 0x4001\,081C, \quad b = 7
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the offset from the peripheral base.**

$$
\Delta A = A - 0x4000\,0000 = 0x0001\,081C
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the alias word offset.**

$$
\text{word\_offset} = 32 \cdot 0x0001\,081C + 4 \cdot 7
$$

$$
= 0x0021\,0838 + 0x1C = 0x0021\,0854
$$

**Step 4 — Add the alias base.**

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x4200\,0000 + 0x0021\,0854
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{alias}} = 0x4221\,0854}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification by writing pseudo-code semantics.**

A write of `1` to address `0x4221 0854` will atomically set bit 7 of the byte at `0x4001 081C`. A write of `0` will atomically clear it. The hardware handles the read-modify-write internally — verified.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Register Behaviour Across Modes

| Register | Thread-Privileged | Thread-Unprivileged | Handler | Banked? |
|----------|:------------------:|:--------------------:|:-------:|:-------:|
| R0–R12  | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | No |
| MSP (R13) | ✅ R/W | ❌ Blocked (CONTROL[1]=0 → MSP inaccessible from PSP-active context) | ✅ R/W | **Yes** |
| PSP (R13) | ✅ R/W (if SPSEL=1) | ✅ R/W | ❌ Not used | **Yes** |
| LR (R14) | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W (holds EXC_RETURN on entry) | No |
| PC (R15) | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | No |
| APSR flags | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W | No |
| CONTROL | ✅ R/W | ✅ R/W (but cannot return to privileged) | ❌ R/O | No |
| PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI | ✅ R/W | ❌ R/O | ✅ R/W | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cortex-M Programmer's Model Register File

The following Mermaid block diagram renders the complete register file as it appears in the ARMv7-M Architecture Reference Manual, modified for KTU teaching conventions (clean uppercase labels, no markdown formatting inside nodes).

```mermaid
graph TD
    REG_FILE["CORTEX M REGISTER FILE 16 x 32 BIT MAIN REGISTERS"]
    GP["R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7 R8 R9 R10 R11 R12 GENERAL PURPOSE 32 BIT"]
    SP_MSP["R13 MSP MAIN STACK POINTER PRIVILEGED OS KERNEL"]
    SP_PSP["R13 PSP PROCESS STACK POINTER UNPRIVILEGED THREAD"]
    LR["R14 LR LINK REGISTER RETURN ADDRESS OR EXC RETURN"]
    PC["R15 PC PROGRAM COUNTER NEXT INSTRUCTION ADDRESS"]
    SPSR["xPSR APSR IPSR EPSR N Z C V Q ISR NUMBER T BIT"]
    CTRL["CONTROL REGISTER SPSEL PRIVILEGE"]
    REG_FILE --> GP
    REG_FILE --> SP_MSP
    REG_FILE --> SP_PSP
    REG_FILE --> LR
    REG_FILE --> PC
    REG_FILE --> SPSR
    REG_FILE --> CTRL
    SP_MSP -. SELECTED WHEN SPSEL 0 .-> SP_SELECT["ACTIVE STACK POINTER"]
    SP_PSP -. SELECTED WHEN SPSEL 1 .-> SP_SELECT
    SP_SELECT --> STACK_MEM["SRAM REGION 0x20000000 FULL DESCENDING STACK"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — 4 GB Linear Memory Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    MAP["CORTEX M 4 GB LINEAR ADDRESS SPACE 32 BIT"]
    M0["0x00000000 0x1FFFFFFF CODE REGION 512 MB FLASH AND VECTOR TABLE"]
    M1["0x20000000 0x3FFFFFFF SRAM REGION 512 MB INCLUDES BIT BAND ALIAS 0x22000000"]
    M2["0x40000000 0x5FFFFFFF PERIPHERAL REGION 512 MB INCLUDES BIT BAND ALIAS 0x42000000"]
    M3["0x60000000 0x7FFFFFFF EXTERNAL RAM 512 MB"]
    M4["0x80000000 0x9FFFFFFF EXTERNAL DEVICE 512 MB"]
    M5["0xA0000000 0xDFFFFFFF RESERVED VENDOR SPECIFIC"]
    M6["0xE0000000 0xFFFFFFFF SYSTEM REGION 512 MB NVIC SYSTICK MPU DEBUG"]
    MAP --> M0
    MAP --> M1
    MAP --> M2
    MAP --> M3
    MAP --> M4
    MAP --> M5
    MAP --> M6
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Exception Entry/Exit (How the Model Behaves at Run Time)

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP1["STEP 1 EXCEPTION TRIGGERED BY NVIC OR SVC INSTRUCTION"]
    STEP2["STEP 2 HARDWARE AUTOMATICALLY PUSHES R0 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR ONTO ACTIVE STACK"]
    STEP3["STEP 3 LR IS LOADED WITH SPECIAL VALUE EXC RETURN"]
    STEP4["STEP 4 PC IS LOADED WITH VECTOR FROM VECTOR TABLE AT 0x00000000"]
    STEP5["STEP 5 IPSR UPDATED WITH EXCEPTION NUMBER PROCESSOR ENTERS HANDLER MODE"]
    STEP6["STEP 6 ISR EXECUTES USING MSP PRIVILEGED ACCESS"]
    STEP7["STEP 7 ON RETURN INSTRUCTION LR VALUE USED BY HARDWARE TO RESTORE R0 R3 R12 PC xPSR"]
    STEP8["STEP 8 CONTROL RESTORED THREAD MODE RETURNS TO ORIGINAL CONTEXT"]
    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5
    STEP5 --> STEP6
    STEP6 --> STEP7
    STEP7 --> STEP8
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **stacking order (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR — 8 words = 32 bytes)** is a **fixed architectural constant** and is one of the most commonly tested facts in KTU ESE Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Define the Programmer's Model of a microcontroller. List the main registers visible to the programmer in the ARM Cortex-M architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Programmer's Model** is the abstract architectural view of the CPU visible to the software developer, specifying all registers, memory addressing rules, operating modes, and data types that the instruction set can access.

The main registers visible in the ARM Cortex-M3/M4 Programmer's Model are:

1. **R0 – R12** → General-purpose 32-bit registers.
2. **R13 (SP)** → Stack Pointer, **banked** into MSP and PSP.
3. **R14 (LR)** → Link Register, stores return address or `EXC_RETURN`.
4. **R15 (PC)** → Program Counter.
5. **xPSR** → Combined Application, Interrupt, and Execution PSR.
6. **CONTROL**, **PRIMASK**, **FAULTMASK**, **BASEPRI** → Special control registers.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for register list with role)**

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*
**Explain the difference between MSP and PSP. When is each used?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MSP — Main Stack Pointer | PSP — Process Stack Pointer |
|--------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Default user | OS kernel, exception handlers | Application threads |
| Active during | Reset, Handler mode, Thread-Privileged (default) | Thread-Unprivileged (SPSEL=1) |
| Privileged access | Yes | Yes (only via CONTROL write) |
| Stack memory | Located in SRAM | Located in SRAM |
| Banked? | Yes | Yes |

**Selection logic:** Controlled by the **SPSEL bit** in the **CONTROL register**. `SPSEL = 0` → MSP, `SPSEL = 1` → PSP.

**(3 marks — 1 for each row of distinction, 1 for SPSEL mention)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q-A(a)** (7 Marks)
*[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
**With a neat diagram, explain the ARM Cortex-M Programmer's Model. Describe in detail the role of R0–R15 and xPSR.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The Cortex-M Programmer's Model is a **RISC-based 32-bit register file** with **16 main registers (R0–R15)**, **banked SP**, and a **composite xPSR**.

**R0 – R12 (2 marks):** True general-purpose 32-bit registers. R0–R3 used for **argument passing** in AAPCS. R4–R11 are **caller-saved** (preserved across calls). R12 is the **Intra-Procedure-call scratch register (IP)**.

**R13 — SP (1 mark):** Banked into **MSP** and **PSP**. Both are 32-bit. Stack model is **Full Descending**.

**R14 — LR (1 mark):** Stores the return address on `BL`/`BLX`. On exception entry, the CPU loads a special constant `EXC_RETURN` into LR.

**R15 — PC (1 mark):** Always points to the next instruction. Writes to PC act as a branch.

**xPSR (1 mark):** Composed of three sub-PSRs — APSR (flags: N, Z, C, V, Q), IPSR (current exception number), and EPSR (Thumb state and IT/ICI bits).

**Diagram (from Section 4.1 above) — self-draw in exam for full marks.**

---

#### **Q-A(b)** (7 Marks)
*[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
**Derive the bit-band alias address formula for the peripheral region of a Cortex-M3. Given a register at address `0x4002 0010`, find the alias address of bit 5.**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** Follow the **Section 3.1.2 derivation** above, line by line. State:

* Bit-band region base = $0x4000\,0000$
* Alias base = $0x4200\,0000$
* Word offset = $32 \cdot (A - 0x4000\,0000) + 4 \cdot b$
* Final formula: $A_{\text{alias}} = 0x4200\,0000 + 32 \cdot (A - 0x4000\,0000) + 4 \cdot b$

**Numerical computation (2 marks):**

Given: $A = 0x4002\,0010$, $b = 5$.

$$
\Delta A = 0x4002\,0010 - 0x4000\,0000 = 0x0002\,0010
$$

$$
\text{offset} = 32 \cdot 0x0002\,0010 + 4 \cdot 5 = 0x0004\,0020 + 0x14 = 0x0004\,0034
$$

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x4200\,0000 + 0x0004\,0034 = \mathbf{0x4204\,0034}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **Q-B(a)** (7 Marks)
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Explain the 4 GB linear memory map of the ARM Cortex-M3. Mention the role of bit-band regions with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Map description (4 marks):** Tabulate the 8 regions (Code, SRAM, Peripheral, External RAM, External Device, System, Reserved). For each, state the base address, size (512 MB each), and intended use.

**Bit-banding concept (2 marks):** A 1-MB bit-band region in SRAM (`0x2000 0000 – 0x200F FFFF`) and Peripheral (`0x4000 0000 – 0x400F FFFF`) space is **expanded** into a 32-MB alias region starting at `0x2200 0000` and `0x4200 0000` respectively. Each bit of each byte in the bit-band region maps to a **full 32-bit word** in the alias, allowing **atomic single-bit access**.

**Example (1 mark):** Bit 3 of `0x4002 0010` maps to word at $0x42000000 + 32 \cdot 0x00020010 + 4 \cdot 3 = 0x4204\,003\text{C}$. A write of `1` to this address sets the bit atomically.

---

#### **Q-B(b)** (7 Marks)
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Discuss the operating modes and privilege levels in the Cortex-M Programmer's Model. How is the active stack pointer selected?**

**Model Answer:**

**Modes & Privilege (4 marks):**

* **Thread mode** — entered on reset; can be Privileged or Unprivileged.
* **Handler mode** — entered on exception; always Privileged.
* **Privilege** is controlled by the **CONTROL[0] bit**:
  * `0` = Privileged.
  * `1` = Unprivileged (irreversible except via exception).
* Unprivileged code **cannot** access system control space (`0xE0000000+`), NVIC, or certain peripherals.

**Stack selection (3 marks):**

* **CONTROL[1] (SPSEL)** selects the active SP:
  * `0` → **MSP** active.
  * `1` → **PSP** active.
* After reset, **MSP is active** and is loaded from the vector table's first word (`0x0000 0000`).
* In **RTOS** designs, kernel code uses MSP, and each task thread uses its own PSP — enabling stack isolation, faster context switches, and robust memory protection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Programmer's Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Architecture" with "Programmer's Model"** — Architecture is the *implementation* (gates, pipelines, buses); the **Programmer's Model is the *contract* between hardware and software**. Examiners deduct marks for conflating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting that SP is BANKED, not just "R13"** — saying "R13 is the stack pointer" without mentioning **MSP and PSP** is a guaranteed **−1 to −2 marks** penalty.
> 3. **Bit-band formula sign errors** — the most common error is writing `32 × A` instead of `32 × (A − base)`. Examiners *specifically* test for the subtraction term.
> 4. **Misnaming PSR flags** — `C` is **Carry (not "Carry-in")**, `V` is **Overflow (not "Vertical")**. Spell out the names.
> 5. **Endianness** — never write "Cortex-M is big-endian" — most devices are **little-endian by default** (configurable via AIRCR.ENDIANNESS).
> 6. **Forgetting the stacking order** on exception entry: **R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR** — any deviation = −1 mark.
> 7. **Writing PC bit[0] = 1 in Thumb** — this is a fault condition. Always **PC is word-aligned** for branch targets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Programmer's Model**
>
> - The **Programmer's Model** is the **software-visible CPU abstraction** (registers + memory map + modes).
> - **16 main registers** (R0–R15), all 32-bit, plus **banked SP** (MSP/PSP), plus **xPSR**, **CONTROL**, **PRIMASK**, **FAULTMASK**, **BASEPRI**.
> - **R0–R12** are general-purpose. **R0–R3** = argument passing (AAPCS). **R4–R11** = callee-saved. **R12** = IP scratch.
> - **R13 (SP)** is banked: **MSP** (kernel/handlers) and **PSP** (threads). **Stack is Full Descending.**
> - **R14 (LR)** = return address; holds `EXC_RETURN` after exception.
> - **R15 (PC)** = next instruction address; aligned to 2 bytes (Thumb-2).
> - **xPSR** = APSR (N, Z, C, V, Q) + IPSR (exception #) + EPSR (Thumb/IT).
> - **Memory map** is a **flat 4 GB** space: Code (0x0), SRAM (0x2), Peripheral (0x4), External (0x6/0x8), System (0xE).
> - **Bit-banding** in SRAM and Peripheral regions gives **atomic bit access** via 32-MB alias regions. Formula: $A_{\text{alias}} = A_{\text{alias\_base}} + 32 \cdot (A - A_{\text{region\_base}}) + 4 \cdot b$.
> - **Modes:** Thread (Privileged/Unprivileged) and Handler (always Privileged).
> - **Privilege** = `CONTROL[0]`. **SPSEL** = `CONTROL[1]`. **Endianness** = configurable, defaults to **Little-Endian**.
> - **Exception entry stacking order (fixed):** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR — 8 words × 4 bytes = **32 bytes**.
> - **Thumb-2 only** ISA; 16-bit and 32-bit instructions freely mixed; no ARM (32-bit ARM mode) state on Cortex-M.
> - **Real-time impact:** Dual SP + privilege = RTOS task isolation; Bit-band = ISR-safe GPIO; xPSR flags = conditional execution.

---

**End of Notes — Programmer's Model (Module 1, PCRAT302)**

topics.title: Behavior of the Application Program Status Register (APSR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Behavior of the Application Program Status Register (APSR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** is a **32-bit, dedicated, special-purpose status register** in the **ARM Cortex-M** processor core that holds the four arithmetic condition flags (N, Z, C, V), the **Q (saturation) sticky flag**, and a set of reserved bits. It is one of the three Program Status Registers (PSRs) in the Cortex-M architecture — the others being the **IPSR** (Interrupt Program Status Register) and the **EPSR** (Execution Program Status Register). These three are accessible as a combined register when required.

The APSR is mapped into the **System Control Space (SCS)** at address **`0xE000ED00`** region and is one of the most frequently read registers during decision-making (branching, conditional execution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** APSR is studied under *Module 1 – ARM Cortex-M Architecture*, specifically the sub-topic *Registers, Special Registers, and PSR behavior*. It forms the **foundation for all conditional branch instructions** (Bxx, Bxx) in the **Thumb-2 instruction set**.

The APSR bit allocation is shown in the table below.

| Bit Range | Width | Name | Type | Function |
|-----------|-------|------|------|----------|
| [31]      | 1     | **N** | Read/Write | Negative / Sign flag |
| [30]      | 1     | **Z** | Read/Write | Zero flag |
| [29]      | 1     | **C** | Read/Write | Carry / No-Borrow flag |
| [28]      | 1     | **V** | Read/Write | Overflow flag |
| [27]      | 1     | **Q** | Read/Write | Sticky saturation flag |
| [26:0]    | 27    | **RESERVED** | RAZ/WI | Read-As-Zero / Writes Ignored |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **quality-control supervisor in a textile factory**. When a roll of cloth is produced (an *operation*), you don't just check the final length — you also stamp five small indicator cards (stickers) on the **inspection report**:

- One sticker says **"Negative" (N)** → did the cloth feel "negative" (i.e., darker, representing a negative sign)?
- One sticker says **"Zero" (Z)** → was the length exactly zero?
- One sticker says **"Carry" (C)** → did we have any leftover material overflow?
- One sticker says **"Overflow" (V)** → did we lose any material during packing (i.e., the bag tore)?
- One sticker says **"Saturation" (Q)** → did the dye pot max out (sticky: stays glued once stuck)?

The **APSR is this inspection report**. Every time the CPU's Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) finishes an arithmetic, logical, or shift operation, it **automatically** stamps the five indicator cards based on the result. The **supervisor's boss** (the program counter / branch logic) later reads these cards to decide *"Should I go to the 'IF-TRUE' section, or skip to the 'IF-FALSE' section?"* — analogous to conditional branching in C (`if(a==b)`) or assembly (`BEQ label`).

> [!NOTE]
> The APSR is **hardware-updated**, not software-controlled. The programmer can *read* the flags using instructions like `MRS` and can *write* to N, Z, C, V, and Q explicitly using `MSR`. The reserved bits (26:0) are **read-as-zero and writes-ignored** (RAZ/WI).

## 1.3 Real-World Relevance

| Engineering Domain | APSR Usage |
|--------------------|------------|
| **Embedded Firmware Development (STM32)** | Implementing `if`, `while`, `for` loops in C — the compiler emits `IT` (If-Then) blocks that read APSR flags. |
| **Motor Control (BLDC/PMSM drivers)** | Saturating arithmetic (SSAT/USAT) sets the Q flag to indicate potential precision loss in PID controllers. |
| **Communication Protocols (UART/SPI)** | Byte-parity error detection uses Z flag. Carry flag is used in multi-precision arithmetic for 64-bit/128-bit operations. |
| **Cryptography / DSP** | V flag detection in modular arithmetic to detect wrap-around errors in filter chains. |
| **Automotive ECU / Safety-Critical Systems** | ISO 26262 — overflow (V flag) must be checked to avoid undetected computational faults. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ARM Cortex-M uses **APSR condition flags to support 14 conditional branch instructions** (BEQ, BNE, BGT, BLT, BHI, BLS, etc.). Mastering APSR behavior is therefore **non-negotiable** for writing efficient assembly, understanding disassembly, and qualifying in KTU practical exams.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Flags — Behavioral Logic

Each flag in the APSR is updated **only by data-processing instructions that explicitly affect flags**, namely:

- Arithmetic: `ADD, ADDS, ADC, SUB, SUBS, SBC, RSB, MUL, MLS, MLA, SDIV, UDIV`
- Logical: `AND, ORR, EOR, BIC, ORN, TST, TEQ`
- Compare: `CMP, CMN`
- Move: `MOV, MVN, MOVS` (when S suffix is present)
- Shift: `LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX`
- Saturation: `SSAT, USAT, SSAT16, USAT16`
- Multiply-Accumulate: `MLA, MLS, SMLAL, SMULL, UMLAL, UMULL`

> [!NOTE]
> **The S-suffix rule:** In Thumb-2/ARM assembly, the `S` suffix (e.g., `ADDS` instead of `ADD`) tells the assembler to **update APSR flags**. Without the `S`, flags are **NOT** modified. The C compiler in KTU practicals almost always emits the `S` variant for instructions that feed conditional branches.

### 2.1.1 The N Flag (Negative / Sign Flag)

- **Definition:** `N` is set to the **value of bit[31] of the result** after a data-processing operation.
- **Logic:** If the MSB of the result is 1 → result is **negative** in two's complement representation → N = 1. If MSB = 0 → N = 0.
- **Update Equation:**

$$N = R[31]$$

where **R** is the destination register holding the result. This means the N flag is **purely a copy of the sign bit**, not an independent computation. It is *the most straightforward* of the four flags to predict.

### 2.1.2 The Z Flag (Zero Flag)

- **Definition:** `Z` is set to 1 if the **result is exactly zero (all 32 bits are 0)**. Otherwise Z = 0.
- **Update Equation:**

$$Z = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } R = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For comparison operations `CMP X, Y` and `CMN X, Y`:
- `CMP X, Y` computes `X − Y` and updates flags. So `Z=1 ⟺ X == Y`.
- `CMN X, Y` computes `X + Y` and updates flags. So `Z=1 ⟺ X == −Y`.

> [!TIP]
> **CMP is implemented in hardware as SUBS with the destination register discarded (Rd=PC+0 is a no-op or special case).** This is why CMP always updates APSR flags.

### 2.1.3 The C Flag (Carry / No-Borrow Flag)

The C flag has **three different meanings** depending on the operation class. This is the most-asked KTU question.

| Operation Class | Update Rule for C |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Addition** (ADD, ADDS, ADC, CMN) | `C = carry-out` from bit[31] (i.e., 1 if unsigned overflow occurred). |
| **Subtraction** (SUB, SUBS, SBC, RSB, CMP) | `C = NOT borrow` from bit[31] (i.e., 1 if no borrow, 0 if borrow). |
| **Logical AND/ORR/EOR/TST/TEQ** | `C = 0` (always cleared — undefined in some ARM AArch32, but in Cortex-M it is forced to 0). |
| **Logical ORR (with shift)** | C = last bit shifted out of the barrel shifter. |
| **Shifts/Moves** (MOV, MVN, LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR) | C = last bit that was *shifted out* of the source operand. |
| **Multiplication** (MUL, MLA) | Undefined (do not rely on it). |

**Update Equation (Addition):**

$$C_{out} = \text{carry propagated out of bit[31]}$$

Equivalently:

$$C = \left( A \mathrel{\&} B \right) \;\mid\; \left( A \mathrel{\&} \text{cin} \right) \;\mid\; \left( B \mathrel{\&} \text{cin} \right)$$

**Update Equation (Subtraction — borrow = NOT carry):**

$$C = 1 \iff X \geq Y \quad \text{(in unsigned interpretation, where result is X - Y)}$$

### 2.1.4 The V Flag (Overflow Flag — Signed)

The V flag detects **two's-complement signed overflow** — i.e., cases where the result's sign is *incorrect* because the true mathematical result exceeded the representable range $[-2^{31}, 2^{31}-1]$.

| Operation Class | Update Rule for V |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Addition** (ADD/S, ADC) | `V = carry_into[31] ⊕ carry_out_of[31]` |
| **Subtraction** (SUB/S, SBC, RSB) | `V = borrow_into[31] ⊕ borrow_out_of[31]` |
| **Multiplication** (MUL, MLA) | V = (result overflows into the sign bit) — implementation-defined. |
| **Logical** (AND, ORR, EOR, BIC) | V = 0 (always cleared). |
| **Shifts** | V = 0 (Cortex-M convention). |

**Update Equation (Addition):**

$$V = C_{in[31]} \oplus C_{out[31]}$$

**Update Equation (Subtraction):**

$$V = B_{in[31]} \oplus B_{out[31]}$$

where $B_{in}$ and $B_{out}$ are the borrow-in and borrow-out of the sign-bit position. Note that subtraction `X − Y` can be restated as `X + (~Y + 1)`, so borrow-in and borrow-out naturally emerge from the equivalent add.

### 2.1.5 The Q Flag (Sticky Saturation Flag)

The **Q flag is *sticky***, meaning once set, it **remains set until explicitly cleared** by software (e.g., `MSR APSR_nzcvq, #0` or using `MRS`+`BIC` sequence). It is set automatically by the saturation instructions:

- `SSAT` (Signed Saturate) — sets Q if input exceeds signed range.
- `USAT` (Unsigned Saturate) — sets Q if input exceeds unsigned range.
- `SSAT16`, `USAT16`, `SMLABB`, `SMLATB`, `SMLABT`, `SMLATT`, `SMULBB`, `SMULTB`, `SMULBT`, `SMULTT` (QADD, QSUB family, optional in Cortex-M3, standard in Cortex-M4).

**Update Equation:**

$$Q = Q_{previous} \;\mid\; (\text{saturation occurred in current operation})$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Flag | Operation Type | Mathematical Rule | KTU Memory Trick |
|------|----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **N** | All data-processing | `N = Result[31]` | "**N** = **N**egative = MSB of result" |
| **Z** | All data-processing | `Z = 1 if Result = 0, else 0` | "**Z**ero or **Z**ilch" |
| **C** | Addition | `C = carry out of bit[31]` | "**C**arry goes out the door" |
| **C** | Subtraction | `C = 1 if no borrow, 0 if borrow` | "**C**ompleted without borrowing" |
| **C** | Logical (AND/ORR/EOR) | `C = 0` | "Logical ops have no carry" |
| **C** | Shifts/MOV | `C = last bit shifted out` | "Carry falls out of the shifter" |
| **V** | Addition (signed) | `V = Cin[31] ⊕ Cout[31]` | "**V**iolated the sign — XOR the carries" |
| **V** | Subtraction (signed) | `V = Bin[31] ⊕ Bout[31]` | "**V**iolated by borrow" |
| **V** | Logical (AND/ORR/EOR) | `V = 0` | "Logical cannot overflow" |
| **Q** | Saturation (SSAT/USAT) | `Q ← Q ∨ Saturation` | "**Q**uit — I am saturated" |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of APSR

1. **Compiler Translation of High-Level Code:** When a KTU student writes `if (a == b) { ... }` in C, the Keil/IAR/GCC ARM compiler emits a `CMP` followed by `BEQ` (Branch if Equal). The hardware's decision to take the branch is **entirely governed by Z = 1**. Without the APSR, no conditional code would be possible.

2. **Multi-Precision Arithmetic (64-bit / 128-bit):** When computing on 64-bit numbers using two 32-bit registers (e.g., `LO:RHI`), the `C` (carry) flag is used to propagate the carry from the low-32-bit `ADDS` into the high-32-bit `ADC`. This is a frequent 14-mark question in KTU board exams.

3. **Signed Overflow Detection in Safety-Critical Code:** In automotive ECUs (ABS, airbag), V flag must be checked explicitly via `BVS` (Branch on Overflow Set) to comply with **MISRA-C** guidelines.

4. **Saturation in DSP Filters:** The Q flag is critical for DSP filter implementations on Cortex-M4 (e.g., FIR/IIR filters for audio codecs), where the accumulator can saturate.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — V Flag for Signed Addition (Cout ⊕ Cin Derivation)

We now derive the standard textbook rule `V = C_in[31] ⊕ C_out[31]` from first principles, as KTU board examiners frequently award full marks only when this is shown explicitly.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider adding two 32-bit two's-complement operands `X` and `Y`:

$$S = X + Y$$

The two's-complement range for any 32-bit signed number is `[-2^31, 2^31 - 1]`, i.e., sign bit `S[31]` represents `−2^31` if set.

**Overflow happens if and only if the signs of the addends agree, but the sign of the result disagrees.** Formally:

$$V = 1 \iff (X[31] = Y[31]) \land (S[31] \neq X[31])$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Proof

Let us denote the **carry into** bit position $i$ as $c_i$ and the **carry out** of bit position $i$ as $c_{i+1}$, with $c_0 = 0$ (no carry into the LSB).

**Step 1: Express the sum bit using XOR.**

For any bit position $i$:

$$S[i] = X[i] \oplus Y[i] \oplus c_i$$

**Step 2: Express the carry-out.**

From the standard full-adder relation:

$$c_{i+1} = (X[i] \land Y[i]) \lor (X[i] \land c_i) \lor (Y[i] \land c_i)$$

**Step 3: Specialize to bit 31 (the sign bit).**

$$S[31] = X[31] \oplus Y[31] \oplus c_{31}$$

$$c_{32} = (X[31] \land Y[31]) \lor (X[31] \land c_{31}) \lor (Y[31] \land c_{31})$$

**Step 4: Compute the XOR of the two carries.**

We need $V = c_{31} \oplus c_{32}$ (this is the *final* derivation, the *defining* property of the V flag).

$$c_{31} \oplus c_{32} = c_{31} \oplus \big[(X[31] \land Y[31]) \lor (X[31] \land c_{31}) \lor (Y[31] \land c_{31})\big]$$

**Step 5: Apply Boolean identity.**

Using $A \oplus (B \lor C) = (A \oplus B) \land (A \oplus C) \lor (B \land C)$ iteratively, and the fact that the three terms inside the brackets are mutually exclusive only when *at most one input is 1*:

$$c_{31} \oplus c_{32} = (X[31] \oplus Y[31]) \land (X[31] \oplus c_{31}) \land (Y[31] \oplus c_{31})$$

Hmm — this is a complex route. A cleaner derivation is by **case analysis**.

### 3.1.3 Case-Analysis Proof (Cleaner, Board-Friendly)

Let $X[31] = s_X$, $Y[31] = s_Y$, $c_{31} = c_{in}$, $S[31] = s_S$, $c_{32} = c_{out}$.

| Case | $s_X$ | $s_Y$ | $s_X \oplus s_Y$ | Result Signed? | $c_{out}$ | $c_{in}$ | $c_{in} \oplus c_{out}$ |
|------|-------|-------|------------------|----------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1    | 0     | 0     | 0                | 0 or 1 (no overflow: both positive) | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| 2    | 0     | 0     | 0                | (impossible) | 1 | 0 | **0** (not possible) |
| 3    | 0     | 1     | 1                | 0 or 1 (no overflow: opposite signs) | 0 or 1 | 0 or 1 | 0 |
| 4    | 1     | 1     | 0                | 0 = overflow (both negative, result positive) | 0 | 1 | **1** |
| 5    | 1     | 1     | 0                | 1 = no overflow (both negative, result negative) | 1 | 1 | **0** |
| 6    | 1     | 0     | 1                | 0 or 1 (opposite signs, no overflow) | 0 or 1 | 0 or 1 | 0 |

**Conclusion of the table:** Overflow occurs **only in Case 4** (both operands negative, but result's sign bit is 0). In Case 4, $c_{in} = 1$ and $c_{out} = 0$, hence $c_{in} \oplus c_{out} = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$. In all other cases the XOR evaluates to 0. **Therefore:**

$$\boxed{V = c_{in}[31] \oplus c_{out}[31]}$$

This is the canonical ARM architectural definition of the V flag for signed addition.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Trace APSR Behavior for `SUBS R0, R1, R2`

Given: `R1 = 0x00000003`, `R2 = 0x00000005`. The instruction `SUBS R0, R1, R2` computes `R0 = R1 − R2` and updates APSR.

**Step 1: Compute the result.**

$$R0 = 0x00000003 - 0x00000005 = 0xFFFFFFFE$$

**Step 2: Update N flag.**

$$N = R0[31] = 1$$

**Step 3: Update Z flag.**

$$R0 \neq 0 \implies Z = 0$$

**Step 4: Update C flag (subtraction rule).**

Since `R1 < R2` (unsigned), there is a borrow:

$$C = 0 \quad \text{(borrow occurred)}$$

Equivalently, in two's-complement hardware: $\overline{R2} + 1 = 0xFFFFFFFB$, then `R1 + 0xFFFFFFFB + 0 (initial cin) = 0x00000003 + 0xFFFFFFFB = 0xFFFFFFFE` with **no carry out of bit 31**, so $C = 0$. Confirmed.

**Step 5: Update V flag (subtraction rule).**

Subtraction: $V = B_{in}[31] \oplus B_{out}[31]$, where borrow is equivalent to NOT carry. With $c_{in} = 0$ (no borrow into bit 0) and $c_{out} = 0$ (no carry out of bit 31, equivalently borrow out = 1):

$$V = \overline{0} \oplus \overline{0} = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$$

**Step 6: Final APSR bit pattern.**

| Bit  | N  | Z  | C  | V  | Q  | Reserved |
|------|----|----|----|----|----|----------|
| Value| 1  | 0  | 0  | 0  | unchanged | 0 |

A `BLT label` (Branch on Less Than, signed) would be taken because **N ≠ V (1 ≠ 0)**, confirming R1 < R2 in signed sense, which is the correct semantic.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Multi-Precision 64-bit Addition

**Goal:** Compute $R_{H}:R_{L} = X_{H}:X_{L} + Y_{H}:Y_{L}$ (64-bit add).

```
ADDS  R_L, R_L, R_Y_L      ; low 32-bit add, updates C with carry-out
ADC   R_H, R_H, R_Y_H      ; high 32-bit add-with-carry, uses C
```

Suppose $X = 0x00000001\,FFFFFFFF$ and $Y = 0x00000000\,00000005$.

**Step 1: Low half — `ADDS R_L, R_L, R_Y_L`.**

$$0xFFFFFFFF + 0x00000005 = 0x00000004 \;\; \text{with carry out} = 1$$

- N = 0 (MSB of 0x00000004 is 0)
- Z = 0
- C = 1
- V = 0

**Step 2: High half — `ADC R_H, R_H, R_Y_H`.**

$$0x00000001 + 0x00000000 + 1 = 0x00000002$$

- N = 0
- Z = 0
- C = 0
- V = 0

**Final 64-bit result:** $0x00000002\,00000004$ ✓.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flag-Update Behaviour by Instruction

| Instruction | N | Z | C | V | Q |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ADDS, ADCS`     | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – |
| `SUBS, SBCS, RSBS` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – |
| `ANDS, ORRS, EORS, BICS, TST, TEQ` | ✓ | ✓ | (0 for AND/ORR/EOR; undefined for BIC/TST) | 0 | – |
| `MOVS, MVNS`     | ✓ | ✓ | (depends on shifter) | 0 | – |
| `LSLS, LSRS, ASRS, RORS` (with S) | ✓ | ✓ | (last bit shifted out) | 0 | – |
| `RRXS`           | ✓ | ✓ | (bit shifted into C) | 0 | – |
| `MULS, MLAS`     | ✓ | ✓ | undefined | undefined | – |
| `SDIV, UDIV`     | – | – | – | – | – |
| `CMP, CMN`       | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – |
| `SSAT, USAT`     | – | – | – | – | ✓ (sticky) |
| `REV, RBIT, CLZ` | – | – | – | – | – |
| `LDR, STR, LDM, STM` | – | – | – | – | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why doesn't LDR/STR update APSR?** Load/store instructions access memory only; they do not pass through the ALU, hence **none of the data-processing flags are affected**. This is a **favourite trick question** in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Analytic Example — When N is 1 but the result is "positive" (sign confusion)

Consider `0x80000000 + 0x80000000 = 0x00000000` (signed overflow case).

- Result = 0x00000000
- N = Result[31] = **0**
- Z = **1** (result is zero)
- C = **1** (carry out of bit 31)
- V = $c_{in}[31] \oplus c_{out}[31] = 1 \oplus 1 = \mathbf{0}$

Wait — the result is 0x00000000, but the true signed sum is `-2^31 + -2^31 = -2^32`, which is **outside** the signed range. V should be 1! Let me re-check.

Actually, `0x80000000 + 0x80000000` in 32-bit unsigned = `0x100000000`, truncated to 32 bits = `0x00000000`, with carry out = 1. Now check the V-flag by carries: bit 31: $1+1 = 10_2$ produces **carry_in = 0** (no carry *into* the sign bit) and **carry_out = 1**. So $V = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$. ✓

Correcting the table:

- N = 0, Z = 1, C = 1, V = **1**.

This single bit V=1 is the **only indication** that signed overflow occurred. Without checking V, the programmer would think the result is +0 — a critical bug.
