# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 APSR Bit Layout (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **32-bit layout of the Application Program Status Register** in the ARM Cortex-M architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["APSR 32-bit Register\nAddress 0xE000ED00 offset 0x00"]
    A --> B["Bit 31: N flag\nNegative / Sign"]
    A --> C["Bit 30: Z flag\nZero"]
    A --> D["Bit 29: C flag\nCarry / No-Borrow"]
    A --> E["Bit 28: V flag\nSigned Overflow"]
    A --> F["Bit 27: Q flag\nSticky Saturation"]
    A --> G["Bits 26 to 0\nRESERVED\nRead-As-Zero\nWrites Ignored"]
```

## 4.2 ALU-to-APSR Data Flow (Functional Block Architecture)

This schematic shows how the ALU result, the carry-chain hardware, and the shifter feed the APSR flags.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OpA["Operand A\nRsrc1 32-bit"]:::in
    OpB["Operand B\nRsrc2 32-bit"]:::in
    Shf["Barrel Shifter\nLSL / LSR / ASR / ROR / RRX"]:::proc
    Add["32-bit Adder\nFull Adders c0 to c31"]:::proc
    Logic["Logic Unit\nAND / ORR / EOR / BIC"]:::proc
    Res["Result Bus 32-bit\nR[31:0]"]:::out
    NCalc["N = R[31]"]:::flag
    ZCalc["Z = NOT R[31] AND R[30] ... R[0]"]:::flag
    CCalc["C = carry out of bit 31\nOR last bit shifted out"]:::flag
    VCalc["V = Cin[31] XOR Cout[31]"]:::flag
    APSR["APSR Register\nN Z C V Q + RES"]:::reg
    OpB --> Shf
    OpA --> Add
    Shf --> Add
    OpA --> Logic
    Shf --> Logic
    Add --> Res
    Logic --> Res
    Res --> NCalc
    Res --> ZCalc
    Add --> CCalc
    Add --> VCalc
    NCalc --> APSR
    ZCalc --> APSR
    CCalc --> APSR
    VCalc --> APSR
    classDef in fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#0c1a4a;
    classDef proc fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#3a1a00;
    classDef out fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#022c22;
    classDef flag fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#500724;
    classDef reg fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#1e1b4b;
```

## 4.3 APSR Update Decision Tree

A decision tree for choosing the correct flag-update rule based on the instruction class.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Instruction Decoded"]:::root
    IsS["Has S-suffix?"]:::root
    IsAlu["Data-processing?"]:::root
    IsAdd["ADD / ADC / CMN?"]:::root
    IsSub["SUB / SBC / RSB / CMP?"]:::root
    IsLog["AND / ORR / EOR / BIC / TST / TEQ?"]:::root
    IsShift["MOV / MVN / shift op with S?"]:::root
    IsSat["SSAT / USAT / QADD / QSUB?"]:::root
    NoUpd["No APSR update"]:::leaf
    AddRule["N=Result[31]\nZ=Result==0\nC=Cout[31]\nV=Cin[31] XOR Cout[31]"]:::rule
    SubRule["N=Result[31]\nZ=Result==0\nC=NOT Borrow[31]\nV=Bin[31] XOR Bout[31]"]:::rule
    LogRule["N=Result[31]\nZ=Result==0\nC=0\nV=0"]:::rule
    ShiftRule["N=Result[31]\nZ=Result==0\nC=last bit shifted out\nV=0"]:::rule
    SatRule["Q=Q_prev OR Saturation"]:::rule
    Start --> IsS
    IsS -- No --> NoUpd
    IsS -- Yes --> IsAlu
    IsAlu -- No --> NoUpd
    IsAlu -- Yes --> IsAdd
    IsAdd -- Yes --> AddRule
    IsAdd -- No --> IsSub
    IsSub -- Yes --> SubRule
    IsSub -- No --> IsLog
    IsLog -- Yes --> LogRule
    IsLog -- No --> IsShift
    IsShift -- Yes --> ShiftRule
    IsShift -- No --> IsSat
    IsSat -- Yes --> SatRule
    IsSat -- No --> NoUpd
    classDef root fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a;
    classDef leaf fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d;
    classDef rule fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46;
```

## 4.4 APSR-to-Branch Condition Mapping (Cortex-M)

This is the **decoder** that maps the four condition flags (N, Z, C, V) to the **14 conditional branch suffixes** used in `Bxx` instructions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APSR["APSR Flags\nN Z C V"]:::reg
    APSR --> EQ["BEQ\nZ=1"]
    APSR --> NE["BNE\nZ=0"]
    APSR --> CS["BCS / BHS\nC=1"]
    APSR --> CC["BCC / BLO\nC=0"]
    APSR --> MI["BMI\nN=1"]
    APSR --> PL["BPL\nN=0"]
    APSR --> VS["BVS\nV=1"]
    APSR --> VC["BVC\nV=0"]
    APSR --> HI["BHI\nC=1 AND Z=0"]
    APSR --> LS["BLS\nC=0 OR Z=1"]
    APSR --> GE["BGE\nN=V"]
    APSR --> LT["BLT\nN!=V"]
    APSR --> GT["BGT\nZ=0 AND N=V"]
    APSR --> LE["BLE\nZ=1 OR N!=V"]
    classDef reg fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3;
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the status flags present in the Application Program Status Register (APSR) of ARM Cortex-M and briefly state the purpose of the Q flag. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The APSR of ARM Cortex-M contains five status flags:

1. **N (Negative flag, bit 31):** indicates the sign of the result (N = result[31]).
2. **Z (Zero flag, bit 30):** indicates whether the result is zero.
3. **C (Carry flag, bit 29):** indicates unsigned carry or no-borrow in subtraction.
4. **V (Overflow flag, bit 28):** indicates signed two's-complement overflow.
5. **Q (Saturation flag, bit 27):** indicates that saturation has occurred in a `SSAT`, `USAT`, or related instruction.

**Purpose of the Q flag:** The Q flag is a *sticky* flag — once set, it remains 1 until cleared by software (e.g., via `MSR APSR_nzcvq, #0x0`). It records the **historical occurrence of saturation** in DSP/saturation instructions, allowing the program to detect cumulative precision loss in repeated saturation operations (e.g., in a FIR filter running over many samples). It does **not** get cleared by subsequent non-saturating operations, hence the term "sticky".

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: For each of the following instructions executed on an ARM Cortex-M3, write the resulting values of the N, Z, C, and V flags in the APSR. Assume R0 = 0x80000000, R1 = 0x00000001. (3 Marks)**

- (i) `ADDS R2, R0, R1`
- (ii) `SUBS R2, R0, R1`
- (iii) `CMP R0, R1`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) `ADDS R2, R0, R1`:** R0 + R1 = 0x80000000 + 0x00000001 = 0x80000001.

- N = 1 (MSB of 0x80000001 is 1)
- Z = 0
- C = 0 (no carry out of bit 31; 1+0+0 at bit 31 produces no carry)
- V = 1 (signed overflow — the positive operand added to the minimum negative gives a result that "wraps". $V = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$? Let me recompute: bit 31: 1+0+0 = 1, no carry in, no carry out → $V=0$.) **Correction:** $V = 0$ (no carry-in to bit 31, no carry-out from bit 31, so XOR = 0). 

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-evaluation:** Strictly `0x80000000 + 0x00000001` mathematically equals `-2^31 + 1 = -2147483647`, which **is** within signed range `[-2^31, 2^31-1]`. Hence there is **no signed overflow**, and V = 0. ✓

- **Final for (i):** N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=0.

**(ii) `SUBS R2, R0, R1`:** R0 − R1 = 0x80000000 − 0x00000001 = 0x7FFFFFFF.

- N = 0
- Z = 0
- C = 1 (no borrow; R0 ≥ R1 unsigned)
- V = 1 (signed overflow — subtracting a positive from the minimum negative gives an out-of-range positive result)
- **Final for (ii):** N=0, Z=0, C=1, V=1.

**(iii) `CMP R0, R1`:** This is the same as `SUBS R2, R0, R1` with the result discarded.

- N = 0, Z = 0, C = 1, V = 1. **Final for (iii):** N=0, Z=0, C=1, V=1.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay-Level Question (1 × 14 Marks)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part-B questions carry an **internal choice**. The student attempts **ONE** of the two options provided. Both options are given below.

### **Option A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Question A(a):** Explain the bit fields of the Application Program Status Register (APSR) in ARM Cortex-M3 with a neat diagram. Describe the difference between the C flag behavior in addition and subtraction, with one example each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
APSR (32-bit, address 0xE000ED00 + 0x00)
+---+---+---+---+---+-------------------------------+
| N | Z | C | V | Q |      RESERVED (RAZ/WI)        |
|31 |30 |29 |28 |27 |              26..0            |
+---+---+---+---+---+-------------------------------+
```

**Bit Field Description (2 marks):**

| Bit | Name | Description |
|-----|------|-------------|
| 31  | N | Negative (sign) flag = result[31] |
| 30  | Z | Zero flag = 1 if result = 0 |
| 29  | C | Carry / No-Borrow flag |
| 28  | V | Signed overflow flag |
| 27  | Q | Sticky saturation flag |
| 26..0 | RESERVED | Read-As-Zero, Writes Ignored |

**C Flag Behavior — Addition (1 mark):** For `ADDS`, `ADC`, `CMN`, the C flag = **carry out of bit 31** (i.e., 1 if the unsigned addition overflowed the 32-bit range).

**Example (1 mark):** `0xFFFFFFFF + 0x00000001 = 0x00000000` with C = 1.

**C Flag Behavior — Subtraction (1 mark):** For `SUBS`, `SBC`, `RSB`, `CMP`, the C flag = **NOT borrow out of bit 31** (i.e., 1 if the unsigned minuend was ≥ subtrahend, 0 otherwise). This counterintuitive naming exists because subtraction is implemented as `A + (~B) + 1`; the carry-out of this *equivalent addition* is 1 exactly when no borrow occurred.

**Example (1 mark):** `0x00000005 − 0x00000003 = 0x00000002` with C = 1 (no borrow); `0x00000003 − 0x00000005 = 0xFFFFFFFE` with C = 0 (borrow).

**Conclusion:** The dual meaning of C (carry-out vs. no-borrow) is preserved to ensure that **unsigned ≥** comparison can be performed with a single `SUBS`-based `CMP` followed by `BCS`/`BHI` (Branch on Carry Set / Unsigned Higher).

---

#### **Question A(b):** With a suitable assembly code, explain how the APSR flags are used to perform a 64-bit signed addition on ARM Cortex-M. Show the flag transitions for one example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup (2 marks):** Let `X = X_{H}:X_{L}` and `Y = Y_{H}:Y_{L}` be 64-bit operands stored as `[X_H, X_L, Y_H, Y_L]` registers. We want `R_{H}:R_{L} = X + Y`. The C flag of the low-32-bit addition must be propagated into the high-32-bit addition.

**Code (2 marks):**

```assembly
        ADDS    R_L, R_L, R_Y_L      ; Low 32-bit add, sets C
        ADC     R_H, R_H, R_Y_H      ; High 32-bit add with carry
        BVS     overflow_handler     ; Optional: branch on signed overflow
```

**Example (2 marks):** Let X = 0x00000001_FFFFFFFF, Y = 0x00000000_00000005.

- **Step 1:** `ADDS R_L, R_L, R_Y_L` → 0xFFFFFFFF + 0x00000005 = 0x00000004 with C = 1.
- **Step 2:** `ADC R_H, R_H, R_Y_H` → 0x00000001 + 0x00000000 + 1 (carry) = 0x00000002 with C = 0.
- **Result:** 0x00000002_00000004 ✓ (decimal: 8589934596).

**Signedness check (1 mark):** For signed interpretation, a `BVS` (Branch if V Set) check is also needed if signed 64-bit overflow is to be detected — but the V flag computed by `ADC` only reflects overflow of the high 32-bit, so a full signed-64-bit overflow check requires additional logic (see ARM ARM v7-M, section A7.7.6).

---

### **Option B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### **Question B(a):** Explain, with a neat diagram, the APSR flag-update rules for the following instructions executed on an ARM Cortex-M3: (i) `ADDS`, (ii) `SUBS`, (iii) `ANDS`, (iv) `LSLS`, (v) `SSAT`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Unified Flag-Update Table (5 marks):**

| Instruction | N | Z | C | V | Q |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ADDS Rdn, Rm, Rn`     | N = Result[31] | Z = (Result == 0) | C = Carry out of bit 31 | V = Cin[31] ⊕ Cout[31] | unchanged |
| `SUBS Rdn, Rm, Rn`     | N = Result[31] | Z = (Result == 0) | C = NOT Borrow out of bit 31 | V = Bin[31] ⊕ Bout[31] | unchanged |
| `ANDS Rdn, Rm, Rn`     | N = Result[31] | Z = (Result == 0) | **C = 0** | **V = 0** | unchanged |
| `LSLS Rd, Rm, #n`      | N = Result[31] | Z = (Result == 0) | C = last bit shifted out of MSB | **V = 0** | unchanged |
| `SSAT Rd, #n, Rm`      | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | **Q = Q_prev ∨ Saturation** |

**Narrative Description (2 marks):**

- For `ADDS`, the flags reflect the unsigned and signed correctness of the sum. N and V together indicate signed overflow via the `BVS`/`BVC` and `BGE`/`BLT` branches.
- For `SUBS`, the same rules apply, but C is the *inversion* of borrow — so `BCS` after a `CMP` tests for unsigned ≥.
- `ANDS` is a bitwise AND; logically, no carry is possible, so C and V are forced to 0.
- `LSLS` shifts left; the bit that "falls out" of the top end goes into C. V is forced to 0 in the Cortex-M.
- `SSAT` is a saturation operation; it does not affect N, Z, C, V at all — only Q is set if saturation occurred.

---

#### **Question B(b):** Describe the Q flag in detail. Why is it called "sticky"? Write a small C-equivalent pseudocode and an ARM assembly fragment that demonstrates the sticky behavior using `SSAT` and clearing of the Q flag via `MSR`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Q Flag Description (2 marks):** The Q flag is bit 27 of the APSR. It is **set** when a saturation instruction (`SSAT`, `USAT`, `SSAT16`, `USAT16`, `QADD`, `QSUB`) detects that the input operand cannot be represented in the destination bit-width and was therefore clamped to the maximum or minimum representable value.

**Why Sticky? (2 marks):** Once Q is set to 1, it remains 1 *even if subsequent operations do not saturate*. It is only cleared when the program executes a write to APSR that explicitly clears bit 27, e.g.:

```assembly
        MSR     APSR_nzcvq, #0      ; Clear all five flag bits including Q
```

This "sticky" semantics is essential for detecting **cumulative saturation** in DSP pipelines. A FIR filter, for example, might run thousands of multiply-accumulate operations per sample; checking Q at the end tells the user "at least one of those thousands saturated" — a critical warning for precision.

**Pseudocode (1 mark):**

```c
// C-equivalent of sticky Q flag
uint8_t Q = 0;          // APSR Q bit
int32_t sstr_asm(int32_t x, int n) {    // SSAT simulation
    int32_t lo = -(1 << (n-1));
    int32_t hi = (1 << (n-1)) - 1;
    if (x > hi) { Q = 1; return hi; }
    if (x < lo) { Q = 1; return lo; }
    return x;            // Q unchanged
}
```

**Assembly Demonstration (2 marks):**

```assembly
        MOV     R0, #0x7FFFFFFF     ; Large positive
        SSAT    R1, #16, R0         ; Saturate to 16-bit signed (clamp to 0x7FFF), Q = 1
        ; ... many other instructions that do NOT saturate ...
        ; At this point, Q is still 1 (sticky).
        MSR     APSR_nzcvq, #0      ; Software clears Q
        ; Q is now 0.
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Do not confuse C flag semantics between ADD and SUB.** Many students write "C = carry out" for subtraction, which is wrong; for SUB, C is the *negation* of borrow. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 
> 2. **V flag is for *signed* overflow only.** Saying "V = carry out" is wrong. V is **carry-in XOR carry-out** of the sign bit (bit 31).
> 
> 3. **Q flag is sticky.** Many students forget this and say "Q is set/cleared by every instruction". Examiners may give partial credit, but a clear mention of *sticky* + *software-clearable* is required for full marks.
> 
> 4. **Reserved bits 26:0 are RAZ/WI.** Writing to them does *not* corrupt the register, and reading them always returns 0. This is a frequent 1-mark MCQ.
> 
> 5. **LDR/STR do not update APSR.** A common exam trap: students claim that `LDR R0, [R1]` followed by `BEQ` will branch based on the loaded value. **It will not** — the BEQ uses flags from the *previous* data-processing instruction.
> 
> 6. **Compiler-generated code uses the S-suffix implicitly.** When the KTU practical exam asks to "demonstrate conditional branching", remember to use `ADDS`/`SUBS`/`CMP` (with S), not `ADD`/`SUB`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **APSR = 5 flags: N, Z, C, V, Q** + 27 reserved bits. Address 0xE000ED00.
- ✅ **N = result[31]** — copy of the sign bit of the result. Most trivial flag.
- ✅ **Z = 1 iff result = 0** — set on equality after CMP. Triggers BEQ.
- ✅ **C flag has dual meaning:** carry-out (for ADD family) vs. no-borrow (for SUB family). Used for unsigned ≥ comparison via `BCS` / `BHI`.
- ✅ **V = carry_in ⊕ carry_out (at bit 31)** for signed ADD. Detects two's-complement signed overflow.
- ✅ **Q is sticky** — set by `SSAT`/`USAT`/saturation ops, only cleared by software (`MSR APSR_nzcvq, #0`).
- ✅ **The S-suffix is mandatory** for any instruction that should update APSR.
- ✅ **Logical ops (AND/ORR/EOR) clear C and V** — these are "pure" bitwise operations with no arithmetic overflow.
- ✅ **Shifts put the last bit shifted out into C**, V is cleared.
- ✅ **Multi-precision arithmetic uses C as the carry link** between `ADDS` and `ADC`.
- ✅ **LDR/STR do NOT modify APSR** — only ALU instructions do.
- ✅ **14 conditional branches (Bxx) read APSR** to decide whether to take the branch.
- ✅ **Reserved bits 26:0 are RAZ/WI** — Read-As-Zero, Writes-Ignored.
- ✅ **The Q flag is essential in DSP** for cumulative saturation detection in FIR/IIR filters.
- ✅ **For KTU 2024 ESE, focus areas:** flag-update rules table, C-flag dual meaning, V-flag derivation, multi-precision code, Q-flag stickiness, and 14-branch mapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Memorize the **flag-update table (Section 3.4)** and the **branch-suffix mapping (Section 4.4)**. These two diagrams alone can answer 8 out of 10 APSR-related questions. Combine them with the **V-flag derivation (Section 3.1)** for full marks on 14-mark essay questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of microcontrollers and embedded systems, a **Memory System** is defined as a hierarchical, organized collection of storage elements (registers, caches, RAM, ROM, and secondary storage) that work cooperatively to store binary instructions, data, and temporary computational results required by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) and peripherals during program execution.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCRAT302**, the memory system forms the backbone of any microcontroller-based architecture, determining the **speed, cost, power consumption**, and **functional capability** of the embedded design. The design of an efficient memory system is a critical engineering task because no single memory technology simultaneously optimizes for **speed, density (capacity), volatility, and cost per bit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to differentiate between memory types, explain the memory hierarchy, calculate memory addressing capacity, and analyze the trade-offs (speed vs. cost vs. volatility) that govern memory selection in embedded systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Office Desk Library"

Imagine you are a student preparing for an exam (your CPU is doing calculations). You have three places to keep your books (memory):

1. **Your Study Desk (Registers & Cache)** – You keep only the *one or two books* you are reading *right now* here. Access is instant, but the desk is tiny and very expensive (like a small mahogany desk).
2. **Your Bookshelf at Home (Main Memory / RAM)** – You keep all the books you need for the *current semester* here. It is fast to access (a few seconds), large enough for many books, but you can only keep a limited number.
3. **The Library / Godown (Secondary Storage – HDD, Flash, SD Card)** – You store *all your old books, notes, and archives* here. It is huge and cheap, but going there takes a lot of time (you have to travel).

This is exactly how a microcontroller's memory system works! The CPU keeps the **most-used data in fast, small memory (registers)**, **frequently accessed variables in medium-speed memory (RAM)**, and the **program code & large data blocks in large, slow, non-volatile memory (Flash/ROM)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are used universally (and expected in KTU exams) when describing any memory device:

- **Access Time ($t_A$):** The time delay between a memory read request and the moment the data is available. Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Cycle Time ($t_C$):** The minimum time period between two successive memory operations. Measured in **ns**.
- **Volatility:** Property of losing stored data when power is removed. **Volatile = loses data** (RAM); **Non-volatile = retains data** (ROM, Flash).
- **Density:** The number of bits that can be stored per unit area. Higher density = smaller physical size for same capacity.
- **Cost per bit:** Typically measured in **micro-cents per bit (µ¢/bit)** or **cents per megabyte**.
- **Power Consumption:** Static (leakage) + Dynamic (switching) power, measured in **milliwatts (mW)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Memory Hierarchy:**
> Memory hierarchy is the structured arrangement of different memory types in a computer/embedded system, ordered by **access speed, cost, and proximity to the CPU**. The fundamental principle is: *Faster, smaller, costlier memory closer to the CPU; slower, larger, cheaper memory farther away.*

## 1.4 Why Memory System Design Matters in Embedded Systems

In KTU's PCRAT302 context, embedded systems have **strict constraints** that make memory design critical:

- **Determinism:** Real-time systems (e.g., anti-lock braking, pacemakers) MUST have *predictable* memory access times. Unpredictable cache misses can cause system failure.
- **Power Budget:** Battery-powered IoT devices (e.g., wearables, sensor nodes) cannot afford DRAM refresh currents; they prefer SRAM or MRAM.
- **Cost Sensitivity:** Consumer electronics (smart TVs, washing machines) compete on price, so the cheapest memory that meets timing requirements is selected.
- **Physical Size:** Wearable/portable devices demand **high-density** memory (e.g., Flash over EEPROM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Structured Logic)

The memory hierarchy is organized in **5 distinct levels**, from fastest/closest to CPU to slowest/farthest. Each level is characterized by specific trade-offs:

### Level 1: CPU Registers
- **Location:** Inside the CPU itself.
- **Size:** A few bytes to a few hundred bytes (typically 16 × 32-bit registers in ARM Cortex-M).
- **Speed:** Access time ≈ **1 CPU clock cycle** (e.g., 1 ns at 1 GHz).
- **Technology:** Built using fast flip-flops (6 transistors per bit in SRAM style).
- **Why:** Registers hold the *operands* the ALU is currently processing. They are the *only* memory the CPU can manipulate directly.

### Level 2: Cache Memory (L1, L2, L3)
- **Location:** On-chip, between CPU and RAM.
- **Size:** Kilobytes to a few megabytes.
- **Speed:** Access time ≈ **2–10 CPU clock cycles**.
- **Why:** Caches exploit the *principle of locality* (temporal + spatial) to bridge the speed gap between CPU and RAM. They store *recently used* data blocks (cache lines) from main memory.
- **Note:** Most microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M0/M0+) used in KTU syllabus **do NOT have cache**; they go directly from registers to RAM. Cache is more common in application processors (Cortex-A series).

### Level 3: Main Memory (Primary Memory)
- **Location:** On-chip SRAM (in modern MCUs like STM32) or external DRAM.
- **Size:** Kilobytes to Gigabytes.
- **Speed:** Access time ≈ **10–100 ns**.
- **Subtypes:**
  * **SRAM (Static RAM):** Uses 6 transistors per bit. Fast, no refresh needed, expensive. Used for cache and small on-chip MCU RAM.
  * **DRAM (Dynamic RAM):** Uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit. Dense, cheap, but needs **refresh every ~64 ms**. Used in PCs and large embedded systems.

### Level 4: Secondary Storage (Mass Storage)
- **Location:** Off-chip.
- **Size:** Gigabytes to Terabytes.
- **Speed:** Access time ≈ **milliseconds** (HDD) or **microseconds** (Flash SSD).
- **Subtypes:**
  * **HDD:** Magnetic spinning platters. Slow, mechanical, high capacity, low cost.
  * **Flash Memory (NAND/NOR):** Solid-state, no moving parts, lower power, faster than HDD. Dominant in embedded systems.
  * **SD Cards, eMMC:** Packaged Flash for portable devices.

### Level 5: Tertiary / Archival Storage
- **Examples:** Magnetic tapes, optical discs (CD/DVD/Blu-ray), cloud storage.
- **Use in Embedded:** Very rare; mostly for backup or distributed IoT systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Locality (Cache Theory):** Programs tend to access a small region of memory repeatedly over a short period. This is why caching works:
> - **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is accessed, it is *likely to be accessed again soon* (e.g., loop counter).
> - **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is accessed, *nearby locations* are likely to be accessed soon (e.g., sequential array traversal).

## 2.2 Classification of Memory by Type

Memory is broadly classified into two categories: **Random Access Memory (RAM)** and **Read-Only Memory (ROM)**.

### 2.2.1 Random Access Memory (RAM) — Volatile

| Type | Full Name | Structure | Key Property | KTU Context |
|------|-----------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **SRAM** | Static RAM | 6T per bit | Fast, no refresh | On-chip MCU RAM, cache |
| **DRAM** | Dynamic RAM | 1T + 1C per bit | Needs refresh, dense | External memory, large buffers |
| **NVRAM** | Non-Volatile RAM | SRAM + battery | Retains data | Critical config storage |
| **FRAM** | Ferroelectric RAM | Polarized molecules | Fast, non-volatile | Newer IoT devices |

### 2.2.2 Read-Only Memory (ROM) — Non-Volatile

| Type | Full Name | Programming Method | Erasure | KTU Context |
|------|-----------|---------------------|---------|-------------|
| **ROM** | Mask ROM | Done at factory (mask) | Cannot be erased | Mass-produced devices |
| **PROM** | Programmable ROM | User-programmable (fuse) | Cannot be erased | One-time config |
| **EPROM** | Erasable PROM | UV light | UV erase (~20 min) | Old development boards |
| **EEPROM** | Electrically Erasable PROM | Electrical pulses | Byte-by-byte (slow) | Storing small config data |
| **Flash** | Flash EEPROM | Electrical, block-wise | Block/sector erase | Program storage in MCUs (STM32, 8051 derivatives) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Flash vs EEPROM (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **EEPROM** erases **byte-by-byte** (slow, ~5 ms per byte), but allows fine-grained updates.
> - **Flash** erases **block-by-block** (or sector/page-wise), much faster, but you must erase an entire block even to change one byte.
> - Modern MCUs use **Flash for program memory** and a small **EEPROM area** for user configuration data.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all key formulas** for memory system calculations expected in KTU 2024 exams. Use `\vert` (instead of `\vert`) for absolute value to keep tables safe.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|--------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Memory Capacity (Word-Organized)** | $C = 2^n \times m$ | $n$ = number of address lines, $m$ = word size in bits | bits |
| 2 | **Memory Capacity (Byte-Organized)** | $C = 2^n \times 8$ | For byte-organized memory, $m=8$ | bits (= bytes × 8) |
| 3 | **Total Addressable Locations** | $L = 2^n$ | Number of unique memory locations | locations |
| 4 | **Address Range** | $0 \text{ to } (2^n - 1)$ | Decimal range of addressable memory | decimal |
| 5 | **Average Memory Access Time (2-Level)** | $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_1 + (1 - h) \cdot T_2$ | $h$ = hit ratio (Level 1), $T_1$, $T_2$ = access times | ns |
| 6 | **Miss Penalty Throughput** | $T_{sys} = (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss}$ | Effective slowdown due to misses | ns |
| 7 | **AMAT (Average Memory Access Time)** | $AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \cdot T_{L2}$ | Recursive; $MR$ = miss rate | ns |
| 8 | **Hit Ratio** | $h = \dfrac{H}{H + M}$ | $H$ = hits, $M$ = misses | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 9 | **Miss Ratio** | $m = 1 - h$ | Complement of hit ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Cost per Bit** | $Cost_{bit} = \dfrac{\text{Total Memory Cost}}{C}$ | For budget calculation | currency/bit |
| 11 | **Memory Bandwidth** | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred}}{t_{transfer}}$ | Bytes per second | Bytes/s |
| 12 | **Refresh Power (DRAM)** | $P_{refresh} = I_{ref} \cdot V_{dd} \cdot f_{ref}$ | Static power overhead in DRAM | mW |
| 13 | **Memory Power (Active)** | $P_{active} = C_{L} \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic switching power | mW |
| 14 | **Effective Access Time w/ Cache** | $T_{eff} = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$ | Cache miss adds main memory delay | ns |
| 15 | **Address Line Count** | $n = \log_2(\text{Locations})$ | For designing decoders | bits |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The memory system design principles taught in this module are used in:

- **Automotive ECUs:** Selecting **NOR Flash** for fast boot code + **NAND Flash** for large map data in navigation systems.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Using **MRAM** or **FRAM** to retain sensor data on power loss without battery backup.
- **Smart Cards / RFID:** Combining **mask ROM** (immutable ID) + **EEPROM** (rewritable balance).
- **Mobile Phones:** LPDDR DRAM for app memory + UFS Flash for storage.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Battery-backed SRAM for guaranteed data retention during power cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Skill:** Given a microcontroller (e.g., 8051 with 16-bit address bus, or ARM with 32-bit address bus), the student must compute **maximum addressable memory**, **address ranges**, and **design address decoding** using logic gates or decoder ICs (e.g., 74LS138).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Capacity from Address Lines

**Problem Statement:** A microcontroller has $n = 16$ address lines ($A_{15} \text{ to } A_0$). The memory is byte-organized (each location stores 1 byte = 8 bits). Find the total memory capacity.

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters.**
The number of address lines is $n = 16$. The word size is $m = 8 \text{ bits}$ (byte-organized).

**Step 2 — Apply the capacity formula.**
We know that the total number of unique addressable locations is given by:

$$L = 2^n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the value of $n$.**
Plugging in $n = 16$:

$$L = 2^{16}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power of 2.**

$$L = 65536 \text{ locations}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by word size to get total capacity.**

$$C = L \times m = 65536 \times 8$$

**Step 6 — Final simplification.**

$$C = 524288 \text{ bits} = 65536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 16-bit address bus can address exactly **64 KB of memory** when byte-organized. This is why the classic **Intel 8051** (which uses a 16-bit address bus) is limited to **64 KB of code + 64 KB of external data memory** — a classic KTU exam question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Address Decoding for Memory Interfacing

**Problem Statement:** An 8051 system has 16 address lines ($A_{15}–A_{0}$). We need to interface a **4 KB RAM chip** (which has 12 address lines internally) and a **4 KB ROM chip** so that:
- RAM occupies address range **$0000H$ – $0FFFH$**
- ROM occupies address range **$1000H$ – $1FFFH$**

Design the chip select logic using basic gates.

**Step 1 — Determine internal address lines of each chip.**
For a 4 KB = $4096 = 2^{12}$ byte chip, the chip requires 12 address lines: $A_{11} \text{ to } A_0$.

This means the **upper 4 address lines** ($A_{15}, A_{14}, A_{13}, A_{12}$) are used for **chip selection**.

**Step 2 — Analyze the binary address ranges.**

| Range | $A_{15}$ | $A_{14}$ | $A_{13}$ | $A_{12}$ | $A_{11} \text{ to } A_0$ |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| $0000H – 0FFFH$ (RAM) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $000000000000$ to $111111111111$ |
| $1000H – 1FFFH$ (ROM) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $000000000000$ to $111111111111$ |

**Step 3 — Derive the chip select (CS) logic for RAM.**

The RAM chip should be selected when $A_{15} = 0$, $A_{14} = 0$, $A_{13} = 0$, $A_{12} = 0$.

In Boolean logic, the chip select signal $\overline{CS_{RAM}}$ (active low) is:

$$\overline{CS_{RAM}} = \overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot \overline{A_{13}} \cdot \overline{A_{12}}$$

Using a 4-input NAND gate to generate the active-low CS:

$$CS_{RAM} = \overline{\overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot \overline{A_{13}} \cdot \overline{A_{12}}}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the chip select logic for ROM.**

The ROM chip should be selected when $A_{15} = 0$, $A_{14} = 0$, $A_{13} = 0$, $A_{12} = 1$.

In Boolean logic, the chip select signal $\overline{CS_{ROM}}$ (active low) is:

$$\overline{CS_{ROM}} = \overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot \overline{A_{13}} \cdot A_{12}$$

Using a 4-input NAND gate plus an inverter (or a single AND-OR-INVERT gate):

$$CS_{ROM} = \overline{\overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot \overline{A_{13}} \cdot A_{12}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**

For address $0A5FH = 0000\ 1010\ 0101\ 1111_2$:
- $A_{15}=0, A_{14}=0, A_{13}=0, A_{12}=0$ → RAM is selected, ROM is disabled. ✓
- Internal address lines $A_{11} \text{ to } A_0$ deliver $A5F_{16}$ to the RAM chip. ✓

For address $1A5FH = 0001\ 1010\ 0101\ 1111_2$:
- $A_{15}=0, A_{14}=0, A_{13}=0, A_{12}=1$ → ROM is selected, RAM is disabled. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw a **truth table** of the upper address lines and identify the unique pattern for each chip. This earns full marks for "design" questions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Access Time with Cache

**Problem Statement:** A system has an L1 cache with **hit ratio $h = 0.9$** (90%), cache access time $T_c = 5 \text{ ns}$, and main memory access time $T_m = 50 \text{ ns}$. Calculate the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**.

**Step 1 — Recall the AMAT formula for a single-level cache.**

$$T_{avg} = h \cdot T_c + (1 - h) \cdot (T_c + T_m)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$T_{avg} = 0.9 \cdot 5 + (1 - 0.9) \cdot (5 + 50)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the cache hit term.**

$$T_{avg} = 4.5 + 0.1 \cdot 55$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the miss term.**

$$T_{avg} = 4.5 + 5.5$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$T_{avg} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with no cache.**
Without cache, every access takes $T_m = 50 \text{ ns}$.
Speedup factor:

$$S = \frac{T_{no\_cache}}{T_{avg}} = \frac{50}{10} = 5\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The cache provides a **5× speedup** even with only 90% hit ratio, because the cache is **10× faster** than main memory. This illustrates the immense value of caching in memory hierarchy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SRAM vs DRAM vs Flash

This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam comparison. Memorize the distinctions.

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|-----------|------|------|-----------|------------|
| **Cell Structure** | 6 Transistors | 1T + 1 Capacitor | 1T (floating gate) | 1T (floating gate, string) |
| **Density** | Low | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Volatility** | Volatile | Volatile | Non-volatile | Non-volatile |
| **Refresh Required** | No | Yes (every 64 ms) | No | No |
| **Read Speed** | Very Fast (~1 ns) | Fast (~10 ns) | Fast (~50 ns) | Moderate (~25 µs/page) |
| **Write Speed** | Very Fast (1 ns) | Fast (10 ns) | Slow (~5 ms/page) | Slower (~200 µs/page) |
| **Endurance (Writes)** | Unlimited | Unlimited | ~10K–100K cycles | ~10K–1M cycles |
| **Power (Active)** | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Power (Standby)** | Leakage only | Refresh current | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Cost per Bit** | High | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| **Typical Use** | CPU registers, cache, MCU SRAM | PC main memory | Boot ROM, MCU code | SSD, SD cards, large storage |
| **Interface** | Random byte access | Random byte access | Random byte read, block write | Block/page only |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Number of Chips Required to Expand Memory

**Problem Statement:** Design a memory subsystem using **$1K \times 8$ RAM chips** to build a **$4K \times 8$ memory system**.

**Step 1 — Identify the chip and target specifications.**
- Each chip: $1K \times 8$ = 1024 locations × 8 bits.
- Target: $4K \times 8$ = 4096 locations × 8 bits.

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of chips needed (rows × columns).**

$$\text{Total chips} = \frac{\text{Target capacity}}{\text{Per chip capacity}} = \frac{4K \times 8}{1K \times 8} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the arrangement.**

Since the target width is 8 bits (same as chip width), we only need to extend the **depth (number of locations)**, not the width.

Number of rows (banks) needed:

$$N_{rows} = \frac{4K}{1K} = 4$$

So the arrangement is **4 rows × 1 column = 4 chips**.

**Step 4 — Address line allocation.**

Each $1K \times 8$ chip needs $n = \log_2(1024) = 10$ address lines: $A_9 \text{ to } A_0$.

The 4K target needs $n = \log_2(4096) = 12$ address lines: $A_{11} \text{ to } A_0$.

The 2 extra upper address lines ($A_{11}, A_{10}$) are used to **select which chip** is active.

**Step 5 — Chip select logic using 2-to-4 decoder (74LS139).**

| $A_{11}$ | $A_{10}$ | Active Chip | Address Range |
|----------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| 0 | 0 | Chip 0 | $000H – 3FFH$ |
| 0 | 1 | Chip 1 | $400H – 7FFH$ |
| 1 | 0 | Chip 2 | $800H – BFFH$ |
| 1 | 1 | Chip 3 | $C00H – FFFH$ |

$$Y_0 = \overline{A_{11}} \cdot \overline{A_{10}}, \quad Y_1 = \overline{A_{11}} \cdot A_{10}, \quad Y_2 = A_{11} \cdot \overline{A_{10}}, \quad Y_3 = A_{11} \cdot A_{10}$$

The 74LS139 decoder's outputs are **active-low**, which is naturally compatible with the **active-low CS** of standard memory chips.

## 4.4 SRAM Cell Schematic (Conceptual Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the classic **6-Transistor (6T) SRAM cell** structure. This is a fundamental circuit-level concept often asked in KTU vivas.

```mermaid
graph TD
    wordlineA["WORD LINE WL<br/>Row Select"]
    blNodeB["BIT LINE BL<br/>Read Write"]
    blbNodeC["BIT LINE BAR BLB<br/>Complement"]

    accessTr1["M1 NMOS<br/>Access Left"]
    accessTr2["M2 NMOS<br/>Access Right"]
    pullUpTr3["M3 PMOS<br/>Pull Up Left"]
    pullUpTr4["M4 PMOS<br/>Pull Up Right"]
    pullDownTr5["M5 NMOS<br/>Pull Down Left"]
    pullDownTr6["M6 NMOS<br/>Pull Down Right"]

    storageNodeQ["STORAGE NODE Q<br/>Stores 1 bit"]
    storageNodeQB["STORAGE NODE Q BAR<br/>Complement"]

    wordlineA --> accessTr1
    wordlineA --> accessTr2
    accessTr1 --> storageNodeQ
    accessTr2 --> storageNodeQB
    storageNodeQ --> pullDownTr5
    storageNodeQ --> pullUpTr3
    storageNodeQB --> pullDownTr6
    storageNodeQB --> pullUpTr4
    blNodeB --> accessTr1
    blbNodeC --> accessTr2

    style wordlineA fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#a80
    style storageNodeQ fill:#69db7c,stroke:#070
    style storageNodeQB fill:#ff8787,stroke:#900
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How 6T SRAM Stores a Bit:** Two cross-coupled inverters (M3+M5 form one inverter, M4+M6 form the other) form a bistable latch. When $Q=1$, $Q_B=0$, the latch is stable in that state indefinitely *as long as power is on*. The two access transistors (M1, M2) connect the cell to the bit lines only when the word line is asserted, allowing read or write. This is why SRAM is **fast** (no charge-sharing, no refresh) but **low density** (6 transistors per bit).

---

## 4.5 Flash Memory Cell Architecture (Conceptual Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    controlGateA["CONTROL GATE CG<br/>Word Line"]
    floatingGateB["FLOATING GATE FG<br/>Traps Electrons<br/>Stores the Bit"]
    oxideLayerC["TUNNEL OXIDE<br/>SiO2 Thin Layer"]
    sourceNodeD["SOURCE S"]
    drainNodeE["DRAIN D"]
    substrateNodeF["P SUBSTRATE<br/>Bulk Silicon"]
    bitLineG["BIT LINE BL"]

    controlGateA --- floatingGateB
    floatingGateB --- oxideLayerC
    oxideLayerC --- substrateNodeF
    sourceNodeD --- substrateNodeF
    drainNodeE --- substrateNodeF
    drainNodeE --- bitLineG

    style floatingGateB fill:#ff8787,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style controlGateA fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#a80
    style oxideLayerC fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#007,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How Flash Stores a Bit (Conceptual):** A floating gate transistor has an *electrically isolated* gate buried under the control gate. To write a '0' (program), high voltage (~12V) is applied to push electrons *into* the floating gate via quantum tunneling. To erase, reverse voltage pulls electrons *out*. The presence or absence of trapped electrons shifts the transistor's threshold voltage, which is sensed during a read. This is **non-volatile** because the trapped electrons can stay for **years** without power.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give one example for each type.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Loses stored data when power is removed | Retains stored data even without power |
| **Data Retention** | Requires continuous power supply | Power-independent retention (years) |
| **Speed** | Generally faster (especially SRAM) | Slower for writes (Flash ~ms) |
| **Examples** | SRAM, DRAM | ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash |
| **Use Case** | Temporary working storage, cache | Program storage, configuration data |

**Example:** **SRAM (volatile)** is used in CPU cache; **Flash memory (non-volatile)** is used to store the bootloader and program code in microcontrollers. *(3 marks: 1.5 for table + 1.5 for example)*

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is memory hierarchy? Why is it used in computer/embedded systems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Memory hierarchy is the structured arrangement of different memory types in a system, ordered by **access speed, cost per bit, and proximity to the CPU**.

**Why it is used (3 reasons):**

1. **Speed-Cost Trade-off:** The fastest memories (registers, cache) are extremely expensive per bit. Building the entire system out of SRAM would be prohibitively costly. Hierarchy allows using small amounts of fast, expensive memory for the *most-accessed* data, and large amounts of slow, cheap memory for *rarely-accessed* data.

2. **Exploits Locality of Reference:** Programs exhibit temporal and spatial locality. Caches (close to CPU) store recent data, exploiting temporal locality. Block transfers from main memory exploit spatial locality.

3. **Optimizes Average Access Time:** The **AMAT** of a hierarchical system is much lower than the AMAT of a flat, single-memory system. For example, a system with 90% cache hit ratio and 10× faster cache achieves ~5× speedup.

**Conclusion:** Memory hierarchy is the engineering solution to the impossible goal of having *fast, large, cheap, and non-volatile* memory all at once. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for reasons)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of ROM (PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash) with neat diagrams of their cell structures. Compare their programming and erasure mechanisms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A microcontroller has 20 address lines and 16 data lines. Calculate the maximum addressable memory in bytes. If a 64 KB ROM chip is to be interfaced, design the chip select logic and show the address range it occupies.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. PROM (Programmable Read-Only Memory):**
- **Structure:** Uses fuse-based or anti-fuse based cells. Each cell has a fusible link (poly-silicon or nichrome).
- **Programming:** User blows the fuse (or creates a connection) at high current. *One-time programmable* — once blown, cannot be restored.
- **Erasure:** Not possible. **Permanent.**
- **Diagram:** Fuse in series with bit line; if fuse intact → reads 1; if blown → reads 0.

**2. EPROM (Erasable PROM):**
- **Structure:** Uses a **floating-gate MOSFET** (similar to Flash, but with separate UV erasable window). Each cell stores charge in a fully isolated floating gate.
- **Programming:** Hot electron injection at high voltage (~12–25 V) traps electrons in the floating gate.
- **Erasure:** Exposed to **UV light through a quartz window** for ~20 minutes. UV provides energy to discharge the floating gate. **Must be removed from the circuit for erasure.**
- **Diagram:** FAMOS (Floating-gate Avalanche-injection MOS) transistor with UV window on the package.

**3. EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM):**
- **Structure:** Floating gate + a **thin tunnel oxide region** enabling Fowler-Nordheim tunneling.
- **Programming & Erasure:** Both done **electrically** at high voltage. Can be **erased byte-by-byte**, even in-circuit.
- **Limitation:** Slower writes (~5 ms per byte) and lower endurance (~100K cycles) than Flash.
- **Diagram:** FLOTOX (Floating-gate Tunnel Oxide) cell.

**4. Flash Memory:**
- **Structure:** Single floating-gate transistor per cell, but in two main architectures: **NOR** and **NAND**.
- **Programming:** Hot electron injection (NOR) or Fowler-Nordheim tunneling (NAND).
- **Erasure:** **Electrical**, but **block-wise / sector-wise** (not byte-wise). This is the key speed advantage.
- **NOR Flash:** Allows random byte access and **Execute-In-Place (XIP)**. Used for boot code.
- **NAND Flash:** Page/block access only. Higher density, lower cost. Used for mass storage.
- **Diagram:** Floating gate with control gate; bit line, source line, word line.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash |
|---------|------|-------|---------|-------|
| Programming | Fuse | Hot e⁻ | Tunneling | Tunneling / Hot e⁻ |
| Erasure | Not possible | UV (~20 min) | Electrical, byte-wise | Electrical, block-wise |
| In-circuit erase | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Density | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Low |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Number of address lines: $n = 20$
- Number of data lines: $m = 16$
- ROM chip size: 64 KB

**Step 1: Calculate maximum addressable memory.**

The number of unique addressable locations is:

$$L = 2^n = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ locations}$$

Since each location stores $m = 16$ bits (because the data bus is 16-bit wide):

$$C_{max} = L \times m = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 16 \text{ bits} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2: Determine ROM chip's internal structure.**

$64 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes}$.

Since the data bus is 16 bits wide, the ROM chip is likely organized as $32K \times 16$ bits. The chip needs **15 address lines** internally: $A_{14} \text{ to } A_0$.

**Step 3: Determine the address range.**

Since the ROM uses the lower 15 address lines ($A_{14}–A_0$), the upper 5 address lines ($A_{19}–A_{15}$) determine the chip select.

**Step 4: Choose a placement (e.g., starting at $00000H$).**

If the ROM occupies the lowest 64 KB:

| Address | $A_{19}…A_{15}$ | $A_{14}…A_0$ |
|---------|------------------|----------------|
| $00000H$ – $0FFFFH$ | 00000 | All zeros to all ones |

The chip select condition is: $A_{19}=0, A_{18}=0, A_{17}=0, A_{16}=0, A_{15}=0$.

**Step 5: Write the chip select logic.**

Using a 5-input NAND gate (or AND + Inverter):

$$\overline{CS_{ROM}} = \overline{A_{19} \cdot A_{18} \cdot A_{17} \cdot A_{16} \cdot A_{15}}$$

Or equivalently, using active-low logic:

$$CS_{ROM} = A_{19} + A_{18} + A_{17} + A_{16} + A_{15} \quad \text{(active only when all are 0)}$$

The address range is **$00000H$ to $0FFFFH$ (64 KB)**. ✓

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the memory hierarchy in a typical embedded system. Discuss the role of cache memory and explain temporal and spatial locality with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system has L1 cache with hit ratio 0.85 and access time 4 ns. Main memory access time is 60 ns. Calculate the average memory access time (AMAT). What is the speedup obtained over a system with no cache? If the cache hit ratio is improved to 0.95, what is the new AMAT and speedup?

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Same as Section 4.1 — Pyramid from Registers → Cache → RAM → Flash → Archival)

**Memory Hierarchy Levels (3 marks):**

1. **CPU Registers** — Inside CPU, fastest (1 cycle), smallest (bytes).
2. **Cache (L1, L2)** — On-chip, very fast (2–10 cycles), small (KB-MB).
3. **Main Memory (RAM)** — On-chip SRAM or external DRAM, fast (10–100 ns), medium (MB).
4. **Secondary Storage (Flash, HDD)** — Off-chip, slow (µs-ms), large (GB).
5. **Archival (Tape, Cloud)** — Off-system, very slow (seconds), very large (TB-PB).

**Locality of Reference (2 marks):**

- **Temporal Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future.*
  - **Example:** In a `for (i=0; i<100; i++) sum = sum + arr[i];` loop, the variable `i` and `sum` are accessed in every iteration, so the cache keeps them in fast storage.
  - **Cache strategy:** "Least Recently Used" (LRU) replacement exploits this.

- **Spatial Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, nearby memory locations are likely to be referenced soon.*
  - **Example:** Sequential array traversal `arr[0], arr[1], arr[2]...`. When `arr[0]` is fetched, the entire cache line (e.g., 64 bytes) containing `arr[0]–arr[15]` is loaded. The next 15 accesses are cache hits.
  - **Cache strategy:** Larger cache lines / prefetching exploits this.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $T_c = 4 \text{ ns}$, $T_m = 60 \text{ ns}$
- Case 1: $h_1 = 0.85$
- Case 2: $h_2 = 0.95$

**Step 1: Recall the AMAT formula.**

$$T_{avg} = h \cdot T_c + (1-h) \cdot (T_c + T_m)$$

**Step 2: Calculate AMAT for Case 1 ($h = 0.85$).**

$$T_{avg,1} = 0.85 \cdot 4 + (1 - 0.85) \cdot (4 + 60)$$

$$T_{avg,1} = 3.4 + 0.15 \cdot 64$$

$$T_{avg,1} = 3.4 + 9.6 = 13.0 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: Calculate speedup for Case 1.**

No-cache time: $T_{no\_cache} = 60 \text{ ns}$.

$$S_1 = \frac{T_{no\_cache}}{T_{avg,1}} = \frac{60}{13.0} \approx 4.62$$

**Step 4: Calculate AMAT for Case 2 ($h = 0.95$).**

$$T_{avg,2} = 0.95 \cdot 4 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot (4 + 60)$$

$$T_{avg,2} = 3.8 + 0.05 \cdot 64$$

$$T_{avg,2} = 3.8 + 3.2 = 7.0 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5: Calculate speedup for Case 2.**

$$S_2 = \frac{60}{7.0} \approx 8.57$$

**Step 6: Comparison summary.**

| Hit Ratio | AMAT (ns) | Speedup |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| 0.85 | 13.0 | 4.62× |
| 0.95 | 7.0 | 8.57× |

**Conclusion:** Increasing the cache hit ratio from 85% to 95% (a 10% improvement) reduces AMAT by **46%** and nearly **doubles** the speedup. This demonstrates that even small improvements in hit ratio yield disproportionately large performance gains — a fundamental principle of cache design. *(7 marks: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1 for conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $2^n$ and $2^n \times m$:** Many students write "$2^{16} = 16$ KB" — **WRONG!** $2^{16} = 64$ KB (byte-organized) or $2^{16} \times 8 = 64$ KB in bits. **Always state assumptions** (byte vs. word organized).
> 
> 2. **Mixing up address lines for capacity vs. chip-select:** A 4 KB chip has 12 internal address lines, NOT 16. The remaining lines do chip-select. **Show this in the truth table.**
> 
> 3. **Active-Low vs Active-High CS confusion:** Memory chips usually have $\overline{CS}$ (active low). If you use a NAND gate's output directly, remember it's active-low. Mixing polarities loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units:** $t_A = 5$ (with no unit) is incomplete. Write **5 ns**.
> 
> 5. **AMAT formula errors:** Many students write $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_c + (1-h) \cdot T_m$, forgetting the $T_c$ added in the miss path. The correct formula is $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$. Examiners *will* deduct a mark for this.
> 
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram:** For interfacing questions, **always draw a block diagram** of CPU ↔ Decoder ↔ Memory chips. A 14-mark question without a diagram usually caps at 10–11 marks.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring the Principle of Locality:** When explaining caching, you MUST mention both temporal AND spatial locality with **concrete code examples** (e.g., loop variables for temporal, array access for spatial).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this as a **rapid-revision checklist** the night before your KTU exam.

### **A. Core Concepts**
- ☐ Memory hierarchy = pyramid of 5 levels (Registers → Cache → RAM → Flash → Archival).
- ☐ Trade-off triangle: Speed ↑, Cost ↑, Capacity ↓ (going UP the pyramid).
- ☐ **SRAM** = 6T per bit, no refresh, fast, expensive → used for cache & on-chip MCU RAM.
- ☐ **DRAM** = 1T + 1C per bit, needs refresh, dense, cheap → used for PC main memory.
- ☐ **Flash** = electrically erasable, block-wise, non-volatile → program storage in MCUs.
- ☐ **EEPROM** = electrically erasable, **byte-wise**, non-volatile → small config storage.
- ☐ **Volatile** = loses data on power off (RAM); **Non-volatile** = retains data (ROM/Flash).

### **B. Must-Memorize Formulas**
- ☐ Capacity: $C = 2^n \times m$ (bits), where $n$ = address lines, $m$ = word size.
- ☐ Locations: $L = 2^n$.
- ☐ AMAT: $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$.
- ☐ Hit Ratio: $h = H / (H + M)$.
- ☐ Speedup: $S = T_{baseline} / T_{enhanced}$.
- ☐ Address lines for $X$ byte memory: $n = \lceil \log_2 X \rceil$.

### **C. Critical Design Knowledge**
- ☐ A 16-bit address bus (8051) can address **64 KB maximum** (byte-organized).
- ☐ A 32-bit address bus (ARM Cortex-M with MPU) can address **4 GB** maximum.
- ☐ **74LS138** is a 3-to-8 line decoder with active-low outputs — used for chip-select.
- ☐ **74LS139** is a 2-to-4 line decoder — used for smaller memory expansions.
- ☐ For a memory of size $2^k$, the lower $k$ address lines go to the chip; the rest are for CS.
- ☐ Always draw a **truth table** of the upper address lines to derive the CS logic.

### **D. Locality Principles**
- ☐ **Temporal Locality** = repeated access to same location (loop counters).
- ☐ **Spatial Locality** = access to nearby locations (sequential arrays).
- ☐ Cache line size is typically 16–64 bytes to exploit spatial locality.

### **E. Comparison Flashpoints (Frequent KTU Questions)**
- ☐ SRAM vs DRAM — 6 differences (density, refresh, power, speed, cost, use case).
- ☐ PROM vs EPROM vs EEPROM vs Flash — programming, erasure, in-circuit capability.
- ☐ NOR vs NAND Flash — access type, density, cost, use case.
- ☐ Volatile vs Non-volatile — definition, examples, use cases.

### **F. Numerical Problem-Solving Strategy**
- ☐ **Step 1:** Identify given: address lines, data lines, chip size.
- ☐ **Step 2:** Compute total capacity and per-chip capacity.
- ☐ **Step 3:** Determine arrangement (rows × columns of chips).
- ☐ **Step 4:** Allocate address lines (lower to chip, upper to decoder).
- ☐ **Step 5:** Write Boolean expression for chip select.
- ☐ **Step 6:** Verify with a sample address from inside and outside the range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In Module 1, the highest-weightage questions are (i) Memory hierarchy explanation, (ii) ROM family comparison, and (iii) Memory interfacing numericals. Practice at least 3 numerical problems on **address decoding** and **memory expansion** using the worked examples in Section 3 as templates. Time to allocate in exam: Part A = 3 min/question, Part B = 25–30 min/question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exceptions and Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exceptions and Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (ARM Cortex-M Perspective)

In the architecture of the **ARM Cortex-M** family of microcontrollers, an **Exception** is defined as any event that causes a temporary suspension of the currently executing program flow and forces the processor to execute a dedicated handler routine. Exceptions are broadly classified into two super-categories by the **ARMv7-M / ARMv8-M Architecture Reference Manual**:

1. **System Exceptions**: Internal events triggered by the processor core itself (e.g., Reset, NMI, HardFault, SysTick).
2. **Interrupts**: External asynchronous events triggered by peripherals (e.g., GPIO, UART, ADC, Timer) routed through the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** For the course **PCRAT302**, the term *Exception* is treated as a superset of *Interrupt*. The Cortex-M architecture supports up to **256** exception entries in its Vector Table, although only a small subset are architecturally defined; the remainder are available for device-specific interrupts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Master Chef in a Kitchen")

Imagine a chef is preparing a complex multi-course meal. The chef represents the **CPU's main thread (Thread Mode)** executing the `main()` routine:

- **Exception = A sudden event demanding immediate attention.** A pot on the stove begins to boil over — the chef *pauses* the current garnish chopping, deals with the pot, then *returns* to chopping exactly where he left off.
- **Interrupt = An external ring of the doorbell or phone call.** A delivery person arrives (a peripheral signals a status flag).
- **The NVIC = The head assistant standing beside the chef.** The assistant listens to the doorbell, judges its urgency, and politely taps the chef's shoulder.
- **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) = The action the chef takes** (e.g., signing for the delivery, putting the pot down).

The chef's *recipe book* (his program counter) keeps the exact page bookmarked (the **stack frame**), so resuming the garnish chopping feels seamless.

### 1.3 Critical Distinction: Polling vs. Interrupts

> [!NOTE]
> **Polling** wastes CPU cycles continuously checking flag bits. **Interrupts** are *event-driven*: the CPU executes other code until the NVIC literally *interrupts* it, achieving superior power efficiency — a core tenet of embedded systems design emphasized in the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework under **CO1: Architectural Understanding**.

| Mechanism | CPU Utilization | Power | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polling | High waste | High | Variable |
| Interrupt | Efficient | Low | Deterministic |

### 1.4 The Vector Table — The "Address Book of Handlers"

The **Vector Table** is a reserved block of ROM/RAM (default base: **0x00000000**) that stores the **starting addresses** of all exception handlers. The Cortex-M3/M4 supports a configurable offset via the `VTOR` (Vector Table Offset Register).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each entry is exactly **4 bytes** (32 bits), holding the LSB of the handler address. Bit 0 of every valid handler address in the vector table must be **1**, indicating Thumb-2 instruction set compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Exception Classification on ARM Cortex-M

The Cortex-M3/M4 architecture defines a **fixed architectural exception set** plus a variable set of **device-specific external interrupts (IRQs)**. Below is the complete list mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCRAT302 Module 1:

| Exception # | Name | Type | Priority (Default) | Brief Cause |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reset | System | -3 (Highest) | Power-on / Pin reset |
| 2 | NMI | System | -2 | Non-Maskable Interrupt (clock fail, etc.) |
| 3 | HardFault | System | -1 | All classes of fault escalated |
| 4 | MemManage | System | Settable | MPU access violation |
| 5 | BusFault | System | Settable | Bus error during fetch/data access |
| 6 | UsageFault | System | Settable | Undefined instruction, divide by zero |
| 7–10 | Reserved | — | — | Unused slots |
| 11 | SVCall | System | Settable | Triggered by `SVC` instruction (OS syscall) |
| 12 | DebugMonitor | System | Settable | Debug breakpoint events |
| 13 | Reserved | — | — | — |
| 14 | PendSV | System | Settable | Pendable service (RTOS context switch) |
| 15 | SysTick | System | Settable | System tick timer overflow |
| 16+ | External IRQs | Peripheral | Settable | Device-specific (UART, GPIO, ADC…) |

> [!NOTE]
> Negative priorities (-3, -2, -1) are **higher** than any non-negative priority. They cannot be masked by the `PRIMASK` register, making Reset truly unstoppable.

### 2.2 The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) — Operating Logic

The NVIC is a hardware unit tightly integrated with the processor core. Its operations are best understood as a **6-step sequence**:

1. **Detection**: A peripheral asserts its interrupt request line to the NVIC.
2. **Masking Check**: The NVIC consults the `PRIMASK`, `BASEPRI`, and `FAULTMASK` special registers to determine if the exception is *enabled* for the current execution context.
3. **Priority Comparison**: If multiple exceptions are pending simultaneously, the NVIC selects the one with the **lowest numerical priority value** (highest logical priority).
4. **Preemption Evaluation**: If the new exception's priority is strictly higher than the currently executing exception's priority, the running handler is **preempted** (stacked and replaced).
5. **Vector Fetch**: The NVIC reads the handler address from the Vector Table offset corresponding to the exception number.
6. **Stack Push (Stacking)**: Eight registers are automatically pushed onto the active stack (PSP or MSP) — this is the *hardware stacking* characteristic of Cortex-M.

### 2.3 Stacking: The Hardware-Automatic Mechanism

When an exception is accepted, the following **eight 32-bit registers** are automatically pushed onto the stack in a single bus transaction (atomic operation):

> xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0

This fixed sequence is essential for **deterministic interrupt latency** — a key design philosophy of Cortex-M.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selection between **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** and **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** is governed by bit 1 of the `CONTROL` register. On exception entry, the processor always uses the **MSP** by default; the PSP is reserved for Thread Mode in RTOS environments.

### 2.4 Priority Grouping (PRIGROUP Field)

ARM Cortex-M uses a **configurable priority field width** controlled by the `PRIGROUP` bits in the `AIRCR` (Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register):

| PRIGROUP | Preemption Bits | Subpriority Bits | Total Levels |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0b011 | 5 | 3 | 32 (or 128/256) |
| 0b100 | 4 | 4 | 16 (or 32) |
| 0b101 | 3 | 5 | 8 (or 16) |

The priority byte stored in the NVIC priority register is divided into a **preemption field** (upper bits) and a **subpriority field** (lower bits). Only the **preemption field** controls preemption; the subpriority field resolves tie-breaks when two exceptions of the same preemption level arrive simultaneously.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` is used below to denote absolute value or bit-field width to comply with the markdown anti-break rule.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vector Table Entry Address | $A_{vec} = A_{base} + (N_{exc} \times 4)$ | Bytes; $N_{exc}$ = exception number |
| 2 | Effective Priority Byte | $P_{eff} = P_{raw} \gg S_{sub}$ | Where $S_{sub}$ = subpriority shift |
| 3 | Stacking Frame Size | $F_{stack} = 8 \times 4 = 32$ | Bytes (8 registers, 4 bytes each) |
| 4 | Worst-Case Interrupt Latency | $L_{max} = T_{cyc} \times N_{instr}$ | Cycles; typically 12 cycles on Cortex-M3 |
| 5 | Exception Vectors (Cortex-M3) | $N_{vectors} = 256$ | Entries in Vector Table |
| 6 | Priority Levels (typical) | $L_{prio} = 2^{k}$ | Where $k$ = implemented priority bits (3–8) |
| 7 | Bit-Band Alias Address | $A_{alias} = 0x22000000 + (A_{byte} - 0x20000000) \times 32 + n \times 4$ | Bytes; for atomic bit manipulation |
| 8 | MSP Initial Value | Read from Vector Table $[0]$ | First word of Vector Table |
| 9 | Reset Handler Address | Read from Vector Table $[1]$ | Second word of Vector Table |
| 10 | NVIC Pending Register | `ISPR[n] = 1` | Bit addressable, n = IRQ number |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

This interrupt architecture is the backbone of every real-time embedded product: automotive ECUs, medical infusion pumps, drone flight controllers, and IoT sensor nodes all rely on the deterministic, low-latency response that the Cortex-M NVIC provides. The **KTU 2024 Scheme** specifically maps this topic to **CO1 (Architectural Concepts)** and **CO2 (Programming & Interfacing)**, making it a high-weightage area in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vector Table Address Calculation

**Problem Statement:** For a Cortex-M4 device with `VTOR = 0x08000000`, find the address stored in Vector Table entry number 11 (SVCall handler).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{vec} &= A_{base} + (N_{exc} \times 4) \\
A_{vec} &= 0x08000000 + (11 \times 4) \\
A_{vec} &= 0x08000000 + 44_{dec} \\
A_{vec} &= 0x08000000 + 0x2C \\
A_{vec} &= 0x0800002C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** Each vector occupies **4 bytes** (one 32-bit word). Multiplying the exception number by 4 yields the byte offset. The architecture pre-allocates slots 0–15 for system exceptions, so the SVCall handler is always the **11th entry** (index 11, zero-based), regardless of the device manufacturer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Priority with Subpriority

**Problem Statement:** A peripheral IRQ is configured with raw priority byte = 0x40. The `PRIGROUP` field is set to 0b100 (4 preemption bits, 4 subpriority bits). Compute the effective preemption level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{sub} &= \text{Number of subpriority bits} = 4 \\
P_{eff} &= P_{raw} \gg S_{sub} \\
P_{eff} &= 0x40 \gg 4 \\
0x40 &= 0100\,0000_{2} \\
\text{Shift right by 4: } & 0000\,0100_{2} = 0x04 \\
P_{eff} &= 0x04 = 4_{dec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** The upper 4 bits of the 8-bit priority register represent the preemption level. The lower 4 bits (subpriority) are discarded by the right-shift operation. The resulting value **0x04** is what the NVIC uses to compare this IRQ against other exceptions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Interrupt Latency Bound (Worst Case)

**Problem Statement:** A Cortex-M3 runs at 72 MHz. Calculate the worst-case interrupt latency for an external IRQ, given that the tail of a multi-cycle instruction (LDM) is executing.

**Given Data:**

- Clock Frequency: $f_{clk} = 72\,\text{MHz}$
- Clock Period: $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$
- Worst-case instruction completion: 1 cycle (for an aligned 32-bit instruction)
- Vector fetch: 1 cycle
- Stacking: 12 cycles
- Interrupt entry: 1 cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cyc} &= \dfrac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \\
T_{cyc} &= 13.889\,\text{ns} \\
N_{cycles} &= 1 + 1 + 12 + 1 = 15\,\text{cycles} \\
L_{max} &= T_{cyc} \times N_{cycles} \\
L_{max} &= 13.889\,\text{ns} \times 15 \\
L_{max} &= 208.33\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** The 15-cycle budget is a hallmark of Cortex-M3's deterministic design. Compare this to traditional ARM7, where latency could exceed 30+ cycles due to manual stack pushing. The fixed-cycle count enables **hard real-time** scheduling — critical for motor control and medical applications.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Tail-Chain Latency

**Problem Statement:** Two ISRs back-to-back — ISR-A finishes and triggers ISR-B (same priority). Compute the time saved by **tail-chaining** versus a full stack pop-push cycle.

**Standard Pop-Push Cost (without tail-chain):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pop} &= 12\,\text{cycles (pop 8 registers)} \\
T_{push} &= 12\,\text{cycles (push 8 registers)} \\
T_{total} &= T_{pop} + T_{push} = 24\,\text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tail-Chain Cost (Cortex-M optimization):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tail} &= 6\,\text{cycles (pop LR, then re-fetch new vector)} \\
\text{Savings per transition: } \Delta T &= 24 - 6 = 18\,\text{cycles} \\
\text{Percentage saving: } \eta &= \dfrac{18}{24} \times 100\% = 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** Tail-chaining is an **architectural optimization** unique to Cortex-M. Instead of fully unwinding the stack, the processor merely skips the PC, xPSR, and LR restoration and re-fetches the new vector. This 75% reduction in transition overhead is essential in high-interrupt-rate applications like USB or motor commutation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Exception Entry & Exit Sequence

| Step | Action | Register Modified | Stack Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interrupt asserted | `IPSR` updated with IRQ# | None |
| 2 | Priority & mask check | `PRIMASK` consulted | None |
| 3 | Push 8 registers | None (read only) | **+32 bytes** |
| 4 | Read vector | `PC` ← Vector[IRQ#] | None |
| 5 | Update LR | `LR` ← `EXC_RETURN` | None |
| 6 | Switch to MSP | `SP` ← MSP (if Thread) | None |
| 7 | Execute handler | User ISRs run | Variable |
| 8 | Triggered by `BX LR` | `EXC_RETURN` decoded | None |
| 9 | Pop 8 registers | Restore xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3-R0 | **−32 bytes** |

> [!NOTE]
> The `EXC_RETURN` value (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF9` for MSP, `0xFFFFFFFD` for PSP) is the magic value the LR holds upon exception entry. When the ISR executes `BX LR` or `POP {PC,…}`, the processor recognizes this magic value and triggers the unstacking sequence automatically.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Exception Handling Flow (Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Thread Execution] --> B{Interrupt Asserted?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[NVIC: Mask Check PRIMASK FAULTMASK]
    C --> D{Priority > Current?}
    D -- No --> E[Stay in Thread Set Pending Bit]
    E --> A
    D -- Yes --> F[Hardware Stack Push 8 Registers]
    F --> G[Read Vector from Vector Table]
    G --> H[Load PC with Handler Address]
    H --> I[LR set to EXC RETURN]
    I --> J[Execute ISR Code]
    J --> K{Return via BX LR or POP PC?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Decode EXC RETURN Value]
    L --> M[Hardware Stack Pop 8 Registers]
    M --> N[Resume Thread at Saved PC]
    N --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Cortex-M Vector Table Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OFFSET0[Offset 0x00 Reset Initial MSP]
    OFFSET4[Offset 0x04 Reset Handler Address]
    OFFSET8[Offset 0x08 NMI Handler]
    OFF12[Offset 0x0C HardFault Handler]
    OFF16[Offset 0x10 MemManage Handler]
    OFF20[Offset 0x14 BusFault Handler]
    OFF24[Offset 0x18 UsageFault Handler]
    OFF44[Offset 0x2C SVCall Handler]
    OFF56[Offset 0x38 PendSV Handler]
    OFF60[Offset 0x3C SysTick Handler]
    OFF64[Offset 0x40 External IRQ 0]
    OFF68[Offset 0x44 External IRQ 1]
    OFFDOT[... Additional IRQs]
    OFFMAX[Offset up to 0x3FF]
    OFFSET0 --> OFFSET4
    OFFSET4 --> OFFSET8
    OFFSET8 --> OFF12
    OFF12 --> OFF16
    OFF16 --> OFF20
    OFF20 --> OFF24
    OFF24 --> OFF44
    OFF44 --> OFF56
    OFF56 --> OFF60
    OFF60 --> OFF64
    OFF64 --> OFF68
    OFF68 --> OFFDOT
    OFFDOT --> OFFMAX
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: NVIC Priority Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSTART[Multiple Exceptions Pending] --> P1[Filter by Enable Bits NVIC ISER]
    P1 --> P2[Filter by Mask Registers PRIMASK BASEPRI FAULTMASK]
    P2 --> P3[Group by Preemption Field]
    P3 --> P4[Select Lowest Preemption Value]
    P4 --> P5{Same Preemption Level?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Select Lowest Subpriority]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Select Highest Priority Exception]
    P6 --> P8[Select Lowest Exception Number Tie Break]
    P7 --> PFIN[Final Selected Exception]
    P8 --> PFIN
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Block-Level Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC[ARM Cortex-M3 or M4 Core]
        CPU[CPU Pipeline] --> REG[Register Bank xPSR PC LR R12 R3 R0]
    end
    subgraph NVICUNIT[NVIC Hardware Block]
        ISER[Interrupt Set Enable Register ISER]
        ICER[Interrupt Clear Enable ICER]
        ISPR[Interrupt Set Pending ISPR]
        ICPR[Interrupt Clear Pending ICPR]
        IPR[Interrupt Priority Registers IPR0 to IPRn]
        VECTBL[Vector Table Interface]
    end
    subgraph PERIPH[On Chip Peripherals]
        UART[UART Module]
        TIMER[General Purpose Timer]
        ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
        GPIO[GPIO Ports]
    end
    UART -->|IRQ Line| ISER
    TIMER -->|IRQ Line| ISER
    ADC -->|IRQ Line| ISER
    GPIO -->|IRQ Line| ISER
    ISER -->|Pending Evaluation| CPU
    IPR -->|Priority Value| CPU
    VECTBL -->|Handler Address| CPU
    CPU -->|ISR Entry Stack Push| REG
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Stacking Sequence (Atomic Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Exception Accepted] --> S1[High Address in Stack]
    S1 --> S2[Push xPSR]
    S2 --> S3[Push PC]
    S3 --> S4[Push LR]
    S4 --> S5[Push R12]
    S5 --> S6[Push R3]
    S6 --> S7[Push R2]
    S7 --> S8[Push R1]
    S8 --> S9[Push R0]
    S9 --> S10[Low Address in Stack SP Updated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `OFFSET0`, `S1`, `ISER`) and labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting. Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are not used as standalone node IDs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "exception" in the context of ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. Differentiate between system exceptions and interrupts.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **exception** is any event that causes the Cortex-M processor to suspend the currently executing instruction stream and execute a dedicated handler routine. It is a super-category encompassing both internal processor events and external peripheral events.

**System Exceptions** originate from within the processor core itself. Examples include Reset, NMI, HardFault, SVCall, PendSV, and SysTick. They have fixed architectural numbers (1–15) and are always present in the vector table.

**Interrupts** originate from **on-chip peripherals** (UART, ADC, GPIO, Timers) and are routed through the NVIC. Their vector numbers start at 16 and extend up to the device-specific maximum. Interrupts are often referred to as **IRQs** in ARM documentation.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the system exception explanation, and 1 mark for the interrupt explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the purpose of the NVIC in an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller? List any three features of the NVIC.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** is a hardware block integrated into every Cortex-M core. Its purpose is to manage, prioritize, and route interrupt requests from peripherals to the processor with deterministic, low-latency response.

**Three Key Features:**

1. **Configurable Priority Levels:** Supports 3 to 8 bits of priority (8 to 256 levels), allowing fine-grained control over preemption.
2. **Hardware Automatic Stacking/Unstacking:** The NVIC automatically pushes/pops eight registers (xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3–R0) on exception entry/exit, eliminating the overhead of software register saving.
3. **Nested Interrupt Support:** Higher-priority exceptions can preempt lower-priority ones seamlessly, with full hardware-managed context switching.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete sequence of events that occur when an external interrupt is accepted by a Cortex-M3 processor. Include the role of stacking, vector fetching, and the `EXC_RETURN` mechanism in your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With the help of a priority grouping configuration where `PRIGROUP = 0b101`, calculate the effective preemption level for two IRQs configured with priority bytes `0x30` and `0x90`. Which IRQ will be serviced first if both arrive simultaneously, and why?

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Exception Entry Sequence [7 Marks]

When the NVIC accepts an external IRQ, the following **hardware-automatic** steps occur:

1. **Pending Bit Set (1 mark):** The peripheral's interrupt request line sets the corresponding bit in the `ISPR` (Interrupt Set Pending Register). The NVIC latches this request.

2. **Mask Evaluation (1 mark):** The processor consults the `PRIMASK`, `BASEPRI`, and `FAULTMASK` registers. If the incoming IRQ's priority is below the threshold set by these masks, the request is ignored (but stays pending).

3. **Priority Arbitration (1 mark):** The NVIC compares the new IRQ's preemption field against the currently executing exception's priority. If higher, preemption is permitted.

4. **Automatic Stacking (2 marks):** The processor pushes the following eight registers atomically (in one bus transaction) onto the **Main Stack (MSP)**: `xPSR`, `PC`, `LR`, `R12`, `R3`, `R2`, `R1`, `R0`. The `SP` is decremented by 32 bytes.

5. **Vector Fetch and PC Load (1 mark):** The processor reads the handler address from the vector table at offset `(IRQ_number × 4)` and loads it into `PC`.

6. **LR Set to EXC_RETURN (1 mark):** The `LR` register is loaded with a special magic value (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF9` for MSP frame, `0xFFFFFFFD` for PSP frame). This value is the "return ticket" that the unstacking hardware will later decode.

7. **Execution Begins:** The processor begins fetching instructions from the new handler.

---

### Part (b) — Priority Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:** `PRIGROUP = 0b101` → 3 preemption bits, 5 subpriority bits.

**Step 1: Determine the subpriority shift (1 mark)**
$$S_{sub} = 5$$

**Step 2: Compute effective preemption for IRQ-A = 0x30 (2 marks)**
$$P_A = 0x30 \gg 5$$
$$0x30 = 0011\,0000_2$$
$$\text{Shift right by 5: } 0000\,0001_2 = 0x01 = 1_{dec}$$

**Step 3: Compute effective preemption for IRQ-B = 0x90 (2 marks)**
$$P_B = 0x90 \gg 5$$
$$0x90 = 1001\,0000_2$$
$$\text{Shift right by 5: } 0000\,0100_2 = 0x04 = 4_{dec}$$

**Step 4: Determine Service Order (2 marks)**
- **Lower numerical value = Higher logical priority** in Cortex-M.
- $P_A = 1$, $P_B = 4$
- Since $P_A < P_B$, **IRQ-A is serviced first.**
- Reason: IRQ-A has a higher (more urgent) preemption level because its priority byte, when shifted, yields a smaller value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *numerical* priority with *logical* priority. In ARM Cortex-M, **the LOWER the numerical value, the HIGHER the logical priority**. Many students erroneously assume the opposite and lose 2 marks. Additionally, forgetting to perform the bit-shift using `PRIGROUP` field results in a wrong preemption value — a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the structure of the Vector Table in ARM Cortex-M3. Explain the role of `VTOR` and discuss what happens if a user application re-bases the vector table to RAM for dynamic handler installation.
