**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the worst-case interrupt latency for a Cortex-M3 running at 25 MHz, given that the system is executing a 32-bit aligned instruction at the moment the interrupt arrives. Show all derivations and state the underlying assumption for the worst-case scenario.

---

**Model Solution Outline for Question B (Full Step-by-Step):**

### Part (a) — Vector Table Structure [7 Marks]

The Vector Table is a **256-entry array** of 32-bit words located at a base address determined by the `VTOR` register. The first 16 entries are architecturally defined system exception vectors; entries 16–255 are device-specific IRQ vectors.

**Key Roles (7 marks distribution):**

- **Entry 0 (1 mark):** Holds the **initial Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** value loaded into `SP` on Reset.
- **Entry 1 (1 mark):** Holds the **Reset Handler** address (entry point of `main()`).
- **Entries 2–15 (2 marks):** Hold handlers for NMI, HardFault, MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault, SVCall, DebugMonitor, PendSV, and SysTick.
- **Entries 16+ (1 mark):** Hold device-specific IRQ handlers.
- **VTOR Role (1 mark):** Allows relocation of the vector table to any address aligned to the implementation-defined boundary (typically 128 or 256 words). Used for bootloader handoff and OS kernel re-vectoring.
- **RAM Relocation Note (1 mark):** When re-based to RAM, a bootloader can dynamically patch handler addresses — useful for firmware updates and modular ISR registration. Risk: RAM must be non-volatile (battery-backed) or the vector table must be reinitialized on every boot.

### Part (b) — Worst-Case Latency [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f_{clk} = 25\,\text{MHz}$, instruction = 32-bit aligned.

**Step 1: Compute clock period (1 mark)**
$$T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{25 \times 10^6} = 40\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 2: Identify the cycle budget (3 marks)**
- Instruction completion: **1 cycle** (aligned 32-bit ARM/Thumb-2 instruction)
- Vector fetch: **1 cycle**
- Stacking of 8 registers: **12 cycles** (Cortex-M3 specification)
- Pipeline flush + entry: **1 cycle**
- **Total:** $N = 1 + 1 + 12 + 1 = 15\,\text{cycles}$

**Step 3: Compute latency (2 marks)**
$$L_{max} = 15 \times 40\,\text{ns} = 600\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 4: State the assumption (1 mark)**
The worst-case assumption is that the processor is mid-way through a multi-cycle instruction (e.g., `LDM`/`STM` or `DIV`) when the interrupt arrives. For an aligned 32-bit instruction, the penalty is bounded to 1 extra cycle. For unaligned 32-bit instructions, the penalty could be higher (up to 2 cycles extra on Cortex-M3, which is why bit-banding and aligned access are recommended).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert MHz to ns by computing $1/f$ first — loses 1 mark.
> 2. Missing the alignment assumption for 32-bit instructions — loses 1 mark.
> 3. Confusing Cortex-M3 (12 cycles stacking) with Cortex-M0 (15 cycles) or Cortex-M4 (12 cycles) — loses 1 mark. Always state the core variant explicitly.
> 4. Students often write "latency = 15 ns" instead of "15 cycles" — the unit must be **cycles** during derivation, then converted to **time**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Exceptions and Interrupts (Cortex-M)**

- [ ] **Exception = Super-category** of system events; **Interrupt = Subset** triggered by peripherals.
- [ ] Cortex-M supports **256 vector entries**, each **4 bytes** wide.
- [ ] The **NVIC** is the central hardware block managing all maskable interrupts and system exceptions.
- [ ] On exception entry, **8 registers** are stacked automatically: `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` (32 bytes total).
- [ ] The `EXC_RETURN` magic value is loaded into `LR` and triggers unstacking on `BX LR` / `POP {PC}`.
- [ ] **Lower priority value = Higher logical priority** in ARM (this is the #1 source of student errors).
- [ ] `PRIGROUP` field splits the priority byte into preemption (upper) and subpriority (lower) fields.
- [ ] `VTOR` allows relocating the vector table; default base is `0x00000000` (or `0x08000000` on STM32).
- [ ] **Worst-case interrupt latency** on Cortex-M3/M4 ≈ **12–15 cycles** depending on instruction alignment.
- [ ] **Tail-chaining** saves up to **75%** of cycle overhead between back-to-back ISRs of equal priority.
- [ ] Negative-priority exceptions (Reset, NMI, HardFault) cannot be masked by `PRIMASK`.
- [ ] The `PRIMASK` register blocks all maskable exceptions; `BASEPRI` blocks only exceptions below a threshold; `FAULTMASK` elevates priority to HardFault level.
- [ ] The **MSP** is the default stack pointer for exception handling; the **PSP** is used by Thread Mode in RTOS contexts.
- [ ] For RTOS use, `PendSV` is the standard mechanism for **deferred context switching** — set the pending bit and let the CPU switch contexts at the safest point.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — PCRAT302 Module 1 — Topic: Exceptions and Interrupts**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based education framework, mapped to CO1 (Architectural Understanding) and CO2 (Programming and Interfacing). All formulas, derivations, and diagrams adhere to ARM Cortex-M3/M4 architecture reference specifications.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Control Block (SCB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** System Control Block (SCB) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **System Control Block (SCB)** is a fundamental on-chip control unit in ARM Cortex-M based microcontrollers that provides **system implementation information** and **system control**. It is a programmer's model component of the ARM Cortex-M processor core, mapped to a specific region of the **System Control Space (SCS)** at the base address `0xE000E000`. The SCB is responsible for managing core processor functions such as: - **Reset behavior and control** - **Exception and interrupt handling configuration** - **Vector table relocation** - **System fault management and diagnostics** - **Sleep mode (low-power) configuration** - **Configuration of priority levels and stacking** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SCB is a key part of the ARM Cortex-M architecture. For the PCRAT302 course, students must know the SCB registers, their addresses, functions, and how they are used to control system behavior. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Think of the **System Control Block (SCB)** as the **"Central Command and Control Office"** of a large building (the microcontroller). Imagine a massive office complex (the Cortex-M MCU) where: - **Thousands of workers (peripherals, GPIO, timers)** are doing different jobs - **Alarms (interrupts and exceptions)** can go off at any time - **Power management (electricity)** needs to be controlled based on usage - **Errors (faults)** need to be logged and addressed The **SCB** is the **control room** that sits at the heart of this building. It: 1. **Maintains the master register** (CPUID) identifying who this building belongs to. 2. **Controls the alarm response system** (ICSR) – which alarms are active, what priority they have. 3. **Holds the building's emergency exit map** (VTOR) – pointing to the vector table. 4. **Manages the master key** (AIRCR) – used for resets and unlocking debug features. 5. **Decides when to put the building to sleep** (SCR) – power-saving modes. 6. **Records any structural faults** (CFSR, HFSR) – for diagnostics. Without the SCB, the microcontroller would be like a building without a control room — alarms could not be answered, faults could not be logged, and the system would not know how to behave on power-up. ### 1.3 Location in Memory Map > [!NOTE] > The SCB registers are **memory-mapped** in the System Control Space, which is accessible via the **Private Peripheral Bus (PPB)** of the ARM Cortex-M core. **Base address of SCS:** `0xE000E000` **Base address of SCB:** `0xE000ED00` This means all SCB registers are accessed through normal memory read/write operations in C, typically using structures provided by CMSIS (Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architecture & Key Responsibilities The SCB is part of the **ARM Cortex-M processor's System Control Space (SCS)**. The SCS is divided into several blocks: | Block | Address Range | Purpose | |-------|---------------|---------| | **SysTick** | `0xE000E010` – `0xE000E0FF` | System tick timer | | **NVIC** | `0xE000E100` – `0xE000ECFF` | Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller | | **SCB** | `0xE000ED00` – `0xE000ED3F` | System Control Block | | **MPU** | `0xE000ED90` – `0xE000EDB8` | Memory Protection Unit | The **SCB specifically** handles the following responsibilities: 1. **CPU Identification:** Provides information about the processor core type, revision, and variant. 2. **Interrupt Priority Grouping:** Defines how the priority bits in the NVIC are split between preemption and sub-priority. 3. **Vector Table Management:** Allows relocation of the vector table. 4. **System Reset Control:** Manages software resets and resets via the AIRCR register. 5. **Sleep Mode Control:** Manages entry into sleep/deep-sleep modes. 6. **Fault Status Reporting:** Provides detailed information about faults that have occurred. ### 2.2 Complete SCB Register Map (Formula/Cheat Sheet) The SCB contains the following key registers. **All offsets are from the SCB base address `0xE000ED00`.** | Offset | Register | Full Name | Reset Value | Key Function | |--------|----------|-----------|-------------|--------------| | `0x000` | **CPUID** | CPU Identifier Register | `0x410FC241` | Identifies core type & revision | | `0x004` | **ICSR** | Interrupt Control and State Register | `0x00000000` | Controls NMI, PendSV, SysTick | | `0x008` | **VTOR** | Vector Table Offset Register | `0x00000000` | Relocates vector table | | `0x00C` | **AIRCR** | Application Interrupt and Reset Control | `0xFA050000` | Reset, priority grouping, endian | | `0x010` | **SCR** | System Control Register | `0x00000000` | Sleep/deep-sleep modes | | `0x014` | **CCR** | Configuration and Control Register | `0x00000000` | Fault/trap behavior config | | `0x018` | **SHPR1** | System Handler Priority 1 | `0x00000000` | Priority of MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault | | `0x01C` | **SHPR2** | System Handler Priority 2 | `0x00000000` | Priority of SVCall | | `0x020` | **SHPR3** | System Handler Priority 3 | `0x00000000` | Priority of PendSV, SysTick | | `0x024` | **SHCSR** | System Handler Control and State | `0x00000000` | Enables system handlers & fault status | | `0x028` | **CFSR** | Configurable Fault Status Register | `0x00000000` | MemMgmt, Bus, Usage fault status | | `0x02C` | **HFSR** | HardFault Status Register | `0x00000000` | HardFault details | | `0x030` | **DFSR** | Debug Fault Status Register | `0x00000000` | Debug event faults | | `0x034` | **MMFAR** | MemManage Fault Address Register | `0x00000000` | Faulting address (MemMgmt) | | `0x038` | **BFAR** | BusFault Address Register | `0x00000000` | Faulting address (Bus fault) | | `0x03C` | **AFSR** | Auxiliary Fault Status Register | `0x00000000` | Vendor-defined fault info | ### 2.3 Detailed Bit-Level Analysis of Key Registers #### 2.3.1 CPUID Register (Offset `0x000`) | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[31:24]` | Implementer | ARM = `0x41` | | `[23:20]` | Variant | Major revision (r = 1) | | `[19:16]` | Constant | Reads as `0xF` | | `[15:4]` | PartNo | Cortex-M parts (e.g., `0xC24`) | | `[3:0]` | Revision | Minor revision (p = 1) | > [!NOTE] > **For Cortex-M3:** Reset value is `0x412FC231`. **For Cortex-M4:** Reset value is `0x410FC241`. #### 2.3.2 ICSR Register (Offset `0x004`) - **VECTPENDING [21:12]** – Exception number of the highest pending enabled exception. - **PENDSTSET [26]** – Set SysTick pend bit (write 1 to trigger). - **PENDSVSET [28]** – Set PendSV pend bit (write 1 to trigger). - **NMIPENDSET [31]** – Set NMI pend bit. #### 2.3.3 VTOR Register (Offset `0x008`) | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[31:7]` | TBLOFF | Vector table base address (aligned to 128 bytes) | | `[6:0]` | Reserved | Must be 0 | **Formula:** $$\text{Vector Table Base Address} = \text{TBLOFF} \ll 7$$ #### 2.3.4 AIRCR Register (Offset `0x00C`) This is one of the most important registers. Key fields: | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[31:16]` | **VECTKEY** | Must write `0x05FA` to write to this register | | `[15]` | **ENDIANESS** | Data endianness (1 = big, 0 = little) | | `[10:8]` | **PRIGROUP** | Priority grouping (binary point position) | | `[2]` | **SYSRESETREQ** | Request system reset (read as 0) | | `[1]` | **VECTCLRACTIVE** | Clear all active state | | `[0]` | **VECTRESET** | Reset Cortex-M core (except debug) | **Critical formula for priority grouping:** $$\text{Implemented Priority Bits} = 8 - \text{PRIGROUP}$$ The number of priority levels = $2^{(\text{Implemented Priority Bits})}$. #### 2.3.5 SCR Register (Offset `0x010`) | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[4]` | **SEVONPEND** | Wake CPU from sleep on pending interrupt | | `[3]` | Reserved | — | | `[2]` | **SLEEPDEEP** | 0 = sleep, 1 = deep sleep | | `[1]` | **SLEEPONEXIT** | Sleep on exit from ISR | #### 2.3.6 CCR Register (Offset `0x014`) | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[9]` | **STKALIGN** | Force 8-byte stack alignment on exception entry | | `[8]` | **BFHFNMIGN** | Ignore bus faults in NMI/HardFault handlers | | `[4]` | **DIV_0_TRP** | Trap on divide by zero | | `[3]` | **UNALIGN_TRP** | Trap on unaligned access | | `[1]` | **USERSETMPEND** | Allow unprivileged SW access to PendSV/SysTick | | `[0]` | **NONBASETHRDENA** | Allow any thread to enter thread mode | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The SCB is used in every real-world embedded system for: 1. **Fault Diagnostics:** When a system crashes, engineers read CFSR, HFSR, MMFAR, and BFAR to understand **why** it crashed — critical for automotive ECUs, medical devices. 2. **RTOS Implementation:** PendSV is used for context switching. SysTick provides the OS tick. Both are managed via the SCB's ICSR register. 3. **Low-Power IoT Devices:** The SCR register controls deep-sleep entry — essential for battery-powered devices that may sleep for years. 4. **Bootloader Implementation:** The VTOR register is used to relocate the vector table to bootloader or application regions in memory. 5. **System Recovery:** The AIRCR's SYSRESETREQ allows software to force a clean reset of the MCU. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet Summary Table | Concept | Formula / Rule | |---------|----------------| | SCS base address | `0xE000E000` | | SCB base address | `0xE000ED00` | | Vector table alignment | Must be aligned to 128 bytes (lower 7 bits = 0) | | AIRCR write key | Must write `0x05FA` in upper 16 bits to allow write | | Implemented priority bits | $8 - \text{PRIGROUP}$ | | Number of priority levels | $2^{8-\text{PRIGROUP}}$ | | Reset value of CPUID (M3) | `0x412FC231` | | Reset value of CPUID (M4) | `0x410FC241` | | SLEEPDEEP bit | `0x00` = sleep, `0x01` = deep sleep | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Computing the Vector Table Base Address from VTOR **Problem Statement:** A Cortex-M4 microcontroller has `SCB->VTOR = 0x08010000`. Determine the actual base address of the vector table in memory. **Step-by-Step Solution:** The VTOR register holds the vector table offset in bits [31:7]. Bits [6:0] are reserved (always 0). **Step 1:** Write the VTOR register value. $$\text{VTOR} = 0x08010000$$ **Step 2:** Recognize the encoding of the VTOR register. The vector table base address is stored such that bits [6:0] are always zero. This means the value stored is already the base address, **but** the alignment requirement is enforced — the lower 7 bits of the base must be zero (i.e., it must be 128-byte aligned). **Step 3:** Verify alignment. $$0x08010000 \ \& \ 0x7F = 0x00$$ The lower 7 bits are zero, so the alignment is correct. **Step 4:** Conclusion. $$\boxed{\text{Vector Table Base Address} = 0x08010000}$$ The vector table starts at memory address `0x08010000`. The first entry (initial stack pointer value) is at `0x08010000`, the second entry (Reset handler address) is at `0x08010004`, and so on. ### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Computing Priority Grouping and Number of Levels **Problem Statement:** The application interrupt and reset control register (AIRCR) has `PRIGROUP = 0x05`. Compute: 1. The number of implemented priority bits. 2. The number of priority levels. 3. The number of bits used for sub-priority (within a preemption group). **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Recall the formula relating implemented priority bits to PRIGROUP. $$\text{Implemented Priority Bits} = 8 - \text{PRIGROUP}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute PRIGROUP = 5. $$\text{Implemented Priority Bits} = 8 - 5 = 3$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the number of priority levels. $$\text{Number of Priority Levels} = 2^{3} = 8$$ So there are **8 distinct priority levels**. **Step 4:** Determine sub-priority bits. In ARM Cortex-M, the priority register contains 8 bits. The implemented bits are in the **upper** portion of these 8 bits. The grouping works as follows: - **Group priority (preemption) bits** = `PRIGROUP` = `5` - **Sub-priority bits** = `Implemented bits − Group priority bits` = `3 − 5` Since `3 < 5`, this configuration has **0 sub-priority bits** and **3 preemption bits**. This means there are 8 preemption levels and **no sub-priority** differentiation. Interrupt nesting is possible across all 8 levels. **Step 5:** Final summary. $$\boxed{\text{Priority Levels} = 8, \quad \text{Sub-priority Levels} = 1 \text{ (none)}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Verifying the AIRCR Write Key **Problem Statement:** To reset the system via AIRCR, a software engineer writes `0x05FA0004` to the AIRCR register. Explain why this value works. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Decompose the 32-bit write value. $$0x05FA0004 = \underbrace{0x05FA}_{\text{VECTKEY (upper 16 bits)}} \quad \underbrace{0x0004}_{\text{lower 16 bits}}$$ **Step 2:** Decode the VECTKEY field. The VECTKEY field must equal `0x05FA` for any write to the AIRCR register to succeed. This acts as a "password" to prevent accidental writes that could reset the system. $$\text{VECTKEY} = 0x05FA \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 3:** Decode the lower 16 bits. In `0x0004`: - Bit 2 (`SYSRESETREQ`) = 1 - All other bits = 0 Setting `SYSRESETREQ = 1` requests a **system reset** of the microcontroller. **Step 4:** Conclusion. The write `0x05FA0004` is valid because: 1. The VECTKEY password is correct. 2. Only the SYSRESETREQ bit is set, requesting a controlled reset. If the developer had written `0x12340004` instead, the write would have been **ignored** by hardware because the key would be incorrect. ### 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: Diagnosing a Hard Fault from CFSR and HFSR **Problem Statement:** After a system crash, the registers are observed as follows: - `CFSR = 0x00008000` - `HFSR = 0x40000000` - `BFAR = 0x20001234` Determine the type of fault and the faulting address. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Decode the CFSR register. The CFSR register has three 8-bit sections: - Bits [7:0]: MemManage fault status - Bits [15:8]: BusFault status - Bits [31:16]: UsageFault status Given `CFSR = 0x00008000`, only bit 15 is set, which is in the BusFault status region. **Step 2:** Identify the specific BusFault flag. In the BusFault status byte (`0x80`): - Bit 7 (`BFARVALID`) = 1 → BFAR contains a valid faulting address. Other BusFault flags (bits 0–6) are all 0, meaning the fault is not from bus error during stacking/unstacking, imprecise bus error, or other specific causes. The fault is a generic **bus fault** with a precise address. **Step 3:** Decode the HFSR register. `HFSR = 0x40000000`. Bit 30 is `FORCED`, indicating that a configurable fault was escalated to a HardFault because it could not be handled. **Step 4:** Identify the faulting address from BFAR. $$\text{BFAR} = 0x20001234$$ This is the address in SRAM where the bus access failed. **Step 5:** Conclusion. A **precise bus fault** occurred at address `0x20001234`, which was escalated to a HardFault. Common causes: - Accessing a peripheral with the clock disabled. - Accessing an invalid memory region. - DMA conflicts. ### 3.5 Analytical Problem 5: SCR and Low-Power Mode Configuration **Problem Statement:** Configure the SCB to put the MCU into deep-sleep mode upon exit from any ISR, and also wake on pending interrupts. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the relevant SCR register bits. - `SEVONPEND` (bit 4): Wake on pending interrupt. - `SLEEPDEEP` (bit 2): Use deep-sleep. - `SLEEPONEXIT` (bit 1): Sleep when exiting any ISR. **Step 2:** Compute the required SCR value. $$\text{SCR} = (1 \ll 4) \ \vert\ (1 \ll 2) \ \vert\ (1 \ll 1)$$ $$\text{SCR} = 0x10 \ \vert\ 0x04 \ \vert\ 0x02$$ $$\text{SCR} = 0x16$$ **Step 3:** Apply this in code. $$\boxed{\text{SCB->SCR} = 0x16;}$$ **Step 4:** Verify. - After every ISR returns, the CPU enters deep sleep (`SLEEPONEXIT = 1`). - If an interrupt is pending (even if not active), the CPU will wake (`SEVONPEND = 1`). - The sleep will be deep-sleep, not regular sleep (`SLEEPDEEP = 1`). This configuration is typical for ultra-low-power IoT sensor nodes. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: System Control Space (SCS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[System Control Space
Base 0xE000E000] --> B[SysTick Timer
0xE000E010] A --> C[NVIC
0xE000E100 - 0xE000ECFF] A --> D[SCB
0xE000ED00 - 0xE000ED3F] A --> E[MPU
0xE000ED90] D --> D1[CPUID
0xE000ED00] D --> D2[ICSR
0xE000ED04] D --> D3[VTOR
0xE000ED08] D --> D4[AIRCR
0xE000ED0C] D --> D5[SCR
0xE000ED10] D --> D6[CCR
0xE000ED14] D --> D7[SHPR1 SHPR2 SHPR3
0xE000ED18-0xE000ED20] D --> D8[SHCSR
0xE000ED24] D --> D9[CFSR
0xE000ED28] D --> D10[HFSR
0xE000ED2C] D --> D11[DFSR
0xE000ED30] D --> D12[MMFAR
0xE000ED34] D --> D13[BFAR
0xE000ED38] D --> D14[AFSR
0xE000ED3C] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff style C fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff style D fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px style E fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#bf360c,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: SCB Functional Responsibilities ```mermaid flowchart LR SCB[System Control Block] SCB --> R1[CPU Identification
via CPUID register] SCB --> R2[Exception Priority
Grouping via AIRCR] SCB --> R3[Vector Table
Relocation via VTOR] SCB --> R4[System Reset
via AIRCR.SYSRESETREQ] SCB --> R5[Low Power Modes
via SCR] SCB --> R6[Fault Diagnostics
via CFSR HFSR BFAR] SCB --> R7[Stack Alignment
via CCR.STKALIGN] SCB --> R8[System Handler
Control via SHCSR] R2 --> App1[RTOS Applications] R3 --> App2[Bootloaders] R5 --> App3[IoT Devices] R6 --> App4[Automotive Safety] R8 --> App5[Real-time Systems] style SCB fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px style R1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R4 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R5 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R6 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R7 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style R8 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style App1 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style App2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style App3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style App4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style App5 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.3 Data Flow Diagram: Fault Diagnostic Process via SCB ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Program Execution] --> F1{Fault Occurs?} F1 -->|No| Start F1 -->|Yes| F2{Fault Handler
Enabled?} F2 -->|UsageFault Handler| UFSR[Read UFSR Bits 16-31
of CFSR] F2 -->|BusFault Handler| BFSR[Read BFSR Bits 8-15
of CFSR] F2 -->|MemManage Handler| MMFSR[Read MMFSR Bits 0-7
of CFSR] F2 -->|No handler| HF[HardFault Triggered] UFSR --> RR[Read fault registers] BFSR --> RR MMFSR --> RR HF --> HFSR[Read HFSR] RR --> AA{BFARVALID
bit set?} AA -->|Yes| RBFAR[Read BFAR for address] AA -->|No| CONT[Continue diagnostics] RBFAR --> LOG[Log fault details] HFSR --> LOG CONT --> LOG LOG --> CLR[Write 1 to clear
fault bits] CLR --> Restart[Optional: System Reset
via AIRCR.SYSRESETREQ] style Start fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17 style F2 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17 style HF fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff style Restart fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#002171,color:#ffffff style LOG fill:#4a148c,stroke:#1a0033,color:#ffffff style CLR fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#bf360c,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define the System Control Block (SCB) and state its base address. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model] **Model Answer:** The **System Control Block (SCB)** is a part of the ARM Cortex-M processor's System Control Space that provides **system implementation information** and **system control**. It handles CPU identification, interrupt priority grouping, vector table relocation, system reset, fault management, and low-power configuration. **Base address of SCB:** `0xE000ED00` **(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 for base address, 1 for any one function)** --- #### Question 2: What is the function of the AIRCR register's VECTKEY field? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model] **Model Answer:** The **VECTKEY field** (bits [31:16] of AIRCR) is a write-protection password that must equal `0x05FA` for any write to the AIRCR register to take effect. This prevents accidental writes to critical control bits such as `SYSRESETREQ` and `PRIGROUP`, which could otherwise cause unintended system resets or interrupt priority changes. **(3 marks – 2 for explanation, 1 for the key value `0x05FA`)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in PCRAT302 typically contain two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. Below we present two alternative question choices. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A(a)** Explain the structure and functions of the System Control Block (SCB) in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. List its key registers with their offsets from the SCB base address. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **System Control Block (SCB)** is a key component of the ARM Cortex-M processor's **System Control Space (SCS)**. It is memory-mapped starting at the address `0xE000ED00` and provides essential system-level control functions. **Major Functions of SCB:** 1. **CPU Identification:** Identifies the processor core type, revision, and variant via the CPUID register. 2. **Interrupt Priority Grouping:** Controls the binary point location that splits priority bits into preemption and sub-priority fields (via AIRCR.PRIGROUP). 3. **Vector Table Management:** Allows the vector table to be relocated to any 128-byte aligned location in memory (via VTOR). 4. **System Reset:** Generates software-controlled system resets (via AIRCR.SYSRESETREQ). 5. **Sleep Mode Control:** Configures sleep and deep-sleep modes (via SCR). 6. **Fault Diagnosis:** Provides detailed status registers for various fault types (CFSR, HFSR, BFAR, MMFAR). **Key SCB Registers and Offsets:** | Offset | Register | Purpose | |--------|----------|---------| | `0x000` | CPUID | CPU identification | | `0x004` | ICSR | Interrupt control state | | `0x008` | VTOR | Vector table offset | | `0x00C` | AIRCR | Application interrupt and reset | | `0x010` | SCR | System control (sleep modes) | | `0x014` | CCR | Configuration and control | | `0x018`–`0x020` | SHPR1, SHPR2, SHPR3 | System handler priorities | | `0x024` | SHCSR | System handler control and state | | `0x028` | CFSR | Configurable fault status | | `0x02C` | HFSR | Hard fault status | | `0x030` | DFSR | Debug fault status | | `0x034` | MMFAR | MemManage fault address | | `0x038` | BFAR | Bus fault address | | `0x03C` | AFSR | Auxiliary fault status | **Conclusion:** The SCB is essential for managing system-level behavior in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, providing controlled mechanisms for reset, interrupt handling, fault diagnosis, and low-power operation. **(7 marks — 3 for explanation, 2 for register list, 2 for concluding importance)** --- **Q.A(b)** Explain the working of the AIRCR register. How is it used to perform a software system reset? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register (AIRCR)** at offset `0x00C` is one of the most important SCB registers. It controls system reset, interrupt priority grouping, and endianness. **Register Layout:** | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[31:16]` | VECTKEY | Write key — must be `0x05FA` | | `[15]` | ENDIANESS | Data endianness (1 = big, 0 = little) | | `[10:8]` | PRIGROUP | Priority grouping bits | | `[2]` | SYSRESETREQ | System reset request | | `[1]` | VECTCLRACTIVE | Clear active vector state | | `[0]` | VECTRESET | Reset Cortex-M core | **Write Protection via VECTKEY:** The upper 16 bits form a **write-protection key**. Any write to AIRCR must have `VECTKEY = 0x05FA`; otherwise, the write is **ignored by hardware**. This prevents accidental resets or priority reconfigurations. **Performing a Software System Reset:** To trigger a system reset, the SYSRESETREQ bit (bit 2) is set to 1. The C code sequence is: ``` NVIC_SystemReset(); // CMSIS standard function ``` This function essentially executes: 1. Set `SCB->AIRCR = (0x05FA << 16) | (SCB->AIRCR & 0x0700) | 0x04;` 2. The mask `0x0700` preserves the current PRIGROUP. 3. The `0x04` sets SYSRESETREQ. 4. A Data Synchronization Barrier (`__DSB()`) ensures the write completes. 5. The CPU enters reset on the next clock cycle. **Priority Grouping:** The PRIGROUP bits divide the 8 priority bits into preemption (group) and sub-priority fields. The formula is: $$\text{Implemented Priority Bits} = 8 - \text{PRIGROUP}$$ For example, `PRIGROUP = 5` gives 3 preemption bits and 0 sub-priority bits, yielding 8 priority levels. **Conclusion:** The AIRCR register is a write-protected multi-function register that combines reset control, priority grouping, and endian configuration in a single interface. **(7 marks — 2 for layout, 3 for reset mechanism including code, 2 for priority grouping)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **Q.B(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the System Control Space of the ARM Cortex-M processor and the location of SCB within it. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **System Control Space (SCS)** is a 4 KB memory region in the ARM Cortex-M processor's private peripheral bus, starting at the address `0xE000E000`. The SCS contains several sub-modules that handle system-level operations. **Block Diagram of SCS:** ``` 0xE000E000 +-------------------+ | SysTick | (0xE000E010) | Timer | 0xE000E0FF +-------------------+ | NVIC | (0xE000E100 - 0xE000ECFF) | (Interrupts) | 0xE000ECFF +-------------------+ | SCB | (0xE000ED00 - 0xE000ED3F) | (System Ctrl) | 0xE000ED8F +-------------------+ | MPU | (0xE000ED90 - 0xE000EDB8) | (Memory Prot.) | 0xE000EDB8 +-------------------+ ``` **Description of Each Block:** 1. **SysTick Timer (0xE000E010):** A 24-bit down-counter that provides a periodic interrupt for OS tick or simple timing functions. It has four registers: CTRL, LOAD, VAL, and CALIB. 2. **NVIC (0xE000E100 – 0xE000ECFF):** The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller manages external and internal interrupts. It supports up to 240 interrupt sources with programmable priorities. 3. **SCB (0xE000ED00 – 0xE000ED3F):** The System Control Block provides system implementation information and control. It includes CPUID, ICSR, VTOR, AIRCR, SCR, CCR, SHPR1–3, SHCSR, CFSR, HFSR, DFSR, MMFAR, BFAR, and AFSR. 4. **MPU (0xE000ED90 – 0xE000EDB8):** The Memory Protection Unit (optional, present in some Cortex-M3/M4 implementations) provides memory region protection. **Significance:** The SCB is the most configuration-rich part of the SCS, providing fault handling, reset, sleep, and priority grouping functionality. **(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for block descriptions, 2 for SCB emphasis)** --- **Q.B(b)** Explain the CFSR and HFSR registers. How are they used in fault diagnosis in embedded systems? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Configurable Fault Status Register (CFSR)** at offset `0x028` and **HardFault Status Register (HFSR)** at offset `0x02C` are the primary fault diagnostic registers of the SCB. **CFSR Register Layout:** The CFSR is divided into three 8-bit sub-registers: 1. **MMFSR (bits 0–7) — Memory Management Fault Status:** - Bit 0: IACCVIOL (Instruction access violation) - Bit 1: DACCVIOL (Data access violation) - Bit 4: MUNSTKERR (Stacking error) - Bit 5: MSTKERR (Unstacking error) 2. **BFSR (bits 8–15) — Bus Fault Status:** - Bit 8: IBUSERR (Instruction bus error) - Bit 9: PRECISERR (Precise data bus error) - Bit 10: IMPRECISERR (Imprecise data bus error) - Bit 11: UNSTKERR (Bus unstacking fault) - Bit 12: STKERR (Bus stacking fault) - Bit 15: BFARVALID (BFAR register holds valid address) 3. **UFSR (bits 16–31) — Usage Fault Status:** - Bit 16: UNDEFINSTR (Undefined instruction) - Bit 17: INVSTATE (Invalid EPSR.T or IT state) - Bit 18: INVPC (Invalid PC load) - Bit 19: NOCP (No coprocessor) - Bit 24: UNALIGNED (Unaligned access) - Bit 25: DIVBYZERO (Divide by zero) **HFSR Register Layout:** | Bits | Field | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `[31]` | DEBUGEVT | Debug event caused the fault | | `[30]` | FORCED | Configurable fault escalated to HardFault | | `[1]` | VECTTBL | Bus fault on vector table read | **Fault Diagnosis Process:** In a typical fault handler: 1. **Read CFSR** to determine which type of fault occurred (memory, bus, or usage). 2. **Read HFSR** to determine if it escalated to HardFault. 3. **Read BFAR/MMFAR** (if BFARVALID/MMARVALID bits are set) to find the exact faulting address. 4. **Log the fault** for debugging. 5. **Clear the fault bits** by writing 1 to them, allowing the system to continue. **Real-World Use Case:** In automotive ECUs, fault diagnostic registers are read during system bring-up to ensure that all memory regions and peripheral accesses are valid. In safety-critical systems, faults are logged to non-volatile memory for post-incident analysis. **Conclusion:** The CFSR and HFSR registers provide a powerful fault diagnosis mechanism that helps embedded engineers pinpoint the exact cause of system crashes, significantly reducing debugging time in production systems. **(7 marks — 2 for CFSR structure, 2 for HFSR structure, 3 for fault diagnosis process and real-world use)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the AIRCR VECTKEY:** Many students try to set `SYSRESETREQ` without writing `0x05FA` in the VECTKEY field, causing the write to silently fail. This is the **#1 mark-loser** in AIRCR-related questions. > 2. **Wrong VTOR alignment:** Forgetting that VTOR must be aligned to 128 bytes (lower 7 bits = 0) costs full marks. > 3. **Confusing CFSR sub-registers:** Students often mix up MMFSR, BFSR, and UFSR bit positions. Memorize the offsets: MMFSR = bits 0–7, BFSR = 8–15, UFSR = 16–31. > 4. **Misidentifying CPUID reset values:** Cortex-M3 = `0x412FC231`, Cortex-M4 = `0x410FC241`. Writing the wrong value loses a mark. > 5. **Forgetting fault clear:** Fault status bits must be cleared by writing 1 to them; otherwise, the fault appears persistent. > 6. **Confusing VECTRESET vs SYSRESETREQ:** VECTRESET resets only the Cortex-M core; SYSRESETREQ resets the entire system (including peripherals). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **Quick Revision Checklist — System Control Block (SCB):** - ⭐ **Base Address:** SCB is located at `0xE000ED00` within the System Control Space. - ⭐ **System Control Space (SCS):** Starts at `0xE000E000`, contains SysTick, NVIC, SCB, and MPU. - ⭐ **CPUID Register:** Reset value `0x412FC231` (Cortex-M3) or `0x410FC241` (Cortex-M4). Read-only register identifying the core. - ⭐ **VTOR Register:** Allows vector table relocation. **Must be 128-byte aligned** (lower 7 bits = 0). - ⭐ **AIRCR Register:** Has the VECTKEY write-protection password `0x05FA`. Controls `SYSRESETREQ`, `VECTRESET`, `PRIGROUP`, and `ENDIANESS`. - ⭐ **Priority Grouping Formula:** Implemented priority bits = `8 − PRIGROUP`. Number of levels = `2^(8−PRIGROUP)`. - ⭐ **SCR Register:** Controls sleep modes. Bits: `SEVONPEND` (bit 4), `SLEEPDEEP` (bit 2), `SLEEPONEXIT` (bit 1). - ⭐ **CCR Register:** Configures fault trapping — `STKALIGN`, `DIV_0_TRP`, `UNALIGN_TRP`. - ⭐ **CFSR Register:** Three sub-registers — MMFSR (bits 0–7), BFSR (bits 8–15), UFSR (bits 16–31). Used for fault diagnosis. - ⭐ **HFSR Register:** `FORCED` bit (bit 30) indicates escalation from configurable fault. `VECTTBL` bit (bit 1) for vector table read errors. - ⭐ **BFAR and MMFAR:** Provide the exact faulting address when `BFARVALID` (bit 15 of CFSR) or `MMARVALID` (bit 0 of MMFSR) is set. - ⭐ **Fault Clearing:** Fault bits are cleared by writing **1** to them (write-one-to-clear convention). - ⭐ **NVIC and SCB Together:** The SCB manages system-level faults and resets, while the NVIC handles peripheral interrupts. - ⭐ **Real-world Applications:** Bootloaders (VTOR), RTOS (PendSV, SysTick), IoT power management (SCR), automotive diagnostics (CFSR/HFSR). ---

topics.title: Debug
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Debug

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems**, **Debug** refers to the systematic process of identifying, isolating, and correcting errors (bugs) — both hardware and software — in a real-time embedded system during the development, integration, and operational phases. The term is also used to describe the hardware/software **interface and protocol** that allows an external host (PC/Workstation) to gain controlled, non-intrusive access to a target microcontroller for runtime inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Debugging is the methodical procedure of tracing, testing, and correcting the logical, electrical, or timing faults in firmware executed on a microcontroller, using specialized hardware (probes, emulators) and software (simulators, monitors) tools, while preserving the real-time deterministic behavior of the embedded system.*

### Key Terminology Mapping (KTU-Aligned)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bug** | An error in software (logic/syntax) or hardware (timing/wiring) | Source of all debug effort |
| **Firmware** | Software embedded in ROM/Flash of the MCU | The entity being debugged |
| **Host** | External PC running the debugger IDE | Master in the debug link |
| **Target** | The microcontroller under test | Slave in the debug link |
| **Probe** | Hardware interface between Host and Target | Physical link (e.g., JTAG probe) |
| **Breakpoint** | A halt instruction placed in firmware | Pauses CPU for inspection |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **mechanic diagnosing a car** that won't start:

- 🚗 The **car** is the *Target Microcontroller*.
- 🧑‍🔧 **You (the mechanic)** are the *Debugger / Host PC*.
- 🔧 The **diagnostic OBD-II scanner cable** plugged into the car is the *Debug Probe* (e.g., JTAG/BDM).
- 🖥️ The **screen of the scanner** showing RPM, error codes, live fuel data is the *Debugger Software (IDE)*.

Just as a mechanic can:
1. **Listen** to the engine (logic analyzer on address/data bus),
2. **Pause the crankshaft** at a specific point (breakpoint halt),
3. **Read sensor values live** (watching variables/registers),
4. **Test individual spark plugs** (single-stepping through code),

…an embedded engineer uses **debug tools** to similarly inspect, halt, step through, and correct the firmware running on the MCU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Debugging in embedded systems is fundamentally different from desktop software debugging. In desktop apps, you can freely halt the program. In embedded systems, halting the CPU may freeze a motor, lose serial data, or cause a watchdog timer reset — therefore debug must be **non-intrusive** or **time-aware**.

---

## 1.3 Why is Debugging Critical in Embedded Systems?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> 1. **Real-time constraints** — A `for` loop taking 1 ms extra may violate a 100 µs deadline in a control loop.
> 2. **Hardware-software co-dependence** — Bugs may arise from incorrect register configurations, not just C code.
> 3. **No console output** — Unlike PCs, embedded MCUs rarely have a monitor; you must *probe* signals.
> 4. **Cost of field failure** — A bug in a deployed pacemaker or anti-lock braking system (ABS) can be fatal.
> 5. **Silicon-level issues** — Race conditions, bus contention, EMI — all require specialized debug.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Importance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **JTAG Clock (TCK frequency)** | **1 MHz – 50 MHz** | Determines debug speed |
| **JTAG Voltage (VTREF)** | **1.8 V – 5.0 V** | Must match target MCU I/O |
| **BDM Clock** | Typically MCU bus clock ÷ N | Freescale/NXP ColdFire/HCS08 |
| **Breakpoint count (HW)** | **2 – 8** (typical ARM Cortex-M) | Limits simultaneous watchpoints |
| **Trace buffer size** | **4 KB – 64 KB** (ETM/ETB) | Stores execution history |
| **SWD Clock (SWCLK)** | Up to **50 MHz** | ARM Serial Wire Debug speed |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Debug Approaches (Overview Map)

Embedded debugging is broadly classified into **three generations**, each offering increasing sophistication:

1. **Generation 1 — Pre-Silicon (Software-Only):**
   - Software Simulators, Instruction Set Simulators (ISS).
   - No real hardware needed; PC emulates the CPU.

2. **Generation 2 — In-Circuit (Hardware-Assisted):**
   - ROM Monitors, In-Circuit Emulators (ICE).
   - Real MCU, additional hardware socket/overlay.

3. **Generation 3 — On-Chip (Silicon-Integrated):**
   - **BDM**, **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)**, **SWD (ARM)**, **OCD (On-Chip Debug)**.
   - Debug logic is **built into the silicon** of the MCU itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus of *Module 1 – Debug* primarily covers the **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** paradigm — especially **JTAG, BDM, and boundary-scan** — because these are the modern industry-standard methods.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Debug Process Flow (Conceptual Walkthrough)

The end-to-end embedded debug cycle follows **six logical stages**:

### **Step 1 — Problem Statement & Hypothesis Formation**
- Engineer observes a *symptom* (e.g., LED does not blink, UART prints garbage, motor jitters).
- Forms *hypotheses* ranked by probability (power supply → clock → peripheral init → logic bug).

### **Step 2 — Tool Selection**
- Choose between **simulation**, **emulation**, or **on-chip debug** based on:
  - Stage of development (pre-silicon → post-silicon).
  - Real-time needs (does the system need to run at full speed?).
  - Cost and availability of hardware.

### **Step 3 — Probe Attachment & Link Establishment**
- Connect the **debug probe** (e.g., J-Link, P&E Cyclone, Lauterbach TRACE32) to the target.
- The probe negotiates a **handshake** via JTAG/SWD/BDM protocol.
- Host IDE downloads the debug firmware (monitor) or uses existing on-chip logic.

### **Step 4 — Code Download & Verification**
- The compiled `.elf` / `.hex` file is flashed into target's **Flash/ROM** via the probe.
- Verification: a checksum (e.g., CRC-32) is computed and matched.

### **Step 5 — Runtime Control**
- **Run**, **Halt**, **Single-Step**, **Set Breakpoint**, **Watch Variable**, **Read Registers/Memory**.
- Real-time, non-intrusive trace (where supported by ETM/ITM).

### **Step 6 — Diagnosis & Fix**
- Inspect state at the breakpoint, dump call stack, view disassembly.
- Patch code, recompile, reflash, retest.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "PHPRDS":** Problem, Hypothesis, Probe, Run, Diagnose, Solve.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Debug Methods

### **2.2.1 Software Simulation (ISS — Instruction Set Simulator)**

- **Mechanism:** A PC-based program emulates the *entire* MCU — CPU, memory map, peripherals — at the instruction level.
- **Examples:** Keil µVision Simulator, MPLAB X SIM, Renesas e² Studio Simulator, QEMU (ARM).
- **Pros:** Zero hardware; perfect for early code bring-up; deterministic; reproducible.
- **Cons:** Does not model real-time I/O, analog noise, or actual silicon timing.

### **2.2.2 ROM Monitor (Firmware Debug Monitor)**

- **Mechanism:** A small **debugger-resident firmware** is placed in a protected region of Flash/RAM. It communicates with the host over **UART/SPI/USB/CAN**.
- **Functions:** Implements breakpoint, single-step, memory read/write via serial commands.
- **Pros:** Cheap; needs only a UART.
- **Cons:** Uses Flash/RAM; intrusive; cannot debug interrupt handlers easily; halts CPU.

### **2.2.3 In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)**

- **Mechanism:** A **special probe-card** with a matching pin-out replaces the target MCU. The probe has its own bond-out silicon that exposes internal buses.
- **Examples:** HP/Agilent 64700 series (legacy).
- **Pros:** Full real-time visibility of all internal signals; no firmware intrusion.
- **Cons:** Expensive; probe must match each MCU package; largely obsolete.

### **2.2.4 On-Chip Debug (OCD) — The Modern Standard**

- **Mechanism:** Debug logic is **fabricated into the MCU silicon** by the manufacturer. Accessed via standardized serial protocols.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** — 4-wire (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) plus optional TRST.
  - **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** — 2-wire ARM alternative.
  - **BDM (Background Debug Mode)** — Freescale/NXP single-wire or 4-wire.
  - **cJTAG / SWD-daisychain** — compact JTAG2 for low-pin MCUs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — On-Chip Debug (OCD):**
> The OCD module is a small dedicated logic block within the MCU that intercepts the CPU core signals, allowing the external host to halt, step, read/write registers and memory — **without** using any user code space (in advanced MCUs).

---

## 2.3 The BDM (Background Debug Mode) — Detailed Mechanics

**BDM** is a proprietary Motorola/Freescale/NXP debug interface, dominant in **HCS08, ColdFire, HC(S)12, Kinetis** families.

### **Pin Description**

| Pin | Direction | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BKGD** | Bidirectional | Single-wire background debug data |
| **RESET** | Input (to target) | Active-low reset (often shared) |
| **VDD, VSS** | Power | Reference for logic levels |
| (Optional) **DSCLK, TCLK** | Output | Development serial clock, trace clock |

### **BDM Command Set (Conceptual)**

The host sends 17-bit or 33-bit command frames on the BKGD line, including:
- `READ_BYTE`, `WRITE_BYTE`
- `READ_WORD`, `WRITE_WORD`
- `GO` — release CPU to run from current PC
- `TRACE1` — single-step one instruction
- `BACKGROUND` — force CPU into background (halted) mode

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-wire elegance:** BDM is the simplest of all on-chip debug protocols, requiring only one bidirectional pin (BKGD), making it ideal for low-pin-count MCUs.

### **BDM Operational States**

1. **Reset state** — CPU held in reset, BDM logic active.
2. **Normal single-chip mode** — User firmware runs.
3. **Background mode (BDM-active)** — CPU halted, BDM host controls execution.
4. **Wait/Stop modes** — Power-saving, BDM can wake the CPU.

---

## 2.4 The JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) Interface — Detailed Mechanics

**JTAG** stands for **Joint Test Action Group**, which defined the **IEEE 1149.1 Standard Test Access Port and Boundary-Scan Architecture** in 1990. It was originally for **board-level interconnect testing** but was brilliantly repurposed for **MCU debug**.

### **The Four (Mandatory) + One (Optional) JTAG Pins**

| Pin | Full Name | Direction | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input to target | Clock for TAP state machine |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input to target | State machine navigation |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input to target | Serial data INTO target |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output from target | Serial data OUT of target |
| **TRST*** | Test Reset | Input | Optional, asynchronous TAP reset |

### **The TAP (Test Access Port) Controller**

A **16-state finite state machine (FSM)** that sequences the JTAG logic.

The key states are:
- `Test-Logic-Reset`
- `Run-Test/Idle`
- `Select-DR-Scan` / `Select-IR-Scan`
- `Capture-DR` / `Capture-IR`
- `Shift-DR` / `Shift-IR`
- `Update-DR` / `Update-IR`
- `Pause-DR` / `Pause-IR`

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "TMS steers, TCK clocks, TDI feeds data, TDO emits data, TRST resets."

### **JTAG Registers (Conceptual List)**

1. **Bypass Register (BR)** — 1-bit, lets the JTAG chain skip a device.
2. **Boundary-Scan Register (BSR)** — Surrounds every I/O pin for **boundary scan** (the original JTAG purpose).
3. **Instruction Register (IR)** — Holds the current command (e.g., `BYPASS`, `EXTEST`, `SAMPLE/PRELOAD`, `IDCODE`).
4. **Device Identification Register (IDCODE)** — 32-bit silicon ID.

### **JTAG for Debug (Beyond Boundary Scan)**

When a manufacturer adds debug logic, the JTAG IR is extended with vendor-specific instructions such as:
- `INTEST` — internal logic test
- `HALT` — stop CPU
- `SINGLE_STEP` — execute one instruction
- `MEM_READ`, `MEM_WRITE` — access memory
- `DBGACK` — debug acknowledge

### **Boundary-Scan Cell (Per Pin)**

Every JTAG-enabled MCU pin has a dedicated **boundary-scan cell** between the core logic and the physical pad. This cell can:
- **Capture** the pin's logic level
- **Override** the pin to drive a test pattern
- **SAMPLE** data flowing through the pin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Boundary-Scan matters for embedded engineers:**
> When you cannot physically probe a BGA pin (e.g., an ARM Cortex-M in a 0.4 mm pitch BGA), the JTAG boundary-scan chain lets you **virtually probe** that pin by reading its scan cell — invaluable for board bring-up.

---

## 2.5 The SWD (Serial Wire Debug) — ARM's 2-Wire Alternative

**SWD** is a 2-pin protocol introduced by ARM, used widely on **Cortex-M** microcontrollers.

| Pin | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **SWDIO** | Bidirectional data |
| **SWCLK** | Host-driven clock |
| (Optional) **SWO** | Single-wire trace output for `ITM`/`printf` redirection |

**Advantages over JTAG:**
- 2 pins vs. 4–5 (saves MCU pin count).
- Can coexist with JTAG on the same pins via a discovery mechanism (the host drives TMS/SWDIO high for a handshake).
- Supports **overrun detection** — the target can request a stall, useful for slow memory reads.

---

## 2.6 The ICE (In-Circuit Emulator) — Bond-Out Era

Although largely obsolete, ICE is in the KTU syllabus. It uses a **bond-out MCU** — a specially packaged version of the silicon with extra pins exposing internal buses. These pins feed into an external **emulation memory** that overlays the internal ROM/RAM.

**Why it became obsolete:** Modern MCUs integrate the debug logic on-chip (OCD), making external bond-out unnecessary. OCD is cheaper, supports multi-voltage targets, and matches the actual silicon timing.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **JTAG chain length** | $L_{chain} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$ | $L_i$ = bits in device *i*'s IR or DR | Computes total scan time |
| **Total scan time** | $T_{scan} = \dfrac{L_{chain}}{f_{TCK}}$ | $f_{TCK}$ = TCK frequency | Slower chain ⇒ longer download |
| **Breakpoint address (HW)** | $A_{bp} = A_{instr} \cdot n_{bp}$ | Limited by HW unit count | KTU — typically 2/4/6/8 HW units |
| **Trace bandwidth** | $BW_{trace} = f_{CPU} \times n_{bits/sample}$ | $n_{bits}$ per trace packet | ETM/ITM throughput planning |
| **BDM frame length** | $L_{BDM} = 17 \text{ or } 33 \text{ bits}$ | Encodes op + address | HCS08/ColdFire command framing |
| **Watchdog margin** | $T_{WDT} = k \cdot T_{worst-case-task}$ | $k$ typically 1.5 to 2.0 | Ensures WDT is not the cause of false "bug" |
| **Polling vs. interrupt CPU overhead** | $OH_{poll} = \dfrac{N \cdot t_{check}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | $N$ polls per period | Helps debug "CPU overload" symptoms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition:** $f_{TCK} \le \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{setup}}$ to satisfy target input setup time, where $t_{setup}$ is the target's TCK-to-TMS setup specification.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Use-Cases for Each Debug Method:**
> - **JTAG boundary-scan** — Board manufacturing test (BGA pin shorts/opens).
> - **JTAG debug** — ARM Cortex-M firmware development (ST-Link, J-Link).
> - **SWD** — Space-constrained IoT wearables (Nordic nRF52, STM32).
> - **BDM** — Automotive ECU calibration (Freescale S12Z, KEAZ).
> - **ISS** — Safety-critical code verification (DO-178C, ISO 26262) before silicon.
> - **ROM Monitor** — Bootloader + debug combo in legacy 8051 designs.

# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: JTAG Chain Scan Time vs. Clock Frequency

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A JTAG scan chain consists of **3 devices** in daisy-chain. Device A has an Instruction Register of length **4 bits**, Device B has **8 bits**, Device C has **3 bits**. The TCK frequency is **10 MHz**. Compute the **total time to load a single instruction into all three devices** (one bit per TCK cycle per device).

### **Exhaustive Derivation**

**Given:**
- $L_A = 4$ bits
- $L_B = 8$ bits
- $L_C = 3$ bits
- $f_{TCK} = 10 \text{ MHz} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 1 — Concept of daisy-chain:**
In a JTAG daisy chain, the TDO of Device A feeds the TDI of Device B, and TDO of Device B feeds TDI of Device C. To load a complete instruction into all three devices, the host must shift in a total of $L_A + L_B + L_C$ bits serially.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{chain} &= L_A + L_B + L_C \\
L_{chain} &= 4 + 8 + 3 \\
L_{chain} &= 15 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute period of TCK:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{TCK} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{TCK}} \\
T_{TCK} &= \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^6} \\
T_{TCK} &= 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\
T_{TCK} &= 100 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — One bit shifted per TCK cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= L_{chain} \times T_{TCK} \\
T_{scan} &= 15 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} \\
T_{scan} &= 1500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\
T_{scan} &= 1.5 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** It takes **1.5 µs** to clock the instruction into the chain. The *Update-DR* or *Update-IR* state, in which the instruction is latched into the device, occurs after a few additional TCK cycles (typically 2–3), but is negligible here.

**Step 4 — General Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{scan} = \dfrac{L_{chain}}{f_{TCK}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i}{f_{TCK}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering insight:**
Doubling $f_{TCK}$ to **20 MHz** would halve the time to **0.75 µs**, but at the risk of violating the target's $t_{setup}$ on TMS/TDI relative to TCK rising edge. Always consult the MCU's datasheet for $t_{setup}$, $t_{hold}$ specifications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective CPU Overhead from Polling (helps debug "system slow" bugs)

**Problem Statement:**
A polling loop checks a UART flag every **5 µs**, doing 12 instructions per check. The CPU runs at **50 MHz** and each instruction takes 1 cycle. The application has a **5 ms** periodic control loop. What percentage of CPU time is wasted on polling?

### **Exhaustive Derivation**

**Given:**
- $t_{poll} = 5 \text{ µs} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $n_{instr} = 12$ instructions per check
- $f_{CPU} = 50 \text{ MHz}$
- $T_{period} = 5 \text{ ms} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s

**Step 1 — Time per polling iteration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{iter} &= \dfrac{n_{instr}}{f_{CPU}} \\
t_{iter} &= \dfrac{12}{50 \times 10^6} \\
t_{iter} &= 2.4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\
t_{iter} &= 0.24 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of polls in one period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{polls} &= \dfrac{T_{period}}{t_{poll}} \\
N_{polls} &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \\
N_{polls} &= 1000 \text{ polls}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total CPU time wasted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{waste} &= N_{polls} \times t_{iter} \\
T_{waste} &= 1000 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-7} \\
T_{waste} &= 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
T_{waste} &= 240 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Overhead percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{poll} &= \dfrac{T_{waste}}{T_{period}} \times 100\% \\
OH_{poll} &= \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\% \\
OH_{poll} &= 4.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Only **4.8 %** CPU overhead. If your system "feels slow" and you see ~5 % polling overhead, the real culprit is likely something else (e.g., a blocking delay, a long ISR). This kind of calculation is the **first step in debug by measurement**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Breakpoint Count Limitation in Hardware Debug

**Problem Statement (Conceptual):**
An ARM Cortex-M0 has **2 hardware breakpoint comparators**. Each comparator matches a single instruction address. You need to set breakpoints at 5 locations during code integration. Propose a solution.

### **Analytical Reasoning**

**Limitation:**
A **Cortex-M0** has only **2 HW breakpoint units**. Setting a 3rd breakpoint will fail.

**Solution Paths:**

**Path 1 — Strategic breakpoint placement:**
Place breakpoints at the *branch targets* (function entry points) rather than at every line. Two carefully chosen points may suffice.

**Path 2 — Software breakpoints (BKPT instruction):**
Insert the `BKPT 0` assembly instruction (1-byte thumb instruction) directly into Flash. This works *only* if Flash is writable and the debugger can patch the instruction back on resume. The Cortex-M0 supports **unlimited** software breakpoints.

**Path 3 — Watchpoint + halt-on-access:**
Set a hardware **watchpoint** (data breakpoint) on a variable that changes only at the suspect location.

**Trade-off matrix:**

| Method | Count | Intrusiveness | Flash Durability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HW Breakpoint | 2 (Cortex-M0) | None | N/A (uses comparators) |
| SW Breakpoint | Unlimited | High (modifies Flash) | Reduced by 1 erase cycle per breakpoint |
| Watchpoint | 1–2 | None | N/A |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Debug Methods

| Parameter | ISS | ROM Monitor | ICE | JTAG | SWD | BDM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware needed** | None | Target + UART | Special bond-out probe | Probe (cheap) | Probe (cheap) | Probe (cheap) |
| **Pins used** | 0 | 1–2 | 0 (replaces MCU) | 4–5 | 2 | 1–2 |
| **Real-time** | No | No (halts) | Yes | Yes (with ETM) | Yes (with SWO) | Yes |
| **Trace support** | Full sim | No | Full | ETM/ITM | SWO/ITM | Limited |
| **Cost** | Free | Low | Very high | Low | Low | Low |
| **Boundary scan** | No | No | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| **KTU relevance** | High | Medium | Low (legacy) | **Very High** | High | **High** |
| **Silicon vendor** | Any | Any | Few | Most (ARM, MIPS) | ARM only | Freescale/NXP |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Detecting a "Stuck-at-1" Pin via Boundary Scan

**Problem:**
A board has a 144-pin BGA ARM MCU. Pin P4.5 is stuck high, but you cannot probe it physically.

**Boundary-Scan Debug Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Force the TAP to `Test-Logic-Reset`, then to `Shift-IR`.
**Step 2:** Shift in the `SAMPLE/PRELOAD` instruction (typically 4 bits for ARM).
**Step 3:** In `Shift-DR`, clock out the boundary-scan register; the bit corresponding to P4.5 reveals its actual logic level.
**Step 4:** If `1` is captured when the firmware drives `0` to P4.5, you have a **stuck-at-1 fault** — likely a solder bridge or short to VDD.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected}_{P4.5} &= 0 \text{ (firmware drives low)} \\
\text{Observed}_{P4.5} &= 1 \text{ (boundary scan reads high)} \\
\text{Conclusion} &: \text{Stuck-at-1 fault on PCB trace}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is exactly how production-line JTAG testers (e.g., JTAG Technologies, ASSET InterTech) detect PCB faults at 100 % coverage — even on hidden BGA pins.**

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level Debug Architecture (Host ↔ Probe ↔ Target)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of a modern on-chip debug session, showing the data flow between the engineer's PC and the embedded target.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hostA[Host PC IDE]
    probeA[Debug Probe]
    targetA[Target MCU]
    mcuCore[CPU Core]
    ocdBlock[OCD Logic Block]
    bsrBlock[Boundary Scan Cells]
    flashMem[Flash Memory]
    ramMem[RAM Memory]
    periphA[Peripherals GPIO UART ADC]

    hostA -- USB or Ethernet --> probeA
    probeA -- JTAG SWD or BDM --> targetA
    targetA --> mcuCore
    targetA --> ocdBlock
    targetA --> bsrBlock
    mcuCore -- Address and Data Buses --> flashMem
    mcuCore -- Address and Data Buses --> ramMem
    mcuCore -- Internal Bus --> periphA
    ocdBlock -- Control Signals --> mcuCore
    ocdBlock -- Bus Tap --> ramMem
    bsrBlock -- Pin Override --> periphA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The **Host PC IDE** is the user-facing application (e.g., Keil, IAR, Eclipse).
> - The **Debug Probe** is the translator (JTAG/SWD/BDM ↔ USB/Ethernet).
> - The **OCD Logic Block** intercepts the CPU's normal control signals to enable halt, step, and memory access.
> - **Boundary-Scan Cells** wrap every I/O pin for virtual probing.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: JTAG TAP Controller State Machine (Top-Level)

The 16-state JTAG TAP FSM is the conceptual heart of every JTAG transaction. The following Mermaid block represents the major transitions (abbreviated for clarity; see IEEE 1149.1 for the full 16-state graph).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TestLogicReset
    TestLogicReset --> RunTestIdle : TMS low on TCK
    RunTestIdle --> SelectDRScan : TMS high
    RunTestIdle --> SelectIRScan : TMS high twice
    SelectDRScan --> CaptureDR : TMS low
    SelectIRScan --> CaptureIR : TMS low
    CaptureDR --> ShiftDR : TMS low
    CaptureIR --> ShiftIR : TMS low
    ShiftDR --> ShiftDR : TMS low
    ShiftIR --> ShiftIR : TMS low
    ShiftDR --> Exit1DR : TMS high
    ShiftIR --> Exit1IR : TMS high
    Exit1DR --> UpdateDR : TMS high
    Exit1IR --> UpdateIR : TMS high
    UpdateDR --> RunTestIdle : TMS low
    UpdateIR --> RunTestIdle : TMS low
    Exit1DR --> PauseDR : TMS low
    Exit1IR --> PauseIR : TMS low
    PauseDR --> PauseDR : TMS low
    PauseIR --> PauseIR : TMS low
    UpdateDR --> SelectDRScan : TMS high
    UpdateIR --> SelectDRScan : TMS high
    SelectDRScan --> SelectIRScan : TMS high
    SelectIRScan --> SelectDRScan : TMS high
    TestLogicReset --> TestLogicReset : TMS high
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You may be asked to "name the JTAG state from which instructions are latched into the device" — the answer is **`Update-IR`**. The state from which data bits are loaded from TDI is **`Shift-DR`**.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: BDM Single-Wire Transaction (Conceptual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host as Host PC
    participant Probe as BDM Probe
    participant MCU as Target MCU
    participant CPU as CPU Core

    Host->>Probe: Send command (e.g., READ_BYTE 0x0800)
    Probe->>MCU: Drive 17-bit BDM frame on BKGD
    MCU->>CPU: Force CPU into BACKGROUND mode
    CPU-->>MCU: Acknowledge halt
    MCU-->>Probe: Return requested byte on BKGD
    Probe-->>Host: Deliver byte over USB
    Host->>Probe: Issue GO command
    Probe->>MCU: Release CPU, resume execution
    MCU->>CPU: Resume from current PC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The elegance of BDM:** A single bidirectional wire (BKGD) carries both commands and data, bit-by-bit, clocked by the BDM logic inside the target.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: On-Chip Debug Block (Internal Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ocdTop[OCD Module]
    tapCtrl[TAP Controller or BDM Logic]
    bpUnit[Breakpoint Comparators]
    wpUnit[Watchpoint Comparators]
    regAcc[Register Access MUX]
    memAcc[Memory Access MUX]
    traceIface[Trace Interface ETM ITM SWO]
    cpuIf[CPU Core Interface]

    ocdTop --> tapCtrl
    ocdTop --> bpUnit
    ocdTop --> wpUnit
    ocdTop --> regAcc
    ocdTop --> memAcc
    ocdTop --> traceIface
    ocdTop --> cpuIf
    tapCtrl --> cpuIf
    bpUnit --> cpuIf
    wpUnit --> cpuIf
    regAcc --> cpuIf
    memAcc --> cpuIf
    traceIface --> cpuIf
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** All OCD features — JTAG, BDM, breakpoints, trace — are **sub-blocks of a single OCD module** integrated into the silicon. From the CPU's perspective, the OCD looks like just another bus master.
