On a **cache hit** (probability $H$), the CPU fetches from the cache. On a **cache miss** (probability $1 - H$), it must fetch from main memory.

### Step-2: Formulate the weighted average.

$$
t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot t_{main}
$$

### Step-3: Substitute numerical values.

$$
t_{avg} = (0.9)(10) + (0.1)(100)
$$

### Step-4: Compute each term.

$$
t_{avg} = 9 + 10
$$

### Step-5: Final result.

$$
t_{avg} = 19 \text{ ns}
$$

**Conclusion:** The effective average access time is **19 ns**, demonstrating a **~5× speedup** over main memory alone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Address Calculation for a Byte Array

**Problem Statement:** An array `buf` of 50 bytes starts at address 0x2000 in 8051 internal RAM. Find the address of `buf[40]`. *(Indexing starts at 0; stride S = 1 byte.)*

### Step-1: Apply the address formula.

$$
A_{last} = A_{base} + (N-1) \cdot S
$$

### Step-2: Substitute N = 41 (since index 40 means 41st element), S = 1.

$$
A_{40} = 0x2000 + (41-1) \times 1
$$

### Step-3: Simplify.

$$
A_{40} = 0x2000 + 40
$$

### Step-4: Convert 40 to hex.

$$
40_{10} = 0x28
$$

### Step-5: Add.

$$
A_{40} = 0x2000 + 0x28 = 0x2028
$$

**Conclusion:** `buf[40]` is located at address **0x2028**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Selection Decision Table

| Application Requirement | Recommended Memory Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Boot firmware (must persist) | NOR Flash (internal) | Non-volatile, XIP support, fast random read |
| Runtime stack + heap | Internal SRAM | Fast, byte-addressable, no refresh overhead |
| Calibration constants | EEPROM or Flash Emulated EEPROM | Byte-wise erase, ~10⁶ write endurance |
| Large data logging | External NAND Flash / SD card | High density, low cost per bit |
| Battery-backed RTC registers | SRAM + coin cell | Instantaneous read/write, no erase delay |
| Video frame buffer | DRAM / SDRAM | High density, burst access support |
| Real-time DSP scratchpad | Internal SRAM (zero wait-state) | Deterministic access time for ISR latency |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["CPU Registers\n8 to 64 x 16/32 bit\nAccess: 1 cycle"]
    nodeB["Level 1 Cache\nKB range\nAccess: 2 to 5 ns"]
    nodeC["Internal SRAM\nKB range\nAccess: 10 to 50 ns"]
    nodeD["Main DRAM or SDRAM\nMB range\nAccess: 50 to 100 ns"]
    nodeE["Internal Flash NOR\nKB to MB range\nNon volatile\nAccess: 50 to 200 ns"]
    nodeF["External NAND Flash or SD\nGB range\nNon volatile\nAccess: 100 us to ms"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF

    nodeG["Smaller and Faster and Costlier"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeH["Larger and Slower and Cheaper"] -.-> nodeF
```

## 4.2 8051 Harvard Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CODE["PROGRAM MEMORY up to 64 KB"]
        direction TB
        addr0000["0x0000 to 0x0002\nReset vector"]
        addr0003["0x0003\nExternal Int 0 ISR"]
        addr000B["0x000B\nTimer 0 ISR"]
        addr0013["0x0013\nExternal Int 1 ISR"]
        addr001B["0x001B\nTimer 1 ISR"]
        addr002B["0x002B\nSerial Port ISR"]
        mainCode["0x002B onwards\nUser main program"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["INTERNAL DATA MEMORY 128 bytes + 128 SFR"]
        direction TB
        rb["0x00 to 0x1F\nRegister Banks 0 to 3"]
        bitArea["0x20 to 0x2F\nBit Addressable 128 bits"]
        scratch["0x30 to 0x7F\nGeneral Purpose RAM 80 bytes"]
        sfr["0x80 to 0xFF\nSFRs ACC B PSW SP DPTR P0 P1 P2 P3"]
    end

    subgraph XDATA["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY up to 64 KB"]
        extRAM["Accessed via MOVX and DPTR\n16 bit address on Port 0 and Port 2"]
    end

    cpu["8051 CPU\nwith PC and DPTR"] --> CODE
    cpu --> DATA
    cpu --> XDATA
```

## 4.3 Memory-Mapped I/O vs I/O-Mapped I/O Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MM["MEMORY MAPPED I/O Architecture"]
        mmAddr["Single Address Space 0x0000 to 0xFFFF"]
        mmMem["Memory locations"]
        mmIO["I/O devices treated as memory addresses"]
        mmAddr --> mmMem
        mmAddr --> mmIO
    end

    subgraph IOM["I/O MAPPED ISOLATED I/O Architecture"]
        iomMem["Memory Address Space 0x0000 to 0xFFFF"]
        iomIO["Separate I/O Address Space\nIN and OUT instructions"]
        iomCPU["CPU uses two address buses"]
        iomCPU --> iomMem
        iomCPU --> iomIO
    end
```

## 4.4 SRAM 6T Cell Internal Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wl["Word Line WL\nRow Select"] --> aT1["Access NMOS M1"]
    wl --> aT2["Access NMOS M2"]

    aT1 --> sNode["Storage Node S"]
    aT2 --> sNodec["Storage Node S bar"]

    sNode --> inv1["Inverter I1\nPMOS pull up and NMOS pull down"]
    sNodec --> inv2["Inverter I2\nPMOS pull up and NMOS pull down"]
    inv1 --> sNodec
    inv2 --> sNode

    aT1 --> bitL["Bit Line BL"]
    aT2 --> bitLb["Bit Line bar BL bar"]

    sNode -.stores.-> bit0["Bit 0 or 1"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. Why is SRAM preferred for cache memory?**

**Model Answer:**
SRAM (Static RAM) uses **6 transistors per bit cell** (two cross-coupled inverters + 2 access transistors), while DRAM (Dynamic RAM) uses **1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit cell**. SRAM retains data as long as power is supplied and **does not require periodic refresh**, whereas DRAM must be refreshed every ~64 ms because the storage capacitor leaks charge. SRAM has a much **lower access time (5–10 ns)** compared to DRAM (50–100 ns). Hence SRAM is preferred for **cache memory** where speed and deterministic access are critical. The trade-off is that SRAM has **lower density (6T/bit)** and **higher cost per bit** than DRAM. *(3 marks: 1 mark SRAM cell, 1 mark DRAM cell, 1 mark cache justification.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Supplementary]
**What is Harvard architecture? How does it differ from Von Neumann architecture in terms of memory?**

**Model Answer:**
**Harvard architecture** uses **physically separate memory and buses** for **program code (instruction memory)** and **data memory**, allowing simultaneous fetch of instruction and operand in a single cycle. Examples: **8051, ARM Cortex-M**.

**Von Neumann architecture** uses a **single shared memory and bus** for both code and data, so instruction fetch and data access must be **time-multiplexed**, creating the classic *Von Neumann bottleneck*. Examples: **8086, most general-purpose CPUs**.

Key differences: (1) Number of address buses (2 vs 1). (2) Simultaneous code+data access (Yes vs No). (3) Code can be fetched from data memory (Yes in Von Neumann, No in pure Harvard). *(3 marks: 1.5 marks each difference + 0.5 marks for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the various types of ROM used in embedded systems with neat diagrams. Compare their programming and erasure mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

ROM (Read-Only Memory) is a **non-volatile** memory that retains its contents even when power is removed. Various types exist based on programmability:

1. **Mask ROM (MROM):** The data is **hard-wired during IC fabrication** using a custom photomask. The user cannot change it after manufacturing. **Programming:** Done by the IC vendor. **Erasure:** Not possible. **Use:** High-volume consumer products (toys, calculators). **Disadvantage:** Long lead time, high NRE cost.

2. **PROM (Programmable ROM):** Uses **fusible links (nickel-chromium or polysilicon)**. The user can program it **once** by selectively blowing fuses using high-current pulses. **Programming:** One-time, user-performed using PROM programmer. **Erasure:** Not possible. **Use:** Low-volume prototypes.

3. **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Uses a **floating-gate MOSFET (FAMOS)**. Programming injects hot electrons onto the floating gate (using ~12–25 V). The chip has a **quartz window** through which **UV light (~253.7 nm, 20–30 min)** discharges the gate and erases the entire chip. **Erasure:** Bulk erase, UV, off-system.

4. **EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM):** Uses **thin-oxide tunnel injection (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling)**. Can be **byte-wise erased and reprogrammed** in-circuit using ~5–12 V. **Erasure:** Byte-wise, electrical, on-system. **Endurance:** ~10⁶ cycles. **Use:** Storing calibration, configuration, serial numbers.

5. **Flash Memory:** A variant of EEPROM that erases in **blocks (sectors) of 4–128 KB** instead of byte-wise, giving **higher density and faster bulk erase**. Two sub-types:
   - **NOR Flash:** Random access, supports XIP (Execute-In-Place). Used for program memory.
   - **NAND Flash:** Page/block access only, higher density. Used for mass storage.

**Comparison Table (write on answer sheet):**

| ROM Type | Program Method | Erase Method | Granularity | In-Circuit? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mask ROM | Mask (fab) | Not erasable | — | No |
| PROM | Fuse blow | Not erasable | — | No |
| EPROM | Hot electron injection | UV light | Full chip | No |
| EEPROM | Tunnel injection | Electrical | Byte-wise | Yes |
| Flash | Hot electron / tunnel | Electrical | Block-wise | Yes |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per ROM type explanation + 1 mark for comparison table + 1 mark for Flash sub-types.)*

---

**(b)** A microcontroller has 16 KB of program memory. How many address lines are required? If the data bus is 8 bits wide, what is the total memory capacity in bits? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert KB to bytes.

$$
16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16384 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Find number of address lines using $N_{addr} = \log_2(M)$.

$$
N_{addr} = \log_2(16384) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ lines}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute total memory capacity in bits.

$$
C_{mem} = M \times W = 16384 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

$$
C_{mem} = 131072 \text{ bits} = 128 \text{ Kbits}
$$

**Step 4:** If the MCU has 8 KB RAM, 4 KB EEPROM, 16 KB Flash, total memory map size in bytes:

$$
8 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ KB} + 16 \text{ KB} = 28 \text{ KB} = 28672 \text{ bytes}
$$

The address bus required to map the *entire* 28 KB combined memory would be:

$$
N_{addr} = \log_2(28672) \approx 14.81 \implies N_{addr} = 15 \text{ lines}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 16 KB program memory needs **14 address lines** and the total memory is **128 Kbits**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for conversion, 2 marks for address lines, 2 marks for capacity in bits, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

### QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal data memory organization of 8051 microcontroller. Specify the role of the Stack Pointer (SP) and the SFR region. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 has a **Harvard architecture** with separate program and data memory spaces. The **internal data memory** is **128 bytes** (addresses 0x00 to 0x7F) and is divided into three regions:

```
Address Range        Region              Purpose
0x00 - 0x07          Register Bank 0     R0-R7 of bank 0
0x08 - 0x0F          Register Bank 1     R0-R7 of bank 1
0x10 - 0x17          Register Bank 2     R0-R7 of bank 2
0x18 - 0x1F          Register Bank 3     R0-R7 of bank 3
0x20 - 0x2F          Bit-Addressable     16 bytes = 128 bits
0x30 - 0x7F          General Purpose     Scratchpad RAM (80 bytes)
```

**Register Banks:** The active bank is selected by bits **RS0 and RS1** in the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register. Switching banks allows **fast context switching** in ISRs without saving R0–R7 to stack.

**Bit-Addressable Area (0x20–0x2F):** Any of the **128 individual bits** in this region can be set/cleared/tested using single-bit instructions (e.g., `SETB 0x20`, `CLR 0x07`). This is highly efficient for boolean flag manipulation in real-time control.

**General Purpose RAM (0x30–0x7F):** Used for **stack, heap, and user variables**. The stack grows **upward** from the address pointed to by the **Stack Pointer (SP)**. The SP is initialized to **0x07** and is incremented before each PUSH. Thus the stack typically resides in the upper part of the scratchpad.

**SFR Region (0x80–0xFF):** This is a **128-byte region** of *Special Function Registers* mapped onto the same address range as the upper 128 bytes of data memory in 16-bit addressing. Accessing them requires *direct addressing mode* (e.g., `MOV 0x80, A` to load ACC). Key SFRs include:
- **ACC (0xE0)** – Accumulator
- **B (0xF0)** – B register (for MUL/DIV)
- **PSW (0xD0)** – Program Status Word
- **SP (0x81)** – Stack Pointer
- **DPTR (0x82, 0x83)** – Data Pointer (16-bit)
- **P0–P3 (0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, 0xB0)** – Port latches

**Role of SP:** The SP is an 8-bit register that holds the address of the *top* of the stack. On PUSH, SP is incremented, then data is written at the new address. On POP, data is read, then SP is decremented. SP enables nested subroutine calls and interrupt handling.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for block diagram / regions, 2 marks for SFR roles, 2 marks for SP explanation.)*

---

**(b)** Explain Memory-Mapped I/O and I/O-Mapped I/O schemes. List any three differences between them. Which scheme is used in 8051? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Memory-Mapped I/O:** I/O devices are treated as **memory locations**. They are assigned addresses within the **same address space** as memory. The CPU uses standard memory read/write instructions (e.g., `LOAD`, `STORE`) to access both memory and I/O. **No separate control signals** are required.

**I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated I/O):** I/O devices have a **separate, dedicated address space** distinct from memory. The CPU has **special instructions** like `IN` and `OUT` (in 8086) and an **extra control signal** (e.g., `IO/M̅` in 8086) to differentiate between I/O and memory cycles.

**Three Differences:**

| Parameter | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O-Mapped I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | Shared with memory | Separate, isolated |
| Instructions used | Normal `LOAD`/`STORE` | Special `IN`/`OUT` |
| Address space size | Reduced (memory + I/O share) | Full memory address space available |
| Flexibility | Higher (any memory instruction works) | Lower (limited to IN/OUT) |
| Decoding complexity | More complex | Simpler |
| Speed | Slightly slower | Faster (smaller address space) |

**8051 uses Memory-Mapped I/O** for its Special Function Registers (SFRs). For example, Port 1 is accessed at address 0x90 using the standard `MOV` instruction:

```
MOV 0x90, A    ; Write accumulator to Port 1
```

Thus, **all SFRs in 8051 are memory-mapped** in the 0x80–0xFF region.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for each scheme definition, 3 marks for the three differences, 1 mark for 8051 identification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Harvard with Von Neumann** — many students write "Harvard uses single bus." It does *not*; Harvard uses *separate* code/data buses.
> 2. **Address line formula errors** — students often write $N = 2^{M}$ instead of $M = \log_2(N)$. This single error costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up EPROM and EEPROM erasure** — EPROM requires *UV* light (off-system, full chip), EEPROM is *electrical* (in-system, byte-wise). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert KB to bytes** in capacity problems — write $1024 \times N$ explicitly to gain full marks.
> 5. **In 8051 memory questions**, students forget that the *upper 128 bytes* (0x80–0xFF) are SFRs, not RAM. They overlap only in 16-bit addressing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Memory is non-volatile (ROM/Flash) or volatile (RAM)** — this is the *first* classification.
- ✅ **SRAM = 6T cell, no refresh, fast.** **DRAM = 1T1C cell, refresh every ~64 ms, dense.**
- ✅ **ROM evolution:** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM (UV) → EEPROM (electrical, byte) → **Flash (electrical, block)**.
- ✅ **Address lines formula:** $N_{addr} = \log_2(\text{memory size in bytes})$.
- ✅ **Capacity in bits:** $C = M \times W$, where W = data bus width.
- ✅ **Average access time:** $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot t_{main}$.
- ✅ **8051 uses Harvard architecture** — separate 64 KB code (PSEN̅) + 64 KB XDATA (MOVX) + 256 bytes internal data/SFR.
- ✅ **8051 internal RAM = 128 bytes** in three regions: Register Banks, Bit-Addressable, Scratchpad.
- ✅ **8051 uses Memory-Mapped I/O** for its SFRs (0x80–0xFF).
- ✅ **NOR Flash supports XIP**, NAND Flash is block-only — choose based on use case.
- ✅ **Memory hierarchy trade-off:** Speed ↑ = Cost ↑ = Density ↓.
- ✅ **Stack Pointer (SP)** is 8-bit, points to *top of stack*, initialized to 0x07 in 8051.
- ✅ **EEPROM endurance = ~10⁶ cycles**, Flash endurance = ~10⁵ cycles — pick EEPROM for frequently written parameters.
- ✅ **Cache hit ratio improvement from 0.9 → 0.95** in the worked example would lower $t_{avg}$ from 19 ns to 14.5 ns — a *5.5× speedup* — proving the importance of locality.

> **End of Module 3 — Memory Notes** | *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **sensor** is a physical device or transducer that detects, measures, and converts a physical quantity (such as temperature, pressure, light, humidity, or motion) from the real-world environment into an equivalent electrical signal (analog or digital) that can be processed, displayed, or stored by an electronic system such as a microcontroller.

In the context of embedded systems (PCRAT302), sensors form the **input interface** between the physical world and the digital brain (microcontroller) of the system. Without sensors, an embedded system would be "blind" and unable to perceive its environment.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a sensor as the "SENSES" of an embedded system** — just as humans use eyes (vision sensors), ears (sound/acoustic sensors), skin (temperature/pressure sensors), and nose (gas/chemical sensors) to perceive the world, an embedded system uses electronic sensors to perceive its surroundings. The five human senses map directly to the categories of sensors used in embedded design.

### Classification of Sensors

**1. Based on Output Signal Type:**
- **Analog Sensors** — Produce a continuous output voltage/current proportional to the input (e.g., LM35 temperature sensor, LDR).
- **Digital Sensors** — Produce discrete binary output or a digital protocol (e.g., DHT11, DS18B20, ultrasonic HC-SR04).

**2. Based on Power Requirement:**
- **Active Sensors** — Require external power to operate and generate their own signal (e.g., radar, ultrasonic sensor).
- **Passive Sensors** — Directly generate an electrical signal in response to the stimulus without needing external excitation (e.g., thermocouple, photodiode).

**3. Based on Physical Quantity Measured:**
- **Thermal:** LM35, DHT11, DS18B20, Thermistor
- **Optical:** LDR, Photodiode, IR sensor
- **Mechanical:** Strain gauge, Accelerometer (ADXL345), Gyroscope (MPU6050)
- **Chemical:** Gas sensor (MQ-2), pH sensor
- **Proximity/Position:** Ultrasonic (HC-SR04), PIR, Rotary encoder

---

## 1.2 What is an Actuator?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An **actuator** is a physical device that converts an electrical control signal (from a microcontroller or processor) into a physical action such as motion, heat, light, or force. It performs the exact opposite function of a sensor — it is the **output interface** of an embedded system.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an actuator as the "MUSCLES" of an embedded system** — just as human muscles act on signals from the brain (via nerves) to produce movement (walk, lift, speak), actuators act on signals from the microcontroller (via GPIO, PWM, or DAC pins) to produce real-world effects. If a sensor is "input", the actuator is "output".

### Classification of Actuators

**1. Based on Energy Domain:**
- **Electrical Actuators:** DC motor, Stepper motor, Servo motor, Relay, Solenoid
- **Mechanical Actuators:** Rack and pinion, Gears, Pneumatic cylinders
- **Hydraulic Actuators:** Hydraulic cylinder, Hydraulic motor
- **Thermal Actuators:** Heater, Peltier cooler
- **Optical Actuators:** LED, Laser diode, LCD/Display

**2. Based on Motion Type:**
- **Rotational:** DC motor, Servo, Stepper
- **Linear:** Solenoid, Linear actuator
- **Non-motion:** LED, Buzzer, Heater

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PCRAT302 (Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems), the primary focus is on **interfacing electronic sensors and actuators with microcontrollers** such as the 8051, ARM Cortex, or Arduino-class MCUs. Students must understand both the **transduction principle** (how physical quantity → electrical signal and vice versa) and the **interfacing circuitry** required.

---

## 1.3 The Sensor–Microcontroller–Actuator Loop

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental structure of every modern embedded system follows the **Sense → Decide → Actuate** paradigm. Sensors gather data, the microcontroller processes the data using programmed logic, and actuators perform the necessary physical action based on the decision. This forms a closed-loop control system in most practical applications (e.g., a room temperature controller using LM35 + heater).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Performance Characteristics of Sensors

A sensor is never ideal. KTU examiners frequently test your understanding of sensor specifications. Each parameter defines how accurately, reliably, and consistently the sensor operates.

### 2.1.1 Static Characteristics

**1. Accuracy**
The degree of closeness of a measured value to the **true value** of the quantity being measured. Expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output or full-scale reading (FSR).

$$\text{Accuracy} = \pm \frac{\text{Maximum Error}}{\text{True Value}} \times 100\%$$

**2. Precision**
The degree of **reproducibility** of a measurement — how close multiple repeated measurements are to each other, irrespective of whether they are close to the true value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A clock showing 2:58 when it is actually 3:00 is **accurate but not precise** if the error is constant. A clock randomly showing 3:02, 2:55, 3:08, 2:57 is **precise (consistent) but not accurate**.

**3. Resolution**
The smallest incremental change in the input that can be **detected and faithfully represented** in the output. For digital systems, resolution is tied directly to the **ADC bit width**.

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

where $n$ = number of bits of the ADC, and $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage.

**4. Sensitivity**
The ratio of the change in output to the change in input that causes it. A highly sensitive sensor produces a large output for a small change in the input.

$$S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta X_{input}}$$

Units: V/°C, V/lux, mV/kPa depending on the sensor.

**5. Linearity**
The extent to which the actual sensor output curve matches a **straight line**. A perfectly linear sensor obeys:

$$V_{out} = m \cdot X + b$$

where $m$ is the slope (sensitivity) and $b$ is the offset (output when input is zero).

Non-linearity is usually expressed as a **percentage of full-scale output (% FSO)**.

**6. Range (Span)**
The minimum and maximum values of the input that the sensor can measure. For example, LM35 measures 0°C to 150°C.

**7. Hysteresis**
The difference in output for the **same input value** when approached from opposite directions (increasing vs. decreasing). Caused by mechanical friction, magnetic lag, or material properties.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Characteristics

**1. Response Time**
The time required for the sensor output to reach a specified percentage (usually 90% or 95%) of its final steady-state value after a step change in input.

**2. Bandwidth / Frequency Response**
The range of input signal frequencies over which the sensor can accurately respond. Defined as the frequency at which the output amplitude drops to **70.7% (–3 dB)** of its DC value.

**3. Rise Time ($t_r$)**
Time taken for the output to go from **10% to 90%** of its final value for a step input.

**4. Settling Time**
Time for the output to settle and remain within a specified tolerance band (e.g., ±2%) of the final value.

---

## 2.2 Key Performance Characteristics of Actuators

| **Parameter** | **Description** |
|---|---|
| **Stroke / Travel** | Maximum displacement (linear) or rotation (angular) the actuator can produce |
| **Force / Torque** | Mechanical force or rotational torque the actuator can deliver |
| **Speed** | Velocity of motion (mm/s for linear, RPM for rotational) |
| **Power Rating** | Maximum electrical power the actuator can handle without damage (e.g., 12V, 1A) |
| **Efficiency** | Ratio of useful mechanical output power to electrical input power |
| **Response Time** | Time delay between receiving control signal and producing mechanical action |
| **Duty Cycle** | Percentage of time the actuator can be continuously operated (e.g., a servo rated for 50% duty) |

---

## 2.3 ADC Interface — The Critical Bridge

Most sensors output **analog signals**. Microcontrollers work in the digital domain. The **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** bridges this gap.

### 2.3.1 ADC Resolution Formula

$$\text{Step Size (Resolution)} = \frac{V_{ref+}-V_{ref-}}{2^n}$$

where:
- $V_{ref+}$ = upper reference voltage (e.g., 5V)
- $V_{ref-}$ = lower reference voltage (e.g., 0V or GND)
- $n$ = number of ADC bits (e.g., 8, 10, 12, 16)

### 2.3.2 Digital Output Code for a Given Analog Input

$$D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)\right)$$

### 2.3.3 Reconstructing the Original Input

$$V_{in} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example for KTU:** For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5V$ and sensor output $V_{in} = 2.5V$:
> Resolution = $\frac{5}{2^{10}-1} = \frac{5}{1023} \approx 4.89 \text{ mV}$
> Digital code = $\frac{2.5}{5} \times 1023 = 511.5 \approx 512$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta X_{in}}$ | V per unit of measured quantity |
| Accuracy | $\pm \frac{\text{Max Error}}{\text{True Value}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as % of FSR or reading |
| ADC Resolution (Step Size) | $\text{Step} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts per LSB |
| ADC Digital Output | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)\right)$ | Integer count |
| ADC Input Reconstruction | $V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | Reverse calculation |
| Sensor Transfer Function (Linear) | $V_{out} = mX + b$ | $m$ = sensitivity, $b$ = offset |
| Non-linearity Error | $\%\text{FSO} = \frac{V_{actual}-V_{ideal}}{V_{FSO}} \times 100$ | Deviation from ideal line |
| Inverting Amplifier Gain | $A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Signal conditioning stage |
| Non-inverting Amplifier Gain | $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Signal conditioning stage |
| Voltage Divider (Sensor bias) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Used for LDR, thermistor circuits |
| Thermistor B-parameter | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\!\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | $T$ in Kelvin |
| LM35 Output Equation | $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$ | Linear, 0°C → 0V |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Used to control actuator speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes used** — absolute values and divisions are shown using `\vert` or `\mid` notation style to maintain table integrity. The $\vert$ symbol in formulas represents the absolute value or "given that" depending on context.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Application Domain** | **Sensors Used** | **Actuators Used** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Home Automation** | PIR, LDR, DHT11, MQ-2 | Relay, Servo motor, Buzzer | Auto lights, security, climate control |
| **Automotive (ECU)** | MAP, Oxygen, Speed, ABS sensors | Fuel injector, Brake actuator, Motor | Engine control, anti-lock brakes |
| **Healthcare Devices** | Heart rate (ECG), Temperature, SpO2 | Piezo buzzer, Pump, Vibration motor | Patient monitoring, drug delivery |
| **Industrial IoT** | Pressure, Flow, Gas, Humidity | Solenoid valves, Conveyor motors | Process automation, SCADA |
| **Robotics** | IMU (Accel+Gyro), Ultrasonic, IR | DC/Servo/Stepper motors, Gripper | Obstacle avoidance, manipulation |
| **Agriculture** | Soil moisture, DHT11, pH | Water pump, Solenoid, Fan | Smart irrigation, greenhouse control |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for PCRAT302, students are expected to be able to design simple sensor-actuator systems using microcontrollers. A typical 14-mark question may ask you to design a temperature-controlled fan system, an automatic street light, or an obstacle-avoiding robot — combining both sensor and actuator interfacing concepts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: ADC Conversion of an LM35 Temperature Reading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU exam-style derivation problem** (frequently worth 7 marks). Walk through every step carefully — examiners mark **every intermediate calculation step** in the working.

### Problem Statement
An embedded system uses an **LM35 temperature sensor** connected to the **ADC input of an 8051 microcontroller (or ATmega328 / ARM Cortex)**. The ADC has the following specifications:
- **Resolution:** 10 bits
- **Reference voltage ($V_{ref}$):** 5.0 V
- **Sensor output:** $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$ (LM35 transfer function)

Calculate:
1. The **step size (resolution in volts)** of the ADC.
2. The **digital output code** when the temperature is **45°C**.
3. The **smallest temperature change** that can be detected.
4. The **reconstructed digital temperature** if the ADC gives a code of 723.

---

### Step 1 — Determine the Step Size (Resolution in Volts)

The step size of an ADC is the voltage corresponding to 1 LSB (Least Significant Bit) of the digital output.

$$\text{Step Size} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

Substituting $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ and $n = 10$ bits:

$$\text{Step Size} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1}$$

$$= \frac{5.0}{1024 - 1}$$

$$= \frac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Step Size} = 4.887 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.887 \text{ mV/LSB}}$$

**Explanation of Logic:** The 10-bit ADC has $2^{10} = 1024$ possible output codes (from 0 to 1023). These 1024 codes must span the entire 0–5V range. The voltage change required to increment the digital code by 1 is therefore 5.0 V divided by 1023.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the LM35 Output Voltage at 45°C

Using the LM35 transfer function:

$$V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$$

$$V_{out} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/°C} \times 45 \text{°C}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.450 \text{ V} = 450 \text{ mV}}$$

**Explanation of Logic:** LM35 is a linear analog temperature sensor with a sensitivity of exactly 10 mV per degree Celsius. At 0°C, the output is 0V, and the voltage increases by 10 mV for every 1°C rise.

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Digital Output Code for $V_{in} = 0.450 \text{ V}$

$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)\right)$$

Substituting $V_{in} = 0.450 \text{ V}$, $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $n = 10$:

$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{0.450}{5.0} \times 1023\right)$$

$$= \text{round}\!\left(0.09 \times 1023\right)$$

$$= \text{round}\!\left(92.07\right)$$

$$\boxed{D = 92}$$

**Explanation of Logic:** The ADC samples the analog voltage and quantizes it into the nearest integer digital code. The ratio 0.450/5.0 = 0.09 indicates that the input is 9% of the full-scale range, and 9% of 1023 = 92.07, which rounds to 92.

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Smallest Detectable Temperature Change

The smallest detectable voltage change is one step size (1 LSB), which is **4.887 mV**.

Since LM35 produces **10 mV/°C**, the smallest detectable temperature change is:

$$\Delta T_{min} = \frac{\text{Step Size (V)}}{\text{Sensitivity (V/°C)}}$$

$$= \frac{4.887 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{min} = 0.4887 \text{ °C} \approx 0.49 \text{ °C}}$$

**Explanation of Logic:** Even if the temperature changes by only 0.49°C, the LM35 output changes by 4.9 mV — just enough to flip one LSB. Any change smaller than this is "invisible" to this ADC.

---

### Step 5 — Reconstruct the Temperature When Digital Code $D = 723$

First, reconstruct the input voltage:

$$V_{in} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

$$= 723 \times \frac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$= 723 \times 4.887 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 3.534 \text{ V}$$

Now, convert the voltage back to temperature using the LM35 inverse equation:

$$T = \frac{V_{out}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$= \frac{3.534}{0.010}$$

$$\boxed{T = 353.4 \text{ °C}}$$

**Explanation of Logic:** Note that 353.4°C exceeds the LM35's specified range (typically 0–150°C). This is a **valid calculation** but indicates the system is operating outside the sensor's specified safe operating region — a point examiners love to test in follow-up questions.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Designing a Voltage Divider Bias Circuit for an LDR (Light Sensor)

> [!NOTE]
> Another **high-frequency KTU question** — combining Ohm's law, voltage divider analysis, and sensor interfacing.

### Problem Statement
An **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** is used in a voltage divider biasing configuration with a fixed resistor $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The supply voltage is $V_{CC} = 5.0 \text{ V}$. The LDR has the following characteristics:
- In **darkness:** $R_{LDR} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ }\Omega$
- In **bright light:** $R_{LDR} = 100 \text{ }\Omega$

The output voltage is taken across the LDR (between the LDR and ground). Calculate the output voltage in both cases and determine the dynamic range.

---

### Step 1 — State the Voltage Divider Equation

For a standard voltage divider with $R_1$ (top) and $R_{LDR}$ (bottom):

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$$

**Derivation Note:** This is derived from Ohm's law. The current through the series combination is $I = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$. The voltage across $R_{LDR}$ is $V_{out} = I \cdot R_{LDR}$. Substituting gives the formula above.

---

### Step 2 — Calculate $V_{out}$ in Darkness ($R_{LDR} = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$)

$$V_{out, dark} = 5.0 \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000}$$

$$= 5.0 \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}010{,}000}$$

$$= 5.0 \times 0.9901$$

$$\boxed{V_{out, dark} = 4.9505 \text{ V} \approx 4.95 \text{ V}}$$

**Logic:** In darkness, the LDR resistance is enormous compared to $R_1$, so nearly all of $V_{CC}$ drops across the LDR, leaving a high output.

---

### Step 3 — Calculate $V_{out}$ in Bright Light ($R_{LDR} = 100 \text{ }\Omega$)

$$V_{out, light} = 5.0 \times \frac{100}{10{,}000 + 100}$$

$$= 5.0 \times \frac{100}{10{,}100}$$

$$= 5.0 \times 0.009901$$

$$\boxed{V_{out, light} = 0.0495 \text{ V} \approx 49.5 \text{ mV}}$$

**Logic:** In bright light, the LDR resistance is tiny compared to $R_1$, so almost all of $V_{CC}$ drops across $R_1$, leaving only a small output.

---

### Step 4 — Calculate the Dynamic Range

$$\text{Dynamic Range} = \frac{V_{out, dark}}{V_{out, light}} = \frac{4.9505}{0.0495}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dynamic Range} = 100.01 \approx 100 \text{:}1}$$

**Logic:** The ratio of resistances in the two cases is $1{,}000{,}000 / 100 = 10{,}000$, but because the output is non-linear with respect to resistance, the actual dynamic range of the output voltage is only ~100:1. This non-linearity is a key reason LDRs are often replaced by photodiodes or phototransistors in precision applications.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Sensors at a Glance

| **Sensor** | **Quantity** | **Output Type** | **Sensitivity / Transfer** | **Range** | **Interface to MCU** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LM35** | Temperature | Analog (Voltage) | 10 mV/°C | 0 to 150°C | ADC pin |
| **DHT11** | Temp + Humidity | Digital (Single-wire) | — | 0–50°C, 20–90% RH | GPIO + timing protocol |
| **DS18B20** | Temperature | Digital (1-Wire) | Programmable res. 9–12 bit | –55 to +125°C | GPIO + 1-Wire library |
| **LDR (GL55)** | Light | Resistance change | $R \propto 1/\text{lux}$ | ~1 MΩ (dark) to ~100 Ω (light) | Voltage divider → ADC |
| **Photodiode** | Light | Current change | Linear with light intensity | Wide | Transimpedance amp → ADC |
| **HC-SR04** | Distance | Digital (Pulse width) | ~58.2 µs per inch | 2 cm to 400 cm | Trigger + Echo GPIO |
| **ADXL345** | 3-axis Accel. | Digital (I²C / SPI) | ±2g to ±16g selectable | — | I²C / SPI bus |
| **MPU6050** | Accel + Gyro | Digital (I²C) | ±2/4/8/16g, ±250/500/1000°/s | — | I²C bus |
| **MQ-2** | Gas (LPG, CH₄) | Analog + Digital | Resistance drops with gas | 100–10,000 ppm | ADC + comparator |
| **Thermistor (NTC)** | Temperature | Resistance change | $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | –55 to +150°C | Voltage divider → ADC |
| **PIR (HC-SR501)** | Motion (IR) | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | — | ~7 m range, 120° cone | GPIO (interrupt) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Actuators at a Glance

| **Actuator** | **Output Action** | **Control Signal from MCU** | **Typical Specs** | **Drive Circuit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LED** | Light (indicator) | GPIO HIGH/LOW or PWM | 2V, 20 mA | Current-limiting resistor |
| **DC Motor** | Continuous rotation | PWM (variable duty) | 3–12V, 100 mA–5A | H-bridge (L293D, L298N) |
| **Servo Motor (SG90)** | Precise angular position | PWM (1–2 ms pulse) | 4.8–6V, 1.8 kg·cm torque | Direct GPIO + 5V supply |
| **Stepper Motor (28BYJ-48)** | Discrete step rotation | Step pulses (4-wire sequence) | 5V, 200 steps/rev | ULN2003 driver |
| **Relay (5V coil)** | Mechanical switch ON/OFF | GPIO HIGH | 5V coil, 10A/250V AC | Transistor driver + flyback diode |
| **Buzzer (Piezo)** | Sound (beep) | GPIO HIGH/PWM | 3–12V, 5–10 mA | Direct GPIO or transistor |
| **Solenoid Valve** | Linear push/pull | GPIO via MOSFET | 12V, 500 mA | MOSFET/relay driver |
| **7-Segment Display** | Numeric display | 7+ GPIO lines (or BCD decoder) | 2V/segment, 10 mA | Current-limiting resistors |
| **LCD 16x2** | Alphanumeric display | 4/8-bit parallel or I²C | 5V, 1 mA (logic) | PCF8574 I²C expander |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sensor–Microcontroller–Actuator Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHYSICAL WORLD<br/>Temperature, Light,<br/>Pressure, Motion] --> B[SENSOR BLOCK<br/>Transducer]
    B --> C[SIGNAL<br/>CONDITIONING<br/>Amplifier + Filter]
    C --> D[ADC BLOCK<br/>Analog to Digital<br/>Converter]
    D --> E[MCU BLOCK<br/>Microcontroller<br/>Decision Logic]
    E --> F[DRIVER BLOCK<br/>Power Amplification<br/>H-Bridge, Transistor]
    F --> G[ACTUATOR BLOCK<br/>Motor, Relay, LED,<br/>Buzzer, Solenoid]
    G --> H[PHYSICAL WORLD<br/>Physical Action<br/>Rotation, Light, Sound]
    H -. Feedback Loop .-> A

    classDef inputClass fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processClass fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputClass fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef worldClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,H worldClass
    class B,C,D processClass
    class E processClass
    class F,G outputClass
```

**Explanation of the Architecture:**
1. **Physical World** generates the stimulus (temperature rise, light, motion).
2. **Sensor** converts that physical quantity into a raw electrical signal (often a tiny voltage).
3. **Signal Conditioning** amplifies, filters, and linearizes the signal so it matches the ADC input range.
4. **ADC** converts the conditioned analog signal into a digital number.
5. **Microcontroller** runs the algorithm (e.g., if temperature > 40°C, turn on fan).
6. **Driver Circuit** boosts the weak MCU output signal to the power level required by the actuator.
7. **Actuator** produces the physical effect.
8. **Feedback Loop** (dashed line) — in closed-loop systems, the actuator's effect is re-measured by the sensor to ensure accuracy.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Detailed LM35 Temperature Controller Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Ambient Temperature<br/>in °C] --> B1[LM35 SENSOR<br/>10 mV per °C]
    B1 --> C1[Signal Conditioner<br/>Op-Amp Gain Stage<br/>Gain = 5]
    C1 --> D1[ADC CHANNEL 0<br/>10-bit Resolution<br/>Vref = 5V]
    D1 --> E1[MCU 8051 OR ARM<br/>Read ADC<br/>Compare with Threshold]
    E1 --> F1{Decision Logic<br/>T > 40°C ?}
    F1 -- YES --> G1[GPIO HIGH<br/>to Transistor Base]
    F1 -- NO --> H1[GPIO LOW<br/>Fan OFF]
    G1 --> I1[TRANSISTOR DRIVER<br/>2N2222 or MOSFET]
    I1 --> J1[DC FAN OR RELAY<br/>5V or 12V Load]
    H1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Air Flow or<br/>Heater Activation]

    classDef sensorStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mcuStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class B1 sensorStyle
    class C1,D1,E1,F1 mcuStyle
    class G1,H1,I1,J1,K1 outputStyle
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The LM35 produces a low-voltage analog signal (e.g., 400 mV at 40°C).
- The op-amp stage amplifies it 5× to a 0–5V range matching the ADC.
- The MCU reads the ADC value, performs a comparison in software, and drives the fan through a transistor.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Interfacing Classification of Sensors and Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[SENSORS] --> S1[Analog Sensors<br/>LM35, LDR, Thermistor,<br/>Photodiode, Gas Sensor]
    S --> S2[Digital Sensors<br/>DHT11, DS18B20,<br/>HC-SR04, MPU6050]
    S1 --> I1[Requires ADC<br/>for MCU Interface]
    S2 --> I2[Direct GPIO<br/>I2C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire]
    I1 --> M[MCU Processing]
    I2 --> M

    M --> O1[PWM Output<br/>DC Motor, Servo,<br/>LED Dimming]
    M --> O2[Digital GPIO<br/>Relay, Buzzer, Solenoid]
    M --> O3[Communication Bus<br/>LCD, Stepper Driver]
    O1 --> A[ACTUATORS]
    O2 --> A
    O3 --> A

    classDef sensorClass fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef interfaceClass fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mcuClass fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputClass fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class S,S1,S2 sensorClass
    class I1,I2 interfaceClass
    class M mcuClass
    class O1,O2,O3,A outputClass
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Sensors split into analog (need ADC) and digital (direct GPIO/bus).
- The MCU processes data and drives actuators via three primary output channels: **PWM** (for proportional control), **Digital GPIO** (for ON/OFF devices), and **Communication Buses** (for complex peripherals like LCDs or stepper drivers).

---

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning — Op-Amp Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Signal<br/>e.g. 50 mV from Thermocouple] --> B[Instrumentation<br/>Amplifier<br/>Gain = 100]
    B --> C[Low-Pass Filter<br/>fc = 100 Hz<br/>Remove Noise]
    C --> D[Level Shifter<br/>Shift to 0 to 5V Range]
    D --> E[ADC Input Pin<br/>of Microcontroller]

    classDef signalClass fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ampClass fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputClass fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,E signalClass
    class B,C,D ampClass
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- A raw thermocouple signal (~50 mV) is too small for an ADC.
- The instrumentation amplifier boosts it 100× to ~5 V.
- The low-pass filter removes high-frequency noise.
- The level shifter ensures the signal lies in the ADC's 0–5V range.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define a Sensor. List any four static characteristics of a sensor. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **sensor** is a physical device that detects a physical quantity (such as temperature, pressure, light, or motion) and converts it into an equivalent electrical signal that can be measured, recorded, or processed by an electronic system.

**Four Static Characteristics of a Sensor:**

1. **Accuracy:** The closeness of a measured value to the true value, usually expressed as a percentage of full-scale reading (% FSR).
2. **Resolution:** The smallest incremental change in the input that the sensor can detect. For digital sensors, it is determined by the ADC bit depth.
3. **Sensitivity:** The ratio of the change in output to the change in input, defined as $S = \Delta V_{out} / \Delta X_{in}$.
4. **Linearity:** The degree to which the sensor output follows a straight-line relationship with the input. Non-linearity error is expressed as a percentage of FSO.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid static characteristics include **Precision, Range, Hysteresis, Repeatability**, and **Drift**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between an Actuator and a Sensor. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Sensor** | **Actuator** |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal (input device) | Converts an electrical signal into a physical action (output device) |
| **Direction of Energy** | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical |
| **Position in System** | Input side of the MCU | Output side of the MCU |
| **Analogy** | "Senses" of the system | "Muscles" of the system |
| **Example** | LM35 temperature sensor, LDR, DHT11 | DC motor, Servo motor, Relay, LED |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always** write the transfer function / equation of the sensor **before** substituting values. Students who skip this lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Always** mention the unit of every numerical answer. A bare number without units is treated as incomplete.
> - In ADC problems, students often confuse $2^n$ with $2^n - 1$. The total number of codes is $2^n$, but the count of *steps* (intervals) is $2^n - 1$.
> - For LDR / thermistor voltage dividers, students frequently use the wrong resistor position in the divider formula. **Draw the circuit** and label $V_{out}$ explicitly before computing.
> - In PWM motor control questions, you must explain **why** duty cycle variation changes speed (average voltage applied to the motor).

---

### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a sensor–microcontroller–actuator system**. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **10-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ is connected to an **LM35 temperature sensor** in an embedded system. **(7 Marks)**

   (i) Calculate the **ADC resolution** in mV.
   (ii) Determine the **digital output code** when the ambient temperature is **52°C**.
   (iii) Find the **smallest temperature change** that can be detected.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of sensors based on **(i) output type** and **(ii) power requirement**. Give **two examples** for each category. Discuss the working principle of an **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** with a suitable voltage divider circuit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **DC motor** is interfaced to a microcontroller through an **L293D H-bridge driver IC**. The motor is controlled using **PWM** from the MCU. **(7 Marks)**

   (i) Explain the **working of the L293D driver** and why a driver is necessary.
   (ii) If the PWM frequency is **1 kHz** and the **ON time** is **0.6 ms**, calculate the **duty cycle** and explain how it affects the **average voltage** seen by the motor.
   (iii) State the **direction control logic** of the L293D (input pins IN1, IN2 for one motor).

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Sensor–MCU–Actuator System Architecture

The complete functional architecture of an embedded sensor–microcontroller–actuator system consists of **seven functional blocks** arranged in a pipeline. Each block performs a specific transformation in the signal chain.

**1. Sensor Block**
The sensor is the **transducer** that detects a physical stimulus (heat, light, pressure, motion, etc.) from the environment and converts it into a corresponding electrical signal, usually a low-amplitude voltage or current. Example: LM35 (temperature), LDR (light), MQ-2 (gas), HC-SR04 (distance).

**2. Signal Conditioning Block**
The raw sensor output is often too weak, noisy, or not aligned with the ADC's input range. Signal conditioning includes:
- **Amplification** (using op-amp stages) to bring the signal to usable levels.
- **Filtering** (low-pass, high-pass) to remove unwanted noise.
- **Level shifting** to match the ADC voltage range.
- **Linearization** for non-linear sensors like thermistors.

**3. ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) Block**
The conditioned analog signal is sampled and quantized by the ADC into a discrete digital code. The number of bits (8, 10, 12, 16) determines the resolution and the smallest detectable change in the input.

**4. Microcontroller / Processor Block**
The MCU reads the digital value, processes it according to the program logic, performs comparisons or calculations, and generates a decision. Common MCUs: 8051, ATmega328, ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4, ESP32.

**5. Driver / Power Amplifier Block**
The MCU GPIO pins can source/sink only a few milliamperes (~5–20 mA), which is insufficient to drive most actuators. A driver circuit (transistor, MOSFET, relay, H-bridge IC) boosts this signal to the required current/voltage level.

**6. Actuator Block**
The actuator converts the electrical control signal into a physical action — rotation (motor), sound (buzzer), light (LED), heat (heater), or linear motion (solenoid).

**7. Feedback Loop (Optional)**
In closed-loop control systems, the actuator's effect is measured by a sensor and fed back to the MCU, which adjusts its output to maintain the desired state. Example: A thermostat re-reads the temperature to keep room temperature stable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7-Mark Part):** Block diagram = 2 marks, Explanation of each block = 1 mark each × 5 = 5 marks. Total = 7 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: ADC Calculation with LM35

**Given:**
- ADC: 10-bit ($n = 10$)
- $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$
- Temperature: $T = 52 \text{°C}$
- LM35 transfer function: $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$

**(i) ADC Resolution:**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Resolution} = 4.887 \text{ mV/LSB}}$$

**(ii) Digital Output Code at 52°C:**

First, compute the LM35 output:

$$V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times 52 \text{°C} = 520 \text{ mV} = 0.520 \text{ V}$$

Then, convert to digital code:

$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{0.520}{5.0} \times 1023\right)$$

$$D = \text{round}\!\left(0.104 \times 1023\right) = \text{round}\!\left(106.39\right)$$

$$\boxed{D = 106}$$

**(iii) Smallest Detectable Temperature Change:**

$$\Delta T_{min} = \frac{\text{Resolution (V)}}{\text{LM35 Sensitivity (V/°C)}} = \frac{4.887 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{min} = 0.4887 \text{ °C} \approx 0.49 \text{ °C}}$$

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Sensor Classification and LDR

**Classification of Sensors:**

**(i) Based on Output Type:**

- **Analog Sensors** — produce a continuous output signal proportional to the input. Examples: **LM35** (temperature, output in mV), **LDR** (resistance change).
- **Digital Sensors** — produce a discrete digital output (binary, pulse width, or protocol-based). Examples: **DHT11** (single-wire protocol), **DS18B20** (1-Wire protocol).

**(ii) Based on Power Requirement:**

- **Active Sensors** — require external power to operate and generate their own signal. Examples: **Ultrasonic HC-SR04**, **Radar**, **Active IR sensor**.
- **Passive Sensors** — directly generate an electrical response without needing external excitation. Examples: **Thermocouple** (Seebeck effect), **Photodiode** (photovoltaic mode), **LDR**.

**Working Principle of LDR (Light Dependent Resistor):**

An LDR is a passive analog sensor made of a **cadmium sulfide (CdS) semiconductor** whose resistance **decreases** as the intensity of incident light **increases**. In darkness, the LDR's resistance is very high (≈ 1 MΩ); in bright light, it drops to a few hundred ohms.

The mechanism is based on the **photoconductivity** of the semiconductor. Photons striking the CdS material transfer energy to electrons, releasing them from the valence band into the conduction band, thereby increasing the number of free charge carriers and reducing the resistance.

**Voltage Divider Interfacing Circuit:**

In a typical interface, the LDR is connected as the lower resistor in a voltage divider with a fixed resistor $R_1$ across $V_{CC}$. The output $V_{out}$ is taken from the junction of $R_1$ and LDR.

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$$

In **darkness**, $R_{LDR}$ is high, so $V_{out}$ is high (close to $V_{CC}$). In **bright light**, $R_{LDR}$ drops, so $V_{out}$ falls. This voltage can be fed to an ADC to read ambient light level.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: L293D Driver and PWM Control of DC Motor

**(i) Working of L293D and Why a Driver is Needed:**

The **L293D** is a **dual H-bridge motor driver IC** that can drive two DC motors (or one stepper motor) bi-directionally. The H-bridge consists of four transistor switches (two high-side, two low-side) arranged in an H configuration, allowing current to flow in either direction through the motor, thereby controlling rotation direction.

A driver is necessary because:
- MCU GPIO pins can source/sink only **5–20 mA**, while a DC motor typically requires **100 mA – 5 A**.
- Motors generate **back-EMF** and electrical noise that can damage the MCU; the L293D provides built-in **flyback diodes** to protect the controller.
- The L293D accepts **TTL-compatible logic inputs** (from the MCU) and switches **higher-voltage/high-current loads** (up to 36V, 600 mA per channel, or 1.2 A peak) at its output.

**(ii) PWM Duty Cycle Calculation:**

**Given:**
- PWM frequency: $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ → Period $T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms} = 1000 \text{ µs}$
- ON time: $T_{on} = 0.6 \text{ ms} = 600 \text{ µs}$

Duty Cycle:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.6 \text{ ms}}{1.0 \text{ ms}} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{D = 60\%}$$

**Effect on Average Voltage:**

If the motor is supplied with $V_{supply} = 12 \text{ V}$, the average voltage seen by the motor is:

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply} = 0.60 \times 12 = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

Since the DC motor's speed is approximately proportional to the average applied voltage, a **60% duty cycle makes the motor run at roughly 60% of its full speed** (at 100% duty cycle). Adjusting the duty cycle in software gives smooth, variable speed control.

**(iii) Direction Control Logic of L293D:**

For one motor (Motor A), the L293D uses two input pins: **IN1** and **IN2**. The motor's direction is controlled by the digital levels on these pins.

| **IN1** | **IN2** | **Motor A Function** |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Stop (coast) — both low-side off |
| 0 | 1 | Rotate in **one direction** (e.g., clockwise) |
| 1 | 0 | Rotate in **opposite direction** (counter-clockwise) |
| 1 | 1 | Stop (brake) — both high-side on |

The **enable pin (EN1, EN2)** is tied to the **PWM signal** from the MCU, allowing speed control via duty cycle variation. Pin **VCC2** supplies the motor voltage, while **VCC1** (5V) supplies the logic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ A **sensor** converts a **physical quantity → electrical signal**; an **actuator** converts an **electrical signal → physical action**.
- ✅ Sensors are classified as **analog/digital** (by output) and **active/passive** (by power).
- ✅ Actuators are classified by **energy domain** (electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, thermal) and **motion type** (rotational, linear, non-motion).
- ✅ The **transfer function** $V_{out} = mX + b$ is the linear model of most sensors — know how to compute sensitivity ($m$) and offset ($b$).
- ✅ **ADC Resolution** = $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ — the voltage equivalent of 1 LSB.
- ✅ **Digital Code** $D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$ — used to convert analog to digital.
- ✅ **LM35** is a linear analog temperature sensor with **10 mV/°C** sensitivity.
- ✅ **DHT11** is a digital single-wire protocol sensor for temperature + humidity.
- ✅ **LDR** resistance is **inversely proportional** to light intensity; interfaced via a voltage divider.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic HC-SR04** measures distance using **echo pulse width** (≈ 58.2 µs per inch or 29.1 µs per cm).
- ✅ **MPU6050** combines a 3-axis accelerometer and 3-axis gyroscope on a single **I²C** bus.
- ✅ **DC motor speed** is controlled via **PWM duty cycle**; rotation **direction** is controlled via an **H-bridge** (L293D, L298N).
- ✅ **Servo motor** is controlled by a **variable-width pulse** (1 ms = 0°, 1.5 ms = 90°, 2 ms = 180°).
- ✅ **Stepper motor** rotates in discrete **steps** (e.g., 1.8° per step or 200 steps/rev for a full-step motor) controlled by a 4-bit phase sequence through a driver like **ULN2003** or **A4988**.
- ✅ **Relay** is an electrically controlled mechanical switch that requires a **transistor driver** and a **flyback diode** for safe operation.
- ✅ **Signal conditioning** is essential when interfacing low-amplitude, noisy, or non-linear sensor outputs to an ADC.
- ✅ Common interface protocols used by digital sensors: **GPIO (1-Wire), I²C, SPI, UART**.
- ✅ **Resolution and accuracy are not the same**: resolution is the smallest detectable change; accuracy is the closeness to the true value.
- ✅ **Hysteresis** causes the output to differ for the same input on the rising vs. falling direction — relevant for sensors like thermostats and pressure sensors.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, always **write units, draw circuit/block diagrams, and show step-by-step substitutions** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communication Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Communication Interface — Formal Definition

In the context of **embedded systems**, a **Communication Interface** refers to the standardized hardware and protocol mechanism that enables data exchange between a microcontroller and one or more external peripherals, sensors, actuators, or other processing units. The KTU 2024 Scheme defines communication interfaces under Module 3 as the **serial communication protocols** used to transfer data bit-by-bit (or in synchronized frames) over a shared medium, in contrast to parallel communication which uses multiple data lines simultaneously.

The three principal serial communication protocols mandated in the PCRAT302 syllabus are:

1. **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** — Asynchronous, full-duplex, point-to-point.
2. **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)** — Synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave, short distance.
3. **I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)** — Synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master/multi-slave, two-wire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes *interface architecture, frame format, timing diagrams, register-level programming (conceptual), and comparative analysis* of UART, SPI, and I²C. CAN and USB are generally introduced as advanced extensions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are in a classroom and need to send a message to a friend:

- **UART (Asynchronous)** → You write the message on a paper, fold it, and toss it to your friend. There is **no shared clock** between you — but both of you must have **agreed beforehand** on the language, reading speed, and starting/ending signals. The friend must be ready *exactly* when the paper arrives.

- **SPI (Synchronous with dedicated lines)** → You have a **megaphone (Clock line)** that you tap once for every letter you speak. You also have **two separate tubes**: one for talking to your friend and one for them to talk back simultaneously. Fast, but uses more wires and only works in the same room.

- **I²C (Synchronous, shared bus)** → You and your friend share a **single rope** for talking back and forth, plus a **single tapping stick** for clocking. Multiple people can sit in the classroom, each with a unique **address** (roll number). You call out their roll number before sending data. Saves wires but the speed is slower because the rope is shared.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Communication Metrics (Recall-Ready):**
> - **Baud Rate (bps)** — Number of signal changes per second. For UART with N=1 stop bit and no parity, **1 baud = 1 bit/second**.
> - **I²C Standard Mode Speed** — **100 kHz** (Standard), **400 kHz** (Fast), **3.4 MHz** (High-Speed).
> - **SPI Clock Speed** — Application-dependent, typically **1 MHz – 50 MHz**.
> - **I²C Slave Address Width** — **7-bit** (standard) or **10-bit** (extended).
> - **UART Standard Baud Rates** — 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 bps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UART — Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle
UART is a **hardware circuit** that converts parallel data from the CPU into **serial asynchronous form** for transmission, and vice versa for reception. The word *asynchronous* means **no shared clock line** is present between transmitter and receiver.

### 2.1.2 Frame Format (11 bits typical: 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Parity + 1 Stop)

The UART frame is composed of:

1. **Start Bit (1 bit, always LOW / '0')** — Signals the receiver that data is coming.
2. **Data Bits (5, 6, 7, or 8 bits, LSB first)** — The actual payload.
3. **Parity Bit (Optional, 1 bit)** — Error detection: **Even**, **Odd**, **Mark**, or **Space**.
4. **Stop Bit(s) (1, 1.5, or 2 bits, always HIGH / '1')** — Signals end of frame; line returns to idle HIGH.

### 2.1.3 Why "Asynchronous"?
Both ends use **independent baud-rate generators** (often derived from a crystal oscillator via a **baud-rate divisor register**). For successful communication, both ends **must be configured to the same baud rate within ±2–3% tolerance**.

### 2.1.4 Baud Rate Generation (8051 Example)
For the classic 8051, the Timer 1 overflow rate generates the baud rate:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

For ARM/STM32, the baud rate is derived from the system APB clock and a programmable divider (USART_BRR register):

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{16 \cdot \text{USARTDIV}}$$

### 2.1.5 Error Detection
- **Parity Error** — Mismatch in parity bit calculation.
- **Framing Error** — Expected stop bit not received.
- **Overrun Error** — Previous data byte not read before new byte arrived.

---

## 2.2 SPI — Serial Peripheral Interface

### 2.2.1 Operational Principle
SPI is a **synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave** protocol developed by Motorola. It uses **four signal lines** (standard SPI) and is suitable for **short-distance, high-speed** communication (e.g., on-board chips, SD cards, sensors, displays).

### 2.2.2 The Four SPI Lines

| Line | Full Name | Direction | Purpose |
|------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **SCLK** | Serial Clock | Master → Slave | Synchronization clock generated by master |
| **MOSI** | Master Out Slave In | Master → Slave | Data from master to slave |
| **MISO** | Master In Slave Out | Slave → Master | Data from slave to master |
| **SS / CS** | Slave Select / Chip Select | Master → Slave | Active-low select; one line per slave |

### 2.2.3 SPI Clock Polarity (CPOL) and Clock Phase (CPHA)
The combination of CPOL and CPHA defines **four SPI modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)**, which determine when data is sampled and when it is shifted out relative to the clock edge.

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Clock Idle State | Sampling Edge |
|------|------|------|------------------|---------------|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising edge |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling edge |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling edge |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *Master and slave MUST operate in the same SPI mode. Mismatched CPOL/CPHA results in corrupted data even if hardware is wired correctly.*

### 2.2.4 Data Transfer Mechanism
During each clock cycle, **1 bit is shifted from master to slave on MOSI** and **1 bit is shifted from slave to master on MISO**. This simultaneous shifting is what makes SPI **full-duplex**. Data is exchanged through **shift registers** (typically 8-bit wide).

### 2.2.5 Slave Select Logic
- When **SS = HIGH**: Slave is deselected; its MISO line goes to **high-impedance (Hi-Z)**.
- When **SS = LOW**: Slave is selected and listens to SCLK/MOSI.

---

## 2.3 I²C — Inter-Integrated Circuit

### 2.3.1 Operational Principle
I²C is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave** serial bus developed by Philips (now NXP). It uses **only two bidirectional lines**, making it ideal for connecting multiple low-speed peripherals using minimal PCB trace area.

### 2.3.2 The Two I²C Lines

| Line | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **SCL** | Serial Clock Line — Generated by the master |
| **SDA** | Serial Data Line — Bidirectional data |

Both lines are **open-drain**, requiring **pull-up resistors** (typically **4.7 kΩ** for 100 kHz, **2.2 kΩ** for 400 kHz) to VCC.

### 2.3.3 Addressing
Each I²C slave has a **7-bit address** (or 10-bit extended). The address is sent by the master during the address frame. The **R/W bit** (8th bit) follows the 7-bit address, indicating direction: **0 = Write**, **1 = Read**.

### 2.3.4 I²C Frame Structure

$$\underbrace{\text{S} \rightarrow \text{7-bit Address} \rightarrow \text{R/W} \rightarrow \text{Ack} \rightarrow \text{Data Byte} \rightarrow \text{Ack} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{P}}_{\text{I}^2\text{C Transaction}}$$

- **S (Start Condition)** — SDA transitions HIGH → LOW while SCL is HIGH.
- **P (Stop Condition)** — SDA transitions LOW → HIGH while SCL is HIGH.
- **Acknowledge (ACK)** — Receiver pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse.
- **NACK (No Acknowledge)** — Receiver leaves SDA HIGH during 9th clock pulse.

### 2.3.5 Arbitration (Multi-Master)
When two masters start transmission simultaneously, they monitor SDA. If one master sends HIGH while another sends LOW (a "0" wins), the losing master **releases the bus** and retries later. This is called **wired-AND arbitration**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | UART 8051 Baud Rate | $\text{BR} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | TH1 is Timer 1 reload value |
| 2 | UART STM32 Baud Rate | $\text{BR} = \dfrac{f_{\text{CLK}}}{16 \cdot \text{USARTDIV}}$ | USARTDIV is fractional divider |
| 3 | Total UART Frame Time | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bits}}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | Total Bits = 1 + Data + Parity + Stop |
| 4 | SPI Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = f_{\text{SCLK}} \cdot N_{\text{bits}}$ | Limited by slowest peripheral |
| 5 | I²C Pull-up Resistor (min) | $R_{\text{p(min)}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{CC}} - V_{\text{OL}}}{I_{\text{OL}}}$ | $I_{\text{OL}}$ = 3 mA typical |
| 6 | I²C Pull-up Resistor (max) | $R_{\text{p(max)}} = \dfrac{t_r}{0.8473 \cdot C_b}$ | $t_r$ = max rise time, $C_b$ = bus capacitance |
| 7 | Standard I²C Speed | 100 kbps | Philips specification |
| 8 | Fast Mode I²C Speed | 400 kbps | Philips specification |
| 9 | High-Speed Mode I²C | 3.4 Mbps | Philips specification |
| 10 | SPI Mode Selection | Mode = CPOL × 2 + CPHA | Yields 0, 1, 2, 3 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where each protocol shines in industry:**
> - **UART** — Debug console (printf-style logging), GPS modules (NMEA 0183 at 9600 bps), Bluetooth modules (HC-05), GSM modems (AT commands).
> - **SPI** — SD cards, TFT/LCD displays, ADC/DAC chips, Flash memory (W25Q series), high-speed sensors (MPU6050 IMU).
> - **I²C** — EEPROMs (24Cxx), RTC chips (DS1307), temperature sensors (LM75, BMP280), GPIO expanders (PCF8574), low-pin-count sensor hubs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing Timer 1 Reload Value for 8051 UART

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal** and Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to generate a baud rate of **9600 bps** with **SMOD = 0**.

### Step 1 — Write the Baud Rate Equation
The 8051 UART divides the oscillator frequency by 12 (machine cycle), then by 32 (UART fixed divider), and Timer 1's overflow rate provides the time base.

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values
Plug in $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz, SMOD = 0, Target Baud = 9600:

$$9600 = \frac{2^0 \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Denominator
$32 \times 12 = 384$, so:

$$9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 4 — Isolate the (256 − TH1) Term
Rearrange:

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \cdot 9600}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3.6864 \times 10^6}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = 3$$

### Step 5 — Solve for TH1
$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253$$

$$\text{TH1} = 0xFD \text{ (hexadecimal)}$$

### Step 6 — Verify by Reverse Calculation
$$\text{BR} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \cdot 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{1152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU:** The crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz** is deliberately chosen because $11.0592 / 12 = 921.6$ kHz, which is exactly divisible by common baud rates — yielding **0% error**. Using 12 MHz would produce ~8.5% error at 9600 baud.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: UART Total Frame Time Calculation

**Problem:** A UART transmits **8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit** at **115200 baud**. How long does one frame take, and how many full frames can be transmitted in **1 second**?

### Step 1 — Count the Total Bits Per Frame
$$\text{Total Bits} = 1 \text{ (Start)} + 8 \text{ (Data)} + 0 \text{ (Parity)} + 1 \text{ (Stop)} = 10 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Time Per Bit
$$T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}} = \frac{1}{115200} = 8.6805 \, \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Frame Time
$$T_{\text{frame}} = 10 \times T_{\text{bit}} = 10 \times 8.6805 \, \mu s = 86.805 \, \mu s$$

### Step 4 — Compute Frames Per Second
$$\text{Frames/sec} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}} = \frac{1}{86.805 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 11520 \text{ frames/sec}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Effective Data Throughput
Since only 8 of 10 bits are payload data:
$$\text{Throughput} = 8 \times 11520 = 92160 \text{ bits/sec} = 92.16 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficiency** = $8/10 = 80\%$. The start and stop bits are overhead. UART at 115200 baud with 8N1 delivers only **~92.16 kbps of useful data**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: I²C Pull-up Resistor Selection

**Problem:** Design the pull-up resistor for an I²C bus operating in **Standard Mode (100 kHz)** with VCC = 3.3 V, bus capacitance $C_b = 100$ pF, sink current $I_{\text{OL}} = 3$ mA, and maximum rise time $t_r = 1000$ ns.

### Step 1 — Calculate Maximum Pull-up (RC Time-Constant Constraint)
From the I²C specification:
$$R_{p(\text{max})} = \frac{t_r}{0.8473 \cdot C_b}$$

Substitute:
$$R_{p(\text{max})} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{p(\text{max})} = \frac{10^{-6}}{0.8473 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{8.473 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 11.8 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Minimum Pull-up (Sink-Current Constraint)
$$R_{p(\text{min})} = \frac{V_{\text{CC}} - V_{\text{OL}}}{I_{\text{OL}}}$$

$$R_{p(\text{min})} = \frac{3.3 - 0.4}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.9}{0.003} \approx 966.67 \, \Omega \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 3 — Choose a Valid Value
The pull-up must satisfy both:
$$1 \text{ k}\Omega \le R_p \le 11.8 \text{ k}\Omega$$

A standard value of **4.7 kΩ** is the textbook choice for 3.3 V systems at 100 kHz, comfortably within bounds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| **Synchronization** | Asynchronous | Synchronous | Synchronous |
| **Clock Line** | None | SCLK (Master) | SCL (Master) |
| **Data Lines** | 2 (TX, RX) | 2 (MOSI, MISO) | 1 (SDA, bidirectional) |
| **Total Lines (master + 1 slave)** | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| **Duplex Mode** | Full-duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex |
| **Max Practical Speed** | ~1 Mbps (RS-232) | 10–50 MHz+ | 3.4 MHz (HS mode) |
| **Number of Slaves** | 1 (point-to-point) | Many (one SS per slave) | Many (addressed, 7-bit/10-bit) |
| **Addressing** | Not applicable | Hardware (SS line) | Software (7/10-bit address) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Software Complexity** | Low | Low | Higher (Start/Stop/Ack handling) |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Typical Use Case** | Debug, GPS, BT | Displays, Flash, ADC | Sensors, EEPROM, RTC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UART Frame Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TX as UART Transmitter
    participant RX as UART Receiver
    Note over TX,RX: Both idle HIGH (Logic 1)
    TX->>RX: Start Bit (LOW) - 1 bit time
    TX->>RX: D0 (LSB first) - bit 0
    TX->>RX: D1 - bit 1
    TX->>RX: D2 - bit 2
    TX->>RX: D3 - bit 3
    TX->>RX: D4 - bit 4
    TX->>RX: D5 - bit 5
    TX->>RX: D6 - bit 6
    TX->>RX: D7 (MSB) - bit 7
    TX->>RX: Parity Bit (optional)
    TX->>RX: Stop Bit (HIGH) - 1/1.5/2 bit times
    Note over TX,RX: Line returns to idle HIGH
```

## 4.2 SPI Master-Slave Multi-Device Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MASTER["Master MCU"]
    CLK["SCLK - Shared Clock Line"]
    MO["MOSI - Master Out Slave In"]
    MI["MISO - Master In Slave Out"]
    SS1["SS1 - Active LOW"]
    SS2["SS2 - Active LOW"]
    SS3["SS3 - Active LOW"]
    S1["Slave Device 1 - Flash Memory"]
    S2["Slave Device 2 - ADC"]
    S3["Slave Device 3 - LCD Display"]
    
    MASTER --> CLK
    MASTER --> MO
    MASTER --> SS1
    MASTER --> SS2
    MASTER --> SS3
    MI --> MASTER
    S1 --- CLK
    S1 --- MO
    S1 --- MI
    S1 --- SS1
    S2 --- CLK
    S2 --- MO
    S2 --- MI
    S2 --- SS2
    S3 --- CLK
    S3 --- MO
    S3 --- MI
    S3 --- SS3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The master shares SCLK, MOSI, and MISO with all slaves. Each slave has a **dedicated active-low SS line**. To talk to a specific slave, the master pulls that slave's SS LOW while keeping others HIGH.

## 4.3 I²C Bus Architecture with Multiple Slaves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Master 1 - MCU"]
    M2["Master 2 - DSP"]
    SCL["SCL - Serial Clock Line with Pull-up to VCC"]
    SDA["SDA - Serial Data Line with Pull-up to VCC"]
    A1["Address 0x50 - EEPROM"]
    A2["Address 0x68 - RTC DS1307"]
    A3["Address 0x27 - LCD PCF8574"]
    A4["Address 0x76 - Pressure Sensor BMP280"]
    
    M1 --- SCL
    M1 --- SDA
    M2 --- SCL
    M2 --- SDA
    A1 --- SCL
    A1 --- SDA
    A2 --- SCL
    A2 --- SDA
    A3 --- SCL
    A3 --- SDA
    A4 --- SCL
    A4 --- SDA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the pull-ups on both SCL and SDA lines** — this is mandatory for open-drain I²C. Both lines idle HIGH.

## 4.4 I²C Transaction Flow — Master Writes to Slave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A([Start Condition]) --> B[Send 7-bit Address]
    B --> C[Send R/W bit = 0 for Write]
    C --> D{Ack from Slave?}
    D -- NACK --> Z([Stop - Abort Transaction])
    D -- ACK --> E[Send Data Byte]
    E --> F{Ack from Slave?}
    F -- NACK --> Z
    F -- ACK --> G{More Data?}
    G -- Yes --> E
    G -- No --> H([Stop Condition])
```

## 4.5 SPI Mode Timing (Mode 0: CPOL=0, CPHA=0)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as Master SCLK Generation
    participant MOSI as MOSI Line
    participant MISO as MISO Line
    Note over Master: Clock IDLE = LOW (CPOL=0)
    Note over MOSI,MISO: Data sampled on RISING edge (CPHA=0)
    Master->>Master: SCLK goes HIGH
    Note right of MOSI: Data shifted out on rising edge
    Master->>Master: SCLK goes LOW
    Note right of MISO: Slave shifts out new bit
    Master->>Master: SCLK goes HIGH
    Note right of MOSI: Data sampled by slave
```

## 4.6 Comparative Functional Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASYNC["Asynchronous Family"]
        U1[UART]
        U2[USART]
    end
    subgraph SYNC["Synchronous Family"]
        S1[SPI]
        S2[I2C]
        S3[CAN - Advanced]
    end
    MCU["Microcontroller Core"] --> PERIPHERALS["External Peripherals"]
    U1 --> PERIPHERALS
    U2 --> PERIPHERALS
    S1 --> PERIPHERALS
    S2 --> PERIPHERALS
    S3 --> PERIPHERALS
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: UART Frame Format**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Draw and explain the UART frame format with 8 data bits, even parity, and 1 stop bit. Mark the start and stop bits.**

**Model Answer:**

A UART frame consists of the following sequential bits:

| Bit Position | Name | Logic Level | Duration |
|--------------|------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Start Bit | LOW (0) | 1 bit period |
| 2–9 | Data Bits (D0 → D7) | LSB first | 8 bit periods |
| 10 | Parity Bit | Even parity | 1 bit period |
| 11 | Stop Bit | HIGH (1) | 1 bit period |

**Total: 11 bits per frame.**

**Explanation:**
- The **start bit** (always 0) signals the receiver that data transmission has begun and synchronizes the receiver's internal sampling clock.
- The **8 data bits** are sent LSB-first (least significant bit first), which is a UART convention.
