- The **even parity bit** is set such that the total number of 1s in data + parity is **even**. (Example: if data = 10000011 (two 1s), parity = 0; if data = 10000010 (two 1s → even), parity = 0; if data = 10000011 (three 1s → odd), parity = 1.)
- The **stop bit** (always 1) marks the end of the frame and returns the line to its idle HIGH state.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1.5 marks for diagram, 1 mark for explanation, 0.5 marks for parity rule]**

---

### **Question 2: SPI vs I²C Comparison**
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**

**Q: List any six differences between SPI and I²C communication protocols.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | SPI | I²C |
|---|-----|-----|
| 1 | Uses **4 wires** (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) | Uses **2 wires** (SCL, SDA) |
| 2 | **Full-duplex** communication | **Half-duplex** communication |
| 3 | **No addressing** — slaves selected via hardware SS line | **7-bit/10-bit software addressing** |
| 4 | **No acknowledgement mechanism** | Built-in **ACK/NACK** after every byte |
| 5 | **Higher speed** (up to 50 MHz+) | **Lower speed** (max 3.4 MHz in HS mode) |
| 6 | **Single master** typically | Supports **multi-master** with arbitration |
| 7 | No pull-up resistors needed | Requires **pull-up resistors** on SDA & SCL |
| 8 | More GPIO pins consumed | Saves pins, ideal for many peripherals |

**[3 Marks: 0.5 per valid difference, 6 differences required]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **UART frame format**. Calculate the **TH1 value** for an 8051 system with an **11.0592 MHz crystal** to achieve a **baud rate of 19200 bps** with **SMOD = 0**. Verify the calculation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **I²C bus** with a neat block diagram. List the **start condition, stop condition, and acknowledge** procedure. Mention the significance of the **pull-up resistor**.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Diagram (1 mark):** *[Draw UART frame as shown in Section 4.1, with 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit]*

**UART Frame Format (2 marks):**

The UART transmits data asynchronously in frames. Each frame contains:
- **Start bit (1 bit, LOW)** — synchronizes the receiver.
- **Data bits (8 bits, LSB first)** — actual payload.
- **Parity bit (optional)** — error detection.
- **Stop bit (1 bit, HIGH)** — frame termination.

**Baud Rate Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

Substitute the values:

$$19200 = \frac{2^0 \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

Rearrange:

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \cdot 19200} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{7.3728 \times 10^6} = 1.5$$

Since Timer 1 in Mode 2 can only take integer values, we round to either 1 or 2:
- If $256 - \text{TH1} = 2$: BR = 14400 bps (significant error)
- If $256 - \text{TH1} = 1$: BR = 28800 bps (significant error)

**Conclusion:** With 11.0592 MHz crystal, **19200 baud cannot be generated exactly** in Mode 2; the closest standard achievable baud is **14400 or 28800**. A **double-buffered** approach or **different crystal** is required.

**Verification (1 mark):** Checking with TH1 = 0xFE (254): BR = 11,059,200 / (384 × 2) = 14,400 bps. The actual 19200 bps with 11.0592 MHz is not an integer divisor — common KTU trick question.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**I²C Block Diagram (2 marks):**

I²C uses two bidirectional open-drain lines: **SCL (clock)** and **SDA (data)**, both pulled up to VCC via resistors. A master MCU communicates with multiple slaves identified by unique 7-bit addresses.

**Start, Stop, and Acknowledge (3 marks):**

| Event | Condition | Description |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Start (S)** | SDA falls while SCL is HIGH | Initiates a transaction; bus becomes busy |
| **Stop (P)** | SDA rises while SCL is HIGH | Terminates the transaction; bus becomes free |
| **Acknowledge (ACK)** | Receiver pulls SDA LOW during 9th SCL pulse | Confirms byte received successfully |
| **NACK** | SDA remains HIGH during 9th SCL pulse | Signals no receiver / end of read |

**Pull-up Resistor Significance (2 marks):**

- I²C drivers are **open-drain** — they can only pull the line LOW or release it.
- A **pull-up resistor** to VCC ensures the line returns HIGH when no device is actively driving it.
- The resistor value is a **trade-off**: too small → high current draw; too large → slow rise time violates timing.
- Standard value: **4.7 kΩ** for 100 kHz at 3.3 V/5 V; **2.2 kΩ** for 400 kHz.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Resit Pool]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SPI protocol** in detail. With a timing diagram, describe the operation in **Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0)**. How is a specific slave selected in a multi-slave SPI setup?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **UART, SPI, and I²C** in terms of: number of lines, synchronization type, speed, addressing method, and typical applications. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**SPI Protocol (2 marks):**

SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) is a **synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave** protocol developed by Motorola. It is widely used for short-distance, high-speed communication between microcontrollers and peripherals like sensors, SD cards, and displays.

**Signal Lines (1 mark):**

- **SCLK** — Serial Clock, generated by master.
- **MOSI** — Master Out, Slave In (data: master → slave).
- **MISO** — Master In, Slave Out (data: slave → master).
- **SS/CS** — Slave Select / Chip Select (active LOW; one per slave).

**Mode 0 Timing (2 marks):**

In Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0):
- The clock is **LOW when idle**.
- Data is **sampled on the rising edge** of SCLK and shifted out on the falling edge.
- The master generates 8 clock pulses (for 8-bit data) and data appears on MOSI/MISO simultaneously.

*[Draw a timing diagram showing 8 SCLK pulses, MOSI shifting out MSB first, MISO shifting in, both on rising edges.]*

**Slave Selection (2 marks):**

In a multi-slave setup, each slave has a **dedicated active-low SS line** from the master:
- To communicate with Slave 1, the master pulls **SS1 = LOW**, keeps SS2 = HIGH, SS3 = HIGH.
- Slaves with SS = HIGH are deselected; their MISO lines go to **high-impedance**.
- Only the selected slave drives MISO and responds to SCLK/MOSI.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| **Number of Lines** | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) | 2 (SCL, SDA) |
| **Synchronization** | Asynchronous | Synchronous | Synchronous |
| **Speed** | Up to 1 Mbps | 10–50+ MHz | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 3.4 MHz |
| **Addressing** | None (point-to-point) | Hardware (SS pin) | Software (7/10-bit) |
| **Applications** | GPS, Bluetooth, Debug console | SD card, Flash, LCD, ADC | EEPROM, RTC, sensors |

**UART — Advantages & Disadvantages (1.33 marks):**
- **Advantages:** Simple hardware, no clock line needed, full-duplex, widely supported.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to 2 devices, baud-rate mismatch causes errors, no built-in addressing.

**SPI — Advantages & Disadvantages (1.33 marks):**
- **Advantages:** Very high speed, full-duplex, simple protocol, no addressing overhead.
- **Disadvantages:** Uses many pins (4+), no built-in ACK, limited slave count due to SS pin count.

**I²C — Advantages & Disadvantages (1.33 marks):**
- **Advantages:** Only 2 wires, supports many slaves, built-in ACK, multi-master capable.
- **Disadvantages:** Half-duplex, slower than SPI, open-drain requires pull-ups, software complexity (Start/Stop/Ack).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CPOL/CPHA:** Students often swap "sampling edge" with "shift-out edge." Remember: in **CPHA=0, data is sampled on the first edge**; in **CPHA=1, data is sampled on the second edge** of each clock pulse.
> 2. **Wrong bit order in UART:** Always **LSB first** for UART. Many students write MSB first — instant 0.5 mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting pull-ups in I²C diagrams:** An I²C diagram without **pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL** is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 4. **Confusing UART with USART:** USART = Universal **Synchronous/Asynchronous** Receiver Transmitter. UART is the **asynchronous subset** only.
> 5. **Wrong frame bit count:** A common mistake is counting only data bits, ignoring start/stop/parity. Always state **total bits per frame** explicitly.
> 6. **SPI SS pin logic:** Forgetting that **SS is active-LOW**. SS = 0 selects, SS = 1 deselects.
> 7. **I²C Start vs Stop:** Start = SDA **falls** while SCL is HIGH. Stop = SDA **rises** while SCL is HIGH. Drawing it backwards loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision — Communication Interface (Module 3):**

- **UART is ASYNCHRONOUS** — no clock line; both sides must agree on baud rate within ±2–3%.
- **UART frame order (memorize):** **Start (0) → Data (LSB first) → Parity (optional) → Stop (1)**.
- **8051 baud rate formula** uses Timer 1 in Mode 2 with crystal-derived divisor; TH1 = 256 − (f_osc / (384 × Baud)).
- **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred because it divides cleanly into standard baud rates (9600, 19200) — zero error.
- **SPI is SYNCHRONOUS + FULL-DUPLEX**, uses **4 wires** (minimum), and uses **dedicated SS lines per slave**.
- **SPI has 4 modes** based on CPOL and CPHA; **master and slave must match mode**.
- **SPI has no built-in acknowledgement** — relies on software to verify received data.
- **I²C uses only 2 wires** (SCL, SDA) but is **half-duplex** and slower than SPI.
- **I²C lines are open-drain → pull-up resistors are MANDATORY** (typically 4.7 kΩ).
- **I²C addressing** is **7-bit** (standard) or **10-bit** (extended); **8th bit is R/W**.
- **I²C Start = SDA falls while SCL HIGH**; **Stop = SDA rises while SCL HIGH**.
- **ACK = SDA LOW** on 9th clock; **NACK = SDA HIGH** on 9th clock.
- **I²C supports multi-master** with **wired-AND arbitration** — a sending 0 wins over 1.
- **UART →** Debug, GPS, Bluetooth; **SPI →** SD cards, Flash, high-speed sensors; **I²C →** EEPROMs, RTCs, low-speed sensor hubs.
- **Total UART bits per frame** = 1 (Start) + Data bits + Parity (0 or 1) + Stop bits (1, 1.5, or 2).
- **Effective data rate** for UART 8N1 at 115200 baud = 92,160 bps (80% efficiency).
- **For KTU derivations:** Always show step-by-step substitution, isolate the unknown, and verify the result.
- **Memorize the comparison table** — comparative questions appear in nearly every KTU ESE paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Firmware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Embedded Firmware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Embedded Firmware?

**Embedded Firmware** is the pre-written, low-level software instruction set that is permanently stored in the read-only memory (ROM), flash memory, or EEPROM of an embedded device. It is the "soul" of the embedded system — a tightly coupled, hardware-specific program that directly controls the behaviour, timing, and peripheral interaction of the microcontroller or microprocessor without the abstraction layers of a general-purpose Operating System (OS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Embedded Firmware refers to the machine-readable instructions and associated data structures, residing in non-volatile memory of an embedded system, that orchestrate hardware peripherals, manage real-time tasks, and implement the device's functional logic. Unlike application software, firmware is tightly bound to a specific hardware target and is typically updated infrequently.*

The word **firmware** itself was coined by **Ascher Opler** in **1967** to describe the intermediate nature of software — softer than hardware (which is fixed) but firmer than application software (which is fully flexible).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Embedded Firmware as the Head Chef's Recipe Book**
> 
> Imagine a fully automated robotic restaurant:
> - The **hardware** (ovens, stoves, robotic arms) is the *kitchen equipment*.
> - The **firmware** is the *permanent recipe book written on a copper plate* bolted to the kitchen wall. It contains step-by-step instructions: "When the timer reaches 350 °C, rotate the motor 90° CW."
> - The **application data / user input** is the *customer's order* that comes in dynamically.
> - You can occasionally *update the recipe book* (firmware update), but it is rarely changed and never re-written at runtime like a normal software program.

Just as the head chef follows recipes verbatim to produce predictable meals, the CPU fetches firmware instructions verbatim from ROM/Flash to produce deterministic hardware behaviour — this is the **determinism** property critical in real-time embedded systems.

### 1.3 Firmware vs. Software — Key Distinctions

| Property | Embedded Firmware | General Application Software |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | ROM, Flash, EEPROM (non-volatile) | HDD, SSD (volatile storage) |
| **Execution** | Bare-metal or RTOS-hosted | Hosted on a full OS (Windows/Linux) |
| **Update Frequency** | Very low (months/years) | Frequent (days/weeks) |
| **Hardware Dependency** | Tightly bound to MCU/MPU | Hardware-agnostic |
| **Resource Constraints** | Extreme (KB-level RAM, MHz CPU) | Relaxed (GB RAM, multi-GHz CPU) |
| **Determinism** | Hard real-time required | Soft real-time / best-effort |
| **User Visibility** | Invisible to end-user | Direct user interaction |

### 1.4 Core Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Typical embedded firmware size: **1 KB – 4 MB** depending on complexity.
> - Typical MCU clock frequency: **8 MHz – 600 MHz** for the systems covered in PCRAT302.
> - Firmware execution is **instruction-cycle bound**, not OS-scheduler bound.
> - The standard **boot vector** location for ARM Cortex-M is **0x0000 0000** (initial MSP) followed by **0x0000 0004** (Reset_Handler address).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Firmware Stack — Layered Architecture

Embedded firmware is conventionally organized into a **3-tier architectural stack** for modularity and portability:

### Tier-1: Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
- The lowest firmware layer.
- Provides **register-level definitions**, bit masks, and inline functions to manipulate peripherals.
- Examples: `GPIO_SetPin(PORTB, PIN5)`, `UART_Init(9600)`.
- **Why it exists:** It insulates higher layers from MCU-specific register maps. A change in MCU (e.g., PIC to ARM) only requires re-writing HAL — the rest of the firmware remains intact.

### Tier-2: Board Support Package (BSP) / Middleware
- Contains **device drivers** (LCD, EEPROM, sensors, motor drivers).
- Includes **protocol stacks** (I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, USB).
- Implements **RTOS services** (task scheduling, semaphores, queues) if used.
- This is the "translation layer" between raw silicon and meaningful application logic.

### Tier-3: Application Layer
- The **business logic** of the embedded product.
- Implements the actual product behaviour — e.g., anti-lock braking algorithm, washing machine drum sequence, IoT sensor sampling logic.
- Calls HAL/BSP APIs and never touches raw registers.

## 2.2 Firmware Design Approaches

There are three classical design paradigms used in PCRAT302:

| Approach | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Super Loop (Polled)** | Single `while(1)` infinite loop calling subroutines sequentially | Simple, deterministic, zero overhead | Cannot handle concurrent events, scales poorly |
| **Interrupt-Driven (Foreground/Background)** | Background super-loop + ISR foreground | Responsive to asynchronous events | ISR complexity, race conditions |
| **RTOS-Based** | Multi-tasking using a Real-Time OS kernel (FreeRTOS, etc.) | Modular, scalable, supports concurrency | Higher memory footprint, scheduling overhead |

## 2.3 The Firmware Build Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline:** Source Code → Preprocessor → Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Locator → Object/Binary Image → Flash Loader

The build process transforms human-readable Embedded C/Assembly into a **hexadecimal image** (.hex, .bin, .elf) that the programmer hardware writes into the target's non-volatile memory.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instruction cycle time | $T_{cy} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | $T_{cy}$ in seconds, $f_{clk}$ in Hz |
| 2 | Loop execution time | $T_{loop} = N \times T_{cy}$ | $N$ = instruction count per iteration |
| 3 | Timer reload value (16-bit) | $Reload = 2^n - \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{cy} \times Prescaler}$ | $n = 16$ for 16-bit timer |
| 4 | Baud rate divisor | $BRD = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{16 \times Baud}$ | UART baud rate generator |
| 5 | Flash endurance | $N_{cycles} \leq 10^5$ writes | Typical NOR Flash specification |
| 6 | Polling CPU load | $Load = \dfrac{\sum T_{task}}{T_{period}}$ | Expressed as percentage (0–100 %) |
| 7 | Worst-case interrupt latency | $t_{latency} = t_{current} + t_{save} + t_{vector}$ | Sum of current instruction + context save + vector fetch |
| 8 | Memory footprint (Flash) | $Size_{firmware} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Sum of all .text, .rodata, .data sections |
| 9 | RAM static usage | $RAM_{static} = Stack + Heap + .bss + .data$ | Bytes |
| 10 | RTOS context switch time | $t_{cs} = 2 \times t_{save} + t_{scheduler}$ | CPU cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Formula 1–3 are essential for **timer programming**. Formula 4 is critical for **UART configuration** in KEIL/STM32Cube. Formula 6 determines whether a super-loop design will meet real-time deadlines — if $Load > 70\%$, switch to RTOS or interrupt-driven design.

## 2.5 Firmware Update Mechanisms

Embedded systems use three primary firmware update strategies:

1. **In-Circuit Programming (ISP):** Uses a bootloader accessible via UART/USB/SD card. Common in field-deployed devices.
2. **In-Application Programming (IAP):** The running firmware writes a new image to a separate Flash bank and triggers a bootloader swap. Enables **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** in IoT.
3. **JTAG/SWD Programming:** Used in development/production; not user-accessible.

> [!WARNING]
> A robust firmware update mechanism **must** include: a verified boot image, dual-bank Flash (A/B partitioning), CRC/checksum validation, and a **rollback path** in case the new image fails to boot — otherwise a "bricked" device results.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Timer 1 Reload Value Calculation for PIC/AVR

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Type):** Configure Timer 1 of an ARM Cortex-M0 running at **$f_{clk} = 8 \text{ MHz}$** to generate an interrupt every **$T_{desired} = 1 \text{ ms}$** using a **16-bit timer** with a prescaler of **1**. Compute the reload value.

### Step 1 — Compute the instruction cycle time

The Cortex-M0 core takes 1 clock cycle per instruction, so:

$$T_{cy} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} = 0.125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 125 \text{ ns}$$

This is the time taken for **one timer tick** when the prescaler is 1.

### Step 2 — Compute the number of timer counts needed

The total number of timer increments required to elapse the desired interval is:

$$N_{counts} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{cy} \times Prescaler}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N_{counts} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{125 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \times 1} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.25 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$N_{counts} = 8000 \text{ ticks}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the 16-bit reload formula

For a 16-bit up-counter that overflows at $2^{16} = 65536$, the reload value $R$ is:

$$R = 2^{16} - N_{counts} = 65536 - 8000 = 57536$$

### Step 4 — Convert to hex for register programming

$$R = 57536_{10} = \text{E0C0}_{16}$$

Thus the timer registers must be loaded as:

$$\text{TMR1H} = \text{E0}_{16}, \quad \text{TMR1L} = \text{C0}_{16}$$

### Step 5 — Verification of interrupt frequency

The timer overflows every $N_{counts} = 8000$ ticks, so the interrupt period is:

$$T_{overflow} = 8000 \times 125 \text{ ns} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} \checkmark$$

The interrupt frequency is:

$$f_{int} = \frac{1}{T_{overflow}} = 1 \text{ kHz} \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Firmware Design Decision Tree

| Engineering Requirement | Recommended Firmware Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single sensor sampling every 100 ms | **Super Loop** | Trivial logic, no concurrency, minimum RAM |
| UART RX + button press + LED blink | **Interrupt-Driven (Foreground/Background)** | Async events, simple response needed |
| IoT node with Wi-Fi, MQTT, sensors, OTA | **RTOS-Based (FreeRTOS)** | Multiple concurrent tasks, modular code |
| Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) | **Bare-metal + ISR (hard real-time)** | Microsecond deadlines, certification-grade determinism |
| Wearable health monitor with BLE | **RTOS (e.g., Zephyr/MyNewt)** | Power management + multi-protocol stack |
| Washing machine controller | **Super Loop + state machine** | Sequential phase transitions, human-scale timing |

## 3.3 Derivation: Memory Footprint Estimation

For a typical embedded C program, the total Flash footprint is:

$$Size_{firmware} = S_{text} + S_{rodata} + S_{data}$$

where:
- $S_{text}$ = size of executable machine code (instructions)
- $S_{rodata}$ = size of constants, string literals
- $S_{data}$ = size of initialized global variables

The RAM usage is:

$$RAM_{total} = S_{data} + S_{bss} + S_{stack} + S_{heap}$$

**Example:** A PIC18F4550 project compiled with XC8 produces:
- .text = 12 KB, .rodata = 2 KB, .data = 256 B
- .bss = 512 B, stack = 256 B, heap = 0 (no `malloc`)

$$\text{Flash used} = 12\text{KB} + 2\text{KB} + 256\text{B} = 14.25 \text{ KB (out of 32 KB available)}$$

$$\text{RAM used} = 256\text{B} + 512\text{B} + 256\text{B} = 1024 \text{ B (out of 2 KB)}$$

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{14.25}{32} \times 100\% \approx 44.5\%$$

This must remain under **~80%** to leave room for OTA update staging.

## 3.4 Bootloader Vector Table Derivation

In ARM Cortex-M, the first 8 bytes of Flash contain the **initial Main Stack Pointer** and **Reset_Handler** address:

$$\text{Address 0x00000000} \rightarrow \text{Initial MSP value}$$
$$\text{Address 0x00000004} \rightarrow \text{Reset\_Handler entry point}$$

The vector table contains $N$ entries, each 4 bytes long:

$$Size_{vector} = (N + 16) \text{ bytes (aligned to 128-byte boundary for Cortex-M0)}$$

For 32 IRQ sources, the vector table occupies $48 \times 4 = 192$ bytes, padded to **256 bytes** alignment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Embedded Firmware System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer - Business Logic] --> B[BSP - Middleware - Drivers - Protocol Stacks]
    B --> C[HAL - Hardware Abstraction Layer]
    C --> D[MCU Registers - GPIO - UART - TIMERS - ADC - I2C]
    D --> E[Physical Hardware - Silicon Peripherals]

    F[Build Tools - Compiler - Assembler - Linker] --> A
    G[Flash Programmer - JTAG - SWD - UART ISP] --> D
    H[RTOS Kernel - Scheduler - Tasks - Queues] --> B
    I[Bootloader - Primary - Secondary] --> D
    J[OTA Update Module - Wi-Fi - MQTT] --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **firmware stack layered on top of hardware**. Notice that the RTOS Kernel is an *optional middleware component*; bare-metal systems bypass it entirely. The build tools feed the application, while the Flash programmer writes the final image into non-volatile memory.

## 4.2 Firmware Development Lifecycle Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Step 1 - Requirements and Specs] --> A2[Step 2 - Hardware Bring-Up and Datasheet Study]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 - Peripheral Driver Development - HAL]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 - BSP and Middleware Integration]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 - Application Logic - State Machine or RTOS Tasks]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 - On-Target Debugging - SWD - JTAG - Breakpoints]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 - Unit and Integration Testing]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 - Power and Memory Profiling]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9 - Field Deployment and OTA Path Planning]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10 - Maintenance and Versioning]
```

## 4.3 Super-Loop vs. RTOS — Concurrency Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SL[Super Loop Model]
        SL1[init - HAL Init] --> SL2[Task 1 - Read Sensor]
        SL2 --> SL3[Task 2 - Update LCD]
        SL3 --> SL4[Task 3 - Check Button]
        SL4 --> SL2
    end

    subgraph RT[RTOS Model]
        RT1[Task A - Sensor - Priority 2] --> RTQ[Ready Queue]
        RT2[Task B - LCD - Priority 1] --> RTQ
        RT3[Task C - Comm - Priority 3] --> RTQ
        RTQ --> RTS[Scheduler - Preemptive]
        RTS --> RT1
        RTS --> RT2
        RTS --> RT3
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Super Loop** is a single sequential thread of execution — CPU load is $100\%$ in tasks combined but context switches are zero. The **RTOS** pre-emptively switches between tasks based on priority, with each task appearing to run concurrently.

## 4.4 Firmware Update (OTA) Boot Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Power-On Reset] --> B2{Boot Pin - Bank Select}
    B2 -->|Bank A Valid| B3[Load Bank A Firmware]
    B2 -->|Bank A Invalid| B4{Bank B Valid}
    B4 -->|Yes| B5[Load Bank B - Rollback]
    B4 -->|No| B6[Recovery Mode - Wait for ISP]
    B3 --> B7[Run Application]
    B5 --> B7
    B6 --> B8[Accept New Image via UART]
    B8 --> B9[Write to Inactive Bank]
    B9 --> B10[Swap Bank Pointer]
    B10 --> B2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Embedded Firmware. How is it different from application software?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
Embedded firmware is the low-level software permanently stored in non-volatile memory (ROM/Flash/EEPROM) of an embedded device, designed to directly control hardware peripherals with hardware-specific instructions and minimal abstraction. It is tightly coupled to the target hardware, requires deterministic real-time behaviour, and operates under strict resource constraints (KB-level memory, MHz CPU).

**Differences from application software:**

| Parameter | Firmware | Application Software |
|---|---|---|
| Execution platform | Bare-metal / RTOS | Hosted OS (Windows/Linux) |
| Hardware binding | Tightly bound | Hardware-agnostic |
| Update cycle | Rare (months/years) | Frequent (days/weeks) |
| Resource budget | Severely limited | Abundant |
| Determinism | Hard real-time | Best-effort |
| User visibility | Transparent | Direct UI interaction |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for tabular differentiation)*

---

### Q2. List any three firmware design approaches with one advantage and one limitation each.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Super Loop / Polled Architecture:** Simple structure using a single `while(1)` infinite loop. *Advantage:* Zero overhead and deterministic timing. *Limitation:* Cannot handle concurrent events; CPU is fully occupied even when idle.

2. **Interrupt-Driven (Foreground/Background):** Background main loop + ISR foreground. *Advantage:* Responsive to asynchronous events. *Limitation:* ISR complexity, race conditions, and stack overflow risks.

3. **RTOS-Based Architecture:** Uses a real-time OS kernel for multi-tasking. *Advantage:* Modular, scalable, supports true concurrency. *Limitation:* Higher memory footprint and scheduler overhead.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per approach with its trade-off)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose marks** by confusing firmware with software in definitions.
> - Always **state units** when computing timer reload values (e.g., $f_{clk} = 8 \text{ MHz}$, not just "8M").
> - In RTOS questions, **never say "OS" alone** — always specify *Real-Time Operating System* and the scheduling policy.
> - In bootloader/OTA questions, **explicitly mention CRC validation and rollback** — this is a 2-mark differentiator.
> - Diagrams (even simple flowcharts) carry 2–3 marks in design questions — always draw one.

---

### ✅ CHOICE 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) Explain the three-tier firmware architecture (HAL, BSP, Application) with suitable examples. Why is HAL critical for firmware portability? (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

The embedded firmware is conventionally organized as a **three-tier layered stack** to enforce modularity, reusability, and hardware portability.

**Tier 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):**
This is the lowest firmware layer, providing inline functions, macros, and register definitions for direct peripheral manipulation. Example for STM32:

```c
// HAL-level GPIO toggle (illustrative pseudocode)
HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5);
```

The HAL converts raw register operations like `GPIOA->ODR ^= (1<<5)` into a readable, function-call interface.

**Tier 2 — Board Support Package (BSP) / Middleware:**
The BSP contains complete device drivers and protocol stacks. It uses the HAL primitives to build higher-level operations. Examples include `LCD_WriteString("Hello")`, `EEPROM_ReadByte(0x50)`, or `I2C_Master_Transmit(0x68, buf, len)`. Protocol stacks like TCP/IP, MQTT, and USB also reside here.

**Tier 3 — Application Layer:**
The application layer holds the product's business logic — the actual functional behaviour. It exclusively calls BSP APIs. Example:

```c
// Application calls BSP API
if (bsp_button_is_pressed(BUTTON_OK)) {
    lcd_display_menu();
    sensor_value = bsp_read_temperature();
}
```

**Why HAL is critical for portability:**
- **Code reusability:** The same application code can be ported to a different MCU family (e.g., PIC → ARM) by simply swapping the HAL and BSP layers.
- **Faster time-to-market:** Engineers don't repeatedly write register-level code for every new project.
- **Maintainability:** Bug fixes and silicon errata are addressed only in HAL, leaving the upper layers untouched.
- **Vendor support:** Major vendors (STMicroelectronics, NXP, Microchip) provide certified HALs that handle complex peripheral intricacies (DMA, interrupts, timing).

*(7 marks: 2 for explaining each layer + 1 for example + 2 for HAL importance)*

---

#### Q.A (b) With a neat diagram, explain the firmware build process from source code to hex image. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

The firmware build process converts human-readable source code into a binary image that can be programmed into the target MCU's non-volatile memory. The pipeline consists of the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Preprocessing:**
Handles `#include`, `#define`, and macro expansions. Output: Translation unit (`.i` file).

**Stage 2 — Compilation:**
The preprocessed C/Assembly code is translated into MCU-specific assembly instructions. Output: Assembly file (`.s`).

**Stage 3 — Assembly:**
The assembler converts assembly mnemonics into **relocatable object code** (machine code with unresolved addresses). Output: `.o` or `.obj` files.

**Stage 4 — Linking:**
The linker combines multiple object files and library code (`libc.a`, `libm.a`) into a single **relocatable executable**, resolving external symbol references.

**Stage 5 — Locator (Linker Script Processing):**
The locator assigns **absolute physical addresses** to code/data sections based on the target's memory map (Flash, RAM addresses from the datasheet). Output: ELF/Dwarf debug file.

**Stage 6 — Object-to-Hex Conversion:**
The `objcopy` tool converts the ELF file into **Intel HEX (.hex)** or **binary (.bin)** format compatible with flash programmers.

```
   ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐
   │ Source .c  │    │ Preproc .i │    │ Compiler   │    │ Assembler  │
   │ + .h files │──▶ │ Translation│──▶ │  .s file   │──▶ │ .o relocat │
   └────────────┘    └────────────┘    └────────────┘    └────────────┘
                                                                │
        ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐      │
        │ Flash Prog │    │ objcopy    │    │ Linker +   │◀─────┘
        │ .hex/.bin  │◀── │ .hex/.bin  │◀── │ Locator    │
        └────────────┘    └────────────┘    └────────────┘
                                              ▲
                                              │
                                       Library .a files
```

**Final Output:** A `.hex` file is fed to a programmer (JTAG/SWD/UART) which writes the image byte-by-byte into the target MCU's Flash memory.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per stage for first 5 stages + 1 for diagram + 1 for final programmer step)*

---

### ✅ CHOICE 2 — Question B (14 Marks) *(Alternative Selection)*

#### Q.B (a) Compare Super Loop, Interrupt-Driven, and RTOS firmware architectures. For an IoT wearable device with BLE, multiple sensors, and OTA updates, justify the best choice. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Super Loop | Interrupt-Driven | RTOS-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concurrency | None (sequential) | Limited (event-driven) | Full (preemptive multi-tasking) |
| Code Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| RAM Footprint | Minimal (~100 B) | Moderate (~1 KB) | High (~4–16 KB) |
| Determinism | Strong | Good for periodic events | Depends on scheduler policy |
| Power Efficiency | Poor (busy-wait) | Good (sleep in idle) | Excellent (tickless idle) |
| OTA Capable | Difficult | Possible | Native support |
| Best For | Simple controls | Periodic sampling | Multi-protocol, complex apps |

**Justification for IoT Wearable with BLE + Sensors + OTA:**

The optimal architecture is **RTOS-based (e.g., FreeRTOS or Zephyr)** for the following reasons:

1. **Concurrency:** The device must simultaneously sample multiple sensors (heart rate, accelerometer, temperature), manage BLE communication, handle button events, and execute an OTA update — none of which fit in a single super loop.
2. **Power Management:** An RTOS supports **tickless idle mode** — the CPU sleeps when no task is runnable, only waking on interrupt or task deadline. Critical for battery-powered wearables.
3. **Modular OTA:** A/B dual-bank firmware updates with rollback require a dedicated bootloader task + main application task running in parallel.
4. **Priority Pre-emption:** A safety-critical sensor (e.g., fall detection) can be assigned **higher priority** to pre-empt BLE transmission, ensuring the hard real-time constraint is met.
5. **Stack maturity:** FreeRTOS, Zephyr, and MyNewt are all open-source, vendor-supported, and certified for use in medical/wearable devices (IEC 62304).

The super loop is rejected because CPU would be 100% busy. Interrupt-driven is rejected because managing BLE, OTA, and 4+ sensors via ISRs would create an unmaintainable callback spaghetti.

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison table + 5 for IoT justification with 5 specific reasons)*

---

#### Q.B (b) Explain the role of a bootloader in embedded firmware. Describe the OTA firmware update procedure with bank-swap rollback. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of a Bootloader:**
A bootloader is a small, trusted firmware program that executes immediately after reset. Its primary roles are:

1. **Initialize hardware:** Set up clocks, watchdog, and basic peripherals.
2. **Validate the main application image** using a CRC32/SHA-256 checksum stored in Flash.
3. **Select which bank to boot** (Bank A or Bank B) in dual-image systems.
4. **Provide an In-System Programming (ISP) path** to accept new firmware via UART/USB/SD card.
5. **Jump to the main application** by loading the application's stack pointer and reset handler from the vector table.

**OTA Firmware Update Procedure with A/B Bank-Swap Rollback:**

```
Step 1:  New firmware image is downloaded (via Wi-Fi/BLE) into the
         INACTIVE bank (e.g., Bank B) while Bank A continues running.

Step 2:  Bootloader verifies the new image:
         - CRC32 checksum validation
         - Digital signature verification (optional, for security)

Step 3:  Bootloader writes a "pending swap" flag in a dedicated Flash
         sector and resets the device.

Step 4:  On next boot, bootloader detects the swap flag, marks Bank A
         as the backup, and jumps to Bank B.

Step 5:  Application in Bank B runs a self-test. If successful, it
         confirms the new image and erases Bank A for future updates.

Step 6:  ROLLBACK PATH: If Bank B fails to boot (watchdog reset,
         missing vector table, CRC error), the bootloader detects
         the failure via a "boot counter" and automatically falls
         back to the last known-good Bank A image.
```

**Key advantages of this approach:**
- The device is **never bricked** — a working image always exists.
- The user experiences **zero downtime** — update happens silently in the inactive bank.
- Field updates can be **rolled back** if bugs are discovered post-deployment.

**Memory requirement:** The Flash must be partitioned as `Bank A (50%) | Bank B (50%) | Bootloader (16 KB) | Config Sector (4 KB)`.

*(7 marks: 1 for bootloader role + 4 for OTA procedure + 2 for rollback explanation)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Firmware** = software stored in non-volatile memory that tightly controls hardware. Coined by *Ascher Opler in 1967*.
- ✅ The **3-tier firmware stack** is **HAL → BSP/Middleware → Application**.
- ✅ Three design paradigms: **Super Loop**, **Interrupt-Driven**, and **RTOS-Based**.
- ✅ Build pipeline: **Preprocess → Compile → Assemble → Link → Locate → objcopy → Flash**.
- ✅ For a 16-bit timer: $Reload = 65536 - N_{counts}$ where $N_{counts} = T_{desired} / T_{cy}$.
- ✅ UART baud divisor: $BRD = f_{clk} / (16 \times Baud)$.
- ✅ ARM Cortex-M vector table starts at **0x00000000** (MSP) and **0x00000004** (Reset_Handler).
- ✅ Flash endurance is typically **$10^5$ write cycles** — firmware updates must use wear-levelling.
- ✅ OTA updates require **dual-bank A/B partitioning** with **CRC validation** and **rollback**.
- ✅ RTOS is mandatory for **multi-tasking IoT nodes**; super loop suffices for trivial controls.
- ✅ **CPU load** for super loop must be **< 70%** to allow headroom for ISRs and exceptions.
- ✅ Always mention **units** in derivations — examiners award marks for correct units.
- ✅ Drawing a **diagram** in design questions can fetch **2–3 extra marks** even if the explanation is brief.
- ✅ Firmware is **hardware-bound**; application software is **hardware-agnostic** — this distinction is a favourite 2-mark question.
- ✅ The **bootloader** is the first code to run; it validates, swaps, and jumps to the main app.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other System Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other System Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Other System Components" in Embedded Systems

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems*, the topic **"Other System Components"** refers to the suite of **on-chip and off-chip peripherals** integrated into or interfaced with a modern microcontroller (like the 8051, PIC, MSP430, or ARM Cortex-M family) that complement the **CPU, Memory, and basic I/O** to enable real-world embedded applications.

These components are the **"muscles and senses"** of the embedded system — they convert real-world analog signals into digital form, generate precise timing waveforms, protect the system from faults, and manage power consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):** *Other System Components* includes the architectural and functional study of **ADC, DAC, PWM generation, Watchdog Timer, and Power Management / Sleep modes** in microcontrollers.

The major building blocks covered are:

| # | Component | Primary Function |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter) | Converts real-world analog signals → digital data |
| 2 | **DAC** (Digital-to-Analog Converter) | Converts digital data → analog output signals |
| 3 | **PWM** (Pulse Width Modulation) | Generates variable-duty-cycle digital pulses for power/control |
| 4 | **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** | Resets the system if software becomes unresponsive |
| 5 | **Power Management Unit (PMU)** | Reduces power via Sleep / Idle / Power-down modes |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body:**
> - The **CPU** is the **brain** (decision-making).
> - **Memory (RAM/ROM)** is the **memory** of the brain.
> - **ADC** is the **senses** (eyes, ears, skin) — converts light/sound/pressure (analog) into nerve impulses (digital).
> - **DAC** is the **vocal cords / muscles** — converts thought (digital) into speech/motion (analog).
> - **PWM** is like **blinking a flashlight fast** to dim a light — average intensity is controlled.
> - **Watchdog Timer** is a **nurse** — if the patient (software) stops responding, the nurse calls for resuscitation (reset).
> - **Sleep Modes** are like **hibernation** — the body shuts down non-essential systems to conserve energy.

This analogy makes it intuitive: every peripheral serves a specific biological/physical equivalent in an embedded system.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **ADC Resolution** is measured in **bits** (commonly **8, 10, 12, 16 bits**). Higher bits ⇒ finer voltage granularity.
- **ADC Step Size (LSB):** $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ where $n$ = number of bits.
- **DAC Settling Time:** typically **1 µs to 10 µs**.
- **WDT Time-out:** typically **a few milliseconds to seconds**, depending on clock source.
- **Power modes:** Active current ≈ **mA**, Sleep current ≈ **µA**, Power-down ≈ **nA**.
- **PWM Frequency Range:** typically **1 kHz to 1 MHz** depending on application.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)

### 2.1.1 Operational Theory

An ADC samples a continuous analog voltage and produces a discrete binary code.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- The analog input $V_{in}$ is compared against a reference voltage $V_{ref}$.
- The ADC produces a digital output $D$ such that:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$$
- The smallest distinguishable voltage change is the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**:
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$
- Quantization Error (always present) is bounded by:
$$-\frac{V_{LSB}}{2} \le Q_e \le +\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}$$

### 2.1.2 Types of ADC (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Resolution | Speed | Use Case |
|------|------------|-------|----------|
| **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | 8–16 bit | Medium (µs) | Most MCUs (8051, PIC, MSP430) |
| **Flash ADC** | 6–8 bit | Very fast (ns) | Oscilloscopes, video |
| **Dual-Slope / Integrating** | 16–24 bit | Slow (ms) | Digital voltmeters |
| **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** | 16–24 bit | Slow–Medium | Audio, precision sensors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is the **most commonly used** in microcontrollers. It uses a binary search algorithm to find the digital code in exactly **$n$ clock cycles** for an $n$-bit ADC.

---

## 2.2 Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)

### 2.2.1 Operational Theory

A DAC reconstructs an analog voltage from a digital input code.

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{D}{2^n}$$

Where:
- $D$ = digital input code (decimal value 0 to $2^n - 1$)
- $n$ = number of bits
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage

### 2.2.2 Common DAC Architectures

- **R-2R Ladder DAC** — uses only two resistor values, easy to fabricate in ICs.
- **Weighted Resistor DAC** — uses resistors of values $R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$.
- **PWM-based DAC** — uses a low-pass filter on PWM output (very common in MCUs without a true DAC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** DACs are used in **function generators, audio playback, motor speed control, programmable voltage sources, and waveform synthesis**.

---

## 2.3 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

### 2.3.1 Operational Theory

A PWM signal is a digital waveform with a fixed period $T$ but variable pulse width (duty cycle).

$$D_{cycle} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$

The **average DC value** delivered to a load is:

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D_{cycle}$$

### 2.3.2 PWM Generation Modes

| Mode | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Fast PWM** | Counter counts up, clears on match — high frequency |
| **Phase-Correct PWM** | Counter counts up then down — symmetric, less harmonic distortion |
| **Phase-Frequency PWM** | Variable frequency, fixed duty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** PWM is used to **emulate a DAC** when no hardware DAC exists — by passing the PWM through an **RC low-pass filter**, the average DC voltage can be controlled digitally.

---

## 2.4 Watchdog Timer (WDT)

### 2.4.1 Operational Theory

The Watchdog Timer is a hardware timer that **resets the microcontroller** if the main program fails to periodically "kick" or "service" it.

- The WDT runs off an **independent clock source** (often internal RC oscillator ≈ **32 kHz** to **1 MHz**).
- The main program must write a specific value (e.g., `0xAAAA` then `0x5555` to WDT register in MSP430) before the timer expires.
- If software hangs → WDT expires → **system reset (POR or BOR)**.

**WDT Time-out Equation:**

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{N_{divider} \times Count_{max}}{f_{WDT\_clock}}$$

### 2.4.2 Why a WDT?

- Protects against **software bugs, infinite loops, EMI-induced lockups, deadlocks**.
- Mandatory in **automotive (ISO 26262), medical, and industrial** systems.

---

## 2.5 Power Management / Sleep Modes

### 2.5.1 Operational Theory

Modern MCUs support multiple low-power modes to conserve energy in battery-powered applications.

| Mode | CPU | Peripherals | Clock | Typical Current |
|------|-----|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| **Active** | On | On | Main | ~1–10 mA |
| **Idle (Sleep)** | Off | On | Main | ~100 µA |
| **Power-down (Standby)** | Off | Off | LFXT or none | ~1 µA – nA |
| **Off / Hibernate** | Off | Off | None | ~nA |

The **wake-up source** can be:
- External interrupt (GPIO edge)
- Watchdog timer overflow
- RTC alarm
- Brown-out reset
- Communication peripheral (e.g., UART start bit)

**Energy Saved Equation:**

$$E_{saved} = (I_{active} - I_{sleep}) \times V_{DD} \times T_{sleep}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sleep modes are **critical** for **IoT wearables, wireless sensor nodes, biomedical implants (pacemakers)**, where battery life of years is required.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | ADC step size | Volts |
| $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | ADC digital output | Integer |
| $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | DAC output voltage | Volts |
| $D_{cycle} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100$ | PWM duty cycle | % |
| $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D_{cycle}$ | PWM average DC | Volts |
| $T_{WDT} = \dfrac{N_{div} \times Count_{max}}{f_{clk}}$ | Watchdog time-out | Seconds |
| $E_{saved} = (I_a - I_s) \cdot V_{DD} \cdot t$ | Energy saved in sleep | Joules |
| SNR $= 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | Ideal ADC SNR (n bits) | dB |
| $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (TOP + 1)}$ | PWM frequency | Hz |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where are these components used?**
> - **ADC**: Smart agriculture (soil moisture), ECG machines, temperature logging.
> - **DAC**: Audio amplifiers, motor controllers (BLDC), function generators.
> - **PWM**: LED dimming, servo motor control, SMPS, DC motor speed.
> - **WDT**: Automotive ECUs, anti-lock braking, flight controllers.
> - **Power Management**: Smartwatches, RFID tags, IoT battery nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Quantization & Resolution

### Given:
- Reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V
- Resolution $n = 10$ bits (a common 10-bit SAR ADC)

### Step 1: Compute the LSB (Step Size)

The smallest voltage the ADC can distinguish:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

Substituting $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V and $n = 10$:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024}$$

$$V_{LSB} = 0.0048828125 \text{ V} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 2: Compute Digital Output for a Given Input

Suppose $V_{in} = 2.5$ V (half of $V_{ref}$).

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2.5}{0.0048828125} \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \lfloor 512.0 \rfloor = 512$$

In binary (10-bit): $D = 10\ 0000\ 0000$

### Step 3: Compute Quantization Error

The maximum quantization error is:

$$Q_e = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \frac{4.88 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 4: Compute Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for an Ideal n-bit ADC

$$\text{SNR}_{ideal} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

For $n = 10$:

$$\text{SNR} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DAC Output Voltage

### Given:
- 8-bit DAC
- $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V
- Digital input $D = 0b10110010 = 178_{10}$

### Step 1: Compute the Output Voltage

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{D}{2^n}$$

$$V_{out} = 3.3 \times \frac{178}{2^8} = 3.3 \times \frac{178}{256}$$

$$V_{out} = 3.3 \times 0.6953125 = 2.2945 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2: Compute Full-Scale and Resolution

- **Full-scale output** ($D = 2^n - 1 = 255$):
$$V_{FS} = 3.3 \times \frac{255}{256} = 3.2871 \text{ V}$$

- **Resolution (1 LSB):**
$$V_{LSB(DAC)} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{256} = 0.01289 \text{ V} \approx 12.89 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PWM Average Voltage & Frequency

### Given:
- MCU clock $f_{clk} = 16$ MHz
- Timer prescaler $N = 64$
- Timer TOP value $= 249$ (so it counts 0 to 249)
- Desired PWM period $T_{PWM}$

### Step 1: PWM Frequency

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (TOP + 1)}$$

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{64 \cdot (249 + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{64 \times 250} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16000} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

So $T_{PWM} = 1 / 1000 = 1$ ms.

### Step 2: For a Desired Duty Cycle $D_{cycle} = 60\%$

$$T_{on} = D_{cycle} \times T_{PWM} = 0.60 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 0.6 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: Average Voltage Across the Load (with $V_{CC} = 5$ V)

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D_{cycle} = 5 \times 0.60 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Watchdog Timer Time-out

### Given:
- WDT clock source: internal $32.768$ kHz crystal
- WDT divider: $N_{div} = 32768$
- Counter maximum: $Count_{max} = 256$

### Step 1: Apply the WDT Time-out Formula

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{N_{div} \times Count_{max}}{f_{clk}}$$

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{32768 \times 256}{32768} = 256 \text{ seconds} = 4 \text{ min } 16 \text{ sec}$$

### Step 2: For a 1-Second WDT Time-out

To get $T_{WDT} = 1$ s, if $f_{clk} = 32.768$ kHz:

$$32768 = N_{div} \times Count_{max}$$

Possible: $N_{div} = 128$, $Count_{max} = 256$.

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{128 \times 256}{32768} = \frac{32768}{32768} = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Energy Saved in Sleep Mode

### Given:
- $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V
- Active current $I_a = 5$ mA
- Sleep current $I_s = 10$ µA
- Sleep duration $t = 1$ hour = $3600$ s

### Step 1: Compute Current Saved

$$\Delta I = I_a - I_s = 5 \text{ mA} - 0.010 \text{ mA} = 4.99 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 2: Compute Power Saved

$$P_{saved} = V_{DD} \times \Delta I = 3.3 \times 4.99 \times 10^{-3} = 0.016467 \text{ W} = 16.467 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 3: Compute Energy Saved

$$E_{saved} = P_{saved} \times t = 0.016467 \times 3600 = 59.28 \text{ J}$$

This represents significant battery savings in long-running embedded applications.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quick Reference)

| Parameter | ADC | DAC | PWM | WDT | Power Mgmt |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------------|
| Direction | Analog → Digital | Digital → Analog | Digital (timed) | Internal Timer | System Control |
| Trigger | Sample clock | Data write | Timer compare | Hardware overflow | Software instruction |
| Output | Binary code | Voltage waveform | Digital pulse | Reset signal | Wake / Sleep |
| Power | Medium | Medium–High | Low | Very Low | Reduces power |
| Resolution / Metric | $V_{LSB}$ | $V_{LSB}$ | Duty cycle | Time-out period | Current consumption |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete "Other System Components" Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPUBlock["CPU Core"]
        CPU[CPU / ALU / Registers]
        CLK[System Clock]
    end

    subgraph ADCBlock["ADC Subsystem"]
        ANAIN[Analog Input Pin]
        MUX[Analog Multiplexer]
        SAR[SAR Engine]
        ADCREG[ADC Data Register]
    end

    subgraph DACBlock["DAC Subsystem"]
        DACREG[DAC Data Register]
        DACENG[DAC Engine - R-2R]
        ANAOUT[Analog Output Pin]
    end

    subgraph PWMBlock["PWM Subsystem"]
        TIMER[Timer / Counter]
        COMP[Compare Match Logic]
        PWMPIN[PWM Output Pin]
    end

    subgraph WDTBlock["Watchdog Subsystem"]
        WDTCLK[Independent RC Oscillator]
        WDTCNT[WDT Counter]
        RSTGEN[Reset Generator]
    end

    subgraph PMUBlock["Power Management"]
        PMCTRL[Power Mode Controller]
        OSC1[Main Oscillator]
        OSC2[Low-Freq Oscillator]
        WAKE[Wake-Up Sources]
    end

    CPU --> MUX
    CPU --> DACREG
    CPU --> TIMER
    CPU --> PMCTRL
    MUX --> SAR --> ADCREG
    DACREG --> DACENG --> ANAOUT
    TIMER --> COMP --> PWMPIN
    WDTCLK --> WDTCNT --> RSTGEN
    RSTGEN -.RESET.-> CPU
    OSC1 --> CLK
    OSC2 --> PMCTRL
    PMCTRL --> WAKE
```

---

## 4.2 ADC — Successive Approximation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START([Start Conversion]) --> SETMSB[Set MSB = 1, rest 0]
    SETMSB --> DACINT[Internal DAC generates Vdac]
    DACINT --> COMP{Comparator: Vin vs Vdac}
    COMP -->|Vin greater| KEEP1[Keep MSB = 1]
    COMP -->|Vin smaller| CLEAR1[Clear MSB = 0]
    KEEP1 --> NEXT
    CLEAR1 --> NEXT[Move to next bit]
    NEXT --> MOREBITS{All n bits done?}
    MOREBITS -->|No| SETMSB
    MOREBITS -->|Yes| DONE[Output Digital Code D]
    DONE --> END([End])
```

---

## 4.3 PWM — Compare-Match Waveform Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Timer Reset to 0] --> B{Counter = Compare Value?}
    B -->|No| C[Increment Counter]
    C --> B
    B -->|Yes| D[Set PWM Pin HIGH]
    D --> E{Counter = TOP?}
    E -->|No| F[Increment Counter]
    F --> B
    E -->|Yes| G[Clear PWM Pin LOW]
    G --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Watchdog Timer — Servicing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Power-On / Reset]) --> B[Start WDT Counter]
    B --> C{Main loop executing?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Service WDT - clear or write key]
    D --> E[Reset WDT counter]
    E --> C
    C -->|Software HANG| F[Counter reaches overflow]
    F --> G[Generate System RESET]
    G --> A
```

---

## 4.5 Power Management — Mode Transition State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Idle : Execute SLEEP instruction with IDLE bit
    Active --> PowerDown : Execute SLEEP with PD bit
    Idle --> Active : Any enabled interrupt
    PowerDown --> Active : External interrupt / RTC / WDT
    Active --> Active : NOP
    Idle --> Idle : NOP
    PowerDown --> PowerDown : No wake event
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Watchdog Timer. Why is it essential in embedded systems?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** is a hardware timer that resets the microcontroller if the main program fails to clear it within a predefined time interval.

It operates from an **independent internal clock source**, separate from the main system clock, ensuring it keeps running even when the CPU is frozen.

**Importance:**
1. Recovers the system from **software lockups** caused by bugs, EMI, or deadlocks.
2. Mandatory in **safety-critical applications** (automotive ABS, medical devices, industrial PLCs).
3. Enables **autonomous system recovery** without human intervention.
4. Improves **system reliability and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between ADC and DAC with one example application of each.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **ADC** | **DAC** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Direction | Analog → Digital | Digital → Analog |
| Output | Binary code (integer) | Analog voltage/current |
| Example | Temperature sensor → MCU | MP3 player → Speaker |
| Common Architecture | SAR, Flash, Σ-Δ | R-2R ladder, Weighted Resistor |

- **ADC Example:** Reading a **LM35 temperature sensor** (analog mV output) using a 10-bit ADC of the MCU.
- **DAC Example:** Generating **audio waveforms** in a music player from stored MP3 (digital) data.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the working of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC with a neat diagram and calculate its resolution for $V_{ref} = 5$ V and $n = 12$ bits.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of SAR ADC:**

1. A **sample-and-hold (S/H)** circuit captures the analog input voltage $V_{in}$.
2. The SAR sets the MSB of its register to 1, all other bits to 0. This digital code is fed to an internal DAC.
3. The internal DAC produces an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
4. A **comparator** checks if $V_{DAC} > V_{in}$ or $V_{DAC} < V_{in}$.
5. If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, the MSB is **kept as 1**; else it is **cleared to 0**.
6. The SAR proceeds to the next bit and repeats the binary search.
7. After $n$ comparisons, the final digital code is output.

This is essentially a **binary search algorithm** — it takes exactly **$n$ clock cycles** for $n$-bit resolution.

**Calculation:**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 0.0012207 \text{ V} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

**Resolution** = $1.22$ mV (i.e., the smallest distinguishable voltage change).

**Total codes:** $2^{12} = 4096$ codes (0 to 4095).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A PWM signal has a frequency of 1 kHz and a duty cycle of 30%. Compute (i) the time period, (ii) $T_{on}$, and (iii) the average DC voltage for $V_{CC} = 5$ V.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{PWM} = 1$ kHz, $D_{cycle} = 30\%$, $V_{CC} = 5$ V.

**(i) Time Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) ON Time:**

$$T_{on} = D_{cycle} \times T = 0.30 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 0.30 \text{ ms} = 300 \text{ µs}$$

**OFF Time:**

$$T_{off} = T - T_{on} = 1 \text{ ms} - 0.30 \text{ ms} = 0.70 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Average DC Voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D_{cycle} = 5 \times 0.30 = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: With a block diagram, explain the role of a Watchdog Timer in an embedded system. A WDT uses a 32.768 kHz clock, a divider of 64, and an 8-bit counter. Compute the time-out period.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of WDT:**

A Watchdog Timer is a hardware mechanism designed to **detect and recover from system malfunctions**. It operates independently of the main CPU.

- It is **enabled after reset**.
- The main (foreground) software must **periodically service** the WDT by writing a specific key sequence to the WDT register before the counter overflows.
- If the software fails (due to bugs, infinite loops, deadlocks, EMI), it cannot service the WDT in time.
- The WDT counter **overflows** → triggers a **system reset**, restoring normal operation.

**Block Diagram Description:**

The WDT module contains:
- An **independent clock source** (internal RC or external crystal).
- A **prescaler / divider** to slow down the clock.
- An **8-bit (or 16-bit) counter** that increments with each clock tick.
- A **reset generator** that produces the system reset on overflow.

**Calculation of Time-out:**

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{N_{div} \times Count_{max}}{f_{clk}}$$

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{64 \times 256}{32768} = \frac{16384}{32768} = 0.5 \text{ seconds}$$

So, the WDT resets the system every **500 ms** if not serviced.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the different low-power modes of a microcontroller. Why is power management critical in IoT devices?

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Low-Power Modes:**

1. **Active Mode:** CPU and all peripherals run at full clock speed. Highest current consumption.
2. **Idle (Sleep) Mode:** CPU is halted; peripherals (timers, UART, ADC) continue to operate. Wake-up by any enabled interrupt. Reduced current (~µA).
3. **Power-Down (Standby) Mode:** CPU and most peripherals OFF. Only the WDT, RTC, and external interrupts remain active. Current drops to ~nA.
4. **Off / Hibernate Mode:** Everything is off, only an external event (reset, GPIO edge) wakes the device. Lowest power (~nA leakage only).

**Wake-up Sources:**
- External interrupts (GPIO edge-triggered)
- Watchdog timer overflow
- RTC alarm
- Communication peripheral event (e.g., UART start bit detected)

**Importance in IoT:**

- IoT nodes are typically **battery-powered** and must operate for **years** without replacement.
- Sleep modes allow the device to be in deep sleep (nA) most of the time, waking only briefly to sample sensors and transmit data.
- Example: A **soil moisture sensor node** wakes every 15 minutes, takes a reading, transmits via LoRa, and returns to power-down — extending battery life from days to **5+ years**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE MARKS:**
> 1. **Confusing ADC direction:** Writing the wrong formula ($V_{LSB}$ for ADC vs. DAC).
> 2. **Forgetting units** in PWM frequency/period calculations (Hz vs. seconds).
> 3. **Missing the binary search nature** of SAR ADC — examiners expect $n$ clock cycles for $n$-bit resolution.
> 4. **Not stating WDT is independent** of the main clock — this is a critical distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — KTU examiners award **at least 2 marks** for a labeled block diagram in long-answer questions.
> 6. **In DAC/PWM questions**, students often forget to convert decimal code to binary before substituting in formulas.
> 7. **Power management answers** should explicitly **mention the wake-up source**, not just the modes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **ADC:**
> - $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / 2^n$; $D = \lfloor V_{in} / V_{LSB} \rfloor$; Quantization error $\pm V_{LSB}/2$.
> - SAR ADC uses **binary search**, takes **$n$ cycles**.
> - Higher $n$ → finer resolution, higher SNR ($6.02n + 1.76$ dB).
>
> ✅ **DAC:**
> - $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot (D / 2^n)$; uses **R-2R ladder** (most common in ICs).
> - PWM + low-pass filter can emulate a DAC.
>
> ✅ **PWM:**
> - $D_{cycle} = T_{on}/T \times 100\%$; $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D_{cycle}$.
> - $f_{PWM} = f_{clk} / [N \cdot (TOP + 1)]$.
> - Modes: Fast, Phase-Correct, Phase-Frequency.
>
> ✅ **Watchdog Timer:**
> - $T_{WDT} = (N_{div} \times Count_{max}) / f_{clk}$.
> - Must be **serviced periodically** by software.
> - Operates from **independent clock** — survives main CPU failure.
>
> ✅ **Power Management:**
> - Modes: **Active → Idle → Power-Down → Off**.
> - Current drops: mA → µA → nA.
> - Wake-up via interrupts, RTC, WDT.
> - **Critical for IoT** and battery-operated devices.
>
> ✅ **General Tips for KTU Exam:**
> - Always **draw a block diagram** for 7-mark questions.
> - Show **step-by-step calculations** with units.
> - Mention **practical applications** wherever asked.
> - **Don't skip the binary-to-decimal conversion** in ADC/DAC problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of an Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of an Embedded System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

An **Embedded System** is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based, software-driven, single-functional, and highly constrained computing subsystem that is designed to perform a dedicated task within a larger mechanical, electronic, or industrial framework. The **characteristics of an embedded system** are the set of observable design attributes, behavioural traits, and engineering constraints that collectively define and distinguish an embedded product from a general-purpose computing system (like a PC or server).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Compliant):**
> The characteristics of embedded systems are the **defining features** that govern their **architecture, operation, and design philosophy**, distinguishing them from desktop computing. They include: **Single-functioned, tightly constrained, reactive, real-time, processor-based, hardware-software co-design, low cost, low power, small footprint, and ruggedness**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Washing Machine"

Imagine a typical household **automatic washing machine**. It is not a computer you browse the internet on — yet it contains a microcontroller that decides:
- How long to wash?
- When to drain?
- When to spin?
- When to beep?

It runs **one fixed program** (wash cycle), responds **only to buttons and sensors** (reactive), must finish tasks **within strict time limits** (real-time), costs under ₹20,000 (low-cost), and works reliably for **10+ years** in a humid bathroom (rugged).

> A washing machine is the **perfect analogy** for an embedded system: dedicated, constrained, reactive, and invisible to the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:**
> Examiner questions on Module 4 frequently ask: *"Differentiate embedded system characteristics from general-purpose computers"* or *"Explain any 5 characteristics with examples"*. Mastering these definitions guarantees marks.

---

## 1.3 Primary Categorisation of Characteristics

The characteristics of an embedded system can be broadly classified into three engineering domains:

| Domain | Core Question Answered | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Functional Behaviour** | *What does it do?* | Single-functioned, Reactive, Real-Time |
| **Design Constraints** | *What limits must it obey?* | Tight constraints, Low cost, Low power, Small size |
| **Implementation Nature** | *How is it built?* | Processor-based, HW-SW co-design, Ruggedness |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic — "S-T-R-P-H-L-R":**
> **S**ingle-functioned, **T**ightly constrained, **R**eactive, **P**rocessor-based, **H**W-SW system, **L**ow cost, **R**eal-time. Add **L**ow power, **S**mall size, **R**ugged for the full set.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Matrix

## 2.1 The Nine Core Characteristics — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Single-Functioned / Dedicated Purpose
- An embedded system is designed to execute **one specific task** (or a tightly related set of tasks) repeatedly.
- Unlike a PC, it does **not** load arbitrary user applications.
- **Example:** A digital thermometer only measures and displays temperature; it cannot browse the web.
- **Why:** The hardware (sensors, ADC, display) and software (firmware) are both custom-tailored for that one function.

### 2.1.2 Tightly Constrained
Designers operate under strict, non-negotiable limits:
- **Cost:** Mass-produced units (millions of units) must use cheap components.
- **Power:** Battery-operated devices (IoT, wearables) must sip microwatts.
- **Size:** Wearables and hearing aids demand millimetre-scale PCBs.
- **Memory:** Often only **kilobytes** of RAM and Flash (vs. gigabytes in PCs).
- **Why:** Trade-offs are forced — performance is sacrificed for cost/power.

### 2.1.3 Reactive Operation
- The system continuously **responds to external stimuli (events)** from sensors, user inputs, or communication interfaces.
- It does **not** run a sequential batch program; it waits for events and reacts.
- **Example:** An anti-lock Braking System (ABS) reacts to wheel-lock sensors in microseconds.
- **Why:** The system's correctness depends on *how* and *when* it responds.

### 2.1.4 Real-Time Operation
- The correctness of the system depends not only on the **logical result** but also on the **time at which it is produced**.
- Two categories:
  - **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline = **system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, pacemaker).
  - **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline = **degraded quality** (e.g., video streaming, audio playback).
- **Why:** A late correct answer is often as bad as a wrong answer in safety-critical systems.

### 2.1.5 Processor-Based
- Every embedded system has a **processing element**: a microcontroller (MCU), microprocessor (MPU), Digital Signal Processor (DSP), or Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC).
- Modern systems often use **System-on-Chip (SoC)** combining CPU + memory + peripherals.

### 2.1.6 Hardware–Software Co-Design
- The system is a **hybrid** of fixed hardware (sensors, actuators, ASICs) and programmable software (firmware in C/assembly).
- **Why:** Neither alone suffices — hardware provides speed, software provides flexibility.

### 2.1.7 Low Cost
- Target markets (consumer electronics) demand sub-₹100 to sub-₹1000 BOM (Bill of Materials) cost.
- Achieved through high-volume manufacturing and highly integrated chips.

### 2.1.8 Low Power Consumption
- Measured in **µW to mW** (not Watts like laptops).
- Critical for battery life: a fitness band must run 7+ days on a 50 mAh coin cell.
- Techniques: Sleep modes, clock gating, voltage scaling.

### 2.1.9 Small Size / Footprint
- PCB area is often **< 10 cm²**, sometimes **< 1 cm²** (smart cards, hearing aids).
- Drives the trend toward **SoC integration**.

### 2.1.10 Ruggedness & Reliability
- Must operate flawlessly in **harsh environments**: temperature (-40 °C to +125 °C automotive), vibration, humidity, EMI.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is often mandated at **> 100,000 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These characteristics directly drive the choice of **RTOS vs. bare-metal**, **ASIC vs. FPGA vs. MCU**, and **battery vs. mains-powered** designs. Almost every design decision in an embedded product is justified by referring back to these characteristics.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** The following table frequently appears as a "Compare" question worth 7–14 marks. Memorise the contrasts.

| Characteristic | General-Purpose Computer (PC) | Embedded System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Function** | Multi-purpose, user-defined | Single, pre-defined function |
| **Cost** | ₹30,000 – ₹2,00,000+ | ₹50 – ₹50,000 |
| **Power** | 100 W – 1000 W | 0.1 mW – 5 W |
| **OS** | Full OS (Windows/Linux/macOS) | Often no OS, or RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) |
| **User Interaction** | Keyboard, mouse, monitor, GUI | Buttons, LEDs, touchscreen, sensors |
| **Memory** | GBs of RAM, TBs of storage | KBs–MBs of RAM/Flash |
| **Real-Time** | Best-effort (soft) | Often hard real-time |
| **Update Frequency** | User-installed upgrades | Firmware update (rare) |
| **Lifespan** | 3–6 years | 10–20+ years |
| **Ruggedness** | Office environment | Industrial/military/automotive grade |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Design Constraint Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Method: Mapping Characteristics to Design Choices

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises **why** a characteristic exists and **how** it shapes the final product. Below is the rigorous step-by-step analytical procedure used by embedded system designers to map each characteristic to a design decision.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Function (from "Single-Functioned" trait)

$$\text{Function} = f(\text{Input Sensors}, \text{Output Actuators})$$

**Explanation:** Begin by listing the inputs (temperature sensor, push-button, accelerometer) and outputs (motor, buzzer, LCD). The intersection of these is the **dedicated function** the system must perform. For a digital thermometer:

$$f = \{\text{Temp Sensor (LM35)} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Display (7-segment)}\}$$

---

### Step 2 — Quantify the Time Constraint (from "Real-Time" trait)

The **worst-case execution time (WCET)** must be less than the **deadline**:

$$WCET_{task} \leq Deadline_{task}$$

$$Utilization = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{WCET_i}{Period_i} \leq 1.0 \quad \text{(Liu & Layland bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling)}$$

**Explanation:** For hard real-time tasks, if the CPU utilisation exceeds 100 %, the system **cannot** guarantee deadlines. This forces designers to choose faster processors, optimise firmware in C/assembly, or use dedicated hardware accelerators.

---

### Step 3 — Derive the Power Budget (from "Low Power" trait)

Average power dissipation of an MCU is:

$$P_{avg} = V_{DD} \times I_{active} \times t_{active} + V_{DD} \times I_{sleep} \times t_{sleep}$$

**Example Derivation (Coin-cell fitness band):**
- $V_{DD} = 3.0\ \text{V}$
- $I_{active} = 5\ \text{mA}$ for $t_{active} = 0.5\ \text{s/hour}$
- $I_{sleep} = 10\ \mu\text{A}$ for $t_{sleep} = 3599.5\ \text{s/hour}$

Compute active energy per hour:

$$E_{active} = V_{DD} \times I_{active} \times t_{active} = 3.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 = 7.5\ \text{mJ/hour}$$

Compute sleep energy per hour:

$$E_{sleep} = 3.0 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 3599.5 \approx 107.99\ \text{mJ/hour}$$

Total energy per hour:

$$E_{total} = E_{active} + E_{sleep} \approx 7.5 + 107.99 = 115.49\ \text{mJ/hour}$$

Energy of a 50 mAh coin cell at 3 V:

$$E_{battery} = V \times Q = 3.0 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 540\ \text{J} = 540{,}000\ \text{mJ}$$

Battery life:

$$\text{Life (hours)} = \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{total}\ /\ 1000} = \frac{540{,}000}{115.49} \approx 4676\ \text{hours} \approx 194\ \text{days}$$

**Conclusion:** Power-aware design directly enables a **6+ month** battery life from a coin cell — a hallmark embedded characteristic.

---

### Step 4 — Cost Constraint Mapping (from "Low Cost" trait)

Bill of Materials (BOM) target:

$$C_{BOM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_{component,i} \times N_i) + C_{PCB} + C_{assembly} \leq C_{target}$$

**Explanation:** For a ₹500 retail product, the BOM must typically be **< ₹150** to leave margin for assembly, packaging, logistics, and profit. This forces use of **integrated MCUs** (with built-in ADC, timers, communication) over discrete components.

---

### Step 5 — Ruggedness Verification (from "Ruggedness" trait)

Mean Time Between Failures:

$$MTBF = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}} \quad (\text{hours})$$

For automotive-grade MCUs, the required MTBF is often:

$$MTBF \geq 100{,}000\ \text{hours} \approx 11.4\ \text{years}$$

**Explanation:** This is achieved through wide-temperature silicon (-40 °C to +125 °C), conformal coating, and redundant sensors in safety-critical systems (ISO 26262 compliance).

---

## 3.2 Design Decision Flow Matrix

| Observed Constraint | Engineering Decision Triggered |
| :--- | :--- |
| Real-time (hard) | Choose RTOS / dedicated scheduler; disable interrupts carefully |
| Low power | Use sleep modes, low-power MCUs (e.g., MSP430, STM32L) |
| Low cost | Use SoC MCU; avoid external memory |
| Small size | Use QFN/BGA packages; multi-layer PCB (4–8 layers) |
| Ruggedness | Select AEC-Q100 qualified parts; add watchdog timer |
| Reactive | Use interrupt-driven architecture; avoid polling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Characteristic Map (Mermaid Mind-Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Characteristics of an Embedded System))
    Functional Behaviour
      Single Functioned
      Reactive
      Real Time
        Hard Real Time
        Soft Real Time
    Design Constraints
      Tightly Constrained
        Low Cost
        Low Power
        Small Size
        Limited Memory
    Implementation Nature
      Processor Based
        MCU
        MPU
        DSP
        ASIC
        SoC
      HW SW Co Design
      Ruggedness
        Wide Temperature
        High MTBF
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — How Characteristics Interact Inside a System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Stimulus Event] --> B[Sensor Input Module]
    B --> C[Processor Core MCU]
    C --> D{Realtime Deadline Check}
    D -- Met within WCET --> E[Actuator Output]
    D -- Missed --> F[Safety Shutdown]
    C --> G[Power Management Unit]
    G --> H[Sleep Mode Controller]
    E --> I[Physical World Response]
    F --> I
    J[Cost Memory Size Constraints] -.-> C
    K[Ruggedness Reliability Layer] -.-> C
    L[HW SW Co Design] -.-> C
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Classifying a Real-Time Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Embedded System Under Design] --> Q1{Does a missed deadline cause safety failure?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Hard[Hard Real Time e.g. Airbag ECU]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does a missed deadline degrade quality only?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Soft[Soft Real Time e.g. MP3 Player]
    Q2 -- No --> BestEffort[Best Effort e.g. Email Client]
    Hard --> ChoiceA[Use RTOS, priority scheduling, watchdogs]
    Soft --> ChoiceB[Use lightweight scheduler, buffering]
    BestEffort --> ChoiceC[No strict timing needed]
```

## 4.4 Characteristic-to-Product Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Single Functioned] --> P1[Digital Watch]
    A2[Real Time Hard] --> P2[Anti Lock Braking System]
    A3[Ultra Low Power] --> P3[Pacemaker]
    A4[Tiny Footprint] --> P4[Hearing Aid]
    A5[Ruggedness] --> P5[Aerospace Flight Controller]
    A6[Low Cost High Volume] --> P6[TV Remote Control]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: List any three characteristics that distinguish an embedded system from a general-purpose computer.**

**Model Answer:**
An embedded system differs from a general-purpose computer in the following three primary characteristics:
1. **Single-Functioned:** It performs one dedicated task (e.g., washing machine control), whereas a PC runs many user applications.
