# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define the term "Reliability" of an embedded system. Mention any two factors affecting reliability.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Reliability** is the probability that an embedded system will perform its intended function under designated operating conditions for a specified period of time. Mathematically, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda$ is the failure rate.
>
> **Two factors affecting reliability:**
> 1. **Component Quality & Operating Environment:** Higher-grade components and controlled thermal/vibration environments improve reliability. *(1.5 marks)*
> 2. **Software Defect Density:** Bugs in firmware can cause functional failures even in hardware-reliable systems. *(1.5 marks)*

### Question A2
**Q: Differentiate between Hard Real-Time and Soft Real-Time embedded systems with one example each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Deadline Consequence** | Missing deadline causes **catastrophic system failure** | Missing deadline causes **degraded performance** only |
> | **Guarantee** | Absolute, deterministic timing | Best-effort, statistical |
> | **Example** | Airbag deployment ECU, Pacemaker | Video streaming app, Audio player |
> | **Scheduling** | Rate Monotonic, EDF | FIFO, Priority-based |
>
> *(3 marks — split across definition, distinction, and examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q: (a)** Explain the quality attributes **Reliability**, **Maintainability**, and **Availability** of an embedded system in detail. Derive the relationship between them with suitable equations. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A smart-grid monitoring embedded node has $MTBF = 8000\ \text{hours}$ and $MTTR = 4\ \text{hours}$. Calculate (i) Availability, (ii) Downtime per year, and (iii) State whether this node is suitable for a critical power-distribution application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Reliability:**
Reliability $R(t)$ is the conditional probability that a system performs correctly throughout the interval $[0, t]$, given that it was operational at time $0$. It is expressed using the exponential distribution:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / MTBF}$$

Where $\lambda$ is the constant failure rate (failures per hour). Higher MTBF = higher reliability. *(2 marks for definition + equation)*

**Maintainability:**
Maintainability $M(t)$ is the probability that a failed system is restored to working condition within time $t$. It is characterized by **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**. A system with low MTTR is highly maintainable. Maintenance includes corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive forms. *(2 marks)*

**Availability:**
Availability $A$ is the long-term fraction of time the system is operational. It combines reliability (via MTBF) and maintainability (via MTTR):

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

For a system with $MTBF = 1000$ hours and $MTTR = 1$ hour:

$$A = \frac{1000}{1001} \approx 99.90\%$$

*(2 marks for derivation — refer to Section 3.1 of these notes)*

**Relationship Summary:**
- Reliability is governed by **hardware/software failure rates**.
- Maintainability is governed by **repair and diagnostic efficiency**.
- Availability is the **integrated outcome** of both. A system can be highly reliable but poorly maintainable (low availability) if repairs take long. Conversely, a less reliable system can still achieve high availability if MTTR is very small. *(1 mark for synthesis)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:**
$MTBF = 8000\ \text{hours}$, $MTTR = 4\ \text{hours}$

**(i) Availability:**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 4} = \frac{8000}{8004}$$

$$A = 0.99950 = 99.950\%$$

This corresponds to **3 nines** availability (between 99.9% and 99.99%). *(2.5 marks)*

**(ii) Downtime per Year:**

$$D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 8760$$

$$D_{year} = (1 - 0.99950) \times 8760$$

$$D_{year} = 0.00050 \times 8760$$

$$D_{year} = 4.38\ \text{hours/year}$$

*(2.5 marks)*

**(iii) Suitability for Critical Power Distribution:**

A critical power-distribution smart-grid node typically demands **at least 4 nines (99.99%)** availability, equivalent to a maximum downtime of about **52.6 minutes per year**. The current system has **4.38 hours/year downtime**, which is approximately **5 times higher** than the 4-nines threshold. **Therefore, this node is NOT suitable** in its current form for a critical power-distribution application. To meet the requirement, either:
- Improve MTBF (use industrial-grade components, derating).
- Reduce MTTR (hot-swappable modules, better diagnostics).
- Add parallel redundancy. *(2 marks for justification)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q: (a)** Explain the quality attributes **Safety** and **Security** in embedded systems. Compare and contrast them with two real-world examples each. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the importance of **Energy Efficiency** as a quality attribute. With the dynamic power equation $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$, explain how each parameter affects the total power consumption of a microcontroller. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Safety:**
Safety is the property of an embedded system that guarantees **no harm to human life, environment, or equipment**, even when the system fails. It is enforced through:
- Fail-safe design (graceful degradation to a safe state).
- Redundancy (e.g., dual-channel ECUs in aircraft).
- Watchdog timers.
- Compliance with standards: ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 61508 (industrial), DO-178C (aerospace).

**Examples:**
1. **Automotive Airbag ECU** — must deploy within 10 ms of crash detection; failure to deploy on time = fatality.
2. **Nuclear Reactor Trip System** — must SCRAM (shut down) within milliseconds of anomaly detection.

*(2 marks for definition + safety principles + 2 examples)*

**Security:**
Security is the property of protecting the embedded system from **unauthorized access, tampering, data theft, and cyber-attacks**. It involves:
- Confidentiality (data privacy).
- Integrity (data not modified).
- Authentication (verifying identity).

**Examples:**
1. **IoT Smart Lock** — must prevent unauthorized unlock via replay attacks, BLE spoofing.
2. **ATM Machine** — must protect PIN entry from skimmers and shoulder-surfing.

*(2 marks)*

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Safety | Security |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Concern** | Unintentional failures | Intentional attacks |
| **Threat Source** | Component failure, bug | Adversary, hacker |
| **Standard** | ISO 26262, IEC 61508 | Common Criteria, FIPS 140-2 |
| **Design Approach** | Fail-safe, redundancy | Encryption, authentication |

*(2 marks for comparison)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Importance of Energy Efficiency:**
Energy efficiency is critical in battery-operated embedded systems (IoT sensors, wearables, implantable medical devices) because it directly determines **battery life**, **thermal profile**, and **operational autonomy**. A system with poor energy efficiency requires frequent recharging or bulky batteries, reducing commercial viability.

For example, a wireless sensor node powered by a coin-cell battery (230 mAh, 3 V) must operate for **5–10 years** on a single battery. This is achievable only with sub-milliwatt average power consumption.

*(2 marks)*

**Dynamic Power Equation:**

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1) — fraction of gates toggling per clock cycle.
- $C$ = load capacitance — total parasitic capacitance being charged/discharged.
- $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage.
- $f$ = clock frequency.

**Effect of each parameter:**

1. **Switching Activity $\alpha$:** Power scales **linearly** with $\alpha$. Reducing software-driven switching (e.g., clock gating unused peripherals, efficient coding) directly reduces $\alpha$. Reducing $\alpha$ by 50% halves dynamic power. *(1.25 marks)*

2. **Capacitance $C$:** Power scales **linearly** with $C$. Process-node miniaturization (180 nm → 65 nm → 28 nm) reduces gate capacitance, dramatically reducing power. *(1.25 marks)*

3. **Supply Voltage $V_{dd}$:** Power scales with the **square** of $V_{dd}$. Reducing $V_{dd}$ from 3.3 V to 1.8 V reduces dynamic power by a factor of $(1.8/3.3)^2 = 0.297$, i.e., **70% power saving**. This is why modern MCUs use **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** to throttle voltage during low-load conditions. *(1.25 marks)*

4. **Clock Frequency $f$:** Power scales **linearly** with $f$. Halving the clock halves dynamic power. This is exploited in sleep modes and idle states where the clock is gated off. *(1.25 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** $V_{dd}$ is the **most powerful knob** for power reduction due to its squared effect, but it is also constrained by transistor threshold voltage and performance requirements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF:** MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) applies to **non-repairable** systems (e.g., a flash memory chip). MTBF applies to **repairable** systems (e.g., a complete ECU). Writing MTBF for a satellite sensor will lose a mark.
>
> 2. **Wrong unit for Availability:** Availability is a **dimensionless ratio between 0 and 1**, *not* a percentage in raw form. If the question asks for "percentage availability," multiply $A$ by 100.
>
> 3. **Confusing Safety with Security:** Safety protects against **accidents and unintentional failures**. Security protects against **malicious attacks**. Examiners often give scenarios like "hacking a pacemaker" — this is a **security** question, not a safety question.
>
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps:** In 14-mark numerical questions, the **Availability formula derivation** is often expected. Just stating $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ without justification may lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Mermaid and Markdown pipes:** When drawing reliability block diagrams in written exams, students often forget to label series/parallel blocks. Always annotate the topology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Quality Attributes**

- **Quality attributes** describe *how well* a system performs, not *what* it does. Also called **non-functional requirements** or **"ilities."**
- **Reliability** = Probability of correct functioning over time. $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Governed by component quality and software defect density.
- **Maintainability** = Ease of repair. Characterized by **MTTR**. Includes corrective, adaptive, perfective, preventive maintenance.
- **Availability** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. Expressed in **nines** (90% = 1 nine, 99.999% = 5 nines).
- **Safety** prevents **unintentional** harm; **Security** prevents **intentional** harm. Both are critical for embedded systems.
- **Hard real-time** systems fail catastrophically on missed deadlines. **Soft real-time** systems degrade gracefully.
- **Dynamic Power** $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$. $V_{dd}$ has the **quadratic (largest)** effect on power.
- **Usability** encompasses HMI design, learnability, and error tolerance.
- **Energy Efficiency** is the *primary* quality attribute for battery-powered IoT/wearable systems.
- **Cost**, **Time-to-Market**, and **Portability** are commercial quality attributes that often trade off with technical performance.
- For KTU exam: **always state the formula, then substitute, then compute, then comment** on engineering significance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application-Specific Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application-Specific Embedded System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: APPLICATION-SPECIFIC EMBEDDED SYSTEM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> An **Application-Specific Embedded System (ASES)** is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based computing system, either stand-alone or part of a larger system, that is explicitly architected, optimized, and dedicated to perform a **specific function or a narrow set of related functions** for a particular application domain. Unlike general-purpose computers, the hardware (ASIC/ASIP/SoC), software (firmware), and I/O peripherals are co-designed around a fixed, pre-determined functional specification with strict constraints on power, cost, size, latency, and real-time performance.

> [!NOTE] The Three Pillars of an Application-Specific System
> 1. **Hardware Specialization** — Custom logic, fixed-function accelerators, or optimized instruction sets.
> 2. **Software Tightness** — Bare-metal/RTOS firmware with no superfluous features.
> 3. **I/O Binding** — Peripherals are exactly those needed by the application; no "extra" ports.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] Plain-English Analogy
> - A **General-Purpose Computer** is like a Swiss Army knife — it can do hundreds of tasks (browse, edit, compile) but does none of them with the absolute best efficiency.
> - An **Application-Specific Embedded System** is like a **surgical scalpel** designed for *one* operation — it cannot browse the web or play games, but for its intended task (e.g., controlling a pacemaker's heartbeat rhythm), it is **faster, smaller, cheaper, and more power-efficient** than any general-purpose machine could ever be.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Five Engineering Figures of Merit (Bold = Standard KTU-mandated metrics)
> 1. **Performance / Throughput** — measured in **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) or **DMIPS** (Dhrystone MIPS).
> 2. **Power Dissipation** — measured in **mW** or **µW/MHz**; critical for battery-operated nodes.
> 3. **Unit Cost** — measured in **USD or INR per unit** at production volume.
> 4. **Form Factor (Size)** — measured in **mm² of silicon** or PCB footprint.
> 5. **Time-to-Market (TTM)** — measured in **months**; defines competitive viability.

> [!NOTE] Why "Application-Specific"?
> The phrase emphasizes that the *system identity is bound to the application*. If the application changes substantially, the system must be redesigned. This is fundamentally different from a general-purpose system whose identity is bound to *computational generality*.

---

### 1.4 Sub-Categories of Application-Specific Systems

| Sub-Category | Full Form | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **ASIC** | Application-Specific Integrated Circuit | Custom silicon, lowest power, highest NRE |
| **ASIP** | Application-Specific Instruction-set Processor | Customized instruction set on a processor core |
| **SoC** | System-on-Chip | Integrates CPU + memory + peripherals on one die |
| **FPGA-based** | Field-Programmable Gate Array | Reconfigurable hardware; lower NRE, higher unit cost |

> [!WARNING] Common KTU Pitfall
> Students often confuse **ASIP** with **ASIC**. Remember: an *ASIP is still a processor* (programmable in software with a tailored ISA), whereas an *ASIC is hardwired logic* (no instruction fetch, no programmability beyond mask changes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Characteristics of an Application-Specific Embedded System

### 2.1.1 Tightly Coupled Hardware–Software Co-Design
The defining trait of an ASES is **hardware/software partitioning performed *a-priori***. Designers do not first buy a CPU and then "make it work"; they analyze the algorithm first and decide *which functions go to hardware accelerators and which remain in software*.

### 2.1.2 Fixed, Pre-Determined Functional Specification
The specification is **frozen before design begins**. Any change in spec → re-spin of silicon (in ASIC) or re-compile (in ASIP/SoC with firmware).

### 2.1.3 Real-Time and Reactive Behaviour
Application-specific systems are predominantly **reactive** (they sit idle until an event interrupts them) and must satisfy **hard/soft real-time deadlines**.

### 2.1.4 Constrained Resources
Three canonical constraints (Wolf's Embedded Computing Model):
- **Available memory** (often KB-scale, not GB)
- **Available computation** (often MIPS-scale, not GOPS)
- **Available power** (often mW-scale, not watts)

### 2.1.5 Minimal User Interface
Many ASES devices have **no display, no keyboard**. The "UI" is buttons + LEDs or even just sensor-driven autonomy (e.g., anti-lock braking system).

### 2.1.6 Hybrid Computing Fabric
Modern ASES devices combine:
- A **general-purpose core** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) for control flow.
- **Hardware accelerators** (e.g., crypto engine, DSP, FFT block) for compute hotspots.

---

## 2.2 The Design Flow of an Application-Specific Embedded System

The standard KTU-mandated design flow is presented below as a 9-stage pipeline:

1. **Requirements Specification** — Functional + non-functional (latency, power, cost).
2. **System-Level Modeling** — UML, SysML, or MATLAB/Simulink.
3. **Hardware/Software Partitioning** — Decide which blocks go to HW vs SW.
4. **Hardware Design (RTL)** — VHDL/Verilog HDL; OR schematic entry.
5. **Software Design** — C/C++/assembly; RTOS selection if needed.
6. **Integration & Verification** — Co-simulation, FPGA prototyping.
7. **Implementation** — ASIC tape-out OR FPGA bitstream OR SoC fabrication.
8. **Testing & Validation** — Functional, parametric, environmental.
9. **Deployment & Maintenance** — Field updates, OTA firmware upgrades.

> [!TIP] KTU Memory Mnemonic
> **"R-S-H-S-I-I-T-V-D"** — Requirements, Specification, HW/SW split, HDL coding, Software, Integration, Implementation, Testing, Deployment.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas use absolute value notation with `\vert` to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power (Dynamic CMOS)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts (W) | Battery-life estimation in portable ASES |
| 2 | **Power (Static / Leakage)** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Watts (W) | Sleep-mode energy budget |
| 3 | **Total Energy per Task** | $E = P \cdot t_{exec}$ | Joules (J) | Energy-aware scheduling |
| 4 | **MIPS Rating** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{t_{exec} \times 10^{6}}$ | MIPS | Raw CPU throughput |
| 5 | **DMIPS / MHz** | $\text{DMIPS/MHz} = \dfrac{\text{Dhrystone score}}{f_{clk}}$ | DMIPS/MHz | Compiler-aware benchmark |
| 6 | **Speedup (Amdahl)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | Dimensionless | HW-accelerator ROI |
| 7 | **Cost (Unit)** | $C_{unit} = C_{NRE}/V + C_{fab}$ | Currency / unit | Break-even volume analysis |
| 8 | **Memory Occupancy** | $M_{occ} = \dfrac{\text{Used Flash}}{\text{Total Flash}} \times 100\%$ | % | Firmware footprint planning |
| 9 | **Interrupt Latency** | $t_{lat} = t_{recog} + t_{save} + t_{ISR}$ | µs / cycles | Real-time deadline analysis |
| 10 | **Throughput Bound** | $\text{TP} \le \dfrac{1}{t_{lat} + t_{service}}$ | Events / sec | Event-handler capacity |
| 11 | **Utilization (Processor)** | $U = \dfrac{t_{busy}}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | RTOS schedulability |
| 12 | **Markov / MTTF** | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours | Reliability for safety-critical ASES |

> [!WARNING] KTU Formula Conventions
> - $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1).
> - $C_L$ = load capacitance (Farads).
> - $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage (Volts).
> - $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency (Hz).
> - $f$ = parallelizable fraction in Amdahl's Law; $N$ = parallel units.
> - $C_{NRE}$ = Non-Recurring Engineering cost; $V$ = production volume.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] Why This Topic Matters in Industry
> - **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: An Engine Control Unit (ECU) is an ASES. Mixing the wrong design philosophy (e.g., using a general-purpose x86 CPU in a brake controller) would make the car unsafe.
> - **IoT & Wearables**: ASES design philosophy keeps a fitness band alive for 7 days on a 100 mAh battery.
> - **Aerospace & Defense**: Radiation-hardened ASICs (RHBD) are the gold standard for satellites.
> - **Medical Devices**: Pacemakers, insulin pumps, MRI coils — all are ASES; failure is not an option, so specialization is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy-Aware Task Scheduling Bound (RTOS Context)

A classic KTU Module-4 analytical problem: an ASES runs a periodic real-time task set $\{ \tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n \}$ with worst-case execution time $C_i$ and period $T_i$. Derive the **utilization bound** for schedulability.

### Step 1 — Define Processor Utilization
The fraction of CPU time consumed by task $i$ is the ratio of its worst-case execution to its period.

$$
U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum Utilizations of All Tasks
For a set of $n$ independent tasks, the total utilization is the linear sum of individual utilizations.

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Necessary Schedulability Condition
A *necessary* (but not sufficient) condition for any feasible schedule to exist is that total utilization cannot exceed unity.

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Liu & Layland's Sufficient Bound (Rate Monotonic Scheduling)
For Rate Monotonic Scheduling, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for schedulability of $n$ tasks is:

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This bound comes from the worst-case critical instant analysis where the highest-priority task is released at the same time as all lower-priority tasks. The term $2^{1/n}-1$ tends to $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.

### Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check
For $n = 1$ task: bound $= 1 \cdot (2^1 - 1) = 1$. For $n \to \infty$ tasks: bound $\to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

> [!NOTE] Interpretation
> In an ASES, the firmware engineer must compute $U_{total}$ from the spec; if it exceeds the bound, the system is **non-schedulable** and either the hardware (faster CPU) or the algorithm (reduced $C_i$) must be revised.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dynamic Power Dissipation in an ASES (CMOS ASIC)

For a CMOS gate driving a load $C_L$ at clock $f_{clk}$, the energy dissipated per switching event is $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ (charge/discharge cycle of the load capacitor).

### Step 1 — Energy per Transition
Charging the load from 0 to $V_{dd}$ stores $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$; the same is dissipated as heat during discharge.

$$
E_{trans} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Power per Switching Event
Power = Energy per event × events per second.

$$
P_{per\_event} = E_{trans} \cdot f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Switching Activity Factor $\alpha$
Not every clock cycle toggles every gate; the activity factor $\alpha \in [0,1]$ captures this.

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $\alpha$ accounts for: (a) gates that don't toggle every cycle, (b) clock-gating which suppresses transitions, (c) data-dependent switching (e.g., a bus holding a constant value).

### Step 4 — Total Power (Dynamic + Static)
Add leakage/static power to obtain total chip power.

$$
P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} = \alpha C_L V_{dd}^{2} f_{clk} + I_{leak} V_{dd}
$$

### Step 5 — Energy per Instruction (Application-Specific View)
For a workload executing $N$ instructions at throughput $I$ (instructions per second):

$$
E_{per\_inst} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{I} = \dfrac{\alpha C_L V_{dd}^{2} f_{clk} + I_{leak} V_{dd}}{I}
$$

> [!TIP] KTU Examiner's Favourite Numerical
> *Given $\alpha=0.2$, $C_L=10\text{ pF}$, $V_{dd}=1.8\text{ V}$, $f_{clk}=100\text{ MHz}$, compute dynamic power.*
> $P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 10\times10^{-12} \times (1.8)^{2} \times 100\times10^{6} = 6.48\times10^{-4}\text{ W} = 0.648\text{ mW}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Amdahl's Law for Hardware Acceleration

When an application-specific hardware accelerator replaces a software routine, derive the speedup $S$.

### Step 1 — Define Problem Fractions
Let the original software routine take time $T_{old}$. A fraction $f$ of $T_{old}$ is *parallelizable* on the new hardware (running in time $T_{old}/N$ on $N$ parallel units).

### Step 2 — Write New Execution Time
The new total time is the sequential part + the parallel part.

$$
T_{new} = (1-f) \cdot T_{old} + \dfrac{f \cdot T_{old}}{N}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Speedup
Speedup $S$ is the ratio of old to new time.

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{(1-f) T_{old} + \dfrac{f T_{old}}{N}}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify by Dividing Numerator and Denominator by $T_{old}$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

### Step 5 — Limiting Behaviour
As $N \to \infty$ (infinite parallel hardware), speedup is bounded by the sequential fraction.

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{1-f}
$$

> [!NOTE] ASES Implication
> Even with an *infinitely fast* hardware accelerator, you cannot get more than $1/(1-f)$ speedup. If 20% of the code is inherently sequential ($f=0.8$), the maximum speedup is $1/0.2 = 5\times$. This is why ASES design must *co-design algorithm + hardware*, not just bolt on accelerators.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ASIC vs ASIP vs FPGA vs GP-Processor

| Parameter | **ASIC** | **ASIP** | **FPGA** | **GP Processor (GPP)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Performance | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Power Efficiency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| Flexibility (post-fab) | ✗ (none) | ✓ (firmware update) | ✓★ (re-programmable) | ✓★★★★ |
| NRE Cost | Very High | High | Low | Negligible |
| Unit Cost (high vol.) | Very Low | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Time-to-Market | High | Medium | Low | Lowest |
| Typical Volume | > 100K | 10K – 1M | < 10K | 1 – 10K |
| Example | Mobile SoC radio | Tensilica DSP | ML inference card | Laptop CPU |

---

## 3.5 Case Study: Smart Washing Machine Controller (Classic KTU Example)

A KTU favourite: classify the sub-systems of a washing machine.

| Sub-system | Function | Real-time? | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| User keypad + LCD | UI | No | Slow peripheral |
| Motor driver (PWM) | Drum speed | **Yes (ms)** | HW-timer + ISR |
| Water level sensor | Sensing | **Yes (s)** | ADC + threshold ISR |
| Lid lock | Safety interlock | **Yes (µs)** | HW interrupt |
| Wash cycle FSM | Sequencing | **Yes (min)** | Software state machine |
| Buzzer + LED | Notification | No | GPIO |

> [!TIP] KTU Answering Trick
> "Identify whether the application is **hard** or **soft** real-time." A washing machine is *soft* (missing a deadline by 10 ms doesn't kill anyone). An airbag controller is *hard* (missing a deadline by 1 ms = death).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Application-Specific Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SENSOR INPUT] --> B[CONDITIONING AND ADC]
    B --> C[MCU OR ASIC CORE]
    H[USER INTERFACE KEYS LCD] --> C
    C --> D[ACTUATOR DRIVER PWM GPIO]
    C --> E[COMMUNICATION UART SPI I2C CAN]
    C --> F[POWER MANAGEMENT LDO DC DC]
    C <--> G[MEMORY FLASH AND RAM]
    D --> I[PHYSICAL ACTUATOR MOTOR RELAY]
    E --> J[EXTERNAL NETWORK CLOUD OR HOST]

    classDef hw fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef sw fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef io fill:#548235,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff

    class C,G,F hw
    class A,B,D,E,I,J io
    class H sw
```

> [!NOTE] Diagram Interpretation
> - **Blue** = hardware blocks (silicon / passive).
> - **Red** = software / firmware execution domain.
> - **Green** = I/O crossing the system boundary.

---

## 4.2 The 9-Stage ASES Design Flow (Mermaid Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STAGE 1 REQUIREMENTS SPEC] --> S2[STAGE 2 SYSTEM MODELING UML SIMULINK]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 HW SW PARTITIONING]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 HARDWARE DESIGN RTL HDL]
    S3 --> S5[STAGE 5 SOFTWARE DESIGN C RTOS]
    S4 --> S6[STAGE 6 INTEGRATION AND VERIFICATION]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[STAGE 7 IMPLEMENTATION ASIC FPGA SOC]
    S7 --> S8[STAGE 8 TESTING AND VALIDATION]
    S8 --> S9[STAGE 9 DEPLOYMENT AND FIELD UPDATE]

    classDef stage fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9 stage
```

> [!TIP] KTU Exam Tip
> In the diagram, **S3 (HW/SW Partitioning)** is the *unique* stage of ASES design. General-purpose computing does not have a true partitioning stage because the hardware is fixed. Examiners love asking: *"At what stage is the hardware/software boundary finalized?"* → Answer: **Stage 3**.

---

## 4.3 ASIC vs ASIP vs FPGA Trade-off Map (Mermaid Quadrant)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW_VOLUME_PROTOTYPE["LOW VOLUME AND PROTOTYPE"]
        D1[FPGA BASED ASES]
    end
    subgraph MEDIUM_VOLUME["MEDIUM VOLUME 10K TO 100K"]
        D2[ASIP OR PROGRAMMABLE SOC]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_VOLUME_MASS["HIGH VOLUME AND MASS MARKET"]
        D3[FULL CUSTOM ASIC]
    end
    subgraph ULTRA_LOW_POWER["ULTRA LOW POWER IOT"]
        D4[ASIP WITH HW ACCELERATORS]
    end

    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4

    classDef fill1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fill2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fill3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fill4 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class D1 fill1
    class D2 fill2
    class D3 fill3
    class D4 fill4
```

---

## 4.4 Real-Time Interrupt Service Architecture (ASES Reactive Core)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant EXT as EXTERNAL EVENT
    participant ISR as INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE
    participant SCH as RTOS SCHEDULER
    participant TSK as PERIODIC TASK
    participant HW as HW ACCELERATOR

    EXT->>ISR: Hardware interrupt trigger
    activate ISR
    ISR->>ISR: Save context push registers
    ISR->>SCH: Wake blocked task via semaphore
    deactivate ISR
    SCH->>TSK: Dispatch task to CPU
    activate TSK
    TSK->>HW: Issue job to accelerator
    HW-->>TSK: Return result with done flag
    TSK->>SCH: Release CPU yield
    deactivate TSK
```

> [!WARNING] Sequence-Diagram Caution
> This is the **reactive model** of an ASES. The CPU sleeps in a low-power state until the ISR fires, then services the event. This is why ASES devices achieve **micro-amp idle currents**.

---

## 4.5 Power Management State Machine (PMU inside an ASES)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACTIVE
    ACTIVE --> IDLE: No task pending
    IDLE --> SLEEP: Idle timer expires
    SLEEP --> DEEP_SLEEP: Extended inactivity
    DEEP_SLEEP --> ACTIVE: Wake event RTC GPIO IRQ
    SLEEP --> ACTIVE: Wake event RTC GPIO IRQ
    IDLE --> ACTIVE: New task scheduled

    ACTIVE : CPU at full f_clk
    IDLE : CPU gated, peripherals ON
    SLEEP : PLL off, RAM retained
    DEEP_SLEEP : Core off, RTC only
```

> [!NOTE] KTU Frequently Asked Question
> *"Draw the power-state diagram of an ASES device."* The four canonical states are: **Active, Idle, Sleep, Deep-Sleep** (sometimes **Power-Off**). The transitions are governed by a *Power Management Unit (PMU)*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Pattern Reminder
> Part A carries 3 marks each. Answers should be 4–6 lines plus a labelled diagram wherever possible. Definitions count for 1 mark, explanation 1 mark, example/diagram 1 mark.

---

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an Application-Specific Embedded System. List four key characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An Application-Specific Embedded System (ASES) is a microprocessor- or microcontroller-based system whose hardware, software, and peripherals are **co-designed** to perform a *single, well-defined function* for a specific application, with strict constraints on power, cost, size, and real-time behaviour.

**Four Distinguishing Characteristics (2 marks):**
1. **Tight functional scope** — performs one (or a few related) task(s), not general computation.
2. **Hardware-software co-design** — partitioning is done *a-priori*; both layers are optimized together.
3. **Stringent real-time constraints** — must meet hard/soft deadlines deterministically.
4. **Constrained resources** — limited memory (KB), compute (MIPS), and power (mW), unlike a desktop's GB/GHz/W envelope.

**Example (1 mark — credit if mentioned):** A digital thermometer using an MSP430 reading a thermistor every 100 ms.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between ASIC, ASIP, and FPGA-based design approaches. State one industrial use-case of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Approach | Definition (1 mark) | Use-Case (½ mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **ASIC** | Hardwired silicon designed for *one* application. | Mobile phone baseband chip (Qualcomm Snapdragon) |
| **ASIP** | A processor with a *customized instruction set* tailored to a domain. | Tensilica DSP in audio codecs |
| **FPGA** | Reconfigurable logic fabric; design can be re-flashed. | Aerospace flight-computer prototyping (Xilinx Virtex) |

**Key Difference (½ mark):** ASIC = no post-fab flexibility, lowest power; ASIP = software-programmable with custom ISA; FPGA = hardware-reprogrammable, used for low-volume or prototyping.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Pattern Reminder
> Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Provide diagrams, derivations, and reasoning steps. Examiner awards marks for: correct concept (2), formula/derivation (2), numerical/example (2), diagram (1).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choice 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: **Hardware/Software Co-Design Methodology**

**Question:**
*Explain the Hardware/Software Co-Design methodology for an Application-Specific Embedded System. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the major design steps starting from system specification to implementation. Highlight the role of hardware/software partitioning.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Specification (1.5 marks):**
The first stage captures the functional requirements (what the system must do) and the non-functional requirements (timing, power, cost, size). For example, a portable ECG monitor must sample 4-lead ECG at 500 Hz, run a QRS-detection algorithm, and last > 24 hours on a 600 mAh Li-ion cell.

**Step 2 — Modeling (1 mark):**
The system is modeled at a high level using **UML, SysML, or MATLAB/Simulink**. Use-case diagrams, statecharts, and dataflow diagrams capture behaviour before any RTL or C code is written.

**Step 3 — Hardware/Software Partitioning (2 marks — *most important*):**
This is the **decision stage** where each functional block is allocated to either:
- A **software implementation** running on a CPU/microcontroller (flexible, slower, lower cost), or
- A **hardware implementation** in ASIC/FPGA logic (faster, more power-efficient, higher NRE).

The trade-off is governed by Amdahl's Law: functions on the critical path or in the inner loop are typically hardware-accelerated.

**Step 4 — Hardware and Software Design (1 mark):**
- HW: VHDL/Verilog RTL; logic synthesis; place-and-route.
- SW: C/C++/assembly; RTOS porting; device drivers; BSP.

**Step 5 — Co-Simulation, Verification, Implementation (1 mark):**
Co-simulation runs HW models with SW simultaneously (e.g., Questa-Sim + ARMulator). Verification uses assertion-based checkers. Implementation: ASIC tape-out, FPGA bitstream, or SoC fabrication.

**Step 6 — Testing and Deployment (½ mark):**
Functional, parametric, EMC/EMI, and field testing; OTA firmware updates if supported.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SYSTEM SPECIFICATION] --> B[HIGH LEVEL MODELING]
    B --> C[HW SW PARTITIONING]
    C --> D[HW DESIGN RTL HDL]
    C --> E[SW DESIGN C RTOS]
    D --> F[CO SIMULATION AND VERIFICATION]
    E --> F
    F --> G[IMPLEMENTATION ASIC FPGA SOC]
    G --> H[TESTING]
    H --> I[DEPLOYMENT]
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: **Comparative Design Analysis with Numerical Example**

**Question:**
*A wearable heart-rate monitor is to be designed. Two alternatives are proposed: (i) software implementation on an ARM Cortex-M0+ at 48 MHz, (ii) hardware FFT accelerator in 65 nm CMOS at 100 MHz. The algorithm spends 60% of execution time in the FFT inner loop, which the accelerator parallelizes 8× faster. Using Amdahl's Law, calculate the overall speedup. Comment on the design choice from a power perspective.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters (1 mark):**
- Parallelizable fraction: $f = 0.60$
- Parallelism factor: $N = 8$
- Sequential fraction: $1 - f = 0.40$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law (3 marks):**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.40 + \dfrac{0.60}{8}}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.40 + 0.075} = \dfrac{1}{0.475}
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 2.105}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The system runs ~2.1× faster than pure software. The remaining bottleneck is the 40% sequential part — *no amount of hardware can speed that up*.

**Step 4 — Power Perspective (1.5 marks):**
- The **Cortex-M0+** draws ~3 mA active at 48 MHz → ~10 mW.
- The **HW FFT block** at 100 MHz, with $C_L=50\text{ pF}$, $\alpha=0.3$, $V_{dd}=1.2\text{ V}$:

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.3 \times 50\times10^{-12} \times (1.2)^{2} \times 100\times10^{6} = 2.16\text{ mW}
$$

- Combined accelerator-in-Cortex system: ~12 mW vs. ~150 mW if the same workload ran on a 1 GHz application processor.
- For a 100 mAh battery (360 C of charge), ~12 mW draw → ~30 hours of life — *acceptable for a wearable*.

**Step 5 — Final Design Recommendation (½ mark):**
Use the **HW FFT accelerator on a low-power MCU** — it gives ~2× speedup *and* longer battery life.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: **Real-Time Constraints and Power Optimization in ASES**

**Question:**
*With a neat power-state diagram, explain the power-management mechanism in an Application-Specific Embedded System. Discuss the role of clock gating, dynamic voltage-frequency scaling (DVFS), and sleep modes in extending battery life. Compute the energy saved by switching from Active (50 MHz, 3.3 V) to Sleep (RTC-only, 0.5 mW) for 99% of a 1-hour mission.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Power-State Diagram (1.5 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACTIVE
    ACTIVE --> IDLE: Task done
    IDLE --> SLEEP: Idle timer
    SLEEP --> DEEP_SLEEP: Long inactivity
    DEEP_SLEEP --> ACTIVE: RTC wake
    SLEEP --> ACTIVE: External IRQ
    IDLE --> ACTIVE: New task
```

**Step 2 — Active-Mode Power (1.5 marks):**
Assume $\alpha = 0.2$, $C_L = 20\text{ pF}$, $V_{dd} = 3.3\text{ V}$, $f_{clk} = 50\text{ MHz}$.

$$
P_{active} = 0.2 \times 20\times10^{-12} \times (3.3)^{2} \times 50\times10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{active} = 0.2 \times 20\times10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 50\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{active} \approx 2.178\text{ mW}}
$$

**Step 3 — Sleep-Mode Power (½ mark):**
Given as $P_{sleep} = 0.5\text{ mW}$ (RTC + RAM retention only).

**Step 4 — Mission Energy Calculation (2.5 marks):**
Mission duration: $T = 1\text{ hour} = 3600\text{ s}$.
Active fraction: $1\% = 36\text{ s}$. Sleep fraction: $99\% = 3564\text{ s}$.

$$
E_{active} = P_{active} \times t_{active} = 2.178\text{ mW} \times 36\text{ s} = 78.4\text{ mJ}
$$

$$
E_{sleep} = P_{sleep} \times t_{sleep} = 0.5\text{ mW} \times 3564\text{ s} = 1782\text{ mJ}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 78.4 + 1782 = 1860.4\text{ mJ} \approx 1.86\text{ J}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**
If the system ran continuously in active mode:

$$
E_{all\_active} = 2.178\text{ mW} \times 3600\text{ s} = 7840.8\text{ mJ} \approx 7.84\text{ J}
$$

**Energy saved:**

$$
\Delta E = 7840.8 - 1860.4 = 5980.4\text{ mJ} \approx 5.98\text{ J}
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \dfrac{5.98}{7.84} \times 100\% \approx 76.3\%
$$

> [!NOTE] KTU Interpretation
> By sleeping 99% of the time, the ASES saves **~76% of the energy** — equivalent to extending battery life from ~6 hours to ~25 hours on the same cell. This is the central philosophy of ASES power design.

**Step 5 — Role of Techniques (1 mark):**
- **Clock Gating** — disables the clock to idle blocks → no dynamic switching → zero $P_{dyn}$.
- **DVFS** — reduces $V_{dd}$ and $f_{clk}$ proportionally for non-critical work, gaining *quadratic* power savings (since $P \propto V^2 f$).
- **Sleep Modes** — turn off CPU core, retain RAM/RTC; leakage-only power.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: **Case Study — Smart Card Application-Specific System**

**Question:**
*Explain the architecture of a smart card as an Application-Specific Embedded System. Discuss the security features, the role of the crypto-coprocessor, and the ISO/IEC 7816 communication protocol. List any four industrial applications of smart cards.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture Overview (1.5 marks):**
A smart card contains:
- A **secure microcontroller** (often 8/32-bit, e.g., ARM SC300).
- **ROM** (OS + applet code, 16–256 KB).
- **EEPROM/Flash** (user data, 8–128 KB).
- **RAM** (volatile workspace, 1–8 KB).
- **Crypto-coprocessor** (DES, AES, RSA, ECC accelerators).
- **I/O port** (single contact or contactless RF antenna).
- **True Random Number Generator (TRNG)** for keys.

**Step 2 — Why It Is an ASES (1 mark):**
A smart card's hardware, OS, and I/O are *all* designed for one purpose — secure credential management. There is no general-purpose OS, no display, no user-installed apps beyond pre-loaded applets.

**Step 3 — Security Features (1.5 marks):**
- **Hardware tamper resistance** — mesh of sensors detect voltage glitching, frequency manipulation, light exposure, and physical probing.
- **Memory encryption** — bus traffic between CPU and memory is encrypted.
- **Crypto-coprocessor** — performs RSA-2048, AES-128, SHA-256 in milliseconds without burdening the CPU; sensitive key material never leaves the coprocessor boundary.
- **Liveness detection** — challenges the reader to prove the card is not a clone.
- **Secure boot** — verifies ROM/Flash integrity at power-on.

**Step 4 — ISO/IEC 7816 Protocol (1.5 marks):**
- **Part 1** — physical dimensions and contact layout.
- **Part 2** — electrical signals and transmission protocols (T=0 byte-oriented, T=1 block-oriented).
- **Part 3** — application selection, file system structure (MF, DF, EF).
- **Part 4** — APDU command structure (CLA, INS, P1, P2, Lc, Data, Le).
- Communication is **half-duplex, master-slave**, with the reader always being master.

**Step 5 — Four Industrial Applications (1 mark, ¼ each):**
1. **SIM cards** in mobile phones (GSM/LTE authentication).
2. **Debit/Credit payment cards** (EMV chip-and-PIN).
3. **National ID cards** (e.g., Aadhaar, e-passports).
4. **Public-transit ticketing** and **access-control badges**.

**Step 6 — Power Note (½ mark):**
A contactless smart card harvests RF energy from the reader's field (13.56 MHz) — there is **no battery**, which is a unique ASES design constraint.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on "Application-Specific Embedded System"
> 1. **Conflating "Embedded" with "Application-Specific"** — *all* ASES are embedded, but not all embedded systems are application-specific. A Raspberry Pi running a generic web server is embedded but *not* application-specific.
> 2. **Forgetting the distinction between hard and soft real-time** — always classify. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you use "real-time" without qualifier.
> 3. **Skipping diagrams in Part B** — a Part-B answer without a labelled diagram loses **at least 1 mark**, even if the text is correct.
> 4. **Unit mistakes in power calculations** — always write **mW or µW**; writing bare numbers like "648" without units is a guaranteed ½-mark deduction.
> 5. **Writing "ASIC = ASIP"** — this is a *fatal* conceptual error. ASIP has an ISA, ASIC does not.
> 6. **Ignoring NRE vs unit-cost trade-off** — KTU loves asking: *"When is an FPGA preferable to an ASIC?"* — answer with **volume + TTM**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP] High-Density Bullet Recap — Read Before Exam
> - ✅ **Definition** — ASES = HW + SW co-designed for *one* function.
> - ✅ **5 Metrics** — performance, power, cost, size, TTM.
> - ✅ **4 Sub-categories** — ASIC, ASIP, SoC, FPGA.
> - ✅ **9 Design Stages** — Spec → Model → Partition → HDL → SW → Verify → Implement → Test → Deploy.
> - ✅ **HW/SW Partitioning** is the *unique* step in ASES design.
> - ✅ **Dynamic Power** — $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{dd}^{2} f_{clk}$ (quadratic in $V_{dd}$).
> - ✅ **Amdahl's Law** — $S = 1 / \big[(1-f) + f/N\big]$; limit $= 1/(1-f)$.
> - ✅ **Real-time** — classify as *hard* (deadline = life) or *soft* (deadline = UX).
> - ✅ **Power States** — Active > Idle > Sleep > Deep-Sleep > Off.
> - ✅ **Clock Gating & DVFS** — primary low-power techniques in ASES.
> - ✅ **ISO 7816** — smart card protocol (T=0 byte, T=1 block).
> - ✅ **Crypto-coprocessor** — keeps secret keys inside a hardware boundary.
> - ✅ **NRE vs Volume** — ASIC wins only at high volume; FPGA wins at low volume.
> - ✅ **Energy per Task** — $E = P \cdot t_{exec}$; minimize both.
> - ✅ **MTTF** — reliability metric for safety-critical ASES.
> - ✅ **Real-world examples to quote** — pacemaker, ABS, ECG monitor, washing-machine controller, smart card, IoT sensor node.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Domain Specific Examples of Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Domain Specific Examples of Embedded System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domain-Specific Embedded System** is a computing system engineered with a tightly constrained set of hardware and software resources, purposefully designed and optimized to perform a dedicated function or a narrow family of functions within a particular application domain (such as automotive, healthcare, industrial automation, or consumer electronics), as classified by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems*.

The term **"domain-specific"** signifies that the embedded system is **NOT general-purpose** (unlike a desktop PC). Its hardware (microcontroller/Microprocessor, sensors, actuators, memory) and its software (firmware, RTOS, control algorithms) are co-designed to satisfy the **functional, performance, power, cost, and reliability constraints** of one specific application area.

In KTU parlance, these systems are evaluated using three cardinal metrics:

- **Functionality** — What the system does (control, monitoring, signal processing, communication).
- **Real-time Constraints** — Hard vs. Soft deadlines in responding to stimuli.
- **Resource Constraints** — Limited **ROM (typically 8 KB – 512 KB)**, **RAM (typically 2 KB – 64 KB)**, and **power budget (often < 1 W)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Specialized Mechanic vs. The General Doctor:**
>
> Imagine a hospital. A **General Physician** is like a **general-purpose computer** — they can handle fever, fractures, and heart issues with reasonable proficiency. Now consider a **Cardiothoracic Surgeon** — they have highly specialized tools (heart-lung machine, stents, scalpel of a particular type), a narrow but deep skill set, and operate in a sterile, purpose-built operation theatre.
>
> An **embedded system** is that **Cardiothoracic Surgeon**. It is *not* trying to do everything; it is trying to do *one job brilliantly* within strict constraints. The "domain" (heart surgery, in this case) is analogous to the **application area** — automotive, telecom, healthcare, etc. The specialist's tools map to the **microcontroller + sensors + actuators**, and the surgeon's training maps to the **firmware/embedded software**.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Domain-Specific Embedded Systems?

| **Reason** | **Explanation for KTU Context** |
|---|---|
| **Design Trade-off Insight** | Each domain forces a different *design sweet spot* (e.g., automotive prioritizes reliability; consumer electronics prioritizes cost). |
| **Industry Readiness** | KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) emphasizes employability; most Indian embedded industry openings (TCS, Wipro, Bosch, Continental, Samsung R&D) are domain-specific. |
| **Constraint Mapping** | Helps students understand how the same MCU (e.g., ARM Cortex-M3) is configured differently for a washing machine vs. a heart-rate monitor. |
| **CO Mapping** | Directly addresses **CO4** of PCRAT302 — *"Illustrate the design of domain-specific embedded systems using contemporary microcontrollers."* |

---

## 1.4 Broad Taxonomy of Domain-Specific Embedded Systems

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 categorizes embedded systems by **application domain**. The major classifications are:

1. **Consumer Electronics** — Washing machines, microwave ovens, smart TVs, set-top boxes, digital cameras.
2. **Automotive Embedded Systems (AES)** — Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Engine Control Unit (ECU), Airbag controller, Adaptive Cruise Control.
3. **Healthcare / Biomedical** — Pacemakers, infusion pumps, digital thermometers, MRI machines, wearable ECG monitors.
4. **Industrial Automation** — PLCs, SCADA systems, robotic arms, CNC machines, smart sensors.
5. **Telecommunications** — Mobile phones, routers, base stations, modems, VoIP gateways.
6. **Aerospace & Defense** — Flight control systems (FCS), missile guidance, UAVs, satellite subsystems.
7. **Smart Cards & RFID** — ATM cards, e-Passports, contactless payment, inventory tags.
8. **Networking / Office Automation** — Printers, fax machines, photocopiers, network switches.
9. **IoT & Wearables** — Smartwatches, fitness bands, smart home hubs, environmental sensors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Framework for Classifying Domain-Specific Systems

Every domain-specific embedded system is characterized by a unique combination of the following six design parameters. Mastering these is **the most KTU-relevant analytical skill** for Module 4.

### Parameter 1 — **Functional Density (FD)**
The number of distinct operations the system must perform per unit time.

$$\text{FD} = \frac{N_{\text{operations}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$$

where $N_{\text{operations}}$ is the count of distinct control/monitoring tasks, and $T_{\text{window}}$ is the observation time horizon.

> **Example:** An automotive ECU may execute ~150 operations per millisecond (fuel injection timing, knock detection, lambda sensing), giving $\text{FD} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{5}$ ops/sec. A digital thermometer may only have $\text{FD} \approx 10$ ops/sec.

---

### Parameter 2 — **Real-Time Hardness (RTH)**
A dimensionless classification score used in KTU 2024 Scheme to describe deadline criticality.

| **RTH Class** | **Deadline Miss Consequence** | **Example Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Real-Time** | Catastrophic (loss of life, system failure) | Airbag, Pacemaker, Flight Control |
| **Firm Real-Time** | Severe degradation, no value after deadline | Video frame rendering, real-time pricing |
| **Soft Real-Time** | Graceful degradation, late data still useful | Set-top box streaming, washing machine UI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Hard real-time systems **MUST** use a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) with deterministic scheduling (Rate Monotonic Scheduling, Earliest Deadline First) or run on a bare-metal scheduler. Soft real-time systems can tolerate a General-Purpose OS (GPOS) like Embedded Linux.

---

### Parameter 3 — **Power Budget (PB)**
The maximum average power the system is allowed to draw.

$$P_{\text{budget}} = V_{\text{supply}} \times I_{\text{avg}}$$

Typical KTU reference values:

- **Battery-operated wearables:** $P_{\text{budget}} \approx 1 \text{ mW} - 100 \text{ mW}$
- **Mains-powered consumer electronics:** $P_{\text{budget}} \approx 1 \text{ W} - 50 \text{ W}$
- **Automotive ECUs:** $P_{\text{budget}} \approx 5 \text{ W} - 25 \text{ W}$ per ECU

---

### Parameter 4 — **Memory Footprint (MF)**
Total on-chip and off-chip memory (Flash/ROM + RAM).

$$M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{ROM}} + M_{\text{RAM}}$$

---

### Parameter 5 — **Reliability / MTBF**
Mean Time Between Failures — a critical metric for safety-critical domains.

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$$

For aerospace systems, $\text{MTBF} \geq 10^{6}$ hours. For consumer electronics, $\text{MTBF} \approx 10^{4}$ hours is acceptable.

---

### Parameter 6 — **Cost of Unit (CoU)**
BOM (Bill of Materials) cost per device, a binding constraint in mass-market domains.

$$C_{\text{unit}} = C_{\text{MCU}} + C_{\text{sensors}} + C_{\text{PCB}} + C_{\text{packaging}} + C_{\text{assembly}}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the ESE on Module 4.

| **#** | **Formula / Metric** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Units** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Functional Density | $\text{FD} = N_{\text{ops}} / T_{\text{window}}$ | ops/sec | $10^{0}$ – $10^{6}$ |
| 2 | Power Budget | $P = V \times I$ | W (Watts) | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{2}$ |
| 3 | Energy per Operation | $E_{\text{op}} = P / f_{\text{clk}}$ | Joules/op | $10^{-9}$ – $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | Memory Footprint | $M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{ROM}} + M_{\text{RAM}}$ | Bytes | $10^{3}$ – $10^{7}$ |
| 5 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = T_{\text{op}} / N_{\text{failures}}$ | Hours | $10^{4}$ – $10^{6}$ |
| 6 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz | Domain-specific |
| 7 | Availability | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | 0.99 – 0.999999 (six 9s) |
| 8 | CPU Utilization | $U = (T_{\text{active}} / T_{\text{total}}) \times 100$ | Percent | 40% – 90% |
| 9 | Context Switch Time | $t_{\text{cs}} = k \cdot \text{cycles}$ | µs | 1 – 50 µs |
| 10 | Interrupt Latency | $t_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{recog}} + t_{\text{dispatch}}$ | µs | 0.5 – 10 µs |

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Real-World Engineering Utility

### 2.3.1 Automotive Embedded Systems (AES)

The **automotive domain** is the most-examined application in KTU Module 4. Modern vehicles contain **70–150 ECUs** (Electronic Control Units), networked via **CAN (Controller Area Network)** and **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** buses.

**Critical Sub-Systems:**

- **Engine Control Unit (ECU):** Controls fuel injection, ignition timing, idle speed, and emissions. Runs on a 32-bit MCU (e.g., Infineon TriCore, Freescale PowerPC) at $\sim 100\text{ MHz}$.
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS):** Hard real-time. Monitors wheel-speed sensors at $500\text{ Hz}$; releases brake pressure 15–25 times per second to prevent wheel lockup.
- **Airbag Controller (SRS — Supplemental Restraint System):** Hard real-time. Accelerometer data sampled at $f_{s} \geq 1\text{ kHz}$ must trigger airbag deployment within $\mathbf{15\text{ ms}}$ of crash detection.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Uses radar/lidar at $\sim 77\text{ GHz}$; closes the longitudinal control loop at $50\text{ Hz}$.
- **Electronic Stability Program (ESP):** Combines steering angle, yaw rate, and wheel speed sensors.

---

### 2.3.2 Healthcare / Biomedical Embedded Systems

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 High-Weight Topic:** Biomedical embedded systems are increasingly appearing in KTU ESE questions. Pay special attention to **pacemakers** and **infusion pumps**.

**Critical Sub-Systems:**

- **Pacemaker:** Detects intrinsic heart rhythm via electrodes; if bradycardia is detected, delivers a $0.5\text{ ms}$ – $2.5\text{ V}$ electrical pulse to stimulate the myocardium. **Hard real-time.** Battery life: **7 – 10 years** (the device is implanted; battery replacement requires surgery).
- **Infusion Pump:** Delivers IV medication at a controlled rate (e.g., $0.1\text{ mL/hr}$ to $999.9\text{ mL/hr}$). Uses a stepper motor driven by PWM signals from the MCU.
- **Digital Thermometer:** Uses a thermistor or thermocouple. ADC sampling at $\sim 10\text{ Hz}$ with $\pm 0.1°\text{C}$ accuracy.
- **Patient Monitoring System (ECG, SpO2, BP):** Multi-channel, samples at $f_{s} = 250\text{ Hz}$ to $1\text{ kHz}$ per channel. Uses DSP for QRS complex detection.
- **MRI / CT Scanner Control:** Massive embedded control system; gradient coil currents controlled with $1\text{ mA}$ resolution at $\sim 100\text{ kHz}$ switching.

---

### 2.3.3 Consumer Electronics

- **Washing Machine:** Microcontroller-based timer + motor controller. Drives an induction motor via a TRIAC or VFD. Typical MCUs: 8-bit (e.g., 8051 derivatives). Sensors: water level, temperature, door switch, vibration.
- **Microwave Oven:** Magnetron driven by a high-voltage inverter. The MCU controls cooking time, power level (PWM), and defrost cycles. Includes child-lock and sensor-cook modes.
- **Smart TV:** Quad-core ARM Cortex-A53/A55 running Embedded Linux (Tizen, WebOS, Android TV). Networked via Wi-Fi 6, HDMI-CEC, Bluetooth 5.0.
- **Set-Top Box:** DVB-S2/T2/C tuner → MPEG-4/H.265 decoder. Uses RTOS (e.g., STLinux) on a 32-bit SoC.

---

### 2.3.4 Industrial Automation

- **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC):** Ladder-logic based industrial controller. Cycle time $1\text{ ms}$ – $20\text{ ms}$. I/O counts from 16 to 4096.
- **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):** Hierarchical control with PLCs as field devices and a central HMI.
- **Robotic Arm Controller:** 6-DOF arm uses 6 servo motors driven by PWM at $50\text{ Hz}$. Trajectory planning runs on a 32-bit MCU/DSP.

---

### 2.3.5 Telecommunications

- **Mobile Phone (Smartphone):** Application processor (Cortex-A series) + baseband processor (Hexagon DSP). Multi-radio (5G NR, Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 5.3, NFC, GNSS).
- **Router / Modem:** Network processor (e.g., Qualcomm IPQ) running Embedded Linux with Netfilter/iptables. NAT, firewall, QoS implemented in firmware.
- **Base Station (BTS/eNodeB/gNodeB):** Real-time baseband processing on FPGAs/ASICs with control plane on a general-purpose CPU.

---

### 2.3.6 Smart Cards & RFID

> [!NOTE]
> A **smart card** is a tamper-resistant single-chip embedded system. The chip has CPU (e.g., 8051 core at $\sim 25\text{ MHz}$), ROM ($\sim 200\text{ KB}$), RAM ($\sim 6\text{ KB}$), and EEPROM ($\sim 64\text{ KB}$). Implements ISO/IEC 7816 (contact) or ISO/IEC 14443 (contactless, NFC) protocols. Cryptographic operations (RSA, AES, ECC) are performed in hardware co-processors.

**Sub-categories:** SIM cards, ATM cards (EMV chip), e-Passports, contactless payment cards, ID cards (Aadhaar in India).

---

### 2.3.7 Aerospace & Defense

- **Flight Control System (FCS):** **Triple or quadruple modular redundancy (TMR/QMR)** to survive single-point failures. Uses **ARINC 429 / MIL-STD-1553** data buses. Hard real-time, certified to **DO-178C** (software) and **DO-254** (hardware).
- **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) / Drone:** Flight controller (STM32 / Pixhawk), IMU (gyro + accelerometer + magnetometer), GPS, barometer, optical flow sensor. Runs PX4 or Ardupilot firmware.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sampling Rate Derivation for an ECG Monitor

**Problem (KTU 2024 Exam Pattern):**
> An ECG (electrocardiogram) monitoring embedded system samples the cardiac waveform. The QRS complex contains frequency components up to $f_{\text{max}} = 125 \text{ Hz}$. Compute the minimum sampling rate as per the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**, and determine the ADC bits required for a $\pm 5 \text{ mV}$ input range with a resolution of $10 \text{ µV}$.

### Step 1 — Recall the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem

The theorem states that a bandlimited signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency is at least twice the highest frequency component present in the signal:

$$f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Numerical Value

The maximum frequency of the QRS complex is given as $f_{\text{max}} = 125 \text{ Hz}$. Substituting into the formula:

$$f_{s,\text{min}} = 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}} = 2 \cdot 125 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{s,\text{min}} = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Engineering Safety Factor

In biomedical embedded design, KTU-recommended practice is to oversample by a factor of $k = 2$ to $k = 4$ above the Nyquist limit, to allow for practical **anti-aliasing filter roll-off**:

$$f_{s,\text{design}} = k \cdot f_{s,\text{min}}$$

Taking $k = 4$ for a clinically robust ECG monitor:

$$f_{s,\text{design}} = 4 \cdot 250 \text{ Hz} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Required ADC Resolution

The Least Significant Bit (LSB) of the ADC must be less than or equal to the desired voltage resolution:

$$\text{LSB}_{\text{ADC}} \leq 10 \text{ µV}$$

The full-scale input range is $\pm 5 \text{ mV}$, which gives a peak-to-peak range of:

$$V_{\text{FS}} = 5 \text{ mV} - (-5 \text{ mV}) = 10 \text{ mV}$$

The relationship between ADC resolution $N$ (in bits), full-scale voltage, and LSB is:

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{N}}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $N$

$$\frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{N}} \leq 10 \text{ µV}$$

$$2^{N} \geq \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{10 \text{ µV}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}{10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}}$$

$$2^{N} \geq 1000$$

Taking logarithm base 2 on both sides:

$$N \geq \log_{2}(1000)$$

$$N \geq \frac{\log_{10}(1000)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3}{0.30103}$$

$$N \geq 9.966 \text{ bits}$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, we round up:

$$N = 10 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer

- **Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist):** $f_{s,\text{min}} = 250 \text{ Hz}$
- **Practical sampling rate (with $k = 4$ oversampling):** $f_{s,\text{design}} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$
- **Required ADC resolution:** $N = 10 \text{ bits}$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hard Real-Time Deadline Analysis for an Airbag System

**Problem:**
> An airbag controller is designed using the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm on a single-core MCU. The system has three periodic tasks: (i) crash-detection sensor sampling with period $T_{1} = 5 \text{ ms}$, (ii) ECU diagnostic with $T_{2} = 20 \text{ ms}$, and (iii) deployment actuator check with $T_{3} = 100 \text{ ms}$. Compute their respective utilizations and check if the system is schedulable.

### Step 1 — Recall the Utilization Bound Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973)

For $n$ periodic tasks scheduled by RMS, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for schedulability is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_{i}}{T_{i}} \leq n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

where $C_{i}$ is the worst-case execution time (WCET) of task $i$ and $T_{i}$ is its period.

### Step 2 — List Task Parameters

Assume the WCETs are: $C_{1} = 1 \text{ ms}$, $C_{2} = 3 \text{ ms}$, $C_{3} = 5 \text{ ms}$.

| **Task** | **Period $T_{i}$** | **WCET $C_{i}$** | **Utilization $U_{i}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_{1}$ (Crash sensor) | 5 ms | 1 ms | 0.200 |
| $\tau_{2}$ (ECU diag) | 20 ms | 3 ms | 0.150 |
| $\tau_{3}$ (Deploy check) | 100 ms | 5 ms | 0.050 |

### Step 3 — Compute Each Individual Utilization

$$U_{1} = \frac{C_{1}}{T_{1}} = \frac{1 \text{ ms}}{5 \text{ ms}} = 0.200$$

$$U_{2} = \frac{C_{2}}{T_{2}} = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.150$$

$$U_{3} = \frac{C_{3}}{T_{3}} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{100 \text{ ms}} = 0.050$$

### Step 4 — Compute Total Utilization

$$U_{\text{total}} = U_{1} + U_{2} + U_{3}$$

$$U_{\text{total}} = 0.200 + 0.150 + 0.050 = 0.400$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Liu-Layland Upper Bound for $n = 3$

$$U_{\text{bound}} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$$

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

### Step 6 — Compare and Conclude

$$U_{\text{total}} = 0.400 \leq U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7798$$

> **Conclusion:** The system **is schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The CPU has approximately $40\%$ utilization, leaving $\sim 60\%$ headroom for handling interrupts and aperiodic events (such as the actual crash-triggered airbag deployment command).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Power Budget Calculation for a Wearable Fitness Tracker

**Problem:**
> A fitness tracker (wearable) uses a Cortex-M0+ MCU with active current $I_{\text{act}} = 3 \text{ mA}$ at $V = 3.0 \text{ V}$, sleep current $I_{\text{sleep}} = 5 \text{ µA}$, and an OLED display with $I_{\text{display}} = 12 \text{ mA}$. The MCU is active $0.5\%$ of the time, the display is on $1\%$ of the time, and the rest of the time it sleeps. The battery is $100 \text{ mAh}$ at $3.0 \text{ V}$. Calculate the average current draw and the battery life in days.

### Step 1 — Compute Time-Weighted Average Current

$$\bar{I} = (p_{\text{act}} \cdot I_{\text{act}}) + (p_{\text{display}} \cdot I_{\text{display}}) + (p_{\text{sleep}} \cdot I_{\text{sleep}})$$

where $p_{\text{act}} = 0.005$, $p_{\text{display}} = 0.01$, and $p_{\text{sleep}} = 0.985$.

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\bar{I} = (0.005 \cdot 3 \text{ mA}) + (0.01 \cdot 12 \text{ mA}) + (0.985 \cdot 0.005 \text{ mA})$$

$$\bar{I} = 0.015 \text{ mA} + 0.120 \text{ mA} + 0.004925 \text{ mA}$$

$$\bar{I} = 0.139925 \text{ mA} \approx 140 \text{ µA}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Average Power

$$\bar{P} = \bar{I} \cdot V = 0.140 \text{ mA} \cdot 3.0 \text{ V} = 0.420 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Battery Life

$$t_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}}}{\bar{I}} = \frac{100 \text{ mAh}}{0.140 \text{ mA}}$$

$$t_{\text{life}} = 714.3 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Days

$$t_{\text{life}} = \frac{714.3 \text{ hours}}{24 \text{ hours/day}} \approx 29.76 \text{ days}$$

> **Conclusion:** The wearable will operate for approximately **30 days** on a single $100 \text{ mAh}$ charge — a typical industry benchmark for entry-level fitness bands.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hard vs. Soft Real-Time Domains

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix to structure answers in the KTU ESE.

| **Property** | **Hard RT Domain (e.g., Airbag, Pacemaker)** | **Soft RT Domain (e.g., Smart TV, Set-top Box)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadline Consequence** | System failure or loss of life | Degraded UX, no value |
| **OS Used** | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, bare-metal | Embedded Linux, Android, Tizen |
| **Latency Requirement** | < 10 µs (interrupt dispatch) | < 50 ms (UI thread) |
| **Jitter Tolerance** | < 1% | < 10% |
| **Failure Mode** | Fail-safe state (e.g., default open circuit) | Graceful degradation (e.g., buffered playback) |
| **Certification** | ISO 26262 (auto), DO-178C (aero), IEC 62304 (medical) | CE/FCC, Energy Star |
| **Power Strategy** | Latency over power (wake-on-interrupt) | Power over latency (DVFS, big.LITTLE) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: List any three domain-specific examples of embedded systems and state one distinguishing design constraint for each.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **#** | **Domain Example** | **Distinguishing Design Constraint** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** in an automobile | **Hard real-time** deadline — must respond to wheel-lock within **$\mathbf{\leq 15 \text{ ms}}$** to prevent skidding. |
| 2 | **Cardiac Pacemaker** | **Ultra-low power** — battery life of **7–10 years** is mandatory since the device is implanted and non-replaceable in the field. |
| 3 | **Smart Card (EMV chip in ATM card)** | **Tamper resistance and cryptographic security** — must implement hardware RSA/AES accelerators to prevent side-channel attacks. |

*Distribution: (1 mark per correct example + constraint pair. Three pairs = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

> **Q: Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time embedded systems with one example each.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Hard Real-Time** | **Soft Real-Time** |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadline** | **Must** be met; missing it causes **catastrophic failure** | Deadlines are **desirable**; missing them causes **degraded performance** |
| **Example** | **Airbag deployment system** in a car | **Video streaming** on a smart TV set-top box |
| **OS** | RTOS (VxWorks, QNX) or bare-metal scheduler | GPOS (Embedded Linux) is acceptable |
| **Verification** | Formal methods, schedulability analysis (Rate Monotonic) | Statistical or empirical testing |

*Distribution: 1 mark for hard real-time defn+example, 1 mark for soft real-time defn+example, 1 mark for OS/verification difference.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are two alternative choices — **OR Question A OR Question B**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **automotive Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**. List the sensors, actuators, MCU, and communication bus used, and discuss the hard real-time deadline constraint. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design a **wearable heart-rate monitoring system** based on a microcontroller. Specify the sensor, ADC sampling rate (using Nyquist's theorem for $f_{\text{max}} = 10 \text{ Hz}$), and estimate the average current and battery life for a $200 \text{ mAh}$ battery. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — ABS Architecture (7 Marks)**

**1. Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    ws1["WHEEL SPEED SENSOR FRONT LEFT"]:::sensor
    ws2["WHEEL SPEED SENSOR FRONT RIGHT"]:::sensor
    ws3["WHEEL SPEED SENSOR REAR LEFT"]:::sensor
    ws4["WHEEL SPEED SENSOR REAR RIGHT"]:::sensor
    mcu["32 BIT MCU INFINEON AURIX TC275"]:::core
    hbridge["HYDRAULIC H BRIDGE VALVE ACTUATOR"]:::actuator
    canbus["CAN BUS 500 KBPS"]:::comm
    dash["DASHBOARD WARNING LAMP"]:::display
    ws1 --> mcu
    ws2 --> mcu
    ws3 --> mcu
    ws4 --> mcu
    mcu --> hbridge
    mcu --> canbus --> dash
    classDef sensor fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef display fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**2. Components (2 Marks):**

- **Sensors:** 4× wheel-speed sensors (variable reluctance, $0.5\text{ Hz}$ to $2500\text{ Hz}$ output).
- **MCU:** Infineon AURIX TC275 (TriCore, 32-bit, lockstep dual-core, 200 MHz, ISO 26262 ASIL-D compliant).
- **Actuator:** Hydraulic modulator with solenoid valves (intake, dump, isolation) and an electric pump.
- **Communication:** CAN bus at **500 kbps** to other ECUs (engine, transmission, dashboard).

**3. Real-Time Constraint (2 Marks):**

The control loop runs at **$f_{\text{loop}} = 50 \text{ Hz}$**, i.e., one cycle every **$T_{\text{loop}} = 20 \text{ ms}$**. The system must:
- Sample all 4 wheel speeds.
- Compute slip ratio $\lambda = (v_{\text{vehicle}} - v_{\text{wheel}}) / v_{\text{vehicle}}$.
- Decide release/apply/retain command.
- Drive the actuator within this 20 ms window. **Deadline is hard** — missing it may result in wheel lockup and skid.

#### **Part (b) — Heart-Rate Monitor Design (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Sensor Selection (1 Mark):**
Use a **PPG (Photoplethysmography) sensor** (e.g., MAX30100) with green LED ($530 \text{ nm}$) and photodiode, worn on the wrist/fingertip.

**Step 2 — Sampling Rate (2 Marks):**

Maximum heart-rate signal frequency is $f_{\text{max}} = 10 \text{ Hz}$ (a healthy heart beats at $0.5 - 4 \text{ Hz}$, so we set $f_{\text{max}} = 10 \text{ Hz}$ to include harmonics).

$$f_{s,\text{min}} = 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}} = 2 \cdot 10 = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

Apply $k = 5$ oversampling for noise robustness:

$$f_{s,\text{design}} = 5 \cdot 20 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Power Budget (3 Marks):**

Assume MCU active current $I_{\text{act}} = 5 \text{ mA}$ (Cortex-M0+), sensor LED on-current $I_{\text{LED}} = 20 \text{ mA}$ (active only 5% of time, i.e., $p_{\text{LED}} = 0.05$), and sleep current $I_{\text{sleep}} = 10 \text{ µA}$.

MCU active fraction: $p_{\text{MCU}} = 0.10$.
Sleep fraction: $p_{\text{sleep}} = 0.85$.

$$\bar{I} = (0.10 \cdot 5) + (0.05 \cdot 20) + (0.85 \cdot 0.01)$$

$$\bar{I} = 0.50 + 1.00 + 0.0085 = 1.5085 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Battery Life (1 Mark):**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \frac{200 \text{ mAh}}{1.51 \text{ mA}} = 132.5 \text{ hours} \approx 5.5 \text{ days}$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative OR)** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a **smart card** embedded system. List the components and discuss security features like cryptographic co-processors and TRNG. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the architecture of a **digital camera** as an embedded system. List the image sensor, image signal processor (ISP), memory, and storage interfaces, and discuss the data rate requirements for an 8 MP sensor at 30 fps. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Smart Card Architecture (7 Marks)**

**1. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    c1["CONTACT PAD ISO 7816"]:::ext
    c2["UART IO 9600 BPS TO 115 KBPS"]:::comm
    c3["SECURE 8051 CORE 25 MHZ"]:::core
    c4["ROM 200 KB"]:::mem
    c5["RAM 6 KB"]:::mem
    c6["EEPROM 64 KB"]:::mem
    c7["AES DES RSA ECC CRYPTO HW"]:::secure
    c8["TRNG RNG"]:::secure
    c1 --> c2 --> c3
    c3 --> c4
    c3 --> c5
    c3 --> c6
    c3 --> c7
    c3 --> c8
    classDef ext fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef secure fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**2. Components (2 Marks):**
- **Contact Pad (VCC, RST, CLK, I/O, GND)** — per ISO/IEC 7816-3.
- **CPU:** Secure 8-bit 8051 core running at **$5\text{ MHz}$ – $25\text{ MHz}$**.
- **Memory:** ROM (OS, ~200 KB), RAM (scratch, ~6 KB), EEPROM (user data + keys, ~64 KB, 500,000 erase cycles endurance).
- **Crypto co-processor:** Hardware AES-128/256, RSA-2048, ECC-P256.
- **TRNG (True Random Number Generator):** Entropy source for key generation.
- **Security sensors:** Voltage glitch detector, frequency monitor, light sensor, temperature sensor.

**3. Security Features (3 Marks):**
- **Tamper resistance:** Mesh of sensor lines on the chip detects physical intrusion.
- **Hardware crypto:** Side-channel attack resistance (constant-time operations, masking).
- **Secure storage:** EEPROM write-protected by hardware fuse keys.
- **TRNG:** Non-deterministic source for one-time pads, key generation.
- **Life-cycle states:** Manufacturing → Personalization → User → End-of-life (set by OS, not user).
- **Certifications:** Common Criteria EAL5+ / EAL6+ (e.g., for banking and e-Passport).

#### **Part (b) — Digital Camera Architecture (7 Marks)**

**1. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    lens["LENS OPTICAL ASSEMBLY"]:::opt
    cmos["CMOS IMAGE SENSOR 8 MP 1.2 INCH"]:::sensor
    isp["IMAGE SIGNAL PROCESSOR ISP"]:::core
    dsp["DSP FOR JPEG COMPRESSION"]:::core
    ddr["DDR SDRAM 512 MB BUFFER"]:::mem
    sdc["SD CARD CONTROLLER SDXC"]:::mem
    lcd["LCD DISPLAY HDMI OUTPUT"]:::display
    mcu["CONTROL MCU FOR UI"]:::core
    lens --> cmos --> isp --> dsp --> ddr
    ddr --> sdc
    isp --> lcd
    mcu --> isp
    classDef opt fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef display fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**2. Components (2 Marks):**
- **Image Sensor:** 8 MP CMOS, e.g., Sony IMX477. Outputs Bayer-pattern RAW data.
- **ISP (Image Signal Processor):** Performs demosaicing, noise reduction, white balance, lens shading correction, tone mapping.
- **DSP:** JPEG/HEIF encoding (ASIC or dedicated SoC block).
- **Memory:** DDR3/DDR4 SDRAM (e.g., 512 MB) as frame buffer.
- **Storage:** SD/SDXC card via UHS-I interface (up to 104 MB/s).
- **Display:** LCD panel and/or HDMI output (live view).
- **MCU:** Cortex-M series, runs the user interface, file system (FAT32/exFAT), and USB/Wi-Fi connectivity.

**3. Data Rate Calculation (3 Marks):**

For an **8 MP** sensor (let's take 8,000,000 pixels) at **30 fps**, with each pixel being 10-bit RAW:

$$\text{Data Rate} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ pixels} \times 30 \text{ fps} \times 10 \text{ bits/pixel}$$

$$\text{Data Rate} = 2.4 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/s} = 2.4 \text{ Gbps} = 300 \text{ MB/s}$$

For **4:2:2 YUV** (post-demosaic, 16 bpp) at 30 fps:

$$\text{Data Rate} = 8 \times 10^{6} \times 30 \times 16 = 3.84 \text{ Gbps} = 480 \text{ MB/s}$$

For **JPEG** compressed (~10:1 ratio):

$$\text{Data Rate} = \frac{480 \text{ MB/s}}{10} = 48 \text{ MB/s} \approx 384 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Conclusion:** The MIPI CSI-2 interface between sensor and ISP must support at least **2.4 Gbps** per lane; typically 2 or 4 lanes are used, giving 4.8 Gbps or 9.6 Gbps aggregate. The SD card interface must support at least **48 MB/s** to keep up with JPEG recording — easily handled by UHS-I (104 MB/s).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in PCRAT302 Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "embedded system" with "general-purpose computer":** Students often describe a laptop or desktop as an embedded system. A general-purpose computer is **NOT** an embedded system. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Missing the "real-time" qualifier:** A washing machine is a **soft real-time** system, NOT a hard real-time one. Mixing up the classification loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Forgetting the communication bus:** In an automotive architecture question, if you only describe the ECU and forget **CAN/LIN bus**, you lose 1–2 marks. The bus is what makes the system **networked** and **domain-specific**.
> 4. **Wrong Nyquist formula direction:** Writing $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ as $f_{s} \geq f_{\text{max}}/2$ is a **fatal** error that loses 2 marks.
