> 5. **No MTBF or reliability discussion:** For hard real-time/safety-critical systems (pacemaker, airbag), KTU examiners expect a line on **failure mode and reliability** — without it, you cap at 80% of the marks for that sub-part.
> 6. **Citing only the system, not the constraint:** "Pacemaker" alone is only 0.5 marks. The expected answer is "Pacemaker — hard real-time, 7–10 year battery life, ISO 62304 certified" — a 3-mark answer.
> 7. **Using programming code in non-CS answers:** Per KTU 2024 guidelines, **no Python/C/Java snippets** in theory answers — only **flowcharts, block diagrams, and structured English**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for ESE Module 4:**

- **Definition:** A domain-specific embedded system is a computing system **dedicated** to one application area with **constrained** hardware/software resources.
- **Key Design Parameters (the 6 pillars):**
  - **Functional Density** — ops/time.
  - **Real-Time Hardness** — hard / firm / soft.
  - **Power Budget** — $P = V \times I$.
  - **Memory Footprint** — $M_{\text{ROM}} + M_{\text{RAM}}$.
  - **Reliability (MTBF)** — $T_{\text{op}} / N_{\text{failures}}$.
  - **Unit Cost (BOM)** — sum of component costs.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ (always oversample by $k = 2-5\times$ in practice).
- **RMS Utilisation Bound (Liu-Layland):** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- **Automotive domain:** 70–150 ECUs, **CAN bus** at 500 kbps, **AURIX/RH850** MCUs, ISO 26262 certification.
- **Airbag deadline:** $\mathbf{\leq 15 \text{ ms}}$ from crash detection to deployment.
- **Pacemaker:** MSP430-class MCU, 7–10 year battery, 0.5–2.5 V pulse, 0.5–2.5 ms width.
- **Smart Card:** 8051 core, $\sim 200 \text{ KB ROM}$, $\sim 6 \text{ KB RAM}$, $\sim 64 \text{ KB EEPROM}$, hardware AES/RSA/ECC, TRNG, EAL5+/EAL6+ certified.
- **Smartphone:** Application processor (Cortex-A) + baseband DSP + multi-radio (5G, Wi-Fi, BT, NFC, GNSS).
- **Smart TV:** Cortex-A55 quad-core + Embedded Linux (Tizen/WebOS/Android TV).
- **Washing Machine:** 8051/RL78, 8-bit, sensors (water, temp, door, vib), TRIAC-driven motor.
- **UAV Flight Controller:** STM32F4 (Cortex-M4, 168 MHz), IMU at 1 kHz, PID loops, ESC at 50 Hz PWM.
- **Industrial PLC:** Ladder logic, cycle time 1–20 ms, 16–4096 I/O.
- **Domain vs. RT-class mapping** (the table in §2.4) is the single most exam-relevant artifact.
- **Failure modes for hard RT systems:** Fail-safe (open circuit, default-off) — must be mentioned in 14-mark answers.
- **Certifications to memorize:** ISO 26262 (auto), DO-178C (aero), IEC 62304 (medical), IEC 61508 (industrial), Common Criteria EAL (smart cards).
- **Memorize 3 examples each from:** consumer, automotive, biomedical, telecom, industrial, and smart-card domains — minimum for full marks on definition questions.
- **Always** quantify deadlines in **ms/µs** — qualitative "fast response" answers are penalized.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware-Software Co-Design** is the *concurrent* and *integrated* design methodology in which hardware and software components of an embedded system are designed **simultaneously** rather than sequentially, with the explicit goal of meeting system-level performance, cost, power, and time-to-market constraints through optimal partitioning and co-verification.

In the **traditional design flow**, hardware is designed first, frozen, and then software is written to control the pre-defined hardware. In **co-design**, both domains evolve together from a unified system specification, enabling the designer to dynamically decide which functions must run on **dedicated hardware** (ASICs, FPGAs, hardware accelerators) and which can be executed on a **general-purpose processor** running embedded software.

### Fundamental Issues (The Core Problems Addressed)

The "fundamental issues" in HW-SW co-design refer to the **canonical design challenges** that arise when integrating heterogeneous computational units. They form the **decision matrix** that any co-design framework must solve:

1. **Partitioning Problem** – Which functionality goes to hardware and which to software?
2. **Interface Synthesis** – How do the heterogeneous blocks communicate?
3. **Co-Simulation** – How are both domains validated together before fabrication?
4. **Co-Verification** – How is functional equivalence proven across domains?
5. **Design Representation / Specification** – How is the unified system described?
6. **Tool & Methodology Support** – What EDA frameworks enable the flow?
7. **Trade-off Analysis** – Performance vs Cost vs Power vs Flexibility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Restaurant Kitchen:**
>
> Imagine you are designing a new restaurant kitchen. You have two kinds of "workers":
> - **Hardware Chefs** (specialized equipment like a tandoor oven, espresso machine) — *extremely fast* for one specific dish, but inflexible and expensive to buy.
> - **Software Chefs** (versatile line cooks with a standard stove) — *flexible* and can cook anything, but slower at every single task.
>
> The **menu specification** is your system requirement. The **fundamental issue** is: *Which dishes should be assigned to the specialized hardware chefs, and which to the flexible line cooks?* You must also decide how the two groups **hand off plates** (interface), how you **taste-test** the integrated kitchen (co-simulation), and how you **prove the menu works end-to-end** (co-verification). Co-design is the art of making these decisions *together*, not one after the other.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Co-Design (Industry-Standard KPIs)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | $T_{exec}$ | Seconds / Cycles | Minimized |
| **Throughput** | $\Theta$ | Operations/sec | Maximized |
| **Power Consumption** | $P$ | Watts (W) or mW | Minimized |
| **Silicon Area / Cost** | $A$ | mm² or Gate Equivalents | Minimized |
| **Time-to-Market** | $t_{TTM}$ | Months | Minimized |
| **Flexibility / Reusability** | $F$ | Qualitative Index | Maximized |
| **Reliability** | $R(t)$ | Probability | Maximized |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **mutually conflicting** in nature — improving one often degrades another. The co-design problem is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimization** problem under constraints.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Co-Design Problem

The canonical co-design flow can be broken into **seven sequential but iterative phases**. Each phase exposes a "fundamental issue":

### Phase 1 — System Specification
- The behavior of the system is captured in a **single, unified, executable specification language** (e.g., SystemC, SpecC, VHDL-AMS, or SDL).
- **Fundamental Issue:** *Representation gap* — the spec must be abstract enough for design exploration yet precise enough for synthesis.

### Phase 2 — Partitioning (The Core Issue)
- The specification is divided into **HW components** and **SW components**.
- **Decision Criteria:**
  - Compute-intensive loops → Hardware (parallelism, pipelining).
  - Control-intensive, low-frequency tasks → Software (flexibility).
  - Data-dominated signal processing (DSP kernels) → Hardware accelerator.
  - Decision logic, exception handling → Software (microcontroller/ASIP).

### Phase 3 — Scheduling
- Each task (in either domain) is assigned a **start time** on its respective resource.
- **Fundamental Issue:** *Heterogeneous scheduling* — hardware and software have radically different timing models (clock-cycle deterministic vs preemptive RTOS).

### Phase 4 — Interface Synthesis
- Generates the **communication primitives** (bus protocols, FIFO buffers, shared memory, device drivers) that allow HW and SW to exchange data.
- **Fundamental Issue:** *Protocol mismatch* — hardware typically uses handshaking/buses, software uses function calls/RTOS APIs.

### Phase 5 — Co-Simulation
- The HW is modeled at the **RTL level** and the SW runs on an **Instruction Set Simulator (ISS)**. Both execute *concurrently* in a synchronized simulation environment.
- **Fundamental Issue:** *Speed mismatch* — RTL simulation is $10^3$–$10^6\times$ slower than native software execution.

### Phase 6 — Co-Verification
- Functional equivalence between the **unified specification** and the **partitioned implementation** is formally or empirically proven.
- **Fundamental Issue:** *State-space explosion* — full system verification is computationally intractable.

### Phase 7 — Prototyping & Implementation
- Final HW is fabricated (ASIC) or programmed (FPGA); final SW is cross-compiled and flashed to the target processor.

## 2.2 The Co-Design Trade-off Triangle

> [!NOTE]
> Almost every co-design decision pivots on the **PPAC Trade-off** — the impossibility of simultaneously maximizing all four axes.

$$
\text{Quality} = f(\text{Performance},\ \text{Power},\ \text{Area},\ \text{Cost})
$$

The fundamental principle:
- **Hardware** improves **Performance** and **Power Efficiency** but increases **Area/Cost** and reduces **Flexibility**.
- **Software** improves **Flexibility** and reduces **NRE Cost** but degrades **Performance** and **Power Efficiency**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HW vs SW Performance Ratio** | $S_{hw/sw} = \dfrac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}$ | Quantifies speed-up gained by moving a task to hardware. $S_{hw/sw} > 1$ implies HW is faster. |
| 2 | **Amdahl's Law (HW Acceleration)** | $S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{hw}}}$ | Bounds the maximum system speed-up from accelerating a fraction $f$ of the workload. |
| 3 | **Power-Optimized Partitioning Criterion** | $\text{Choose HW if } \dfrac{E_{sw}}{N_{exec}} > E_{hw,static} + E_{hw,dyn}$ | Decision rule: HW justified when per-execution SW energy exceeds HW's energy budget. |
| 4 | **Communication Overhead Cost** | $T_{comm} = N_{bytes} \cdot t_{byte} + T_{latency}$ | Total time penalty for inter-domain data transfer; reduces effective speed-up. |
| 5 | **Cost-Constrained Design Space** | $C_{total} = C_{hw} \cdot A_{hw} + C_{sw} \cdot LOC \le C_{budget}$ | Hard constraint on hardware area ($\text{mm}^2$) and software size (lines of code). |
| 6 | **Design Space Exploration (DSE) Size** | $\vert DSE \vert = 2^{N_{tasks}} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{M} k_i$ | Combinatorial explosion — $N$ tasks → $2^N$ possible HW/SW partitions. |
| 7 | **Co-Simulation Speed Penalty** | $S_{cosim} = \dfrac{T_{RTL}}{T_{native}} \approx 10^{3}\ \text{to}\ 10^{6}$ | Quantifies the slowdown of mixed-domain simulation. |
| 8 | **Utilization Factor (HW Block)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{idle}}$ | Idle HW wastes area and static power — must be $\ge 0.7$ for cost-effective HW. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** In every KTU answer, always state the **assumptions** before applying a formula. For example, Amdahl's Law assumes the accelerated portion is *perfectly parallelizable*, which is rarely true in co-design.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smartphones:** Co-design decides whether image processing runs on a dedicated ISP (hardware) or the application processor (software).
- **Automotive ECUs:** Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS) — control loops in software, sensor fusion in dedicated ASICs.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Always-on keyword detection uses a low-power HW accelerator; the main OS runs on an application core.
- **AI Accelerators (NPUs/TPUs):** Entire neural network inference is partitioned layer-by-layer between CPU and HW matrix-multiplication unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Co-Design Speed-Up

We derive the maximum achievable system speed-up when only a fraction $f$ of the workload is migrated from software to a hardware accelerator that is $S_{hw}$ times faster.

**Step 1 — Define the original execution time.**
Let the original pure-software execution time be $T_{sw}$. Without loss of generality, normalize it to $T_{sw} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Partition the workload into two fractions.**
$$
T_{sw} = T_{sw,fixed} + T_{sw,accel} = (1-f) + f
$$
where:
- $(1-f)$ is the fraction that **cannot** be accelerated (e.g., I/O waits, sequential dependencies).
- $f$ is the fraction that is migrated to the hardware accelerator.

**Step 3 — Apply the speed-up to the accelerated fraction.**
After HW acceleration, the accelerated portion takes $\dfrac{f}{S_{hw}}$ units of time. The non-accelerated portion remains $(1-f)$.

$$
T_{hw} = (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{hw}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the total system speed-up.**
Speed-up $S_{total}$ is the ratio of the original time to the new time:

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{hw}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Examine the asymptotic limit (the famous Amdahl's ceiling).**
As the hardware becomes infinitely fast ($S_{hw} \to \infty$):

$$
\lim_{S_{hw} \to \infty} S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with infinite hardware speed, the system speed-up is bounded by the **non-acceleratable fraction** $(1-f)$. If only 10% of the code is sequential, $f = 0.9$, then the absolute maximum speed-up is $1/(1-0.9) = 10\times$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important equation** in co-design. It tells the designer: *accelerating the wrong part of the code wastes silicon.*

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Deciding HW vs SW for a FIR Filter

**Problem Statement:**
A DSP application performs a 64-tap FIR filter on 16-bit audio samples at a sampling rate of 44.1 kHz.
- Pure software execution on an ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz: takes $T_{sw} = 18\ \mu\text{s}$ per sample.
- A custom hardware FIR accelerator runs in $T_{hw} = 0.8\ \mu\text{s}$ per sample.
- The accelerator consumes $5\ \text{mm}^2$ silicon area costing ₹50 per mm² in production.

**Step 1 — Compute the speed-up ratio.**
$$
S_{hw/sw} = \dfrac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}} = \dfrac{18\ \mu\text{s}}{0.8\ \mu\text{s}} = 22.5
$$

**Step 2 — Check the real-time constraint.**
The audio sample period is:
$$
T_{sample} = \dfrac{1}{44.1\ \text{kHz}} = 22.68\ \mu\text{s}
$$
- Software alone: $18\ \mu\text{s} < 22.68\ \mu\text{s}$ → **Feasible with 20% CPU slack**.
- Hardware alone: $0.8\ \mu\text{s} \ll 22.68\ \mu\text{s}$ → **Massive slack, but expensive**.

**Step 3 — Apply Amdahl's Law for a hypothetical mixed partition.**
Suppose we can accelerate 80% of the FIR (the inner multiply-accumulate loop), leaving 20% as sequential setup code:
$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{22.5}} = \dfrac{1}{0.2 + 0.0356} = \dfrac{1}{0.2356} \approx 4.24
$$

**Step 4 — Cost-benefit analysis.**
The hardware accelerator costs ₹250 extra per chip. If the product ships in volumes of 100,000 units:
$$
\Delta C_{total} = 5\ \text{mm}^2 \times ₹50/\text{mm}^2 \times 100{,}000 = ₹2.5\ \text{Crore}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering decision matrix.**

| Criterion | Software | Hardware | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Meets real-time? | Yes (with margin) | Yes (huge margin) | Tie |
| Cost | ₹0 | ₹250/unit | **SW** |
| Power efficiency | Moderate | High | **HW** |
| Flexibility (reprogrammable) | Full | None | **SW** |
| Performance headroom | Low | Very High | **HW** |

**Conclusion:** For a low-volume, flexibility-critical product (e.g., a prototype or a multi-standard device), **software wins**. For a high-volume, power-critical product (e.g., a hearable or a sensor node), **hardware wins** despite the cost.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The Seven Fundamental Issues at a Glance

| # | Fundamental Issue | Question Posed | Typical Solution / Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specification Heterogeneity** | How to describe HW & SW in one model? | SystemC, SpecC, UML/MARTE |
| 2 | **Partitioning** | What goes to HW vs SW? | Heuristic search, ILP, Genetic Algorithms |
| 3 | **Scheduling** | When does each task execute? | List scheduling, RTOS-based |
| 4 | **Interface Synthesis** | How do HW & SW talk? | Bus generators, device-driver synthesis |
| 5 | **Co-Simulation** | How to simulate together? | HW-SW co-simulators (e.g., Questa, Verilator + GDB) |
| 6 | **Co-Verification** | Is the design correct? | Assertion-based verification, formal equivalence |
| 7 | **Design Space Exploration** | What is the optimal design point? | Multi-objective optimization (Pareto fronts) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a 14-mark question asks about "fundamental issues," the examiner expects the student to **list all seven** with a *one-line definition* for each, followed by a *deep-dive* on **partitioning and co-simulation** — these are the two highest-weightage sub-issues.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical HW-SW Co-Design Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **iterative co-design methodology** — note the explicit feedback loop between partitioning, estimation, and co-simulation, which is the *defining feature* of co-design (absent in the traditional flow).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unified System Specification] --> B[Functional Modeling]
    B --> C[Initial HW SW Partitioning]
    C --> D[Architecture Selection]
    D --> E[Performance Power Area Estimation]
    E --> F{Meets Constraints?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Interface Synthesis]
    G --> H[HW Synthesis and SW Compilation]
    H --> I[Co Simulation]
    I --> J[Co Verification]
    J --> K{Verified Correct?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Prototype Implementation]
    L --> M[ASIC Fabrication or FPGA Bitstream]
    L --> N[ROM Flash Firmware Image]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style K fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style L fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Co-Designed Embedded System

The figure below maps the **functional interaction** between a generic embedded system's HW and SW domains, showing the *bridge elements* (drivers, bus interfaces, RTOS) that co-design must generate automatically.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HW_DOMAIN["HW DOMAIN Custom Silicon"]
        A1[ASIC or HW Accelerator]
        A2[FPGA Coprocessor]
        A3[DMA Controller]
        A4[Memory Controller]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE["INTERFACE BRIDGE Co Designed"]
        B1[Bus Wrapper AHB AXI]
        B2[Device Driver Layer]
        B3[Interrupt Controller]
        B4[Dual Port RAM or FIFO]
    end

    subgraph SW_DOMAIN["SW DOMAIN Processor Core"]
        C1[Application Layer]
        C2[Middleware or RTOS]
        C3[Bootloader]
        C4[ISR Routines]
    end

    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B1
    A3 --- B4
    A4 --- B4
    B1 --- B2
    B2 --- C2
    B3 --- C4
    B4 --- B2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C4 --> C2
    C2 --> B2

    style HW_DOMAIN fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
    style SW_DOMAIN fill:#dbeafe,color:#000000
    style BRIDGE fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style B1 fill:#fb923c,color:#000000
    style B2 fill:#fb923c,color:#000000
    style B3 fill:#fb923c,color:#000000
    style B4 fill:#fb923c,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 The Co-Design Decision Tree (Partitioning Heuristic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[New Functional Task] --> Q2{Is it compute intensive inner loop?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Meets real time deadline in SW?}
    Q3 -- No --> HW1[Map to HW Accelerator]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Power budget critical?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> HW1
    Q4 -- No --> SW1[Keep in SW]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5{Is it control flow or decision logic?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> SW1
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Does it require frequent updates?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> SW1
    Q6 -- No --> HW1

    style HW1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style SW1 fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    style Q2 fill:#fde68a,color:#000000
    style Q3 fill:#fde68a,color:#000000
    style Q4 fill:#fde68a,color:#000000
    style Q5 fill:#fde68a,color:#000000
    style Q6 fill:#fde68a,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The "fundamental issue" of partitioning is reduced to traversing a decision tree. In **classical co-design** (Kalavade, De Micheli), this is automated using *Design Space Exploration (DSE)* engines that prune the search tree using cost models.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define Hardware-Software Co-Design. List any four fundamental issues addressed by it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Keyword-Driven):**

Hardware-Software Co-Design is the **concurrent design** of hardware and software components of an embedded system starting from a **unified specification**, with the goal of jointly optimizing **performance, power, cost, and time-to-market**.

The four fundamental issues are:
1. **System Specification** — single-language unified description.
2. **HW-SW Partitioning** — assigning tasks to hardware or software.
3. **Interface Synthesis** — generating the communication glue logic.
4. **Co-Simulation and Co-Verification** — validating the mixed-domain system.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 1 mark; any 4 issues = 2 marks (0.5 each). Use *bold* keywords for full credit.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**State and explain Amdahl's Law in the context of HW-SW co-design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Amdahl's Law gives the **theoretical upper bound** on the speed-up obtainable by accelerating a fraction $f$ of the workload using hardware that is $S_{hw}$ times faster:

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{hw}}}
$$

It implies that the overall system speed-up is limited by the **non-accelerated sequential portion** $(1-f)$. For example, if 90% of the code is parallelizable in hardware, the maximum speed-up is $10\times$ — *no matter how fast the hardware is*.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme:** Formula = 1 mark; Meaning of $f$ and $S_{hw}$ = 1 mark; Limitation/implication = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** *Co-Design* with *Co-Simulation*. Co-Design is the **methodology**; Co-Simulation is *one* step within it.
> - **Do NOT write generic answers** like "Hardware is fast, software is flexible." Examiners award marks for **quantitative** reasoning (Amdahl's Law, cost equations, area numbers).
> - **Always draw a labelled block diagram.** Questions without diagrams typically lose **2–3 marks** out of 14.
> - Mention **at least one tool/language** (SystemC, SpecC, VHDL, Verilator) — this is a frequent 2-mark differentiator.
> - For partitioning questions, **show a worked numerical example** (like the FIR filter above). Theoretical-only answers cap at ~9/14.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Comprehensive — Recommended Choice]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **traditional design flow** versus the **co-design methodology** for embedded systems. Use a block diagram to illustrate the iterative feedback loop. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **HW-SW partitioning problem** as a fundamental issue. Discuss how **Amdahl's Law** bounds the achievable speed-up. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Traditional Flow vs Co-Design Flow (7 marks)**

**Traditional (Sequential) Flow:**
1. Hardware is designed and **frozen** first.
2. Software is written *after* the hardware specification is complete.
3. **Disadvantage:** Late software-hardware integration issues, missed optimization opportunities, longer time-to-market.

**Co-Design Flow:**
1. Start from a **unified executable specification** (e.g., SystemC).
2. Iteratively perform **partitioning**, **estimation**, and **refinement**.
3. **Interface synthesis** automatically generates communication glue.
4. **Co-simulation** validates both domains concurrently.
5. Feedback loop allows re-partitioning if constraints are not met.

**Key Differences (Tabular Form for 2 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional Flow | Co-Design Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Order | Sequential HW→SW | Concurrent |
| Specification | Separate per domain | Unified |
| Optimization | Local | Global |
| Time-to-Market | Long | Short |
| Verification | Late | Continuous (co-verification) |

**Diagrammatic Representation (Required for full marks):**
*Insert the flow diagram from Section 4.1 here, with arrows and feedback loops clearly marked.*

---

**(b) HW-SW Partitioning Problem (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Partitioning is the process of assigning each task in the system specification to either a **hardware unit** (ASIC, FPGA, dedicated accelerator) or a **software unit** (running on a general-purpose processor).

**Example (3 marks):** Consider a video encoder (H.264). The **motion estimation** block involves massive nested loops with high computational load — a perfect candidate for **hardware acceleration** (often a dedicated ASIC block). The **bitstream packaging** and **network protocol handling** are control-flow heavy and infrequent — better implemented in **software** on the application processor.

**Application of Amdahl's Law (3 marks):**
Suppose motion estimation is 70% of the encoding time and is 50× faster in dedicated hardware:
- $f = 0.7$, $S_{hw} = 50$

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.7) + \dfrac{0.7}{50}} = \dfrac{1}{0.3 + 0.014} = \dfrac{1}{0.314} \approx 3.18\times
$$

**Inference:** Despite 50× HW speed-up on the dominant block, the *overall* encoder runs only ~3.2× faster. This proves that **partitioning the wrong (non-dominant) function wastes silicon** — a key insight of co-design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — Tool/Verification Focus]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** What is **Co-Simulation**? Explain the **architecture of a typical HW-SW co-simulator** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **challenges** involved. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **interface synthesis problem** as a fundamental co-design issue. How are **device drivers and bus wrappers** automatically generated in modern co-design flows? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Co-Simulation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Co-simulation is the **synchronized, concurrent execution** of a hardware model (typically at the RTL level) and a software model (running on an Instruction Set Simulator, ISS) within a single simulation environment.

**Architecture (4 marks):** A typical co-simulator consists of:

1. **HW Simulator Backend** — e.g., ModelSim/Questa, Verilator (compiled), or Vivado XSim.
2. **SW Instruction Set Simulator (ISS)** — models the target processor (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 ISS).
3. **Backplane / Scheduler** — synchronizes both simulators using a master clock or event-driven mechanism.
4. **Communication Channel** — a shared memory region or socket-based RPC bridge that emulates the physical bus.
5. **Time-Decoupling Modules** — allow the fast SW simulator to run multiple cycles while the HW simulator processes one event.

**Challenges (2 marks):**
- **Speed Mismatch:** RTL is $10^3$–$10^6\times$ slower than native SW.
- **Clock Domain Mismatch:** HW uses multi-clock signals; SW uses single-threaded sequential execution.
- **State Synchronization:** Maintaining consistent global state across two simulators is non-trivial.

---

**(b) Interface Synthesis (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Interface synthesis is the *automatic generation* of the **communication logic, bus protocols, and device drivers** required to allow partitioned hardware and software components to exchange data.

**Bus Wrappers (3 marks):**
- The co-design tool analyzes the **data-width, protocol, and timing** of each HW block.
- It generates a **standardized bus wrapper** (e.g., AMBA APB/AHB/AXI, Wishbone) that translates the HW block's native signals to the system bus.
- This is done using **template-based code generation** — pre-written protocol templates are parameterized with the HW block's port list.

**Device Driver Generation (3 marks):**
- Modern tools (e.g., Xilinx Vivado HLS + PetaLinux, Cadence Stratus) introspect the HW register map.
- They auto-generate **C-level driver stubs** with functions like `device_init()`, `device_read()`, `device_write()`.
- The driver exposes **memory-mapped I/O** APIs that the application software calls — abstracting away the bus transactions.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** If you mention at least **two specific tools** (e.g., Vivado HLS, Cadence Stratus, Synopsys Platform Architect) by name, you gain a 1-mark "industry awareness" bonus.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Co-Design Definition:** Concurrent HW+SW design from a unified specification to jointly optimize **PPAC** (Performance, Power, Area, Cost).
- [x] **Fundamental Issues = 7 canonical problems:** Specification, Partitioning, Scheduling, Interface Synthesis, Co-Simulation, Co-Verification, DSE.
- [x] **Partitioning is the #1 Issue:** Decide HW vs SW based on **compute intensity, real-time deadline, power budget, and flexibility need**.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:**
$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{hw}}}
$$
Bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$ as $S_{hw} \to \infty$.
- [x] **Co-Simulation speed penalty:** $10^3$ to $10^6\times$ slowdown compared to native execution.
- [x] **Interface Synthesis = Bus Wrappers + Device Drivers** (auto-generated in modern flows).
- [x] **Tools to remember by name:** SystemC, SpecC, Verilator, Questa, Vivado HLS, Cadence Stratus.
- [x] **DSE size grows as $2^{N_{tasks}}$** — heuristic or ILP-based pruning is mandatory for $N > 20$.
- [x] **HW wins on:** Performance, Power Efficiency, Predictability.
- [x] **SW wins on:** Flexibility, NRE Cost, Time-to-Prototype, Upgradability.
- [x] **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in 14-mark answers — 2 to 3 marks reserved.
- [x] **Quantitative reasoning** (Amdahl's Law, cost equations) distinguishes a 9/14 answer from a 12/14 answer.
- [x] **Co-Design ≠ Co-Simulation** — co-design is the *methodology*, co-simulation is *one step* inside it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Models in Embedded Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computational Models in Embedded Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Models in Embedded Design** are formal mathematical abstractions used to represent the behavior, structure, and information flow of embedded systems. They provide a disciplined, technology-independent framework to capture *what* the system computes, *when* it computes, and *how* the concurrent/sequential activities synchronize.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for PCRAT302 (Module 4), the principal computational models used in embedded system design include:

1. **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** – A directed graph where nodes denote computations and edges denote data dependencies.
2. **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** – A representation of all paths the program might traverse during execution.
3. **Finite State Machine (FSM)** – A computational model with a finite set of states, transitions, and actions.
4. **Petri Net** – A bipartite graph with places and transitions modeling concurrency and synchronization.
5. **Discrete Event System (DES)** – A model where state changes occur only at discrete instants triggered by events.
6. **Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)** – A specialized DFG where each actor consumes and produces a fixed number of tokens per firing.
7. **Kahn Process Network (KPN)** – A network of concurrent processes communicating via unbounded FIFO channels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. Each **chef** is a *computational actor*. Ingredients (data tokens) move along **conveyor belts** (edges/channels). The kitchen operates by strict **recipes** (FSM rules). A station cannot begin frying until it has both the **patty AND the bun** (data-dependency / synchronization).
>
> - The **kitchen layout** is the *Data Flow Graph*.
> - The **head chef's checklist** is the *Finite State Machine*.
> - The **order ticket flow** is the *Control Flow Graph*.
> - The **pass-window synchronization** (where finished dishes wait until the waiter can collect them) is the *Petri Net Place/Transition concept*.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** All these models answer one core question — *How do we mathematically describe concurrency, timing, and data movement inside an embedded chip?* The choice of model depends on whether the system is **data-dominant**, **control-dominant**, or **event-dominant**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following metrics are central to embedded computational modeling and are highlighted in the KTU board exam:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Moore's Law transistor density | $N$ | doubles every **24 months** |
| State count for FSM | $Q$ | $Q = 2^n$ for $n$ flip-flops |
| Utilization (SDF) | $U$ | $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| Throughput | $T$ | **firings / second** |
| Latency | $L$ | **seconds / sample** |
| Petri Net reachability bound | $M(p)$ | non-negative integer tokens |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Computational Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 4 classifies computational models along **two fundamental axes**: the *dominant behavioral property* and the *timing assumption*.

#### A. Untimed (Functional) Models
- Capture only **what** the system computes.
- Examples: **Data Flow Graphs (DFG)**, **Kahn Process Networks (KPN)**.
- Used early in design for **functional verification** and **architecture exploration**.

#### B. Timed (Performance-Aware) Models
- Capture **when** and **how fast** the system computes.
- Examples: **Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)**, **Timed Petri Nets**, **Discrete Event Systems (DES)**.
- Used for **schedulability analysis**, **WCET estimation**, and **buffer sizing**.

### 2.2 Model-by-Model Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Data Flow Graph (DFG)
- **Node = Actor (Computation)**, **Edge = Data token path**.
- **Operational Rule:** An actor *fires* only when all its input edges carry the required tokens.
- **Properties:**
  - Models **inherent parallelism**.
  - **Deadlock** is detectable via cycle analysis.
- **Engineering Use:** Compilers for DSPs (e.g., TI C6x) and hardware synthesis tools.

#### 2.2.2 Control Flow Graph (CFG)
- **Node = Basic Block**, **Edge = Control transfer (branch/jump)**.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

  where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (usually $P = 1$).
- **Engineering Use:** Software testing, code-coverage analysis.

#### 2.2.3 Finite State Machine (FSM)
- **Components:** $\{Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F\}$
  - $Q$ = finite set of states
  - $\Sigma$ = input alphabet
  - $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ = transition function
  - $q_0$ = initial state
  - $F \subseteq Q$ = final/accepting states
- **Two Flavors:**
  - **Moore Machine:** Output depends only on the *current state*.
  - **Mealy Machine:** Output depends on the *current state AND current input*.
- **Engineering Use:** Protocol stacks (UART, I²C), elevator controllers, washing machines.

#### 2.2.4 Petri Net
- **Components:** $\{P, T, F, M_0\}$
  - $P$ = finite set of *places* (drawn as circles)
  - $T$ = finite set of *transitions* (drawn as bars)
  - $F \subseteq (P \times T) \cup (T \times P)$ = flow relation
  - $M_0$ = initial marking (token distribution)
- **Firing Rule:** A transition $t$ is *enabled* iff every input place has $\ge 1$ token.
- **Engineering Use:** Hardware arbitration, multiprocessor synchronization, safety-critical avionics.

#### 2.2.5 Discrete Event System (DES)
- **State evolves only at discrete event times** $t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots$.
- **Event-driven scheduler:** Maintains a *priority queue* of future events.
- **Engineering Use:** Real-time kernel simulation (VxWorks, FreeRTOS).

#### 2.2.6 Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)
- A DFG where each actor consumes/produces a **fixed, statically known** number of tokens per firing — the **production/consumption rates**.
- **Balance Equation (Lee & Messerschmitt):**

$$
\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = \vec{0}
$$

  where $\Gamma$ is the *topology matrix* and $\vec{q}$ is the *repetition vector*.
- **Engineering Use:** Streaming applications — MP3 decoders, software-defined radio.

#### 2.2.7 Kahn Process Network (KPN)
- **Deterministic** network of concurrent processes communicating via unbounded FIFO channels.
- **Blocking-read semantics:** $\texttt{read}(c)$ suspends if the channel is empty.
- **Engineering Use:** Multiprocessor SoC design (mapped onto NXP, TI multicore DSPs).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every 14-mark Part B question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ edges, $N$ nodes, $P$ components |
| 2 | State Count of FSM | $\vert Q \vert = 2^n$ | $n$ = number of state flip-flops |
| 3 | Moore Output | $y(t) = \lambda(s(t))$ | output is function of state only |
| 4 | Mealy Output | $y(t) = \lambda(s(t), x(t))$ | output is function of state + input |
| 5 | SDF Balance | $\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = \vec{0}$ | $\Gamma$ = topology matrix, $\vec{q}$ = repetition vector |
| 6 | Actor Firing Rate | $r_i = q_i / T$ | $q_i$ = repetitions of actor $i$, $T$ = period |
| 7 | Petri Net Conservation | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot M(p_i) = \text{const}$ | weighted sum of tokens invariant |
| 8 | Throughput | $T = \frac{1}{\text{cycle period}}$ | firings per unit time |
| 9 | Utilization | $U_i = \frac{\text{busy}_i}{\text{total time}}$ | $0 \le U_i \le 1$ |
| 10 | Latency | $L = t_{\text{out}} - t_{\text{in}}$ | response delay |
| 11 | DES Scheduler | $t_{\text{next}} = \min\{t_e \mid e \in \text{pending}\}$ | next event time |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Model | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| **DFG / SDF** | TI Code Composer Studio scheduler, digital filters |
| **CFG** | LLVM compiler pass, gcov test coverage tool |
| **FSM** | CAN bus protocol engine, traffic light controller |
| **Petri Net** | ARINC 653 avionics scheduler, railway interlocking |
| **DES** | OPNET / NS-3 network simulators, FreeRTOS |
| **KPN** | NXP TDM-based audio DSPs, Y-chart system design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

**Problem:** Given a CFG with $E = 11$ edges, $N = 9$ nodes, and $P = 1$ connected component, find the number of independent paths.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs from the KTU board standard:**

- $E = 11$
- $N = 9$
- $P = 1$

**Step 2 — State the canonical McCabe formula:**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 11 - 9 + 2(1) \\
&= 11 - 9 + 2 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- The CFG contains **4 linearly independent execution paths**.
- The **minimum number of test cases** required to achieve *branch coverage* is **4**.
- This is the canonical KTU numerical answer for an $E=11, N=9, P=1$ CFG.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — SDF Balance Equation and Repetition Vector

**Problem:** Consider an SDF graph with 3 actors $\{A, B, C\}$ and the following production/consumption rates:

| Edge | Producer | Consumer | Tokens produced | Tokens consumed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $e_1$ | $A$ | $B$ | 2 | 3 |
| $e_2$ | $B$ | $C$ | 1 | 2 |
| $e_3$ | $C$ | $A$ | 4 | 1 |

Find the **repetition vector** $\vec{q}$.

**Step 1 — Construct the topology matrix $\Gamma$.**

Convention used by KTU (Lee–Messerschmitt): $\Gamma[i][j]$ = tokens *consumed by actor $i$ from actor $j$*, else zero on the diagonal.

$$
\Gamma = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
3 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the balance equation $\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = \vec{0}$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
3 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} q_A \\ q_B \\ q_C \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand into scalar equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{row 1:} \quad & 0\cdot q_A + 0\cdot q_B + 1\cdot q_C = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_C = 0 \\
\text{row 2:} \quad & 3\cdot q_A + 0\cdot q_B + 0\cdot q_C = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_A = 0 \\
\text{row 3:} \quad & 0\cdot q_A + 2\cdot q_B + 0\cdot q_C = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_B = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Correct formulation using the KTU textbook convention.**

The proper KTU definition gives $\Gamma[i][j] = p_{ij} - c_{ij}$ where $p$ = produced, $c$ = consumed. Then:

$$
\Gamma = \begin{bmatrix}
-2 & 3 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 2 \\
1 & 0 & -4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Recompute using the corrected convention:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
-2 & 3 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 2 \\
1 & 0 & -4
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} q_A \\ q_B \\ q_C \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Reduce the homogeneous system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2q_A + 3q_B &= 0 \\
-q_B + 2q_C &= 0 \\
q_A - 4q_C &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

From the third equation: $q_A = 4q_C$.
From the second: $q_B = 2q_C$.
Choose the smallest positive integer $q_C = 1$.

**Step 7 — Final repetition vector:**

$$
\vec{q} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation for the KTU board examiner:**

- Actor $A$ fires **4 times**, $B$ fires **2 times**, $C$ fires **1 time** per *graph iteration*.
- The graph is **consistent** (a valid schedule exists).
- Minimum buffer sizes on edges can be computed by simulating this schedule.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — FSM State Count for $n$ Flip-Flops

**Problem:** How many states can be encoded using $n = 4$ flip-flops?

**Step 1 — Apply the state-count formula:**

$$
\vert Q \vert = 2^n
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert Q \vert &= 2^4 \\
&= 16 \text{ states}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Note for the examiner:**

- $n$ flip-flops encode up to **16 distinct states**.
- For a Moore machine, *unused* states can be exploited as "don't-care" states to minimize logic (KTU viva favorite).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Viva + Theory)

| Property | DFG | CFG | FSM | Petri Net | KPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Captures data flow | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ⚠ Indirect | ✅ Yes |
| Captures control flow | ⚠ Implicit | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Implicit | ⚠ Implicit |
| Models concurrency | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠ Partial | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Determinism | ⚠ May vary | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠ May vary | ✅ Yes |
| Token-based semantics | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Suitable for hardware | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Limited | ⚠ Limited |
| Suitable for software | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Yes |
| Tool support | SDF3, Ptolemy | LLVM, gcov | Stateflow, Questa | CPN Tools, PIPE | Ptolemy, ForSyDe |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Computational Models

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **mapping between the application domain and the appropriate computational model** — a high-yield KTU topic for design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Embedded Application] --> B{Is data dominant?}
    A --> C{Is control dominant?}
    A --> D{Is event dominant?}
    A --> E{Is concurrent and streaming?}

    B -- Yes --> F[Data Flow Graph DFG]
    F --> F1{Static rates known?}
    F1 -- Yes --> G[Synchronous Data Flow SDF]
    F1 -- No --> H[Kahn Process Network KPN]

    C -- Yes --> I[Finite State Machine FSM]
    I --> I1{Output depends on input?}
    I1 -- Yes --> J[Mealy Machine]
    I1 -- No --> K[Moore Machine]

    D -- Yes --> L[Discrete Event System DES]
    L --> L1[Event Scheduler]

    E -- Yes --> M[Petri Net]
    M --> M1{Continuous or discrete time?}
    M1 -- Discrete --> N[Discrete Petri Net]
    M1 -- Timed --> O[Timed Petri Net]
```

### 4.2 SDF Graph for a 3-Stage FIR Filter (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Input Source] -->|1 token| a1[Actor A: Tap Multiply]
    a1 -->|2 tokens| a2[Actor B: Accumulate]
    a2 -->|1 token| a3[Actor C: Output]
    a3 --> sink[Output Sink]
    a2 -->|feedback 1 token| a1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Actor A multiplies input by tap coefficient (produces 2 tokens downstream). Actor B accumulates (consumes 2, produces 1). Actor C delivers the final sample. The self-loop on A represents state feedback. The repetition vector for this canonical 3-actor SDF is $\vec{q} = [4, 2, 1]^T$ as derived in Section 3.2.

### 4.3 FSM State Diagram — Coffee Vending Machine (Moore Variant)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CoinInserted: insertCoin
    CoinInserted --> Selection: anyKey
    Selection --> Brewing: confirm
    Brewing --> Dispensing: brewDone
    Dispensing --> Idle: cupTaken
    Dispensing --> OutOfService: fault
    OutOfService --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Petri Net — Producer/Consumer Synchronization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1((P1: Produced)) --> t1[T1: Produce]
    t1 --> p2((P2: Buffer))
    p2 --> t2[T2: Consume]
    t2 --> p3((P3: Consumed))
    p2 -.delay.-> p1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Compare DFG and Petri Net"*, always mention that **Petri Nets generalize DFGs** by adding explicit *place capacity*, *inhibitor arcs*, and *token-based state*, which DFGs lack.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part A demands a *definition* + *one-line real-world example* + *one distinguishing feature*. Marks awarded: **Definition (2) + Example (1)**.

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a Data Flow Graph (DFG) and a Control Flow Graph (CFG). Give one application area for each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **DFG:** A directed graph where nodes are *computations* and edges are *data dependencies*. An actor can fire only when all its input data tokens are available. **Application:** DSP filter scheduling in TI Code Composer Studio.
- **CFG:** A directed graph where nodes are *basic blocks* of sequential code and edges are *control transfers* (branches, jumps, function calls). **Application:** Software testing — branch-coverage analysis using *gcov*.
- **Key Distinction:** DFG captures *parallelism inherent in data dependencies*, while CFG captures *sequential control structure of imperative programs*.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Moore machine? How does it differ from a Mealy machine?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Moore Machine:** Output $y(t) = \lambda(s(t))$ depends **only on the current state**.
  - Number of states is usually **one greater** than the equivalent Mealy machine for the same problem.
  - Outputs are *synchronous* with state transitions — **glitch-free**.
- **Mealy Machine:** Output $y(t) = \lambda(s(t), x(t))$ depends on **current state AND current input**.
  - Outputs can change *asynchronously* with input changes — **faster response, more glitches**.
- **KTU Shortcut:** *Moore = Output follows State. Mealy = Output follows State + Input.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Question: choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the components of a **Petri Net**. Define **enabling** and **firing** rules. Use a producer–consumer example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **repetition vector** for the SDF graph below and comment on its consistency: **(7 Marks)**

| Edge | Producer $\rightarrow$ Consumer | Production | Consumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| $e_1$ | $A \rightarrow B$ | 1 | 2 |
| $e_2$ | $B \rightarrow C$ | 3 | 1 |
| $e_3$ | $C \rightarrow A$ | 2 | 1 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Petri Net Components and Rules — 7 Marks**

A **Petri Net** is the tuple $\mathcal{N} = \{P, T, F, M_0\}$:

- $P$ = set of **places** (drawn as circles) — represent *conditions or buffers*.
- $T$ = set of **transitions** (drawn as bars) — represent *events or actions*.
- $F \subseteq (P \times T) \cup (T \times P)$ = **flow relation** (directed arcs).
- $M_0$ = **initial marking** — token distribution over places.

**Enabling Rule:** A transition $t \in T$ is **enabled** at marking $M$ iff every input place $p \in {}^{\bullet}t$ satisfies $M(p) \ge 1$.

**Firing Rule:** When an enabled transition $t$ fires, exactly one token is removed from each input place and one token is added to each output place $p \in t^{\bullet}$.

**Producer–Consumer Example (block diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1((P1: Free)) --> tProd[T1: Produce]
    tProd --> p2((P2: Produced))
    p2 --> tCons[T2: Consume]
    tCons --> p3((P3: Consumed))
    p2 -.inhibitor.-> tProd
```

**Marking Sequence (with $M_0 = (1, 0, 0)$):**

1. $M_0 = (1, 0, 0)$: $T_1$ is **enabled** (P1 has a token).
2. Fire $T_1$ → $M_1 = (0, 1, 0)$.
3. $T_2$ is now enabled → fire → $M_2 = (0, 0, 1)$.
4. $T_1$ cannot fire because P1 has no token. **Mutual exclusion achieved.**

**Marks Distribution (for examiner reference):**
- Components definition: 2
- Enabling rule: 1.5
- Firing rule: 1.5
- Producer–consumer diagram + sequence: 2

**(b) SDF Repetition Vector — 7 Marks**

Topology matrix using $\Gamma[i][j] = p_{ij} - c_{ij}$:

$$
\Gamma = \begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 0 & 1 \\
2 & -3 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & -2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Balance equation $\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = \vec{0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-q_A + q_C &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_C = q_A \\
2q_A - 3q_B &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_B = \tfrac{2}{3} q_A \\
q_B - 2q_C &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_B = 2q_C
\end{aligned}
$$

From $q_C = q_A$ and $q_B = 2q_C$: $q_B = 2q_A$. Substituting in row 2:

$$
2q_A - 3(2q_A) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -4q_A = 0
$$

This yields $q_A = 0$, which means **only the trivial solution exists**. The SDF graph is **inconsistent** — no finite repetition vector exists; the graph is *deadlocked under static scheduling*.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Matrix construction: 2
- Balance equation setup: 2
- Solving and consistency comment: 3

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Finite State Machine** model with the formal tuple. Design the FSM (Moore) for a **2-bit up/down counter**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **Kahn Process Network (KPN)** model. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of KPNs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) FSM for 2-bit Up/Down Counter — 7 Marks**

Formal tuple: $\mathcal{M} = \{Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, \lambda\}$ (Moore form).

- $Q = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3\}$ — four states for count values 00, 01, 10, 11.
- $\Sigma = \{U, D\}$ — up or down command.
- $q_0 = S_0$ (initial state, count = 00).
- $\delta$ — transition function (see diagram below).
- $\lambda$ — Moore output: each state emits its 2-bit count value.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S1: U
    S1 --> S2: U
    S2 --> S3: U
    S3 --> S0: U
    S0 --> S3: D
    S1 --> S0: D
    S2 --> S1: D
    S3 --> S2: D
    S0: 00
    S1: 01
    S2: 10
    S3: 11
```

State encoding using $n = 2$ flip-flops: $\vert Q \vert = 2^2 = 4$ states (verified).

**Marks Distribution:**
- Formal tuple: 2
- State/output table: 2
- Diagram: 2
- Flip-flop count justification: 1

**(b) Kahn Process Network — 7 Marks**

A **KPN** is a directed graph $\mathcal{K} = \{P, C\}$ where:

- $P$ = set of *sequential processes* (computations).
- $C$ = set of *unbounded FIFO channels* connecting processes.
- Processes communicate by **write** (non-blocking) and **read** (blocking) operations on channels.

**Block Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process 1] -->|ch1 FIFO| P2[Process 2]
    P2 -->|ch2 FIFO| P3[Process 3]
    P3 -->|ch3 FIFO| P1
    P1 -->|ch4 FIFO| P4[Process 4]
```

**Advantages:**
1. **Deterministic output** — same input always produces the same output regardless of process scheduling.
2. **Natural modeling of streaming / pipelined** embedded applications.

**Limitations:**
1. **Unbounded channel assumption** can lead to *memory exhaustion* in real implementations.
2. **Deadlock detection is NP-hard** in general KPNs (solvable in polynomial time only for SDF subclasses).

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition + diagram: 3
- Two advantages: 2
- Two limitations: 2

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in 'Computational Models' questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Moore and Mealy output equations.** Students often write $y(t) = \lambda(s(t), x(t))$ for Moore — *this loses 2 marks instantly*. Memorize: Moore = state only.
> 2. **Using the wrong sign convention in the SDF topology matrix.** KTU follows Lee & Messerschmitt: $\Gamma[i][j] = p_{ij} - c_{ij}$. Using the *consumed-minus-produced* convention loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 'P = connected components' term in McCabe's formula** — should be $V(G) = E - N + 2P$, not $E - N + 1$. When $P = 1$, both formulas coincide, but full marks require the general form.
> 4. **Drawing Petri Nets with transitions as circles and places as bars** — strict opposite of convention. **Bars = transitions, Circles = places.**
> 5. **Saying "Petri Net is the same as FSM."** They are *not* equivalent. FSMs cannot model *true concurrency* with shared resources; Petri Nets can.
> 6. **Omitting the 'blocking read' semantics of KPN** — losing 1 mark in any KPN question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**

- ✅ A **DFG** fires actors based on *data availability*; a **CFG** orders basic blocks by *control transfer*.
- ✅ **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — minimum number of test cases for branch coverage.
- ✅ **FSM Tuple:** $\{Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, \lambda\}$ for Moore; add input to $\lambda$ for Mealy.
- ✅ State count: $\vert Q \vert = 2^n$ flip-flop-encoded states.
- ✅ **Petri Net Tuple:** $\{P, T, F, M_0\}$ — *places* are circles, *transitions* are bars.
- ✅ **Petri Net Firing Rule:** *enable* when all input places have $\ge 1$ token; *fire* moves 1 token from each input to each output place.
- ✅ **SDF Balance Equation:** $\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = \vec{0}$ gives the **repetition vector**.
- ✅ **Repetition Vector** interpretation: number of firings per actor in one *graph iteration*.
- ✅ **KPN** = deterministic, concurrent processes with unbounded FIFOs and *blocking read*.
- ✅ **DES** = state changes only at discrete event times; uses a *priority queue* scheduler.
- ✅ **Moore vs. Mealy trade-off:** Moore = glitch-free, slower; Mealy = faster, glitchy.
- ✅ **Petri Net vs. FSM:** Petri Nets can model *true concurrency* with synchronization; FSMs cannot.
- ✅ **Tool names to mention (impresses the examiner):** *Ptolemy II* (SDF/KPN), *Stateflow* (FSM), *CPN Tools* (Petri Net), *OPNET* (DES).
- ✅ **KTU viva one-liner:** *"DFG answers WHAT to compute, FSM answers WHEN to change state, Petri Net answers HOW to synchronize concurrent resources."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Firmware Design Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Embedded Firmware Design Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded Firmware** is the specialized software program permanently stored in the read-only memory (ROM, Flash, or EEPROM) of an embedded system that directly controls the hardware resources, executes application logic, and orchestrates peripheral interactions without the abstraction of a full operating system (in bare-metal contexts).

An **Embedded Firmware Design Approach** is the **architectural methodology** used to structure, organize, and schedule the code that runs on the microcontroller's firmware layer. It defines *how* tasks are sequenced, *when* they execute, and *how* they communicate with hardware and with each other.

The four principal approaches recognized in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| # | Approach | Core Philosophy |
|---|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Super-Loop (Foreground/Background)** | A single infinite loop polling tasks sequentially. |
| 2 | **Interrupt-Driven (Event-Driven)** | Hardware events trigger service routines asynchronously. |
| 3 | **State-Machine Based** | System behavior modeled as a finite set of discrete states. |
| 4 | **RTOS-Based (Multitasking)** | A real-time kernel schedules multiple threads. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Single Cook vs. The Head Chef"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a small roadside tea stall.
> - **Super-loop** is a **single cook** who must *personally* keep checking the kettle ("Is water boiling yet? Is it? Is it now?"). The moment a customer orders, he stops checking and serves — but if a kettle boils over while he is busy, it is a disaster. He is the only decision-maker.
> - **Interrupt-driven** is the **same cook with a bell** tied to the kettle. The bell rings *only* when the water actually boils. He can chop glasses, take money, and clean cups — the bell will interrupt him exactly when needed. Much more efficient.
> - **State-machine** is a **cook who follows a strict recipe card** — "If state = BOILING and lid is up, then state = STEEPING for 30 s." The recipe enforces the *order* of operations, eliminating confusion.
> - **RTOS-based** is a **large restaurant with a Head Chef (kernel)** coordinating many **sous-chefs (tasks)**. The head chef allocates stove burners (CPU time) and ensures the appetizer (high-priority task) is always ready before the dessert (low-priority task).

This mental picture is exactly how a microcontroller is structured. A designer **chooses** an approach based on task complexity, real-time deadlines, and hardware constraints.

## 1.3 Why Design Approach Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme outcome **CO4** (of PCRAT302) requires the student to *"design and develop firmware for embedded applications using appropriate architectural approaches."* Marks are awarded for *justifying* the choice of approach with respect to **latency, determinism, code reusability, and power consumption**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Super-Loop (Polled / Foreground–Background) Architecture

This is the **simplest and most widely taught** firmware pattern. The CPU runs one `while(1)` infinite loop that calls each task function in a fixed cyclic order. Background code is the loop itself; the foreground consists of asynchronous Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs).

### 2.1.1 Structural Logic

- **Initialization Phase** — Executed **once** at reset: configure clock, GPIO, peripherals, and global variables.
- **Super-Loop Body** — A `for(;;)` or `while(1)` that sequentially invokes task routines: `task1(); task2(); task3(); ...`.
- **ISRs** — Triggered asynchronously by hardware; they set *flags* that the background loop polls and clears.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" and "How"

- **Why it works:** Determinism is predictable *if* all task execution times are bounded and known. Total cycle time = sum of individual task times.
- **How it fails:** A long task (e.g., a blocking `delay_ms(1000)`) starves every other task. There is no preemption in the background. Worst-case response time of the *n*th task equals the sum of all earlier task durations.

## 2.2 The Interrupt-Driven (Event-Driven) Architecture

In this approach, the main loop is often either empty or runs a low-priority "idle" task, while **most of the real work is done inside ISRs** or in *deferred-procedure-call* (DPC) functions that the ISRs signal.

- **Why:** Achieves sub-microsecond response to hardware events (button press, UART byte, ADC end-of-conversion).
- **How:** A peripheral raises an IRQ → the CPU saves context, branches to the ISR, services the device, restores context, and resumes the loop.
- **Constraint:** ISRs must be **short, non-blocking, and re-entrant-safe**, because they preempt whatever the background was doing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Deferred Procedure Call (Bottom-Half Processing):** When an ISR is too long, it sets a flag and lets a normal-priority background function do the heavy lifting. This keeps ISR latency minimal.

## 2.3 The State-Machine Based Architecture

A **Finite State Machine (FSM)** models the system as a set of discrete states, transition conditions, and actions. Two principal FSM flavors are used in firmware:

1. **Moore Machine** — Output depends *only* on the *current state*.
2. **Mealy Machine** — Output depends on the *current state* **and** the *current input*.

```
Standard FSM Transition:
   State(i)  --[ Event E ]-->  State(j)  /  Action A
```

- **Why it works:** Enforces a strict, auditable flow; eliminates illegal states; excellent for protocol stacks, menu UIs, motor control.
- **How it is coded:** Typically as a `switch(state)` inside the super-loop, or as a *state-transition table* in RAM/Flash for complex systems.

## 2.4 The RTOS-Based (Multitasking) Architecture

A **Real-Time Operating System** kernel (e.g., FreeRTOS, RT-Thread, μC/OS-II) provides:

- **Tasks (Threads)** — Independent functions with their own stacks.
- **Scheduler** — Preemptive priority-based or round-robin.
- **Inter-Task Communication** — Queues, semaphores, mutexes, event flags, mailboxes.
- **Synchronization Primitives** — For shared-resource protection.

### 2.4.1 Scheduling Types

- **Preemptive Priority Scheduling** — A higher-priority task that becomes ready immediately preempts the running task. Guarantees bounded latency for critical work.
- **Cooperative (Non-Preemptive) Scheduling** — Each task yields voluntarily; lower overhead, but a single misbehaving task can hang the system.
- **Round-Robin / Time-Slicing** — Equal time quantum per ready task at the same priority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The scheduling algorithm's worst-case **task latency** is the metric examiners love to test. Latency = (longest time a higher-priority task can hold the CPU) + (context-switch time).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as a quick-reference sheet for exam answers and numerical problems.

| Parameter / Metric | Super-Loop | Interrupt-Driven | State-Machine | RTOS-Based |
|--------------------|------------|------------------|---------------|------------|
| **Worst-Case Response** $T_{resp}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} T_{task,i}$ | ISR vector latency (≈ cycles) | Sum of states until event | Bounded by scheduler |
| **Code Complexity** | Very Low | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| **Determinism** | Medium | High | Very High | Very High (if designed) |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor (busy wait) | High (sleep + wake) | High | Medium |
| **Reusability** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Memory Footprint** | Minimal (≈ 100 B) | Low (≈ 200 B) | Low–Medium | High (1–10 KB+) |
| **Best Use-Case** | Simple toys, blinking LED | Sensor acquisition | Protocol parsers, UI | Multi-sensor, motor + comms |
| **CPU Utilization Formula** | $U = \dfrac{\sum T_{task}}{T_{cycle}}$ | $U = \dfrac{\sum T_{ISR}}{T_{cycle}}$ | $U = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$ | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ |

### 2.5.1 Fundamental Equations (CPU Utilization Bound)

For a set of $N$ periodic tasks under a **preemptive priority scheduler**, the **Liu & Layland** utilization bound for *Rate Monotonic Scheduling* is:

$$
U_{bound} = N \cdot \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
\text{Schedulability test: } \quad U_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;\leq\; N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)
$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time of task $i$ (seconds).
- $T_i$ = Period of task $i$ (seconds).
- $N$ = Number of tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $N \to \infty$, $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx \mathbf{0.693}$. This is the famous **69.3% Liu–Layland bound**. The 2024 scheme expects students to *quote this number* for full marks in design questions.

### 2.5.2 Interrupt Latency Equation

$$
L_{ISR} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{vector} + t_{exec} + t_{restore}
$$

Where:
- $t_{detect}$ = Time for peripheral to assert IRQ line.
- $t_{save}$ = Registers pushed on stack.
- $t_{vector}$ = Time to fetch ISR address from vector table.
- $t_{exec}$ = Time to execute ISR body.
- $t_{restore}$ = Stack pop + return-from-interrupt.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Anti-lock Braking System):** Hard real-time, multiple sensors → **RTOS-based** approach (AUTOSAR OS).
- **Digital Wristwatch:** One display, one button → **Super-loop** is sufficient.
- **Microwave Oven UI:** Time-setting, mode selection → **State-machine** approach.
- **IoT Sensor Node (battery-powered):** Spend 99% time in sleep, wake on GPIO interrupt → **Interrupt-driven** approach.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations that a student can reproduce in an exam to earn full method marks. Every algebraic and logical step is shown.

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time in a Super-Loop

### Problem Statement
A super-loop contains three tasks with worst-case execution times $T_1$, $T_2$, and $T_3$. Derive the maximum latency for Task 3.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Define the cyclic order.**
The main loop executes Task 1 → Task 2 → Task 3 in that order, then repeats.

**Step 2 — Express when Task 3 can start.**
Task 3 can start only **after** both Task 1 and Task 2 have completed in the *current* cycle. Therefore:

$$
t_{start,3} = t_{end,1} + t_{end,2} - t_{cycle,begin}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the response time as the difference between finish and arrival.**
Assume Task 3's request arrives just **after** it has just been serviced in cycle $k$. It must then wait one full cycle minus its own execution:

$$
R_3 = (T_1 + T_2) + T_3
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for n tasks in order.**
For a system of $n$ tasks, the response time of task $k$ (where $1 \le k \le n$) is:

$$
\boxed{R_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i = T_1 + T_2 + \dots + T_k}
$$

**Step 5 — Note the worst-case nature.**
This is the **upper bound**; the *best-case* response is $T_k$ (if the request arrives exactly at the start of the task slot).

**Interpretation:** A long $T_1$ task can starve Task 3 for an unacceptably long time. This is the structural weakness of the super-loop.

## 3.2 Derivation: Liu–Layland Utilization Bound

### Problem Statement
For $N$ independent periodic tasks scheduled by Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), show that the sufficient (but not necessary) utilization bound is $U_{bound} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Define the utilization.**
For each task $i$ with execution time $C_i$ and period $T_i$, its CPU fraction is $u_i = C_i / T_i$.

**Step 2 — Total utilization.**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Critical instant concept.**
Liu & Layland proved the worst-case response occurs when all tasks become ready **simultaneously** ("critical instant"). Under RMS, the highest-frequency (smallest period) task gets the highest priority.

**Step 4 — Construct the response time recurrence.**
For the lowest-priority task $N$, the worst-case response time satisfies:

$$
R_N = C_N + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_N}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ceiling inequality.**
Since $\lceil x \rceil \le x + 1$:

$$
R_N \le C_N + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} \left( \frac{R_N}{T_j} + 1 \right) C_j
$$

**Step 6 — Bounding step for full derivation.**
By substitution and using the property that the bound is tightest when $C_j/T_j$ is maximized, the closed-form sufficient condition becomes:

$$
U \le N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Limiting case.**
As $N \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

$$
\boxed{\lim_{N \to \infty} U_{bound} = \ln(2) \approx 69.31\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** A system using RMS is **guaranteed** to meet all deadlines if total utilization is at or below $N(2^{1/N} - 1)$. For $N = 3$ tasks, this is $3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx \mathbf{0.7798}$ or **77.98%**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Interrupt Latency of an ARM Cortex-M

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Hardware detection.**
The peripheral asserts the IRQ line. The CPU samples it at the end of the current instruction. Let $t_{detect} \le T_{cycle}$ (1 clock).

**Step 2 — Stack push.**
Cortex-M automatically pushes R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR — eight 32-bit registers:

$$
t_{save} = 8 \times T_{cycle} = 8 \text{ cycles (worst case, no wait-states)}
$$

**Step 3 — Vector fetch.**
The vector table contains the ISR address; one memory read with possible wait states. Assume Flash wait = 2:

$$
t_{vector} = 1 \times 3 = 3 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — ISR execution body.**
Let the body take $C_{ISR}$ cycles. For a simple GPIO toggle, $C_{ISR} = 4$.

**Step 5 — Stack pop and return.**

$$
t_{restore} = 8 \times T_{cycle} + 3 = 11 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 6 — Total latency.**

$$
L_{ISR} = 1 + 8 + 3 + 4 + 11 = 27 \text{ cycles}
$$

At a 72 MHz clock:

$$
L_{ISR} = \frac{27}{72 \times 10^6} \approx 0.375 \; \mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{ISR} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{CPU}} \;\text{ seconds}}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Q: A super-loop has three tasks with execution times $T_1 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 3$ ms, $T_3 = 1$ ms. Tasks run in order 1 → 2 → 3. Find (i) total cycle time, (ii) CPU utilization, (iii) worst-case response time of Task 3.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total cycle time.**

$$
T_{cycle} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — CPU utilization.**
Assuming all three tasks consume 6 ms in a 6 ms cycle (no idle time):

$$
U = \frac{\sum T_i}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case response time of Task 3.**

$$
R_3 = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Observation.**
Task 3's best case is $1$ ms (if it just missed its turn) and worst case is $6$ ms. The **jitter** is $5$ ms — extremely poor for time-sensitive work, which is why a super-loop is unsuitable for real-time sensor fusion.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Approach to Use?

| Constraint / Requirement | Recommended Approach |
|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Single blinking LED / 1 button | Super-loop |
| Battery-powered, sleep most of the time | Interrupt-driven |
| UI menu, washing-machine controller | State-machine |
| Motor + UART + sensors simultaneously | RTOS-based |
| Strict < 1 ms deadline on event | Interrupt + RTOS hybrid |
| Memory < 4 KB Flash | Super-loop or State-machine |
| Multiple developers on different modules | RTOS-based (task separation) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use Mermaid syntax. Per the engine safeguards, every node ID is alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and labels contain only clean uppercase text and special characters safe for parsing.

## 4.1 Super-Loop Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[RESET VECTOR] --> A1[INIT CLOCK AND PERIPHERALS]
    A1 --> A2[INIT GLOBAL VARIABLES]
    A2 --> A3{{SUPER LOOP START}}
    A3 --> B1[EXECUTE TASK 1]
    B1 --> B2[EXECUTE TASK 2]
    B2 --> B3[EXECUTE TASK 3]
    B3 --> B4[EXECUTE TASK N]
    B4 --> A3
    X1[ISR UART RX] -.->|INTERRUPT| A3
    X2[ISR TIMER] -.->|INTERRUPT| A3
    X3[ISR GPIO] -.->|INTERRUPT| A3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The solid arrows show the **background** flow (main loop).
- The dashed arrows from `ISR *` blocks show **foreground** events that pre-empt the loop and return control to it.
- Notice the linear, deterministic order — easy to reason about, but inflexible.

## 4.2 Interrupt-Driven Architecture with Deferred Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PERIPHERAL EVENT] --> P2[NVIC IRQ LINE]
    P2 --> P3[CPU SAVES CONTEXT]
    P3 --> P4[ISR SETS EVENT FLAG]
    P4 --> P5[CPU RESTORES CONTEXT]
    P5 --> P6[MAIN LOOP POLLS FLAG]
    P6 -->|FLAG IS SET| P7[DEFERRED HANDLER RUNS]
    P7 --> P6
    P6 -->|FLAG IS CLEAR| P8[ENTER SLEEP MODE]
    P8 -->|WAKES ON NEXT EVENT| P1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The ISR is intentionally **short** (only flag-setting). The heavy lifting happens in `DEFERRED HANDLER`, which is regular background code. The CPU spends most of its time in `SLEEP MODE`, conserving power.

## 4.3 State Machine Architecture (Mealy/Moore)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_IDLE
    S0_IDLE --> S1_READY : EVENT START PRESSED
    S1_READY --> S2_RUN : EVENT TIMER TICK
    S2_RUN --> S3_PAUSE : EVENT PAUSE PRESSED
    S3_PAUSE --> S2_RUN : EVENT RESUME PRESSED
    S2_RUN --> S4_DONE : EVENT COUNTDOWN ZERO
    S3_PAUSE --> S0_IDLE : EVENT STOP PRESSED
    S4_DONE --> S0_IDLE : EVENT ACK RECEIVED
    S0_IDLE --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each node is a discrete state. Edges are labeled with `EVENT` (input condition). The system can never occupy a "ghost" state — every transition is enumerated, which is the key advantage over a free-form super-loop.

## 4.4 RTOS-Based Multitasking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KERNEL[RTOS KERNEL]
        SCH[SCHEDULER]
        SEM[SEMAPHORE POOL]
        QUE[QUEUE POOL]
        TIM[TIMER TICK ISR]
    end
    TIM --> SCH
    SCH --> T1[TASK 1 PRIORITY 5 SENSOR READ]
    SCH --> T2[TASK 2 PRIORITY 3 CONTROL LOOP]
    SCH --> T3[TASK 3 PRIORITY 1 LOG TELEMETRY]
    T1 -->|SIGNAL| SEM
    T2 -->|WAIT| SEM
    T2 -->|SEND DATA| QUE
    T3 -->|RECEIVE| QUE
    SCH --> IDLE[IDLE TASK]
    IDLE -->|NO WORK| PWR[LOW POWER MODE]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **KERNEL** (scheduler + IPC primitives + tick ISR) is the central authority.
- Three tasks at different priorities share the CPU under preemptive scheduling.
- Inter-Task Communication (ITC) is via **semaphores** (for synchronization) and **queues** (for data transfer).
- When no task is ready, the **IDLE task** runs, which can place the MCU in low-power mode.

## 4.5 Comparative Block Architecture — Hybrid Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HYBRID[HYBRID EMBEDDED FIRMWARE]
        H1[HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER HAL]
        H2[DEVICE DRIVERS]
        H3[STATE MACHINE ENGINE]
        H4[INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINES]
        H5[OPTIONAL RTOS TASKS]
    end
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
    H4 --> H3
    H2 --> H5
    H3 --> APP[APPLICATION LOGIC]
    H5 --> APP
    APP --> OUT[ACTUATORS / DISPLAY / NETWORK]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Real industrial firmware almost always **combines** approaches. HAL handles registers, ISRs handle fast events, the state machine enforces logic, and an optional RTOS handles parallel activities. The application layer never touches hardware directly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Questions below are modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) patterns for **PCRAT302 — Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems**, Module 4. Marks are tagged to the official split: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Distinguish between a super-loop based firmware design and an interrupt-driven firmware design. Mention one scenario where each is preferred."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Super-Loop | Interrupt-Driven |
|-----------|------------|------------------|
| Execution model | Sequential polling in `while(1)` | Event-driven via ISRs |
| Response to event | Worst-case = sum of earlier tasks | Worst-case = ISR vector latency |
| Power efficiency | Poor (busy wait possible) | Excellent (CPU sleeps) |
| Code complexity | Very low | Medium (vector table, context save) |

- **Super-loop preferred for:** Simple LED-blinking toy, digital watch with no precise timing requirement.
- **Interrupt-driven preferred for:** Battery-powered IoT sensor node waking on external event.

**(Awarding 3 marks: 1 mark for table-style distinction, 1 mark per correct use-case.)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is a Finite State Machine (FSM) in the context of embedded firmware? State the difference between a Moore and a Mealy machine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **FSM Definition (1 mark):** A Finite State Machine is a computational model where the system is in exactly one of a finite number of *states* at any time, and transitions between states occur only in response to *events*. It is widely used to design deterministic control logic in embedded firmware (e.g., washing-machine controllers, protocol parsers).

> **Difference (2 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Moore Machine | Mealy Machine |
> |--------|---------------|---------------|
> | Output depends on | Current **state only** | Current **state + input** |
> | Output timing | Synchronous with state change | Asynchronous with input change |
> | Typical use | Clocked display drivers | Asynchronous serial receivers (UART) |
> | Number of states | Usually more | Usually fewer |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE format: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as two alternative choices, each with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, PCRAT302]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **super-loop (foreground/background) firmware architecture**. Derive the expression for the worst-case response time of the $k^{th}$ task in a system of $n$ tasks. (7 marks)

**(b)** A super-loop has three tasks with worst-case execution times $T_1 = 4$ ms, $T_2 = 2$ ms, $T_3 = 3$ ms executed in that order. Calculate (i) the total cycle time, (ii) the CPU utilization, and (iii) the worst-case response time of Task 2. If the system must respond to an external interrupt within 0.5 ms, is the super-loop acceptable? Justify. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
